title: - "Distinct roots means distinct complex numbers z, not multiplicities.

identity.short_name: Unit-circle roots of (z+1)^6 - (z^3+1)^2

problem_spec.summary: Count the distinct roots on the unit circle of a polynomial designed so that, after the substitution z=e^{iθ}, both terms share the same complex phase and the equation collapses to a real trigonometric identity solvable via the triple-angle formula.
givens: Restrict attention to roots z with |z|=1.
ask: Determine the number of distinct complex roots of P(z) on the unit circle.

parameter(s).name: n
parameter(s).purpose: Defines the family P_n(z)=(z+1)^{2n}-(z^n+1)^2; the instance uses n=3 (so degree 6 vs squared degree 3).

invariant(s).name: unit_circle_phase_alignment
invariant(s).statement: For z=e^{iθ}, (z+1)^{2n} and (z^n+1)^2 both carry the phase factor e^{inθ}, so P_n(e^{iθ})=0 reduces to a purely real equation 2^{2n-2} cos^{2n}(θ/2) = cos^2(nθ/2).

invariant(s).name: triple_angle_closure_for_n3
invariant(s).statement: When n=3, the reduced equation involves cos(3x), allowing elimination via cos(3x)=4cos^3 x - 3cos x to obtain a polynomial equation in cos x.

essential_assumption(s): z lies on the unit circle so z=e^{iθ} with θ real.
Cancelling the common factor e^{i3θ} is valid since it is never zero.

title: - Python is optional (the arithmetic is powers of 2), but useful for sanity-checking the Aut(Q) formula and the inclusion-exclusion pattern on smaller exponent

identity.short_name: Count H\leqG with g\inH and G/H≅(2^5,2^2), mod 1000

subdomain(s): finite abelian groups and invariant factors
quotienting by a fixed cyclic subgroup
topic_path: Finite abelian p-groups and subgroup counts

problem_spec.summary: Given a finite abelian 2-group G in invariant-factor form, a fixed element g\inG, and a target quotient type Q, count subgroups H\leqG such that g\inH and G/H≅Q. Use the kernel-surjection correspondence with the extra constraint to reduce to counting surjections from G/⟨g⟩ onto Q modulo Aut(Q), then reduce the count mod 1000.
givens: H ranges over subgroups of G such that g \in H and G/H ≅ Q.
Output is requested modulo 1000.
ask: Compute N = #{H \leq G : g \in H and G/H ≅ Q} and output N (mod 1000).

parameter(s).name: G_invariant_exponents
parameter(s).domain: nonincreasing tuple (a1,...,ar) with ai>=1 defining G≅⊕ Z/2^{ai}Z
parameter(s).purpose: ambient finite abelian 2-group

parameter(s).name: fixed_element_description
parameter(s).domain: specified element g in G, typically a 2^t multiple of a generator in one summand
parameter(s).purpose: imposes the constraint g \in H

parameter(s).name: Q_invariant_exponents
parameter(s).domain: nonincreasing tuple (b1,...,bs) with bj>=1 defining Q≅⊕ Z/2^{bj}Z
parameter(s).purpose: required quotient type

parameter(s).name: modulus
parameter(s).purpose: final reduction (here m=1000)

invariant(s).name: containment_reduction_to_quotient
invariant(s).statement: Subgroups H\leqG with g\inH and G/H≅Q correspond to kernels of surjections f:G\toQ with f(g)=0, equivalently to surjections G/⟨g⟩\toQ. Hence N = |Epi(G/⟨g⟩,Q)| / |Aut(Q)|.

invariant(s).name: hom_count_depends_only_on_invariant_factors
invariant(s).statement: |Hom(G,Q)| is determined by the exponents via |Hom(Z/2^a,Q)| = |Q[2^a]| = 2^{Σ_j min(a,bj)}.

invariant(s).name: epimorphisms_via_maximal_subgroups_rank2
invariant(s).statement: For rank-2 Q, maximal subgroups have index 2 and correspond to the 3 nonzero lines in Q/2Q≅(Z/2)^2; inclusion-exclusion over these maximal subgroups yields |Epi(G,Q)|.

essential_assumption(s): All groups are finite abelian 2-groups written in invariant-factor form.
The condition g\inH is interpreted literally as subgroup containment.
Aut(Q) acts freely on Epi(G,Q) by postcomposition, so dividing counts kernels.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The constraint g\inH forces reduction to G' = G/⟨g⟩. The kernel of any surjection G'\toQ gives such an H, and two surjections have the same kernel iff they differ by Aut(Q). Since |Epi(G',Q)| and |Aut(Q)| are uniquely determined from invariant factors and maximal-subgroup inclusion-exclusion, N mod 1000 is unique.

technique(s).name: reduce_by_fixed_element
technique(s).purpose: replace the constrained subgroup count by an unconstrained count on G' = G/⟨g⟩

technique(s).name: kernel_surjection_correspondence
technique(s).purpose: convert subgroup counting to counting surjections modulo Aut(Q)

technique(s).name: count_hom_sets_by_invariant_factors
technique(s).purpose: compute |Hom(Gprime,Q)| and similar Hom-counts to maximal subgroups

technique(s).name: epimorphism_count_via_maximal_subgroups
technique(s).purpose: compute |Epi(Gprime,Q)| using inclusion-exclusion over index-2 maximal subgroups of Q

technique(s).name: compute_aut_size_for_rank2_2group
technique(s).purpose: compute |Aut(Q)| by counting endomorphisms with invertible action on Q/2Q

technique(s).name: final_mod_postprocess
technique(s).purpose: compute N and reduce mod 1000

theorem(s).name: first_isomorphism_theorem
theorem(s).role: kernels of surjections correspond to quotient isomorphism types

theorem(s).name: classification_of_finite_abelian_p_groups
theorem(s).role: ensures invariant-factor form and Hom-count formulas

theorem(s).name: maximal_subgroups_via_Q_mod_2Q
theorem(s).role: maximal subgroups of a rank-2 2-group correspond to lines in Q/2Q

theorem(s).name: endomorphism_is_automorphism_iff_mod_2_is_invertible
theorem(s).role: for finite 2-groups, surjectivity is equivalent to surjectivity on Q/2Q (Burnside/Frattini viewpoint)

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: homomorphism_kernel_image

critical_trick(s): Enforce g\inH by passing to G/⟨g⟩, then count surjections onto Q modulo Aut(Q).
Use inclusion-exclusion over the three maximal subgroups of Q determined by Q/2Q≅(Z/2)^2.

library(s).name: sympy
library(s).reason: exact integer arithmetic and symbolic power-of-2 manipulations (optional)

library(s).name: itertools
library(s).reason: brute-force validation on smaller exponent instances (optional)

task(s).reason: compute Hom-counts and apply inclusion-exclusion for rank-2 quotients

task(s).name: verify_aut_formula_small_cases
task(s).reason: brute-check |Aut(Z/2^a ⊕ Z/2^b)| for small (a,b) to validate the counting rule

task(s).name: mod_1000_reduce
task(s).reason: reduce the final subgroup count modulo 1000

identity.short_name: Count Aut(R)-orbits of ideals with dim_F2(I/2I)=2 in Z4[x]/(x^3,2x)

subdomain(s): ideal-lattice group actions and orbit counting (Burnside)

problem_spec.summary: In a finite quotient ring R, consider the action of Aut(R) on the set of proper nonzero ideals. Restrict to those ideals whose mod-2 quotient I/2I has prescribed F2-dimension, and count the number of Aut(R)-orbits on that constrained set.
givens: Aut(R) acts on ideals by φ \cdot I = φ(I).
= { ideals I ⊊ R : I \neq (0), dim_{F_2}(I/2I)=2 }.
ask: Compute the number of Aut(R)-orbits on .

parameter(s).name: base_ring
parameter(s).purpose: controls 2-torsion and nilpotence behavior (here forces 2 \cdot =0 for proper ideals).

parameter(s).name: quotient_relations
parameter(s).purpose: determines ring structure (here x^3=0 and 2x=0).

parameter(s).name: invariance_scope
parameter(s).purpose: shifts from "Aut-invariant ideals to counting orbits under the full automorphism action.

parameter(s).name: mod_p_dimension_constraint
parameter(s).purpose: selects a constrained stratum of ideals (here p=2, dimension 2).

parameter(s).name: orbit_count_method
parameter(s).purpose: specifies orbit enumeration via fixed-point counts.

invariant(s).name: normal_form_in_R
invariant(s).statement: Every element has a unique representative a+bx+cx^2 with a\inZ/4Z and b,c\inF2, since x^3=0 and 2x=0.

invariant(s).name: maximal_ideal_and_2_annihilation
invariant(s).statement: The unique maximal ideal is =(2,x) consisting of elements with even constant term, and 2=0. Hence for any proper ideal I⊆, we have 2I=0 so I/2I≅I as F2-vector spaces.

invariant(s).name: ideal_as_T_invariant_subspace
invariant(s).statement: For proper ideals I⊆, the ideal condition is equivalent to I being an F2-subspace of  invariant under multiplication-by-x, i.e. T(I)⊆I where T(y)=xy.

invariant(s).name: automorphisms_determined_by_image_of_x
invariant(s).statement: Any automorphism fixes constants and is determined by x↦x+αx^2+2β with α,β\inF2; thus |Aut(R)|=4. Additionally x^2 is fixed by all automorphisms because (x+αx^2+2β)^2=x^2.

essential_assumption(s): Automorphisms preserve 1 and the defining relations x^3=0, 2x=0.
The quotient I/2I is viewed as an F2-vector space via R\toR/2R.
Proper ideals lie in the maximal ideal  and thus satisfy 2I=0 in this ring.

uniqueness_certificate(s).statement: The constrained set  is finite and is completely determined by the invariant-subspace description: dim_F2(I/2I)=2 ⇔ |I|=4 ⇔ I is a 2D T-invariant subspace of  under T(y)=xy. This yields a finite explicit list.

Aut(R) is finite and explicitly parameterized by (α,β)\inF2^2. The orbit count is uniquely determined by the action on the explicit list of ideals, equivalently by Burnside's lemma applied to fixed-point counts.

technique(s).name: explicit_element_normal_form_and_ideal_size_bounds
technique(s).purpose: obtain element normal forms and deduce finiteness and sizes.

title: Degree-4 irreducibles over F3 with parity-projection irreducible over F2

identity.short_name: Degree-4 irreducibles over F3 with parity-projection irreducible over F2

subdomain(s): constrained coefficient patterns via digitwise reduction

problem_spec.summary: Count degree-4 polynomials P over F_3 that are irreducible over F_3, subject to the constraint that the polynomial Q over F_2 formed by reducing each coefficient of P (viewed as an integer in {0,1,2}) modulo 2 via pi(1)=1 and pi(0)=pi(2)=0 has degree 4 and is irreducible over F_2. Output the count as a three-digit zero-padded integer.
givens: P(x) in F_3[x] with deg(P)=4.
pi: {0,1,2} -> F_2 given by pi(0)=0, pi(1)=1, pi(2)=0 (coefficientwise, using the unique reps 0,1,2).
Q(x) = sum_{i=0}^4 pi(a_i) x^i in F_2[x], where P(x)=sum_{i=0}^4 a_i x^i with a_i in F_3.
Constraints: deg(Q)=4 and Q is irreducible over F_2.
Constraint: P is irreducible over F_3.
ask: Determine N = #{ P in F_3[x] : deg(P)=4, P irreducible over F_3, deg(Q)=4, Q irreducible over F_2 }.
Output N as a three-digit, zero-padded integer.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: base field for P

parameter(s).name: r
parameter(s).purpose: target field for projected Q

parameter(s).name: n
parameter(s).purpose: degree where reducibility splits are only 1+3 or 2+2

parameter(s).name: projection_rule
parameter(s).purpose: digitwise coefficient constraint linking P to Q

invariant(s).name: leading_and_constant_for_degree4_Q
invariant(s).statement: If deg(Q)=4 and Q is irreducible over F_2, then Q is monic with constant term 1; hence pi(a_4)=1 and pi(a_0)=1, forcing a_4=1 and a_0=1 in F_3 (since only 1 maps to 1 under pi).

invariant(s).name: forced_coefficients_from_projection
invariant(s).statement: For i=1..3: if Q has coefficient 1 at x^i then a_i=1 in F_3; if Q has coefficient 0 at x^i then a_i is restricted to {0,2} in F_3.

invariant(s).name: irreducible_degree4_over_field_iff_no_linear_or_irreducible_quadratic_factor
invariant(s).statement: Over any field, a degree-4 polynomial is reducible iff it has a linear factor or an irreducible quadratic factor (since the only nontrivial degree splits are 1+3 and 2+2; a product of two reducible quadratics would already have a linear factor).

essential_assumption(s):
The projection is the set-map on representatives {0,1,2} followed by mod 2, not a ring homomorphism F_3 -> F_2.
Irreducibility is tested in F_3[x] and F_2[x] respectively.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The invariants force a_4=a_0=1 and restrict (a_1,a_2,a_3) to a finite discrete set. Listing all irreducible Q of degree 4 over F_2 and lifting them produces a complete finite candidate set. A complete factor-sieve (roots in F_3 plus all monic irreducible quadratics over F_3) decisively accepts or rejects each candidate, yielding a unique count N.

technique(s).name: count_irreducibles_over_F2_via_gauss_formula
technique(s).purpose: establish the number of monic irreducible quartics over F_2 and justify that a short explicit list is exhaustive

title: Weighted split-count after factoring over F5 and splitting over F125

identity.short_name: Weighted split-count after factoring over F5 and splitting over F125

problem_spec.summary: Factor a given polynomial over 𝔽_5 into distinct monic irreducibles. For each irreducible factor, compute how many irreducible factors it has over the cubic extension 𝔽_125 using the gcd(deg,3) rule, then form a weighted degree-squared sum.
givens: 𝔽_5 is the finite field with 5 elements; 𝔽_125 is its cubic extension.
P(x) factors over 𝔽_5 as a product of distinct monic irreducible polynomials P(x)=\prod_{i=1}^k f_i(x).
For each f_i, \omega(f_i) is the number of distinct irreducible factors of f_i over 𝔽_125.
ask: Compute M = \sum_{i=1}^k deg(f_i)^2  \cdot  \omega(f_i).

parameter(s).name: base_prime_p
parameter(s).purpose: chooses the base finite field 𝔽_p

parameter(s).name: extension_degree_m
parameter(s).purpose: specifies the extension 𝔽_{p^m} over which splitting is measured

parameter(s).name: polynomial_P_coeffs
parameter(s).domain: coefficients in 𝔽_p with fixed degree profile
parameter(s).purpose: defines the specific instance polynomial to factor

parameter(s).name: weight_rule
parameter(s).purpose: defines the aggregated invariant to compute

invariant(s).name: unique_factorization_over_𝔽_p
invariant(s).statement: The multiset of monic irreducible factors of P(x) in 𝔽_p[x] is unique up to ordering.

invariant(s).name: extension_splitting_count_rule
invariant(s).statement: For an irreducible f of degree n over 𝔽_p, the number of irreducible factors of f over 𝔽_{p^m} equals gcd(n,m).

invariant(s).name: degree_from_multiplicative_order
invariant(s).statement: If α is a nonzero element of a finite extension of 𝔽_p with multiplicative order t, then the smallest d with α\in𝔽_{p^d} is the multiplicative order of p modulo t; hence the minimal polynomial of α has degree d.

essential_assumption(s): P(x) is squarefree so the factorization has no repeated irreducible factors.
\omega(f_i) counts distinct irreducible factors over 𝔽_{125} (not multiplicities).
Standard finite-field splitting theorem applies: irreducible degree n splits into gcd(n,m) factors over 𝔽_{p^m}.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Uniqueness of irreducible factorization in 𝔽_5[x] fixes the factor degrees uniquely, and the splitting rule \omega(f)=gcd(deg(f),3) fixes \omega-values uniquely. Therefore M is uniquely determined.

technique(s).name: algebraic_grouping_factorization
technique(s).purpose: factor P by grouping into a visible product of lower-degree polynomials.

technique(s).name: linear_root_sieve_for_cubic
technique(s).purpose: certify irreducibility of a cubic by checking it has no roots in 𝔽_5.

technique(s).name: binomial_irreducibility_via_multiplicative_order
technique(s).purpose: show irreducibility by computing the order of a root and deducing its field-of-definition degree.

technique(s).name: extension_field_splitting_count
technique(s).purpose: compute \omega(f_i)=gcd(deg(f_i),m).

technique(s).name: weighted_sum_evaluation
technique(s).purpose: compute M=\sumdeg(f_i)^2 \cdot \omega(f_i).

theorem(s).name: unique_factorization_in_𝔽_q[x]
theorem(s).role: guarantees uniqueness of irreducible factorization over the base field.

theorem(s).name: splitting_of_irreducibles_over_extensions
theorem(s).role: irreducible degree n over 𝔽_q splits into gcd(n,m) irreducibles over 𝔽_{q^m}.

theorem(s).name: degree_via_order_mod_t
theorem(s).role: minimal extension degree containing an element of order t equals ord_t(q).

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: unique factorization over fields
Frobenius/finite-field multiplicative order controls extension degrees

critical_trick(s): factor P by grouping into a cubic and a degree-8 binomial
prove the degree-8 binomial is irreducible via multiplicative order and order-of-5 mod 32
use \omega(f)=gcd(deg(f),3) to avoid explicit factoring over 𝔽_125

task(s).reason: verify P factors as (x^3+2x+1)(x^8+3) in 𝔽_5[x].

identity.short_name: Order-3 automorphisms of F7[x,y]/(x,y)^3 with det=2 linear part (mod 1000)

Use the semidirect-product model Aut(R) ≅ GL(V) ⋉ Hom(V,W), expand the cube using n^3=0 to get a linear condition on the quadratic part, then count admissible linear parts by orbit-stabilizer and count quadratic parts by eigenspace/kernel dimension. Output the result mod 1000.
givens: R = _7[x,y]/(x,y)^3 with maximal ideal =(x̄,ȳ) and ^3=0.
V = /^2 (dimension 2 over _7) and W = ^2 (dimension 3 over _7).
is the set of _7-algebra automorphisms ψ of R such that:

identity.short_name: Count order-2 automorphisms of F7[x]/(x^6)

subdomain(s): composition of truncated power series over finite fields

problem_spec.summary: In R=F7[x]/(x^6), every F7-algebra automorphism is determined by the image of x as a truncated power series with nonzero linear term; impose φ^2=id and count solutions by solving the induced coefficient constraints over F7.
givens: Every F_7-algebra automorphism φ of R is determined by φ(x)=a x + b x^2 + c x^3 + d x^4 + e x^5 (mod x^6), with a \in F_7^× and b,c,d,e \in F_7.
I = { φ \in Aut_{F_7}(R) : φ^2 = id_R }.
ask: Compute |I|.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: base finite field size (here 7)

parameter(s).name: n
parameter(s).purpose: truncation degree in F_p[x]/(x^n) (here 6)

parameter(s).name: k
parameter(s).purpose: order constraint φ^k=id (here 2)

parameter(s).name: series_coefficients
parameter(s).purpose: parametrizes automorphisms via the image of x

invariant(s).name: automorphism_parameterization
invariant(s).statement: φ is an F_p-algebra automorphism iff φ(x)=a x + higher terms (mod x^n) with a\inF_p^×.

invariant(s).name: composition_mod_truncation
invariant(s).statement: φ^k=id is equivalent to f^{∘k}(x) \equiv x (mod x^n) where f(x)=φ(x) as a truncated series.

invariant(s).name: leading_coefficient_constraint
invariant(s).statement: From the x-coefficient in f^{∘k}(x)\equivx, one gets a^k=1 in F_p^× (here a^2=1).

invariant(s).name: triangular_coefficient_system
invariant(s).statement: Expanding f^{∘2}(x) mod x^n yields a (nearly) triangular system of polynomial equations in b,c,d,e solvable by casework on a.

essential_assumption(s): Automorphisms are F_7-algebra automorphisms (fix scalars in F_7).
Composition is computed in R, i.e., all series are truncated mod x^6.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Each automorphism corresponds to a unique coefficient tuple (a,b,c,d,e); the system f^{∘2}(x)\equivx (mod x^6) has finitely many solutions in F_7, so |I| is uniquely determined once the coefficient system is solved.

technique(s).name: translate_order_condition_to_series_identity
technique(s).purpose: rewrite φ^2=id as f∘f(x)\equivx (mod x^6)
outputs: coefficient equations in F_7 for a,b,c,d,e

technique(s).name: solve_linear_term_first
technique(s).purpose: restrict a to square roots of unity in F_7^×

technique(s).name: casework_by_a
technique(s).purpose: simplify the remaining coefficient equations (some factors vanish depending on a)

technique(s).name: count_free_parameters
technique(s).purpose: count solutions as powers of 7 when variables remain free
outputs: solution count per case and total

theorem(s).name: Units criterion for truncated polynomial automorphisms
theorem(s).role: f(x) defines an automorphism iff its linear term is a unit in F_p

theorem(s).name: Finite-field root counting for a^2=1
theorem(s).role: determine the admissible a values in F_7^×

critical_trick(s): Replace φ^2=id by the truncated series identity f∘f(x)\equivx (mod x^6).
Use the linear-term equation a^2=1 to reduce to two cases a=1 and a=-1.
In each case, solve the coefficient equations sequentially and count the remaining free parameters over F_7.

title: Additive/Pexider/Jensen FE with regularity forcing

identity.short_name: Additive/Pexider/Jensen FE with regularity forcing

problem_spec.summary: Classify all functions $f$ satisfying an additive_Cauchy / Pexider_type / Jensen_type identity on a specified domain (e.g., $Q_to_Q$, $R_to_R$, or interval_to_interval). Additional constraint_atoms (bounded/monotone/continuity_forced) force the wild solutions away and yield an affine (or linear) description.
givens: A domain model $D$ is chosen from {reals, rationals, integers, an interval} (parameter).
The function satisfies one of the archetype_families: additive_Cauchy, Pexider_type, or Jensen_type (parameterized).
At least one regularity/constraint_atom is assumed: bounded on an interval, monotone, or continuity_forced.
ask: Determine the full set of functions $f:D\to D$ satisfying the given functional equation and constraint_atoms.

parameter(s).name: domain_model
parameter(s).purpose: Chooses the ambient domain and affects which regularity implies continuity_forced.

parameter(s).name: equation_form
parameter(s).purpose: Selects the functional equation template.

parameter(s).name: regularity_guard
parameter(s).purpose: Selects which constraint_atom eliminates pathological solutions.

invariant(s).name: translation_invariance
invariant(s).statement: The equation implies invariance under translations or midpoints (e.g., $f(x+y)-f(x)-f(y)=0$ or Jensen midpoint condition).

invariant(s).name: rational_scaling
invariant(s).statement: From generate_recursion and equal_inputs one obtains $f(qx)=qf(x)$ for rational $q$ whenever the domain allows it.

invariant(s).name: interface_quantity_T
invariant(s).statement: Define the interface quantity $T=f(1)$ (or slope parameter); the equation forces $f(x)=Tx$ on $Q_to_Q$, and regularity_guard extends this to $R_to_R$ or interval_to_interval.

invariant(s).name: constraint_atoms_rigidity
invariant(s).statement: Constraint_atoms such as injective, surjective, bijective, bounded, or monotone are used to deduce continuity_forced (or to force fixed_points), eliminating wild additive_Cauchy solutions and enabling show_linearity.

essential_assumption(s): The domain_model is closed under required operations (addition and rational scaling as needed).
regularity_guard holds on a nontrivial interval/subset so that continuity_forced can be deduced when working over $R_to_R$.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The equation_form plus force_additivity determines $f$ on the rational skeleton by the single parameter $T=f(1)$ (so $f(q)=qT$ on $Q_to_Q$). Any regularity_guard from {bounded, monotone, injective, surjective, bijective} implies continuity_forced on a nontrivial interval, which uniquely extends the rational formula to $R_to_R$ / interval_to_interval. Thus the solution set is completely and uniquely classified by $T$.

technique(s).name: plug_special_values
technique(s).purpose: Obtain $f(0)$, $f(1)$, and other anchors that seed recursion.

technique(s).name: swap_variables
technique(s).purpose: Derive symmetry relations and cancellation identities that imply additivity.

technique(s).name: equal_inputs
technique(s).purpose: Generate scaling and midpoint identities.

technique(s).name: bounding_trap
technique(s).purpose: Use bounds to show the additive solution is continuous, hence linear.

technique(s).name: classify_piecewise
technique(s).purpose: Handle $x>0$, $x<0$, $x=0$ branches and reassemble.

technique(s).name: force_additivity
technique(s).purpose: Reduce the given equation_form to the additive_Cauchy skeleton by cancellations and standard substitutions.

technique(s).name: show_linearity
inputs: additive_Cauchy on $Q$ or on $R$ plus a regularity_guard
technique(s).purpose: Use bounded/monotone/continuity_forced to rule out pathological additive solutions and conclude $f(x)=Tx$ (or affine) on the full domain_model.

theorem(s).name: additive_Cauchy
theorem(s).role: Characterize solutions on $Q$ and under regularity on $R$.

theorem(s).name: Jensen_type
theorem(s).role: Relate midpoint convexity to linearity under boundedness.

theorem(s).name: Pexider_type
theorem(s).role: Reduce a three-function variant to the additive_Cauchy skeleton.

regularity_eliminates_pathologies

Show that regularity_guard implies continuity_forced, hence show_linearity on $R_to_R$.
If the domain is mixed_domain, classify_piecewise and then stitch via the equation.

title: Multiplicative & d'Alembert_type functional equations

identity.short_name: Multiplicative & d'Alembert_type functional equations

problem_spec.summary: Classify functions $f$ satisfying multiplicative_Cauchy (e.g., $f(xy)=f(x)f(y)$) or dAlembert_type relations (e.g., $f(x+y)+f(x-y)=2f(x)f(y)$ or variants) on $R_to_R$ or interval_to_interval. Constraint_atoms (bounded/monotone/injective) and contradiction_via_growth pin the classical solutions (exponential, trigonometric, or linear).
givens: A domain_model is chosen (typically $R_to_R$ or a multiplicative subgroup of reals).
The functional equation is either multiplicative_Cauchy or dAlembert_type (parameter).
One or more constraint_atoms hold: bounded on an interval, monotone, injective, or bijective.
ask: Determine all solutions $f:D\to\mathbb{R}$ consistent with the equation and constraints, including parity_behavior/periodicity if present.

parameter(s).name: equation_form
parameter(s).purpose: Select the archetype family.

parameter(s).name: domain_model
parameter(s).purpose: Controls allowable substitutions (sign splits, scaling).

parameter(s).name: regularity_guard
parameter(s).purpose: Chooses which constraint_atoms are available to rule out exotic solutions.

invariant(s).name: scaling_relation
invariant(s).statement: equal_inputs and generate_recursion imply a scaling_type relation (e.g., $f(x^n)=f(x)^n$ or a Chebyshev-like recursion).

invariant(s).name: parity_or_period
invariant(s).statement: For dAlembert_type, swap_variables yields parity_behavior (even/odd) and may imply periodicity when bounded.

invariant(s).name: interface_quantity_T
invariant(s).statement: Define $T=f(1)$ and (for additive parametrizations) $c=f'(0)$ when differentiable/forced; the equation reduces the solution set to a low-parameter family determined by $T$ (and parity), then regularity_guard selects allowed parameters.

invariant(s).name: domain_and_constraints
invariant(s).statement: The classification depends on the domain_model (R_to_R versus interval_to_interval) and on constraint_atoms such as injective, bijective, or monotone, which restrict allowable branches of the multiplicative_Cauchy / dAlembert_type solutions.

essential_assumption(s): The domain_model supports the substitutions used (e.g., closure under $xy$ or $x\pm y$).
regularity_guard holds on a set large enough to prevent pathological multiplicative solutions (e.g., bounded on an interval).
uniqueness_certificate(s): For dAlembert_type, generate_recursion yields a Chebyshev-style recursion determining $f(nx)$ from $f(x)$; this forces a low-parameter family (e.g., $\cos(cx)$ / $\cosh(cx)$ / exponentials) indexed by interface data such as $T=f(1)$ and parity_behavior. constraint_atoms (bounded on an interval, monotone, injective/bijective) are then used with contradiction_via_growth to eliminate parameter regimes (e.g., unstable growth or oscillation), leaving a uniquely determined solution family for the given constraints.

technique(s).name: force_multiplicativity
technique(s).purpose: Derive $f(xy)=f(x)f(y)$ or reduce to it by substitutions.

technique(s).name: generate_recursion
technique(s).purpose: Produce Chebyshev-style recursion $f(nx)$ in terms of $f(x)$.

technique(s).name: contradiction_via_growth
technique(s).purpose: Show that non-classical parameters would force unbounded growth, contradicting bounded.

technique(s).name: invariant_extraction
technique(s).purpose: Extract invariants like $f(0)$, $f(1)$ and parity_behavior.

theorem(s).name: multiplicative_Cauchy
theorem(s).role: Classify solutions under mild regularity as exponentials/powers.

theorem(s).name: dAlembert_type
theorem(s).role: Connect solutions to cosine/sine-type recursions or to exponentials via substitution.

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: recursion_from_functional_equation
growth_vs_boundedness

critical_trick(s): Use plug_special_values to identify $f(0)$ and $f(1)$ and normalize the equation.
Apply equal_inputs repeatedly to get a recursion; read off parity_behavior and potential periodicity.
Rule out spurious solutions by contradiction_via_growth against boundedness/monotonicity.

library(s).name: sympy
library(s).reason: Symbolic solving of induced recurrences and simplification of functional identities.

library(s).name: numpy
library(s).reason: Numerical probing of candidate solutions on dense grids to validate periodicity/boundedness claims.

task(s).name: primary_structural_check
task(s).reason: From equation_form, symbolically derive the recursion for $f(nx)$ (Chebyshev polynomials for dAlembert_type or exponent laws for multiplicative_Cauchy). Verify that the recursion together with plug_special_values fixes $f$ on the generated subgroup from interface data (T, parity_behavior).

task(s).name: secondary_independent_check
task(s).reason: Numerically test candidate parameter families on a grid: check bounded/monotone/injective constraints and detect growth by iterating the recursion for $n\le 50$; confirm contradiction_via_growth eliminates the disallowed parameter ranges.

title: Majorization & convexity inequality chains

identity.short_name: Majorization & convexity inequality chains

problem_spec.summary: Optimize or bound a cyclic/nonhomogeneous expression in $n$ real variables (often under a fixed-sum constraint) by transforming it into a form amenable to majorization and convexity. Typical asks: maximize/minimize a sum over adjacent products under a permutation (rearrangement) constraint, or prove a sharp inequality for ordered sequences.
givens: An integer parameter $n\geq 3$ is fixed.
Variables satisfy an ordering or admissible permutation condition (parameterized).
A global constraint is imposed (e.g., $\sum_{i=1}^n x_i = S$ or multiset $\{x_i\}$ fixed).
ask: Determine the extremal value of the target expression $T$ (and the extremizing configuration), using majorization/convexity arguments.

parameter(s).name: n
parameter(s).purpose: Controls dimension for n_variable inequalities and rearrangement structure.

parameter(s).name: constraint_type
parameter(s).purpose: Chooses the constrained_domain on which optimization occurs.

parameter(s).name: convex_kernel
parameter(s).domain: a specified convex/concave function $\phi$ with decidable properties
parameter(s).purpose: Allows Jensen/Karamata style reductions and changes which inequality_chain_design applies.

invariant(s).name: majorization_order
invariant(s).statement: A smoothing/mixing step produces a new vector that majorizes the old one while preserving the chosen invariants (e.g., sum). By Karamata/convexity_concavity this moves the objective monotonically, so the extremal_case must lie on the boundary of the majorization polytope (two-level vectors).

invariant(s).name: cyclic_structure
invariant(s).statement: The target is cyclic (invariant under $(a,b,c)\mapsto(b,c,a)$) and may be nonhomogeneous; after a normalize step it is treated as an $n_variable$ (typically 3-variable) optimization whose extremal_case occurs at a rearrangement-sorted configuration.

invariant(s).name: equality_characterization
invariant(s).statement: equality_case occurs either at uniform vectors ($x_1=\cdots=x_n$) or at boundary two-level vectors forced by Karamata/Jensen equality conditions.

invariant(s).name: interface_quantity_T
invariant(s).statement: Define the interface quantity $T$ as the sorted vector of partial sums (or cumulative distribution) that encodes majorization. Rearrangement/sorting produces $T$, and Karamata/Jensen consume $T$ to certify the extremal bound.

essential_assumption(s): The constrained_domain is compact (or can be compactified by normalize) so an extremal_case exists.
convex_kernel is genuinely convex/concave on the working interval to make Jensen/Karamata strict away from equality_case.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Under convexity_concavity, Karamata implies the objective is Schur-convex/concave on the normalized domain. Iterating smoothing/mixing_variables strictly improves the objective unless at a two-level extremal_case (or the uniform point). This pins a unique candidate value and equality_case pattern (up to cyclic permutation), which is then certified by a direct_derivation inequality_chain_design.

technique(s).name: majorization
technique(s).purpose: Reduce the problem to comparing vectors under majorization and apply Karamata.
outputs: finite set of extremal candidate configurations

technique(s).name: smoothing
technique(s).purpose: Replace two variables by their average while monotone-improving the objective.

technique(s).name: Rearrangement
technique(s).purpose: Identify the optimal permutation ordering for the expression.

technique(s).name: inequality_chain_design
technique(s).purpose: Build a tight chain using Jensen/Chebyshev/Rearrangement tuned to equality_case.

technique(s).name: direct_derivation
inputs: known classical inequalities and a target inequality_chain_design
technique(s).purpose: Derive the target inequality directly by chaining Jensen/Karamata/Rearrangement type bounds without introducing auxiliary variables.
outputs: a completed inequality_chain_design that matches equality_case

theorem(s).name: Jensen
theorem(s).role: Convert sums of convex images into bounds in terms of the mean.

theorem(s).name: Karamata
theorem(s).role: Turn majorization comparisons into inequality statements for convex kernels.

theorem(s).name: Rearrangement
theorem(s).role: Maximize/minimize bilinear sums by matching sorted orders.

theorem(s).name: Chebyshev
theorem(s).role: Relate averages of similarly sorted sequences to averages of products.

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: convexity_and_majorization
monotone_improvement_via_smoothing

critical_trick(s): Use rearrange_terms to isolate a convex kernel $\phi(x_i)$ or a bilinear form suited to Rearrangement.
Apply majorization and then Karamata/Jensen to reduce to extremal_case with at most two distinct values.
Close by an explicit inequality_chain_design whose tightness is certified by the equality_case.

library(s).name: itertools
library(s).reason: Enumerate candidate extremal permutations for small $n$ during verification.

library(s).name: sympy
library(s).reason: Symbolic checking of convexity and of derived inequality chains.

task(s).name: primary_structural_check
task(s).reason: Verify convexity_concavity of the kernel function used in Jensen/Karamata (check second derivative sign on the stated interval) and symbolically confirm the smoothing/mixing step preserves invariants and moves the objective in the claimed direction.

task(s).name: secondary_independent_check
task(s).reason: For small $n$ (e.g., $n=3,4$) brute-force random sampling and permutation scans: generate random nonnegative vectors with fixed sum, compute the objective before/after mixing_variables, and confirm maxima/minima occur at the predicted two-level extremal_case configurations.

title: Symmetric homogeneous inequality via SOS/uvw

identity.short_name: Symmetric homogeneous inequality via SOS/uvw

problem_spec.summary: Given $a,b,c>0$ under a single symmetric normalization (e.g., $abc=1$ or $a+b+c=1$), determine the sharp constant $K$ (or minimum value) in a symmetric homogeneous inequality that becomes a degree-$d$ polynomial inequality after homogenize + normalize, and characterize equality cases.
givens: Variables satisfy $a,b,c>0$.
A normalization constraint is imposed (parameter): either $abc=1$ or $a+b+c=1$.
The target inequality is symmetric in $(a,b,c)$ and homogeneous after homogenize.
ask: Find the optimal constant $K$ such that $F(a,b,c) \geq K$ holds for all admissible $(a,b,c)$, and list all equality cases.

parameter(s).name: constraint_model
parameter(s).purpose: Select the normalization used by normalize.

parameter(s).name: degree_d
parameter(s).purpose: Controls the total degree after homogenize and thus the SOS/uvw complexity.

parameter(s).name: coefficient_profile
parameter(s).purpose: Varies the symmetric polynomial form $F$ while keeping the same SOS/uvw skeleton.

invariant(s).name: symmetric_homogeneous_form
invariant(s).statement: After homogenize and normalize, the task becomes a parameterized three_variable polynomial_inequality: prove $F(a,b,c)\ge K$ for all $(a,b,c)$ in a compact constrained_domain (e.g., $a,b,c>0$ with $abc=1$ or $a+b+c=1$), where $F$ is symmetric and homogeneous of total degree $d$.

invariant(s).name: uvw_reduction_validity
invariant(s).statement: For fixed $u=a+b+c$ and $v=ab+bc+ca$, the expression depends on $w=abc$ in a way that permits uvw_reduction to a univariate inequality in $w$.

invariant(s).name: equality_locus
invariant(s).statement: The equality_case is characterized by the uvw_reduction boundary: typically $a=b=c$ and/or the two-equal family $(a,a,b)$ that attains the extremal_case of the reduced univariate polynomial.

invariant(s).name: interface_quantity_T
invariant(s).statement: Define the interface quantity $T=(u,v)$ where $u=a+b+c$ and $v=ab+bc+ca$ after normalize. The uvw_reduction component treats $T$ as fixed data and reduces the inequality to a univariate polynomial in $w=abc$; solving that univariate problem yields the sharp constant $K$.

essential_assumption(s): The normalization constraint makes the feasible set compact up to scaling so that a minimum/infimum for $F$ is attained.
Coefficient_profile excludes degenerate cases where $F$ factorizes trivially or loses symmetry.
uniqueness_certificate(s): uvw_reduction reduces the symmetric homogeneous inequality to a one-parameter polynomial_inequality in a boundary variable (e.g., $w=abc$) on an explicit interval determined by the constrained_domain. The sharp constant $K$ is forced by the equality_case: solve for $K$ so the reduced polynomial has a double root at the extremal_case (endpoint or critical point). An SOS certificate then proves global nonnegativity for this unique $K$.

technique(s).name: normalize
technique(s).purpose: Fix scale (e.g., $abc=1$) to reduce degrees of freedom and expose polynomial structure.

technique(s).name: uvw_reduction
technique(s).purpose: Reduce the inequality to a univariate problem in $w=abc$ for fixed $(u,v)$.

technique(s).name: SOS
inputs: resulting polynomial in one variable or in $a,b,c$
technique(s).purpose: Produce a sum-of-squares decomposition establishing $F-K\geq 0$.
outputs: explicit nonnegativity certificate and equality_case extraction

technique(s).name: substitution
technique(s).purpose: Lower dimension and simplify to a single-variable polynomial inequality.

technique(s).name: extremal_principle
inputs: continuous symmetric polynomial on a compact constrained_domain
technique(s).purpose: Guarantee an extremal_case exists and justify restricting the search to boundary configurations used by uvw_reduction.
outputs: existence of an extremal_case and reduction to boundary candidates

theorem(s).name: AM_GM
theorem(s).role: Establish baseline lower bounds and identify $a=b=c$ as a natural equality candidate.

theorem(s).name: weighted_AM_GM
theorem(s).role: Handle weighted symmetric terms after normalize.

theorem(s).name: Cauchy_Schwarz
theorem(s).role: Control sums of fractions/quadratic forms appearing after homogenize.

theorem(s).name: Engel_Titu
theorem(s).role: Convert sums of rational terms into a single quadratic form for SOS preparation.

theorem(s).name: Schur
theorem(s).role: Provide a canonical symmetric cubic lower bound used inside uvw_reduction arguments.

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: symmetry_and_homogeneity_as_invariant_information
nonnegativity_via_sum_of_squares

critical_trick(s): Use normalize to eliminate scaling and express all terms as a symmetric polynomial/rational form.
Apply uvw_reduction to justify that the extremal_case occurs at $b=c$ or $a=b=c$.
Finish with an SOS decomposition (often after substitution) that is tight at the equality_case.

library(s).name: sympy
library(s).reason: Symbolic manipulation of symmetric polynomials, factorization, and SOS-style verification.

library(s).name: fractions
library(s).reason: Exact rational arithmetic when $K$ is rational under p_plus_q normalization in generated instances.

task(s).name: primary_structural_check
task(s).reason: Symbolically perform uvw_reduction: rewrite $F(a,b,c)-K$ in $(u=a+b+c,v=ab+bc+ca,w=abc)$, impose the chosen normalize constraint, and obtain a univariate polynomial in $w$. Check that choosing $K$ from the equality_case makes this polynomial nonnegative on the admissible interval (factorization or SOS).

task(s).name: secondary_independent_check
task(s).reason: Numerically sample $10^4$ random triples on the constrained_domain (e.g., $a,b,c>0$ with $abc=1$ via $a=e^x,b=e^y,c=e^{-x-y}$) and verify $F(a,b,c)\ge K$; also scan the two-equal curve $(t,t,\frac{1}{t^2})$ to locate the extremal_case and confirm sharpness.

title: n-variable mean inequalities for sequences

identity.short_name: n-variable mean inequalities for sequences

problem_spec.summary: Given $n$ nonnegative or positive reals (often a finite sequence), derive sharp bounds relating arithmetic, geometric, power means, and elementary symmetric means. Typical asks: determine the best constant or monotonicity relation among means, or bound a symmetric expression of $(x_1,\dots,x_n)$ that is naturally expressed via sequence_inequality.
givens: An integer $n\geq 2$ and exponent parameters (e.g., $p,q$) are given.
Variables satisfy $x_i\geq 0$ (or $x_i>0$ when required by Maclaurin).
A normalization such as $\sum x_i = 1$ or $\prod x_i = 1$ may be imposed via normalize.
ask: Establish the sharp inequality relation among selected means (Power_Mean/Maclaurin) and identify all equality_case configurations.

parameter(s).name: n
parameter(s).purpose: Controls the dimension and which Maclaurin inequality applies.

parameter(s).name: mean_exponents
parameter(s).domain: a decidable set of real exponents (e.g., $p\geq q$)
parameter(s).purpose: Selects which Power_Mean comparisons are required.

parameter(s).name: symmetric_statistic
parameter(s).purpose: Chooses the target expression to be bounded.

invariant(s).name: homogeneity_under_scaling
invariant(s).statement: After homogenize/normalize, the inequality is treated as an $n_variable$ statement depending only on scale-invariant ratios; the family is parameterized by exponents (Power_Mean) and by the choice of symmetric_statistic.

invariant(s).name: majorization_monotonicity
invariant(s).statement: The relevant mean function is Schur-convex/concave so that majorization implies the desired ordering of means.

invariant(s).name: equality_structure
invariant(s).statement: equality_case holds iff $x_1=\cdots=x_n$ (or iff all but one variable are zero in the nonnegative boundary cases allowed by constrained_domain).

invariant(s).name: interface_quantity_T
invariant(s).statement: Let $T_k=\sum_{i=1}^n x_i^k$ (power_sums). The sequence component provides $(T_k)$, and the mean-inequality component (Power_Mean/Maclaurin) consumes $(T_k)$ to derive the desired sequence_inequality and equality_case.

essential_assumption(s): Parameter domains ensure the means are defined (e.g., positivity for geometric mean and Maclaurin).
The constrained_domain includes boundary points only when the targeted inequality remains meaningful (avoid $0^0$ and division by $0$).
uniqueness_certificate(s): For the parameterized mean functional, convexity_concavity (strict for non-degenerate inputs) implies that equality_case occurs only at the fully symmetric point $x_1=\cdots=x_n$ (or at allowed boundary cases). Evaluating the inequality at this equality_case uniquely fixes the sharp constant/ordering in the mean comparison.

technique(s).name: Power_Mean
technique(s).purpose: Compare $M_p$ and $M_q$ by convexity_concavity and normalization.
outputs: monotonicity of $p\mapsto M_p$ and sharp bounds

technique(s).name: Maclaurin
technique(s).purpose: Relate elementary symmetric means to each other and to the geometric mean.

technique(s).name: Holder
technique(s).purpose: Bound products/sums that appear after expanding symmetric expressions.

technique(s).name: Bernoulli
inputs: terms of the form $(1+t)^r$ after normalization
technique(s).purpose: Linearize or bound expressions to close the inequality chain.

theorem(s).name: Minkowski
theorem(s).role: Convert norm inequalities into bounds on sums of radicals/powers.

theorem(s).name: Muirhead
theorem(s).role: Handle symmetric sums once majorization is established.

theorem(s).name: Holder
theorem(s).role: Provide the multiplicative strengthening needed for power-sum bounds.

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: monotonicity_of_means
convexity_concavity

critical_trick(s): Normalize the scale (e.g., $\sum x_i=1$) so the target becomes dimensionless.
Translate the target into mean language (power sums or elementary symmetric sums).
Apply Power_Mean/Maclaurin and then close with Holder/Minkowski/Bernoulli to reach the sharp constant at equality_case.

library(s).reason: Manipulate symmetric sums and verify algebraic reductions for specific parameter choices.

library(s).name: numpy
library(s).reason: Numerically sample random sequences to sanity-check the inequality and locate equality_case numerically.

task(s).reason: Symbolically reduce the target expression to a Power_Mean/Maclaurin comparison under normalize (e.g., substitute $\sum x_i=1$) and verify the resulting inequality is one of the declared classical_inequalities for the chosen mean_exponents.

task(s).name: secondary_independent_check
task(s).reason: Monte Carlo check on the constrained_domain: sample $10^4$ random $n$-tuples (Dirichlet for $\sum x_i=1$) and numerically verify the inequality; additionally test boundary sequences with many zeros to confirm the stated equality_case/boundary behavior.

title: - Language: python (optional; the proof is short by hand, but brute-force verification is straightforward).

identity.short_name: Find red-apple price with Store 3 buying exactly one red (square-first)

subdomain(s): integer equations from transactional balance constraints

problem_spec.summary: Three stores execute fixed purchase/return transactions under shared pricing rules, then spend the remaining balance on red apples; two stores impose divisibility constraints from bulk discounts, and a third store with square-first pricing and a fixed final red quantity pins down the unique listed price R.
givens: Listed prices satisfy G=4, Y=G+2, W=3G-1, so Y=6 and W=11.
Bulk-discount rule (Stores 1 and 2): in any single purchase transaction buying at least 3 of the same type, each unit costs 1 less than its listed price; returns are credited at listed price.
Store 1 starts with balance B1=33, does: buy 2G, return 1Y, buy 1W, then spends remaining balance on red apples in one final purchase transaction.
Store 2 starts with balance B2=47, does: return 1G, buy 3Y, buy 1W, then spends remaining balance on red apples in one final purchase transaction.

Store 3 starts with balance B3=188 and uses square-first pricing: a purchase of q items at per-item price p costs p^2+(q-1)p (using discounted p if bulk discount applies); returns occur only as single-item transactions and are credited p^2.
Store 3 does: return 1G, buy 1Y, buy 2W, then spends remaining balance by buying exactly n3=1 red apple in one final transaction.
ask: Determine the listed price R of a red apple.

parameter(s).name: base_price_G
parameter(s).purpose: anchors all listed prices (here G=4)

parameter(s).name: price_relations
parameter(s).purpose: defines Y and W from G (here Y=G+2 and W=3G-1)

parameter(s).name: unknown_price_R
parameter(s).purpose: the target listed price to solve for

parameter(s).name: bulk_discount_threshold
parameter(s).purpose: triggers per-item discount in a transaction (here threshold=3, discount size=1)

parameter(s).name: square_first_rule
parameter(s).purpose: changes transaction cost calculation in Store 3

parameter(s).name: fixed_final_red_quantity_store3
parameter(s).purpose: fixes the final number of red apples bought in Store 3 (here n3=1)

invariant(s).name: transaction_balance_conservation
invariant(s).statement: Each store's remaining balance equals initial balance plus credits from returns minus costs of purchases under that store's applicable pricing rule.

invariant(s).name: discounted_red_unit_price
invariant(s).statement: In Stores 1 and 2, if the final red purchase buys at least 3 reds then per-red cost is R-1; otherwise per-red cost is R.

invariant(s).name: square_first_cost_formula
invariant(s).statement: In Store 3, a purchase transaction with per-item price p and quantity q\geq1 costs p^2+(q-1)p (with discounted p if bulk discount applies); a single-item return credits p^2.

invariant(s).name: fixed_red_quantity_equation_store3
invariant(s).statement: With n3=1, Store 3's final red purchase costs R^2 and must equal Store 3's remaining balance after its prior transactions.

essential_assumption(s): Listed prices are integers and transactions are executed exactly as described.
In Stores 1 and 2, the final red purchase is a single transaction that spends the entire remaining balance.
In Store 3, the final red purchase buys exactly n3 reds and spends the entire remaining balance.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Stores 1 and 2 restrict R to a small finite set via case splits (n=1,2 vs n\geq3) and divisibility constraints.
Store 3, with square-first pricing and a fixed final red quantity, yields a single equation in R that selects a unique value from the intersection.

technique(s).name: compute_known_prices
technique(s).purpose: reduce known prices to explicit integers

technique(s).name: convert_store_balances_to_constraints_on_R
inputs: store transaction sequences and bulk-discount rule
technique(s).purpose: express remaining balances as either nR (n=1,2) or n(R-1) (n\geq3), forcing divisibility constraints on R
outputs: candidate sets for R from Stores 1 and 2

technique(s).name: intersect_candidate_sets
technique(s).purpose: reduce to a small intersection of possible R values

technique(s).name: verify_store3_square_first_fixed_quantity
inputs: Store 3 square-first cost formula and fixed n3
technique(s).purpose: convert Store 3 into a direct equation in R (typically quadratic), selecting the unique consistent R

theorem(s).name: basic divisibility in Z
theorem(s).role: if a balance equals n(R-1) with integer n, then R-1 divides the balance

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Systems of integer equations with piecewise-defined costs.

critical_trick(s): Translate "spend remaining balance in one transaction into a case split: n=1,2 gives remaining=nR, while n\geq3 forces remaining=n(R-1).
Use Stores 1 and 2 to obtain a small finite candidate set for R.
Use Store 3's square-first rule with a fixed final red quantity to force a quadratic equation R^2 = remaining, yielding a unique R.

library(s).reason: brute-force check candidate R values and small purchase counts for verification

task(s).name: brute_force_R_check
task(s).reason: iterate R in a reasonable range, enforce Store 1/2 divisibility constraints, and verify Store 3's equation

task(s).name: validate_unique_solution
task(s).reason: confirm only one integer R satisfies all store constraints

title: - Python tooling actually used: not required (all computations are short by hand: gcd via P-Q, quadratic discriminant, digit sum, inverse mod 29, order of 3 mod

identity.short_name: GCD \to discriminant \to digit sum \to engineered (2γ+1) derivative \to splitting field \to log modulus

problem_spec.summary: Compute F=gcd(P,Q) for two integer polynomials, take the discriminant δ of the (quadratic) gcd and its digit sum γ. Over F_p, define an engineered polynomial f(x)=x^{pδ}+c x^{2γ+1}-x where c is the inverse of (2γ+1) in F_p. In characteristic p the derivative kills x^{pδ} and the choice of c forces f'(x)=x^{2γ}-1. Determine the smallest finite field extension where x^{2γ}-1 splits using multiplicative order, evaluate log_{p^2}(m), and compute δ modulo 2 \cdot log_{p^2}(m)+γ.
givens: P(x), Q(x) are degree-6 polynomials in ℤ[x].
F(x)=gcd(P(x),Q(x)) in ℤ[x], fixed by requiring positive leading coefficient.
F(x) is quadratic: F(x)=ax^2+bx+c with a>0.
δ = discriminant(F) = b^2-4ac (an integer).
γ = sum of decimal digits of δ.
p is a prime (this instance uses p=29).
Work in _p and define c = (2γ+1)^{-1} \in _p (assumed to exist).
Define f(x)=x^{pδ}+c x^{2γ+1}-x in _p[x] and let f'(x) be its formal derivative.
m is the smallest prime power such that f'(x) splits completely into linear factors over _m.
ask: Compute output = δ mod (2 \cdot log_{p^2}(m)+γ), with the residue taken in {0,...,2 \cdot log_{p^2}(m)+γ-1}.

parameter(s).purpose: determines the gcd F and hence δ and γ

invariant(s).name: discriminant_is_integer

invariant(s).name: derivative_cancellation_in_char_p
invariant(s).statement: In characteristic p, (x^{pδ})' = pδ \cdot x^{pδ-1} = 0, so the x^{pδ} term vanishes in f'(x).

invariant(s).name: engineered_inverse_for_unit_x_2gamma_term
invariant(s).statement: With c=(2γ+1)^{-1} in _p, we have (c x^{2γ+1})' = c(2γ+1)x^{2γ} = x^{2γ}, forcing f'(x)=x^{2γ}-1.

invariant(s).name: splitting_of_xn_minus_1
invariant(s).statement: If gcd(p,2γ)=1, then x^{2γ}-1 splits over _{p^d} iff 2γ | (p^d-1). The minimal such d is ord_{2γ}(p).

invariant(s).name: log_p2_prime_power
invariant(s).statement: If m=p^d then log_{p^2}(m)=d/2, hence 2 \cdot log_{p^2}(m)=d is an integer.

essential_assumption(s): The computed gcd F(x) has degree 2 (quadratic).
2γ+1 is nonzero in _p (equivalently p ∤ (2γ+1)), so c=(2γ+1)^{-1} exists.
gcd(p,2γ)=1 so x^{2γ}-1 has distinct roots and the roots-of-unity splitting criterion applies cleanly.
m is taken among finite fields _{p^d} (prime-power sizes).
uniqueness_certificate(s): F is uniquely determined as the gcd in ℤ[x] with positive leading coefficient, hence δ and γ are deterministic. The coefficient c=(2γ+1)^{-1} is unique in _p, so f' is uniquely x^{2γ}-1. The smallest extension degree d for splitting is uniquely ord_{2γ}(p), hence m=p^d is unique. Since 2 \cdot log_{p^2}(m)=d and γ are fixed, the modulus (d+γ) and the residue δ mod (d+γ) are uniquely determined.

identity.short_name: GCD \to discriminant \to digit sum \to engineered derivative \to splitting field \to affine-log modulus

problem_spec.summary: Compute F=gcd(P,Q) for two integer polynomials, take the discriminant δ of the (quadratic) gcd and its digit sum γ. Over F_p, define an engineered polynomial f(x)=x^{pδ}+c x^{γ+1}-x where c is the inverse of (γ+1) in F_p. In characteristic p the derivative kills x^{pδ} and the choice of c forces f'(x)=x^γ-1. Determine the smallest finite field extension where x^γ-1 splits using multiplicative order, evaluate log_{p^2}(m), and compute δ modulo an affine function 2 \cdot log_{p^2}(m)+γ.

γ = sum of decimal digits of δ.
p is a prime (this instance uses p=7).

Define f(x)=x^{pδ}+c x^{γ+1}-x in _p[x] and let f'(x) be its formal derivative.
m is the smallest prime power such that f'(x) splits completely into linear factors over _m.
ask: Compute output = δ mod (2 \cdot log_{p^2}(m)+γ), with the residue taken in {0,...,2 \cdot log_{p^2}(m)+γ-1}.

title: statement: "Exactly one integer root for P means Q has no integer roots; equivalently, disc(Q) is not a perfect square integer (including 0).

identity.short_name: Count bounded cubics with exactly one integer root among {0,1}

subdomain(s): discriminant criteria for quadratic integer roots and repeated roots

problem_spec.summary: Count monic integer cubics with coefficients in a bounded box that have at least one forced root in {0,1}, no repeated roots, and exactly one integer root; split on which forced root is the unique integer root and filter the quadratic cofactor via a discriminant "not a square condition.
givens: Forced root condition: P(0)P(1)=0 (so at least one of 0 or 1 is a root).
P has no repeated roots (squarefree over ℂ).
P has exactly one integer root.
ask: Count the number of such polynomials P.

parameter(s).name: coefficient_box
parameter(s).purpose: finite search space enabling exact counting

parameter(s).name: forced_root_set
parameter(s).purpose: guarantees at least one small integer root and drives case split

parameter(s).name: uniqueness_of_integer_root
parameter(s).purpose: eliminates cases with two integer roots and forces the remaining quadratic factor to have no integer roots

parameter(s).name: multiplicity_constraint
parameter(s).purpose: excludes repeated roots (discriminant nonzero)

invariant(s).name: forced_root_case_split
invariant(s).statement: Since P(0)P(1)=0 and P has exactly one integer root, the unique integer root r is exactly one of {0,1}.

invariant(s).name: factorization_by_integer_root
invariant(s).statement: If r is a root then P(x)=(x-r)Q(x) with Q monic quadratic with integer coefficients.

invariant(s).name: quadratic_integer_root_test
invariant(s).statement: Q has an integer root iff its discriminant is a perfect square; Q has a repeated root iff its discriminant is 0.

title: - "Perfect square polynomial means Q(x)^2 with Q in Z[x].

identity.short_name: Count non-palindromic bounded quartics with reciprocal product a square

subdomain(s): integer polynomials and squares in Z[x]
gcd factor splitting in polynomial rings

problem_spec.summary: For monic quartics with coefficients in a bounded integer set, impose that the product with the reciprocal polynomial is a perfect square in Z[x], and additionally exclude palindromic polynomials; classify and count the remaining solutions.
givens: Define P*(x)=x^4 P(1/x)=x^4 + c x^3 + b x^2 + a x + 1.
Condition: P(x)P*(x) is a perfect square in Z[x].
Non-palindromic filter: a \neq c.
ask: Count the number of such polynomials P.

parameter(s).name: coefficient_set
parameter(s).purpose: bounds the search space for (a,b,c)

parameter(s).name: reciprocal_operator
parameter(s).purpose: imposes reciprocal symmetry constraints

parameter(s).name: square_condition
parameter(s).purpose: forces even multiplicities of irreducible factors in P \cdot P*

parameter(s).name: nonpalindromic_filter
parameter(s).purpose: excludes the trivial palindromic branch (a=c) where P \cdot P* is automatically a square

invariant(s).name: palindromic_implies_square
invariant(s).statement: If P*=P (equivalently c=a), then P \cdot P* = P^2 is a square in Z[x].

invariant(s).name: gcd_with_reciprocal_self_reciprocal
invariant(s).statement: D=gcd(P,P*) satisfies D*=±D; choosing D monic makes D*=D (self-reciprocal).

invariant(s).name: degree_2_gcd_forces_palindromic
invariant(s).statement: If deg gcd(P,P*)=2, then both the gcd and the remaining quadratic factor have constant term 1 and are self-reciprocal, forcing P*=P (palindromic).

invariant(s).name: coprime_product_square_forcing
invariant(s).statement: If gcd(P,P*)=1 and P \cdot P* is a square in Z[x], then P is a square in Z[x] up to a unit; monicity forces P=Q^2 with Q\inZ[x].

invariant(s).name: odd_degree_not_square
invariant(s).statement: A nonzero square polynomial in Z[x] has even degree; in particular, no cubic in Z[x] is a square.

title: - Optional verification (not required for the derivation):

identity.short_name: Centralizer dimension of a tensor-sum nilpotent in so(35)

problem_spec.summary: Use the Jacobson-Morozov sl2-model and Clebsch-Gordan decomposition to determine the Jordan partition of a tensor-sum nilpotent operator on an orthogonal tensor product space, then compute the dimension of its centralizer in so(U) from the partition via the gl-centralizer and the orthogonal correction term.
givens: V is a 7-dimensional complex vector space with a non-degenerate symmetric bilinear form.
W is a 5-dimensional complex vector space with a non-degenerate symmetric bilinear form.
A \in End(V) is nilpotent with one Jordan block of size 7 and is skew-symmetric w.r.t. the form on V.
B \in End(W) is nilpotent with one Jordan block of size 5 and is skew-symmetric w.r.t. the form on W.
U = V ⊗ W with the induced non-degenerate symmetric bilinear form Q.
T = A ⊗ I_W + I_V ⊗ B.
so(U,Q) = {S \in End(U) : S is skew-symmetric w.r.t. Q}.
ask: Compute dim_C { S \in so(U,Q) : ST = TS }.

parameter(s).name: dim_V
parameter(s).purpose: orthogonal sl2-irrep dimension for V (here 7)

parameter(s).name: dim_W
parameter(s).purpose: orthogonal sl2-irrep dimension for W (here 5)

parameter(s).name: tensor_pattern
parameter(s).purpose: determines the diagonal sl2-action and hence the Jordan partition of T

parameter(s).name: nilpotent_block_sizes
parameter(s).domain: single Jordan block in each factor
parameter(s).purpose: forces A,B to be principal nilpotents (raising operators)

invariant(s).name: orthogonal_structure
invariant(s).statement: The symmetric non-degenerate forms on V and W induce a symmetric non-degenerate form Q on U=V⊗W, and T lies in so(U,Q).

invariant(s).name: sl2_structure
invariant(s).statement: By Jacobson-Morozov, A and B are raising operators of sl2-triples, so V≅ and W≅ as sl2-modules; with the diagonal action on U, the raising operator is T.

invariant(s).name: jordan_partition_of_T
invariant(s).statement: Clebsch-Gordan gives U≅⊕⊕⊕⊕, hence the Jordan partition of T is λ=(11,9,7,5,3).

invariant(s).name: orthogonal_centralizer_dimension_formula
invariant(s).statement: If λ is the Jordan partition of a nilpotent in so, then dim Z_so(T) = (Σ_k (λ'_k)^2 - o(λ))/2, where λ' is the conjugate partition and o(λ) is the number of odd parts of λ.

essential_assumption(s): Field is C (characteristic 0), so Jacobson-Morozov and complete reducibility of sl2-modules apply.
The invariant bilinear forms are symmetric (odd-dimensional irreps), so the sl2-actions land in so.
T is interpreted as the diagonal sl2 raising operator on U.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The sl2-module structures of V and W are uniquely determined by the principal nilpotent hypotheses. Clebsch-Gordan uniquely fixes the decomposition of U and thus the Jordan partition λ of T. The orthogonal centralizer dimension is a deterministic function of λ, hence the answer is unique.

technique(s).name: Jacobson-Morozov sl2-triple construction
technique(s).purpose: realize A and B as sl2 raising operators on irreducible orthogonal modules

technique(s).name: Clebsch-Gordan decomposition
technique(s).purpose: decompose U=V⊗W into irreducible sl2-summands

technique(s).name: gl-centralizer via conjugate partition
technique(s).purpose: compute dim C_gl(T) = Σ (lambda'_k)^2

technique(s).name: orthogonal correction by odd-part count
technique(s).purpose: compute dim Z_so(T) = (dim_C_gl - o(lambda))/2

theorem(s).name: Jacobson-Morozov theorem
theorem(s).role: embeds a nilpotent element into an sl2-triple

theorem(s).name: Clebsch-Gordan rule for sl2
theorem(s).role: decomposes tensor products of sl2 irreducibles

theorem(s).name: C[t]-module centralizer formula
theorem(s).role: gives dim C_gl(T) = Σ (λ'_k)^2 for nilpotent Jordan type λ

theorem(s).name: orthogonal nilpotent centralizer dimension formula
theorem(s).role: gives dim Z_so(T) = (Σ (λ'_k)^2 - o(λ))/2

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Jordan canonical form as a C[t]-module decomposition

critical_trick(s): Interpret T as the diagonal sl2 raising operator on U (Jacobson-Morozov).
Read off Jordan block sizes from the irreducible sl2 decomposition (Clebsch-Gordan).
Use the orthogonal centralizer formula (gl centralizer minus odd-block correction, halved).

title: - "Distinct roots means distinct complex numbers z, not multiplicities.

identity.short_name: Unit-circle root count -> coefficient autocorrelation via Parseval viewpoint

problem_spec.summary: Composite (CHAIN) family: compute bridge quantity T from component A (Unit-circle roots of (z+1)^6  (z^3+1)^2); then use that T as a required parameter in component B (Coefficient lag autocorrelation via Fourier coefficient and cosine moments) to determine the final output (reduced mod 1000). Instance summary: Count unit-circle solutions of a binomial square-difference equation to obtain T, then use T as the exponent in a trinomial-power coefficient autocorrelation computation, reduced mod 1000.
givens: P(x)=(1+x+x^2)^T with coefficients a_k.
ask: Compute C=\sum_{k=0}^{2T-1} a_k a_{k+1} mod 1000.

parameter(s).name: equation_structure
parameter(s).purpose: forces a fixed unit-circle solution count T

parameter(s).name: n
parameter(s).purpose: Defines the family P_n(z)=(z+1)^{2n}-(z^n+1)^2; the instance uses n=3 (so degree 6 vs squared degree 3).

parameter(s).name: exponent
parameter(s).purpose: controls the power on each quadratic factor, hence the degree and sum length

parameter(s).name: linear_coefficient_u
parameter(s).purpose: sets the conjugate-like quadratic pair (x^2+u x+1),(x^2-u x+1) enabling cosine reduction

parameter(s).name: lag_t
parameter(s).purpose: specifies which coefficient correlation Σ a_k a_{k+t} is extracted as a Fourier coefficient

parameter(s).name: T_bridge
parameter(s).domain: derived integer computed from component A invariants
parameter(s).purpose: interface quantity produced by component A and consumed by component B (non-stapled coupling).

invariant(s).name: unit_circle_modulus_for_conjugate_reciprocal_pair
invariant(s).statement: If a quadratic has real coefficients and root product 1, then its nonreal roots are conjugate and lie on |z|=1.

invariant(s).name: autocorrelation_as_coeff_extraction
invariant(s).statement: \sum_k a_k a_{k+t} = [x^t] P(x)P(x^{-1}).

invariant(s).name: unit_circle_phase_alignment
invariant(s).statement: For z=e^{iθ}, (z+1)^{2n} and (z^n+1)^2 both carry the phase factor e^{inθ}, so P_n(e^{iθ})=0 reduces to a purely real equation 2^{2n-2} cos^{2n}(θ/2) = cos^2(nθ/2).

invariant(s).name: triple_angle_closure_for_n3
invariant(s).statement: When n=3, the reduced equation involves cos(3x), allowing elimination via cos(3x)=4cos^3 x - 3cos x to obtain a polynomial equation in cos x.

invariant(s).name: correlation_is_fourier_coefficient
invariant(s).statement: If P(x)= a_k x^k, then _k a_k a_{k+t} = [x^t] P(x)P(x^{-1}) = (1/2)_0^{2} |P(e^{i})|^2 e^{-it} d. For real coefficients, this equals (1/2) |P(e^{i})|^2 cos(t) d.

invariant(s).name: bridge_interface_invariant
invariant(s).statement: The composite is coupled by the interface variable T: component A uniquely determines T, and component B is defined/solved only after substituting this specific T into its parameter slot.

essential_assumption(s): Distinct unit-circle solutions are counted without multiplicity.
z lies on the unit circle so z=e^{iθ} with θ real.
Cancelling the common factor e^{i3θ} is valid since it is never zero.

title: - "Exactly two distinct real roots means one root has multiplicity 2 and the other has multiplicity

identity.short_name: Double-root cubic count -> reciprocal quartic triple-square sum

subdomain(s): rational root theorem and Vieta relations

problem_spec.summary: Composite (CHAIN) family: compute bridge quantity T from component A (Count bounded cubics with exactly one double root and both roots in (2,2)); then use that T as a required parameter in component B (Triple products of squared roots via reciprocal Newton sums (quartic)) to determine the final output (reduced mod 1000). Instance summary: Count monic cubics with a prescribed double root and simple root in {-1,0,1} to get T, then compute a reciprocal quartic triple-product-of-squares sum via reciprocal Newton identities, reduced mod 1000.
givens: Cubic p has a double root r in {-1,0,1} and a simple root s in {-1,0,1}, rs.
T is the number of such cubics.
ask: Compute U mod 1000.

parameter(s).name: root_set
parameter(s).purpose: limits cubic roots to a finite set

parameter(s).name: coefficient_box
parameter(s).purpose: finite search space for enumeration/counting.

parameter(s).name: target_interval

invariant(s).name: repeated_root_factorization
invariant(s).statement: Double root implies p(x)=(x-r)^2(x-s).

invariant(s).name: reciprocal_quartic_inversion_closure
invariant(s).statement: Q_T is palindromic so roots are closed under u->1/u.

invariant(s).name: triple_square_sum_equals_p2
invariant(s).statement: For product 1, _{i<j<k} u_i^2u_j^2u_k^2= 1/u_i^2 = p2.

invariant(s).name: multiplicity_characterization
invariant(s).statement: A real monic cubic has exactly two distinct real roots iff it factors as (xr)^2(xs) with rs real, equivalently iff gcd(P,P) has degree 1 (or discriminant =0 but P not a perfect cube).

invariant(s).name: double_root_rational_then_integer
invariant(s).statement: The double root r is a root of gcd(P,P), hence rational; since P is monic with integer coefficients, the Rational Root Theorem forces r to be an integer dividing c.

invariant(s).name: interval_forces_small_root_set
invariant(s).statement: With r integer and r(2,2), we must have r{1,0,1}. Vieta then gives s=a2r, so s is also an integer and s(2,2) forces s{1,0,1}.

invariant(s).name: coefficient_reconstruction_from_roots
invariant(s).statement: If P(x)=(xr)^2(xs), then (a,b,c)=(-(2r+s), r^2+2rs, r^2 s). Thus each admissible ordered pair (r,s) yields exactly one coefficient triple.

invariant(s).name: complementary_product_identity
invariant(s).statement: For roots r1..r4 with product e4=a4, we have _{i<j<k} r_i^2 r_j^2 r_k^2 = e4^2 _{i=1}^4 1/r_i^2.

invariant(s).name: reciprocal_polynomial_roots
invariant(s).statement: If a40, then s_i=1/r_i are roots of the monic reciprocal polynomial Q(x) = (1/a4) x^4 P(1/x) = x^4 + (a3/a4)x^3 + (a2/a4)x^2 + (a1/a4)x + (1/a4).

invariant(s).name: newton_girard_for_p2
invariant(s).statement: For monic Q(x)=x^4+A1 x^3+A2 x^2+A3 x+A4 with roots s_i and p_k= s_i^k, Newton gives p1=-A1 and p2 = A1^2 - 2A2, uniquely determined by (A1,A2).

invariant(s).name: coefficient_only_closed_form
invariant(s).statement: Combining the reciprocal transform and Newton for p2 yields T = a4^2 \cdot p2 = a4^2 \cdot ((a3/a4)^2 - 2(a2/a4)) = a3^2 - 2 a2 a4, so T is a coefficient polynomial independent of a1.

invariant(s).statement: The composite is coupled by the interface variable T: component A uniquely determines T, and component B is defined/solved only after substituting this specific T into its parameter slot.

essential_assumption(s): Distinctness rs ensures degree 3 with exactly one repeated root.
The interval is open; roots equal to ±2 are excluded.

problem_spec.summary: Composite (CHAIN) family: compute bridge quantity T from component A (Count non-palindromic bounded quartics with reciprocal product a square); then use that T as a required parameter in component B (SL4 adjoint invariant polynomials (degree 50)) to determine the final output (reduced mod 1000). Instance summary: Impose that a quadratic's reciprocal product is a perfect square (excluding palindromic/anti-palindromic degeneracies) to obtain a count T, then use T to set a Hilbert-series coefficient problem for an invariant ring with degrees 2,3,4; output mod 1000.
givens: Strict non-(anti-)palindromic: x^2P_{a,b}(1/x) !=  P_{a,b}(x).
Invariant ring degrees are 2,3,4; U counts solutions to 2i+3j+4k=10+T.
ask: Compute U mod 1000.

invariant(s).statement: R_{a,b}(x) is palindromic by construction.

invariant(s).name: square_coefficient_constraints
invariant(s).statement: If R_{a,b}=(ux^2+vx+w)^2, then b=u^2=w^2 and coefficient matching forces strong constraints on a.

invariant(s).name: hilbert_series_counting
invariant(s).statement: Dimension in degree d equals #{(i,j,k)>=0:2i+3j+4k=d}.

invariant(s).name: palindromic_implies_square
invariant(s).statement: If P*=P (equivalently c=a), then P \cdot P* = P^2 is a square in Z[x].

invariant(s).name: gcd_with_reciprocal_self_reciprocal
invariant(s).statement: D=gcd(P,P*) satisfies D*=±D; choosing D monic makes D*=D (self-reciprocal).

invariant(s).name: degree_2_gcd_forces_palindromic
invariant(s).statement: If deg gcd(P,P*)=2, then both the gcd and the remaining quadratic factor have constant term 1 and are self-reciprocal, forcing P*=P (palindromic).

invariant(s).name: coprime_product_square_forcing
invariant(s).statement: If gcd(P,P*)=1 and PP* is a square in Z[x], then P is a square in Z[x] up to a unit; monicity forces P=Q^2 with QZ[x].

invariant(s).name: odd_degree_not_square
invariant(s).statement: A nonzero square polynomial in Z[x] has even degree; in particular, no cubic in Z[x] is a square.

invariant(s).name: self_duality_of_adjoint
invariant(s).statement: V^*  V as G-modules via the nondegenerate Killing form, so dimensions of invariant subspaces in Sym^d(V^*) and Sym^d(V) agree.

invariant(s).name: chevalley_restriction_for_adjoint
invariant(s).statement: The invariant ring (Sym(V^*))^G is isomorphic (as a graded algebra) to (Sym(h^*))^W where h is a Cartan subalgebra and W is the Weyl group of G.

invariant(s).name: generator_degrees_for_sl4
invariant(s).statement: For sl_4 (type A_3), (Sym(V^*))^G is a polynomial algebra on three homogeneous generators of degrees 2,3,4, corresponding to the elementary symmetric polynomials e2,e3,e4 in eigenvalues under the trace-zero constraint.

invariant(s).name: graded_dimension_as_monomial_count
invariant(s).statement: If (Sym(V^*))^G  C[f2,f3,f4] with deg(f2)=2, deg(f3)=3, deg(f4)=4, then dim (Sym^d(V^*))^G = #{(a,b,c) in Z_{>=0}^3 : 2a+3b+4c=d}.

title: Centered symmetric quartic no-real-root count -> cubic power-sum partial sum

identity.short_name: Centered symmetric quartic no-real-root count -> cubic power-sum partial sum

problem_spec.summary: Composite (CHAIN) family: compute bridge quantity T from component A (Count monic quartics symmetric about 1 with no real roots); then use that T as a required parameter in component B (Partial sum of cubic-root power sums modulo 1000) to determine the final output (reduced mod 1000). Instance summary: Count (a,b) in a centered-even quartic y^4+ay^2+b with positivity on y^2>=0 to get T, then use T as the endpoint of a fixed cubic-root power-sum recurrence sum modulo 1000.
givens: T counts pairs (a,b) for which P_{a,b} has no real roots.
alpha,beta,gamma are roots of y^3-3y+1=0; S_n=alpha^n+beta^n+gamma^n.
ask: Compute N=(\sum_{n=0}^T S_n) mod 1000.

invariant(s).name: even_shift_invariant
invariant(s).statement: After shifting by x->y+1, the quartic becomes y^4+ay^2+b (even).

invariant(s).name: positivity_on_halfline
invariant(s).statement: No real roots iff z^2+az+b>0 for all z>=0 with z=y^2.

invariant(s).name: symmetry_implies_even_after_shift
invariant(s).statement: Let y=x-1 and Q(y)=P(y+1). Then Q is even: Q(-y)=Q(y), so Q(y)=y^4+by^2+d.

invariant(s).name: coefficient_relations_from_center_one_symmetry
invariant(s).statement: For monic quartics with P(2-x)=P(x), necessarily a_3=-4 and a_1=8-2a_2. Equivalently P(x)=(x-1)^4+b(x-1)^2+d with integers b=a_2-6 and d=a_0-a_2+5.

invariant(s).name: positivity_reduction_to_quadratic
invariant(s).statement: With Q(y)=y^4+by^2+d, "no real roots (monic quartic) is equivalent to Q(y)>0 for all y, which is equivalent to z^2+bz+d>0 for all z>=0 via z=y^2.

invariant(s).name: root_polynomial_relation
invariant(s).statement: Each root r satisfies r^3 = 3r - 1.

invariant(s).name: partial_sum_determinism
invariant(s).statement: Once S_0,,S_N are determined, T=_{n=0}^N S_n is uniquely determined.

invariant(s).name: bridge_interface_invariant
invariant(s).statement: The composite is coupled by the interface variable T: component A uniquely determines T, and component B is defined/solved only after substituting this specific T into its parameter slot.

essential_assumption(s): Strict inequality is used so boundary cases (zero) are excluded, guaranteeing no real roots.
Coefficients are integers and the polynomial is monic of degree 4.
"No real roots" is interpreted as P(x)0 for all real x; for monic quartics this is equivalent to strict positivity.
Integer coefficient bounds ensure finiteness of the search/count.
The polynomial is monic of degree 3, so every root satisfies the same cubic relation, yielding a linear recurrence for S_n.
Roots are counted with multiplicity (standard for Vieta/Newton sums).
Modular reduction is applied to integer values obtained from the recurrence.
The bridge quantity T computed from component A is guaranteed to be an integer in the admissible range required by component B (e.g., as an index, bound, or group-action parameter).
uniqueness_certificate(s): The finite case split on a gives a unique integer T.
Given T, the recurrence uniquely determines the partial sum modulo 1000.

Symmetry about x=1 forces the unique representation P(x)=(x-1)^4+b(x-1)^2+d with integers b,d. The coefficient bounds translate to a finite set of integer pairs (b,d). The condition "no real roots is equivalent to a strict quadratic inequality z^2+bz+d>0 on z>=0, which yields necessary-and-sufficient inequalities in (b,d). Therefore the admissible set is a uniquely determined finite set of lattice points, so the final count is unique.
The recurrence S_n = 3S_{n-2} - S_{n-3} together with initial values S_0=3, S_1=0, S_2=6 uniquely determines S_0,,S_20, hence uniquely determines T and T mod 1000.
(N1 necessity) If T is not computed from component A, component B has multiple admissible outputs across different possible parameter choices; thus the composite is ill-posed without the bridge.
(N2 non-factorization) The final answer is not a trivial Combine(ansA, ansB): the computed T restricts/changes the admissible branch structure inside component B before its output is even defined.

title: Conjugated differential operator -> quadratic-probe resultant

identity.short_name: Conjugated differential operator -> quadratic-probe resultant

problem_spec.summary: Composite (CHAIN) family: compute bridge quantity T from component A (Conjugated differential operator on a quadratic (variant)); then use that T as a required parameter in component B (Engineered remainder  quadratic probe resultant product) to determine the final output (reduced mod 1000). Instance summary: Solve two conjugated differential-operator equations for quadratics, form a complex squared-distance bridge parameter T, then compute a cubicquadratic resultant via evaluation at i, reduced mod 1000.
givens: Operators L_+(P)=e^{x} D^2(e^{-x}P) and L_-(P)=e^{-x} D^2(e^{x}P).
Quadratics P_+,P_- satisfy L_+(P_+)=x and L_-(P_-)=x.
ask: Compute N=Res(f,x^2+1) mod 1000.
