invariant(s).statement: L_+(P)=P''-2P'+P and L_-(P)=P''+2P'+P for polynomials P.

invariant(s).name: resultant_quadratic_probe
invariant(s).statement: Res(f,x^2+1)=f(i)f(-i) since x^2+1 splits over C.

invariant(s).name: exponential_conjugation_identity
invariant(s).statement: e^{-cx}(D+)e^{cx}=D++c and e^{cx}(D+)e^{-cx}=D+c, so exponentials can be removed by conjugation to yield a constant-coefficient operator on P.

invariant(s).name: polynomial_identity_principle
invariant(s).statement: If two polynomials agree for all real x, their coefficients match termwise over .

invariant(s).name: engineered_remainder_identity
invariant(s).statement: Since E(x)=xP(x)+Q(x), we have E(x)  Q(x) (mod P(x)), hence for every root r of P, E(r)=Q(r).

invariant(s).name: resultant_equals_product_over_roots
invariant(s).statement: For monic P, Res(P,Q)=_{P(r)=0} Q(r) (counted with multiplicity), so the target product equals a resultant.

invariant(s).name: integer_valued_symmetry
invariant(s).statement: Res(P,Q) is an integer polynomial in the coefficients of P and Q; therefore the product is an integer.

invariant(s).name: bridge_interface_invariant
invariant(s).statement: The composite is coupled by the interface variable T: component A uniquely determines T, and component B is defined/solved only after substituting this specific T into its parameter slot.

essential_assumption(s): Work over C when using i; final resultant is an integer via conjugate pairing.
P is restricted to degree 2, so T_{±} maps within the 3D coefficient space of quadratics.
Equality T_{±}P_{±}=Q holds for all real x, hence as a polynomial identity.
N is taken as the canonical residue in {0,,999}.
Fundamental Theorem of Algebra: P has exactly three complex roots counted with multiplicity.
Work in characteristic 0 so resultants and division algorithm behave as usual.
The product over roots uses multiplicity (standard convention), matching the resultant identity.
The bridge quantity T computed from component A is guaranteed to be an integer in the admissible range required by component B (e.g., as an index, bound, or group-action parameter).
uniqueness_certificate(s): Coefficient matching yields unique P_+ and P_-; hence unique T.
Given T, evaluation at i uniquely determines the resultant, then mod_1000 normalization is deterministic.

Restricting to quadratics, T_{} becomes a linear map with nonzero action on the x^2 coefficient (12a for +, 20a for ), producing a triangular coefficient-matching system that uniquely determines (a,b,c). Subsequent evaluation and modular reduction are deterministic.

Because E(x)=xP(x)+Q(x), the product  E(r_i) equals  Q(r_i)=Res(P,Q). The resultant is uniquely determined by the coefficients of P and Q, hence the integer product is unique. Reducing modulo m gives a unique residue in {0,...,m-1}.
(N1 necessity) If T is not computed from component A, component B has multiple admissible outputs across different possible parameter choices; thus the composite is ill-posed without the bridge.
(N2 non-factorization) The final answer is not a trivial Combine(ansA, ansB): the computed T restricts/changes the admissible branch structure inside component B before its output is even defined.

title: Finite-field inversion 2-cycle count -> central trinomial-type coefficient mod 1000

identity.short_name: Finite-field inversion 2-cycle count -> central trinomial-type coefficient mod 1000

subdomain(s): affine-linear constraint counting over finite fields

problem_spec.summary: Composite (CHAIN) family: compute bridge quantity T from component A (Count F13 degree-8 twisted-reciprocal polynomials with exactly two inversion 2-cycles as roots); then use that T as a required parameter in component B (Coefficient lag autocorrelation via Fourier coefficient and cosine moments) to determine the final output (reduced mod 1000). Instance summary: Count reciprocal quartics over F11 whose roots are exactly two inversion 2-cycles to obtain T, then compute the coefficient [x^T](1+x+x^2)^{2T} via coefficient-extraction/Parseval viewpoint and reduce mod 1000.
givens: Field is F_11; f(x)=x^4+ax^3+bx^2+ax+1 is monic reciprocal.
Goodness: roots in F_11^* form exactly two inversion 2-cycles (no 1 roots).
ask: Compute N = [x^T]A(x) mod 1000.

parameter(s).name: prime
parameter(s).purpose: finite-field instance

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: determines F_q^* cyclic structure and inversion orbits; instance uses q=13.

parameter(s).name: degree
parameter(s).purpose: polynomial degree; instance uses degree=8.

parameter(s).name: twist_exponent_k
parameter(s).purpose: defines twisted reciprocity f(x)=x^k f(1/x) on F_q^*; instance uses k=4.

parameter(s).name: fixed_coefficient
parameter(s).purpose: pins a coefficient in the reduced template; instance fixes a3=5.

parameter(s).name: root_count_target
parameter(s).purpose: target number of distinct roots in F_q^*; instance uses 4.

parameter(s).name: fixed_point_exclusion
parameter(s).purpose: forces roots to come only from inversion 2-cycles, not fixed points.

parameter(s).name: exponent
parameter(s).purpose: controls the power on each quadratic factor, hence the degree and sum length

parameter(s).name: linear_coefficient_u
parameter(s).purpose: sets the conjugate-like quadratic pair (x^2+u x+1),(x^2-u x+1) enabling cosine reduction

parameter(s).name: lag_t
parameter(s).purpose: specifies which coefficient correlation Σ a_k a_{k+t} is extracted as a Fourier coefficient

parameter(s).name: T_bridge
parameter(s).domain: derived integer computed from component A invariants
parameter(s).purpose: interface quantity produced by component A and consumed by component B (non-stapled coupling).

invariant(s).name: inversion_orbit_structure
invariant(s).statement: Inversion fixed points are 1; remaining elements partition into 2-cycles.

invariant(s).name: reciprocal_polynomial_from_inversion_pairs
invariant(s).statement: Root set closed under inversion implies f(x) is reciprocal.

invariant(s).name: coefficient_template_from_twisted_symmetry
invariant(s).statement: Using x^(q-1)=1 on F_q^* and comparing reduced coefficients modulo x^(q-1)-1, the condition f(x)=x^4 f(1/x) on F_13^* forces a5=a6=a7=0 and a4=a0, a3=a1, leaving the template f(x)=x^8 + a0 x^4 + a1 x^3 + a2 x^2 + a1 x + a0.

invariant(s).name: parameter_reduction_by_fixed_coefficient
invariant(s).statement: Fixing the x^3 coefficient a3=5 forces a1=5, so the family is parameterized by (a0,a2)F_13^2.

invariant(s).name: inversion_orbits_in_Fq_star
invariant(s).statement: Inversion partitions F_13^* into fixed points {1,-1} and five 2-cycles: {2,7},{3,9},{4,10},{5,8},{6,11}.

invariant(s).name: root_orbit_closure_under_twisted_symmetry
invariant(s).statement: If rF_13^* and f(r)=0, then 0=f(r)=r^4 f(1/r), so f(1/r)=0. Thus roots occur in inversion-orbits.

invariant(s).name: exact_root_count_to_orbit_selection
invariant(s).statement: With fixed points excluded and root_count_target=4, the root set in F_13^* must consist of exactly two inversion 2-cycles (and no other orbit).

invariant(s).name: orbit_root_conditions_are_affine_lines
invariant(s).statement: For each 2-cycle representative r, the condition f(r)=0 is an affine-linear equation in (a0,a2), i.e. a line in F_13^2. Thus "exactly two 2-cycles are roots corresponds to points lying on exactly two of the five orbit-lines and not on the fixed-point root lines.

invariant(s).name: correlation_is_fourier_coefficient
invariant(s).statement: If P(x)= a_k x^k, then _k a_k a_{k+t} = [x^t] P(x)P(x^{-1}) = (1/2)_0^{2} |P(e^{i})|^2 e^{-it} d. For real coefficients, this equals (1/2) |P(e^{i})|^2 cos(t) d.

invariant(s).name: bridge_interface_invariant
invariant(s).statement: The composite is coupled by the interface variable T: component A uniquely determines T, and component B is defined/solved only after substituting this specific T into its parameter slot.

essential_assumption(s): Distinct polynomials correspond to distinct root multisets in F_11^*.
The symmetry f(x)=x^4 f(1/x) is required only on F_13^*, not as a polynomial identity in F_13[x].
Roots counted are distinct and lie in F_13^* (x=0 excluded).
Fixed-point exclusion f(1)0 is enforced as part of the specification.
P has real coefficients so P(e^{-iθ}) is the complex conjugate of P(e^{iθ}).
Orthogonality of exponentials on [0,2\pi] is used to identify coefficients via integrals.
Even-power cosine moment formula applies: (1/2)_0^{2} cos^{2j}() d = C(2j,j)/4^j.
The bridge quantity T computed from component A is guaranteed to be an integer in the admissible range required by component B (e.g., as an index, bound, or group-action parameter).
uniqueness_certificate(s): There are exactly 4 nontrivial inversion pairs, hence unique T=6.
Given T, the coefficient in A(x) is uniquely determined and reduced mod 1000.

The symmetry and fixed coefficient reduce f to a finite 2-parameter family (a0,a2)F_13^2. Each inversion 2-cycle root condition is a single affine-linear constraint in these parameters. Therefore the valid polynomials correspond to a finite, explicitly enumerable set of parameter points, uniquely determining the count.

Because every element of F_13^* lies in exactly one inversion-orbit, verifying that a parameter point lies on exactly two orbit-lines (and on neither fixed-point line) is equivalent to verifying the exact root-orbit pattern; hence correctness is checkable and the answer is unique.

The polynomial P is fixed by the givens, so the coefficient sequence (a_k) is unique. The target C is the coefficient [x^t] of the uniquely determined Laurent polynomial P(x)P(x^{-1}), equivalently a unique Fourier coefficient of the uniquely determined function |P(e^{iθ})|^2. Therefore C is uniquely determined.
(N1 necessity) If T is not computed from component A, component B has multiple admissible outputs across different possible parameter choices; thus the composite is ill-posed without the bridge.
(N2 non-factorization) The final answer is not a trivial Combine(ansA, ansB): the computed T restricts/changes the admissible branch structure inside component B before its output is even defined.

title: GCD -> discriminant -> digit sum -> finite-field root count of x^{2T+1}-x

identity.short_name: GCD -> discriminant -> digit sum -> finite-field root count of x^{2T+1}-x

subdomain(s): affine-linear constraint counting over finite fields

problem_spec.summary: Composite (CHAIN) family: compute bridge quantity T from component A (GCD  discriminant digit sum  engineered (2+1) derivative  splitting field  log modulus); then use that T as a required parameter in component B (Count F13 degree-8 twisted-reciprocal polynomials with exactly two inversion 2-cycles as roots) to determine the final output (reduced mod 1000). Instance summary: Compute a gcd to obtain a quadratic, take its discriminant and digit sum T, then count roots of x^{2T+1}-x over F29 using cyclic multiplicative group structure; output mod 1000.
givens: D=Disc(F); T is digit sum of |D|.
Over F_29, h(x)=x^{2T+1}-x.
ask: Compute number of roots of h in F_29 mod 1000.

essential_assumption(s): p is prime so F_p^* is cyclic; roots are counted in F_29 only.
The computed gcd F(x) has degree 2 (quadratic).
2γ+1 is nonzero in _p (equivalently p ∤ (2γ+1)), so c=(2γ+1)^{-1} exists.
gcd(p,2γ)=1 so x^{2γ}-1 has distinct roots and the roots-of-unity splitting criterion applies cleanly.
m is taken among finite fields _{p^d} (prime-power sizes).
The symmetry f(x)=x^4 f(1/x) is required only on F_13^*, not as a polynomial identity in F_13[x].
Roots counted are distinct and lie in F_13^* (x=0 excluded).
Fixed-point exclusion f(1)0 is enforced as part of the specification.
The bridge quantity T computed from component A is guaranteed to be an integer in the admissible range required by component B (e.g., as an index, bound, or group-action parameter).
uniqueness_certificate(s): gcd and discriminant are deterministic; digit sum gives unique T.
Cyclic-group counting yields a unique root count for h.

F is uniquely determined as the gcd in ℤ[x] with positive leading coefficient, hence δ and γ are deterministic. The coefficient c=(2γ+1)^{-1} is unique in _p, so f' is uniquely x^{2γ}-1. The smallest extension degree d for splitting is uniquely ord_{2γ}(p), hence m=p^d is unique. Since 2 \cdot log_{p^2}(m)=d and γ are fixed, the modulus (d+γ) and the residue δ mod (d+γ) are uniquely determined.

The symmetry and fixed coefficient reduce f to a finite 2-parameter family (a0,a2)F_13^2. Each inversion 2-cycle root condition is a single affine-linear constraint in these parameters. Therefore the valid polynomials correspond to a finite, explicitly enumerable set of parameter points, uniquely determining the count.

Because every element of F_13^* lies in exactly one inversion-orbit, verifying that a parameter point lies on exactly two orbit-lines (and on neither fixed-point line) is equivalent to verifying the exact root-orbit pattern; hence correctness is checkable and the answer is unique.
(N1 necessity) If T is not computed from component A, component B has multiple admissible outputs across different possible parameter choices; thus the composite is ill-posed without the bridge.
(N2 non-factorization) The final answer is not a trivial Combine(ansA, ansB): the computed T restricts/changes the admissible branch structure inside component B before its output is even defined.

problem_spec.summary: Composite (CHAIN) family: compute bridge quantity T from component A (GCD  discriminant digit sum  engineered derivative  splitting field  affine-log modulus); then use that T as a required parameter in component B (Discriminant mod 1000 via linear+quadratic factor chain) to determine the final output (reduced mod 1000). givens: A={t: gcd(f_t,f_t')1}; T=|A|.
ask: Compute N=Disc(f_T) mod 1000.

invariant(s).name: derivative_cancellation_in_char_p
invariant(s).statement: In characteristic p, (x^{pδ})' = pδ \cdot x^{pδ-1} = 0, so the x^{pδ} term vanishes in f'(x).

invariant(s).name: engineered_inverse_for_unit_leading_term
invariant(s).statement: With c=(γ+1)^{-1} in _p, we have (c x^{γ+1})' = c(γ+1)x^γ = x^γ, forcing f'(x)=x^γ-1.

invariant(s).name: splitting_of_xn_minus_1
invariant(s).statement: If gcd(p,γ)=1, then x^γ-1 splits over _{p^d} iff γ | (p^d-1). The minimal such d is ord_γ(p).

invariant(s).name: log_p2_prime_power
invariant(s).statement: If m=p^d then log_{p^2}(m)=d/2, hence 2 \cdot log_{p^2}(m)=d is an integer.

invariant(s).name: discriminant_as_resultant
invariant(s).statement: Disc(P) = (-1)^{n(n-1)/2} * Res(P, P') / a_n for deg(P)=n and leading coefficient a_n.

invariant(s).name: iterated_three_factor_rule
invariant(s).statement: For monic A,B,C, Disc(ABC)=Disc(A)Disc(B)Disc(C)Res(A,B)^2Res(A,C)^2Res(B,C)^2.

invariant(s).name: resultant_linear_evaluation
invariant(s).statement: For Q in Z[x], Res(x-r,Q)=Q(r).

invariant(s).name: quadratic_discriminant_formula
invariant(s).statement: For monic quadratic x^2+bx+c, Disc = b^2 - 4c.

invariant(s).name: bridge_interface_invariant
invariant(s).statement: The composite is coupled by the interface variable T: component A uniquely determines T, and component B is defined/solved only after substituting this specific T into its parameter slot.

essential_assumption(s): t is integer and bounded as stated.
The computed gcd F(x) has degree 2 (quadratic).
γ+1 is nonzero in _p (equivalently p ∤ (γ+1)), so c=(γ+1)^{-1} exists.
gcd(p,γ)=1 so x^γ-1 has distinct roots and the roots-of-unity splitting criterion applies cleanly.
m is taken among finite fields _{p^d} (prime-power sizes).
The discriminant is taken in the standard convention via resultant with the derivative.
Modular output uses the canonical residue in {0,1,...,m-1}.
Computations are in Z[x] and Q[x], using UFD properties as needed.
The bridge quantity T computed from component A is guaranteed to be an integer in the admissible range required by component B (e.g., as an index, bound, or group-action parameter).
uniqueness_certificate(s): The classification yields a unique finite set A and hence unique T.
Disc(f_T) is uniquely determined by factor discriminants and resultants.

F is uniquely determined as the gcd in ℤ[x] with positive leading coefficient, hence δ and γ are deterministic. The coefficient c=(γ+1)^{-1} is unique in _p, so f' is uniquely x^γ-1. The smallest extension degree d for splitting is uniquely ord_γ(p), hence m=p^d is unique. Since 2 \cdot log_{p^2}(m)=d and γ are fixed, the modulus d+γ and the residue δ mod (d+γ) are uniquely determined.

The discriminant is a deterministic integer-valued function of polynomial coefficients, and the factorization/product identities are equalities; therefore Disc(P) mod m is uniquely determined for fixed P and m.
(N1 necessity) If T is not computed from component A, component B has multiple admissible outputs across different possible parameter choices; thus the composite is ill-posed without the bridge.
(N2 non-factorization) The final answer is not a trivial Combine(ansA, ansB): the computed T restricts/changes the admissible branch structure inside component B before its output is even defined.

title: Ideal-lattice orbits under Aut(F2[x,y]/(x,y)^2) -> digit-sum power

identity.short_name: Ideal-lattice orbits under Aut(F2[x,y]/(x,y)^2) -> digit-sum power

subdomain(s): ideal-lattice group actions and orbit counting (Burnside)

problem_spec.summary: Composite (CHAIN) family: compute bridge quantity T from component A (Count Aut(R)-orbits of ideals with dim_F2(I/2I)=2 in Z4[x]/(x^3,2x)); then use that T as a required parameter in component B (GCD  discriminant  digit sum  engineered derivative  splitting field affine-log modulus) to determine the final output (reduced mod 1000). Instance summary: Classify ideals in the small local ring F2[x,y]/(x,y)^2 and count Aut-orbits using Burnside to obtain T, then use T inside a quadratic discriminant and digit-sum power U^T modulo 1000.
givens: R=F_2[x,y]/(x,y)^2; I is the set of all ideals; G=Aut(R).
T is the number of G-orbits on I.
q(x)=x^2-Tx+1; U is digit sum of |Disc(q)|; output U^T mod 1000.
ask: Compute U^T mod 1000.

title: Involutions in truncated-ring automorphisms -> Gaussian-binomial codim-2 subspace count

identity.short_name: Involutions in truncated-ring automorphisms -> Gaussian-binomial codim-2 subspace count

subdomain(s): composition of truncated power series over finite fields
finite abelian groups and invariant factors
quotienting by a fixed cyclic subgroup

problem_spec.summary: Composite (CHAIN) family: compute bridge quantity T from component A (Count order-2 automorphisms of F7[x]/(x^6)); then use that T as a required parameter in component B (Count HG with gH and G/H(2^5,2^2), mod 1000) to determine the final output (reduced mod 1000). Instance summary: Count involutions in Aut(F7[x]/(x^3)) by composing truncated series to obtain T, then count codimension-2 subspaces containing a fixed vector in F2^T using Gaussian binomial coefficients; output mod 1000.
givens: R=F_7[x]/(x^3); automorphisms are phi_{a,b}(x)=ax+bx^2.
Involution means phi_{a,b}^2=id; T counts such automorphisms.
V=F_2^T with fixed g0; N counts subspaces H containing g with dim(V/H)=2.
ask: Compute N mod 1000.

invariant(s).statement: is an F_p-algebra automorphism iff (x)=a x + higher terms (mod x^n) with aF_p^.

invariant(s).statement: From the x-coefficient in f^{k}(x)x, one gets a^k=1 in F_p^ (here a^2=1).

invariant(s).name: triangular_coefficient_system
invariant(s).statement: Expanding f^{2}(x) mod x^n yields a (nearly) triangular system of polynomial equations in b,c,d,e solvable by casework on a.

invariant(s).name: containment_reduction_to_quotient
invariant(s).statement: Subgroups HG with gH and G/HQ correspond to kernels of surjections f:GQ with f(g)=0, equivalently to surjections G/gQ. Hence N = |Epi(G/g,Q)| / |Aut(Q)|.

invariant(s).name: hom_count_depends_only_on_invariant_factors
invariant(s).statement: |Hom(G,Q)| is determined by the exponents via |Hom(Z/2^a,Q)| = |Q[2^a]| = 2^{Σ_j min(a,bj)}.

invariant(s).name: epimorphisms_via_maximal_subgroups_rank2
invariant(s).statement: For rank-2 Q, maximal subgroups have index 2 and correspond to the 3 nonzero lines in Q/2Q(Z/2)^2; inclusionexclusion over these maximal subgroups yields |Epi(G,Q)|.

invariant(s).name: bridge_interface_invariant
invariant(s).statement: The composite is coupled by the interface variable T: component A uniquely determines T, and component B is defined/solved only after substituting this specific T into its parameter slot.

essential_assumption(s): Vector spaces are over F2; g is nonzero.
Automorphisms are F_7-algebra automorphisms (fix scalars in F_7).
Composition is computed in R, i.e., all series are truncated mod x^6.
All groups are finite abelian 2-groups written in invariant-factor form.
The condition gH is interpreted literally as subgroup containment.
Aut(Q) acts freely on Epi(G,Q) by postcomposition, so dividing counts kernels.
The bridge quantity T computed from component A is guaranteed to be an integer in the admissible range required by component B (e.g., as an index, bound, or group-action parameter).
uniqueness_certificate(s): The involution constraints determine a finite count T uniquely.
Given T, the Gaussian binomial coefficient gives a unique N.

Each automorphism corresponds to a unique coefficient tuple (a,b,c,d,e); the system f^{2}(x)x (mod x^6) has finitely many solutions in F_7, so |I| is uniquely determined once the coefficient system is solved.

The constraint gH forces reduction to G' = G/g. The kernel of any surjection G'Q gives such an H, and two surjections have the same kernel iff they differ by Aut(Q). Since |Epi(G',Q)| and |Aut(Q)| are uniquely determined from invariant factors and maximal-subgroup inclusionexclusion, N mod 1000 is unique.
(N1 necessity) If T is not computed from component A, component B has multiple admissible outputs across different possible parameter choices; thus the composite is ill-posed without the bridge.
(N2 non-factorization) The final answer is not a trivial Combine(ansA, ansB): the computed T restricts/changes the admissible branch structure inside component B before its output is even defined.

title: Order-3 determinant-2 matrices in GL2(F7) -> weighted Hilbert coefficient

identity.short_name: Order-3 determinant-2 matrices in GL2(F7) -> weighted Hilbert coefficient

subdomain(s): Chevalley restriction and Weyl group invariants
graded dimension / Hilbert series coefficient extraction

problem_spec.summary: Composite (CHAIN) family: compute bridge quantity T from component A (Order-3 automorphisms of F7[x,y]/(x,y)^3 with det=2 linear part (mod 1000)); then use that T as a required parameter in component B (SL4 adjoint invariant polynomials (degree 50)) to determine the final output (reduced mod 1000). Instance summary: Count matrices A in GL2(F7) with det=2 and A^3=I by eigenvalue/conjugacy-class analysis to get T, then count weighted-degree monomials for generator degrees 2,3,4 at degree T, reduced mod 1000.
givens: A in GL2(F7) with det(A)=2 and A^3=I; T is the count.
Invariant ring generator degrees are 2,3,4.
ask: Compute U=#{(i,j,k)>=0:2i+3j+4k=T} mod 1000.

parameter(s).purpose: torsion constraint on the linear part and the automorphism cube

parameter(s).purpose: replaces the SL2 constraint by a fixed determinant constraint in GL2

parameter(s).name: modulus
parameter(s).purpose: output reduction (here 1000)

parameter(s).purpose: selects the graded piece of the invariant polynomial ring to be counted

parameter(s).purpose: fixes the Weyl group (S_4) and fundamental invariant degrees (2,3,4) for the adjoint action

invariant(s).statement: A^3=I implies eigenvalues lie in {1,2,4} over F7.

invariant(s).statement: Class size = |GL2| / |centralizer| for a representative.

invariant(s).name: hilbert_monomial_count
invariant(s).statement: U is # solutions to 2i+3j+4k=T for i,j,k>=0.

invariant(s).name: graded_piece_decomposition
invariant(s).statement: V=/^2 is 2-dimensional and W=^2 is 3-dimensional; moreover W≅Sym^2(V) as a GL(V)-module since char(p)\neq2.

invariant(s).name: automorphism_parameterization
invariant(s).statement: Any _p-algebra automorphism of _p[x,y]/(x,y)^3 is determined uniquely by (A,u) with A\inGL(V) and u\inHom(V,W).

invariant(s).name: cube_condition_linearizes
invariant(s).statement: With ^3=0, (A,u)^3=(A^3,(I+T+T^2)u), where T(u)=Sym^2(A)∘u∘A^{-1}. Hence ψ^3=id iff A^3=I and (I+T+T^2)u=0.

invariant(s).name: determinant_fixed_order3_types_in_GL2_F7
invariant(s).statement: Over _7, det(A)=2 and A^3=I with A\neqI forces A to be either the scalar 4I or conjugate to diag(1,2). These two conjugacy types have sizes 1 and 56 respectively.

invariant(s).name: self_duality_of_adjoint
invariant(s).statement: V^*  V as G-modules via the nondegenerate Killing form, so dimensions of invariant subspaces in Sym^d(V^*) and Sym^d(V) agree.

invariant(s).name: chevalley_restriction_for_adjoint
invariant(s).statement: The invariant ring (Sym(V^*))^G is isomorphic (as a graded algebra) to (Sym(h^*))^W where h is a Cartan subalgebra and W is the Weyl group of G.

invariant(s).name: generator_degrees_for_sl4
invariant(s).statement: For sl_4 (type A_3), (Sym(V^*))^G is a polynomial algebra on three homogeneous generators of degrees 2,3,4, corresponding to the elementary symmetric polynomials e2,e3,e4 in eigenvalues under the trace-zero constraint.

invariant(s).name: graded_dimension_as_monomial_count
invariant(s).statement: If (Sym(V^*))^G  C[f2,f3,f4] with deg(f2)=2, deg(f3)=3, deg(f4)=4, then dim (Sym^d(V^*))^G = #{(a,b,c) in Z_{>=0}^3 : 2a+3b+4c=d}.

invariant(s).name: bridge_interface_invariant
invariant(s).statement: The composite is coupled by the interface variable T: component A uniquely determines T, and component B is defined/solved only after substituting this specific T into its parameter slot.

essential_assumption(s): Matrices are counted in GL2(F7) (det nonzero).
The ring is truncated at degree 3 (^3=0), so quadratic corrections do not create higher-degree terms.
char(_7)=7 does not divide 3, so the C3-actions are semisimple.
The "order 3 constraint excludes A=I.
Work over C (characteristic 0), so Chevalley restriction applies and invariant rings behave as expected.
The adjoint action of SL_4 on sl_4 is the standard conjugation action on traceless matrices.
Grading is by total polynomial degree, and dim_C of each graded piece is finite.
The bridge quantity T computed from component A is guaranteed to be an integer in the admissible range required by component B (e.g., as an index, bound, or group-action parameter).
uniqueness_certificate(s): Eigenvalue and determinant constraints uniquely determine the two conjugacy types and thus T.
Given T, the Diophantine count U is unique.

The count is uniquely determined by: (i) classifying/counting A\inGL2(_7) with det=2 and order 3 via orbit-stabilizer, and (ii) for each conjugacy type of A, computing dim ker(I+T+T^2) on Hom(V,W) from eigenvalues. Summing the uniquely determined contributions yields a unique ||, hence a unique residue mod 1000.

The graded algebra (Sym(V^*))^G is uniquely determined up to isomorphism by Chevalley restriction and the Lie type A_3, yielding a polynomial ring with generator degrees 2,3,4. Therefore the degree-d dimension is the unique coefficient in the Hilbert series 1/((1-t^2)(1-t^3)(1-t^4)), equivalently a unique finite count of solutions to 2a+3b+4c=d. Hence dim_C(W) is uniquely determined for each d.
(N1 necessity) If T is not computed from component A, component B has multiple admissible outputs across different possible parameter choices; thus the composite is ill-posed without the bridge.
(N2 non-factorization) The final answer is not a trivial Combine(ansA, ansB): the computed T restricts/changes the admissible branch structure inside component B before its output is even defined.

The sl2-module structures of V and W are uniquely determined by the principal nilpotent hypotheses. ClebschGordan uniquely fixes the decomposition of U and thus the Jordan partition  of T. The orthogonal centralizer dimension is a deterministic function of , hence the answer is unique.

The graded algebra (Sym(V^*))^G is uniquely determined up to isomorphism by Chevalley restriction and the Lie type A_3, yielding a polynomial ring with generator degrees 2,3,4. Therefore the degree-d dimension is the unique coefficient in the Hilbert series 1/((1-t^2)(1-t^3)(1-t^4)), equivalently a unique finite count of solutions to 2a+3b+4c=d. title: Palindromic quartic square-product constraint count -> fourth-difference invariant for root set {0,1,2,T}

identity.short_name: Palindromic quartic square-product constraint count -> fourth-difference invariant for root set {0,1,2,T}

problem_spec.summary: Composite (CHAIN) family: compute bridge quantity T from component A (Count palindromic quartics with \sum_{i<j} r_i^2 r_j^2 = 9 in a bounded coefficient box); then use that T as a required parameter in component B (Pairwise difference fourth powers from quartic roots) to determine the final output (reduced mod 1000). Instance summary: Use Newton identities to express a palindromic quartic's squared-root pairwise product sum as b^2-2a^2+2, count bounded integer solutions giving S=9 to obtain T, then compute Σ_{i<j}(u_i-u_j)^4 for roots {0,1,2,T} via power-sum identity, reduced mod 1000.
givens: S_{a,b}=Σ_{i<j} r_i^2 r_j^2 and constraint S_{a,b}=9 defines admissible pairs.
T counts admissible (a,b). P_T(x)=x(x-1)(x-2)(x-T).
ask: Compute U=Σ_{i<j}(u_i-u_j)^4 mod 1000.

parameter(s).purpose: finite counting window for (a,b)

parameter(s).name: bound_N
parameter(s).purpose: sets the finite coefficient search region for (a,b).

parameter(s).purpose: restricts quartics to a 2D palindromic subfamily.

parameter(s).purpose: specifies the desired value of S(a,b) (instance target is 9).

parameter(s).domain: Integers (or rationals) defining a monic quartic x^4 - e1 x^3 + e2 x^2 - e3 x + e4.
parameter(s).purpose: Controls the elementary symmetric sums (e1,e2,e3,e4) and hence determines the power sums p_k.

parameter(s).name: target_power
parameter(s).purpose: Specifies the exponent in (r_i - r_j)^4 for the pairwise-difference symmetric sum.

invariant(s).name: square_product_sum_formula
invariant(s).statement: For palindromic quartic, Σ_{i<j} r_i^2 r_j^2 = b^2-2a^2+2.

invariant(s).name: fourth_difference_identity
invariant(s).statement: For four numbers, Σ_{i<j}(u_i-u_j)^4=4p4-4p1p3+3p2^2.

invariant(s).name: newton_closed_form_for_pairwise_squared_products
invariant(s).statement: For any monic quartic x^4+a1 x^3+a2 x^2+a3 x+a4 with roots r_i, \sum_{i<j} r_i^2 r_j^2 = a2^2 - 2 a1 a3 + 2 a4.

invariant(s).name: palindromic_specialization_formula
invariant(s).statement: For P_{a,b}(x)=x^4+ax^3+bx^2+ax+1, the closed form becomes S(a,b)=b^2-2a^2+2.

invariant(s).name: bounded_integer_count_reduction
invariant(s).statement: With |a|,|b|\leqN, solving S(a,b)=target reduces to a finite search over a-values and checking whether a derived integer expression is a perfect square within the b-bound.

invariant(s).name: vieta_map
invariant(s).statement: For P(x)=x^4 - e1 x^3 + e2 x^2 - e3 x + e4 with roots r_i, e1 = sum r_i, e2 = sum_{i<j} r_i r_j, e3 = sum_{i<j<k} r_i r_j r_k, e4 = product r_i.

invariant(s).name: newton_degree4
invariant(s).statement: The power sums p_k = sum_i r_i^k satisfy: p1=e1, p2=e1 p1 - 2e2, p3=e1 p2 - e2 p1 + 3e3, p4=e1 p3 - e2 p2 + e3 p1 - 4e4.

invariant(s).name: pairwise_difference_fourth_power_identity
invariant(s).statement: T = sum_{i<j}(r_i - r_j)^4 equals 4p4 - 4p1 p3 + 3p2^2.

invariant(s).name: bridge_interface_invariant
invariant(s).statement: The composite is coupled by the interface variable T: component A uniquely determines T, and component B is defined/solved only after substituting this specific T into its parameter slot.

essential_assumption(s): All computations are over C for roots, but final symmetric invariants are integers.
Polynomial is monic and roots counted with multiplicity (Newton identities apply).
Constant term is 1 so 0 is not a root (though not required for this particular S-expression).
The coefficient box is finite so the counting task is finite.
Polynomial is monic quartic (degree exactly 4) so Vieta/Newton formulas apply directly.
Roots may be complex; symmetric polynomials in roots are still well-defined and equal to coefficient expressions.
Modulo operation is applied to the final integer value of T (after simplification).
The bridge quantity T computed from component A is guaranteed to be an integer in the admissible range required by component B (e.g., as an index, bound, or group-action parameter).
uniqueness_certificate(s): The bounded Pell-type equation has finitely many solutions; thus T is unique.
Given T, the roots of P_T are fixed, hence U is unique.

Newton identities uniquely determine the symmetric quantity S from the coefficients, giving S=a2^2-2a1a3+2a4. Within the palindromic subfamily this becomes S=b^2-2a^2+2, so S=9 is equivalent to b^2=2a^2+7. The bounded box yields finitely many (a,b) to test, producing a unique count.

T is symmetric in the roots and is therefore a polynomial in the elementary symmetric sums (e1,e2,e3,e4), which are uniquely determined by the coefficients of P. Newton identities compute the required power sums uniquely from (e1,e2,e3,e4), so T (and T mod m) is uniquely determined for each instance.
(N1 necessity) If T is not computed from component A, component B has multiple admissible outputs across different possible parameter choices; thus the composite is ill-posed without the bridge.
(N2 non-factorization) The final answer is not a trivial Combine(ansA, ansB): the computed T restricts/changes the admissible branch structure inside component B before its output is even defined.

title: Palindromic quintic real-root count -> orthogonal centralizer dimension for a T-built partition

identity.short_name: Palindromic quintic real-root count -> orthogonal centralizer dimension for a T-built partition

problem_spec.summary: Composite (CHAIN) family: compute bridge quantity T from component A (Count (a,b) with P(1)=0 giving exactly four distinct real roots); then use that T as a required parameter in component B (Centralizer dimension of a tensor-sum nilpotent in so(35)) to determine the final output (reduced mod 1000). Instance summary: Use palindromic structure and the imposed root at 1 to factor a quintic and reduce real-root counting to a quadratic discriminant, yielding a count T; then form a partition from T and compute the orthogonal centralizer dimension via the conjugate-partition sum-of-squares formula, reduced mod 1000.
givens: T counts values of a for which P_a has exactly four distinct real roots.
lambda=(2T+1,2T-1,2T-3) and C=( (lambda'_k)^2 - o(lambda))/2.
ask: Compute C mod 1000.

invariant(s).statement: With b=-a-1, P_a factors as (x-1)^2(x+1)(x^2+(a+1)x+1).

invariant(s).name: palindromic_reciprocal_form
invariant(s).statement: Coefficients satisfy coeff(z^k)=coeff(z^{5-k}) (pattern [1,a,b,b,a,1]), hence P(z)=z^5 P(1/z).

invariant(s).name: forced_root_at_minus_one
invariant(s).statement: P(-1)=0 for all (a,b), so z=-1 is always a root.

invariant(s).name: reciprocal_root_pairing
invariant(s).statement: If r ? 0 is a root of P, then 1/r is also a root with the same multiplicity. Hence real roots other than  1 occur as reciprocal pairs.

invariant(s).name: imposed_root_at_one
invariant(s).statement: The given constraint P(1)=0 is equivalent to a+b+1=0, so b=-a-1.

invariant(s).name: reduction_to_even_reciprocal_quartic
invariant(s).statement: Q(z)=P(z)/(z+1)=z^4+(a-1)z^3+(b-a+1)z^2+(a-1)z+1 is reciprocal of even degree.

invariant(s).name: quadratic_in_t_after_substitution
invariant(s).statement: For z?0, letting t=z+1/z gives Q(z)=z^2 F(t), where F(t)=t^2+(a-1)t+(b-a-1).

invariant(s).name: explicit_factorization_of_F_under_P1_zero
invariant(s).statement: Under b=-a-1, F(2)=0 and by Vieta the other root equals b, so F(t)=(t-2)(t-b).

invariant(s).name: real_t_range_mapping
invariant(s).statement: For real z?0, t=z+1/z satisfies |t|=2. If |t|>2 then z+1/z=t has exactly two distinct real solutions r,1/r. If t=2 then z=1 (double); if t=-2 then z=-1 (double).

invariant(s).name: orthogonal_structure
invariant(s).statement: The symmetric non-degenerate forms on V and W induce a symmetric non-degenerate form Q on U=VW, and T lies in so(U,Q).

invariant(s).name: sl2_structure
invariant(s).statement: By Jacobson-Morozov, A and B are raising operators of sl2-triples, so V≅ and W≅ as sl2-modules; with the diagonal action on U, the raising operator is T.

invariant(s).name: jordan_partition_of_T
invariant(s).statement: Clebsch-Gordan gives U≅⊕⊕⊕⊕, hence the Jordan partition of T is λ=(11,9,7,5,3).

invariant(s).name: bridge_interface_invariant
invariant(s).statement: The composite is coupled by the interface variable T: component A uniquely determines T, and component B is defined/solved only after substituting this specific T into its parameter slot.

essential_assumption(s): Distinct real roots are counted without multiplicity; the (x-1)^2 factor contributes only one distinct root at 1.
a and b are integers constrained to a finite box.
The polynomial is palindromic of odd degree (ensuring reciprocity and the forced root z=-1).
The additional constraint P(1)=0 is imposed (so z=1 is a root and b=-a-1 holds).
Distinct real roots are counted without multiplicity.
Constant term is nonzero (prevents z=0 roots and supports the t=z+1/z reduction).
Field is C (characteristic 0), so JacobsonMorozov and complete reducibility of sl2-modules apply.
The invariant bilinear forms are symmetric (odd-dimensional irreps), so the sl2-actions land in so.
T is interpreted as the diagonal sl2 raising operator on U.
The bridge quantity T computed from component A is guaranteed to be an integer in the admissible range required by component B (e.g., as an index, bound, or group-action parameter).
uniqueness_certificate(s): The discriminant test uniquely determines which a contribute, hence T.
Given T, lambda is fixed and the centralizer formula yields a unique integer C.

Under P(1)=0, b=-a-1 and F(t)=(t-2)(t-b). The distinct real roots of P are {-1} ? (real roots of Q), and real roots of Q correspond exactly to t-roots of F with |t|=2 via t=z+1/z. Thus P has exactly four distinct real roots iff the second t-root b satisfies |b|>2, producing exactly one additional real reciprocal pair {r,1/r}, and no other t-roots exist.
The condition b=-a-1 and |b|>2 together with |a|,|b|=8 defines a finite set of integer pairs (a,b) uniquely, so the resulting count is uniquely determined.

The sl2-module structures of V and W are uniquely determined by the principal nilpotent hypotheses. ClebschGordan uniquely fixes the decomposition of U and thus the Jordan partition  of T. The orthogonal centralizer dimension is a deterministic function of , hence the answer is unique.
(N1 necessity) If T is not computed from component A, component B has multiple admissible outputs across different possible parameter choices; thus the composite is ill-posed without the bridge.
(N2 non-factorization) The final answer is not a trivial Combine(ansA, ansB): the computed T restricts/changes the admissible branch structure inside component B before its output is even defined.

Work in characteristic 0 so resultants and division algorithm behave as usual.
The product over roots uses multiplicity (standard convention), matching the resultant identity.
The discriminant is taken in the standard convention via resultant with the derivative.
Modular output uses the canonical residue in {0,1,...,m-1}.
Computations are in Z[x] and Q[x], using UFD properties as needed.
The bridge quantity T computed from component A is guaranteed to be an integer in the admissible range required by component B (e.g., as an index, bound, or group-action parameter).
uniqueness_certificate(s): Resultant evaluation gives a unique integer T.
Given T, Disc(F) computed from fixed factors is unique; mod_1000 normalization is deterministic.

Because E(x)=xP(x)+Q(x), the product  E(r_i) equals  Q(r_i)=Res(P,Q). The resultant is uniquely determined by the coefficients of P and Q, hence the integer product is unique. Reducing modulo m gives a unique residue in {0,...,m-1}.

The discriminant is a deterministic integer-valued function of polynomial coefficients, and the factorization/product identities are equalities; therefore Disc(P) mod m is uniquely determined for fixed P and m.
(N1 necessity) If T is not computed from component A, component B has multiple admissible outputs across different possible parameter choices; thus the composite is ill-posed without the bridge.
(N2 non-factorization) The final answer is not a trivial Combine(ansA, ansB): the computed T restricts/changes the admissible branch structure inside component B before its output is even defined.

title: Quartic pairwise fourth-difference invariant -> Hilbert coefficient count

identity.short_name: Quartic pairwise fourth-difference invariant -> Hilbert coefficient count

subdomain(s): Chevalley restriction and Weyl group invariants
graded dimension / Hilbert series coefficient extraction

problem_spec.summary: Composite (CHAIN) family: compute bridge quantity T from component A (Pairwise difference fourth powers from quartic roots); then use that T as a required parameter in component B (SL4 adjoint invariant polynomials (degree 50)) to determine the final output (reduced mod 1000). Instance summary: Compute T=Σ_{i<j}(r_i-r_j)^4 from quartic roots using power-sum identities, then use T as a degree parameter in a Hilbert-series monomial count for generators of degrees 2,3,4, reduced mod 1000.
givens: p(x)=x^4-6x^3+11x^2-6x with roots r_1..r_4.
Invariant ring generators have degrees 2,3,4.
ask: Compute U = #{(i,j,k)>=0:2i+3j+4k=T} mod 1000.

parameter(s).purpose: determines T uniquely

parameter(s).purpose: Controls the elementary symmetric sums (e1,e2,e3,e4) and hence determines the power sums p_k.

parameter(s).name: target_power
parameter(s).purpose: Specifies the exponent in (r_i - r_j)^4 for the pairwise-difference symmetric sum.

parameter(s).purpose: Controls output normalization by modular reduction.

parameter(s).purpose: selects the graded piece of the invariant polynomial ring to be counted

parameter(s).purpose: interface quantity produced by component A and consumed by component B (non-stapled coupling).

invariant(s).name: difference_power_sum_identity
invariant(s).statement: For n=4, Σ_{i<j}(r_i-r_j)^4 = 4p4-4p1p3+3p2^2 where pm=Σ r_i^m.

invariant(s).name: hilbert_monomial_count
invariant(s).statement: Degree-T dimension equals #{(i,j,k):2i+3j+4k=T}.

invariant(s).name: vieta_map
invariant(s).statement: For P(x)=x^4 - e1 x^3 + e2 x^2 - e3 x + e4 with roots r_i, e1 = sum r_i, e2 = sum_{i<j} r_i r_j, e3 = sum_{i<j<k} r_i r_j r_k, e4 = product r_i.

invariant(s).name: newton_degree4
invariant(s).statement: The power sums p_k = sum_i r_i^k satisfy: p1=e1, p2=e1 p1 - 2e2, p3=e1 p2 - e2 p1 + 3e3, p4=e1 p3 - e2 p2 + e3 p1 - 4e4.

invariant(s).name: pairwise_difference_fourth_power_identity
invariant(s).statement: T = sum_{i<j}(r_i - r_j)^4 equals 4p4 - 4p1 p3 + 3p2^2.

invariant(s).name: self_duality_of_adjoint
invariant(s).statement: V^* ≅ V as G-modules via the nondegenerate Killing form, so dimensions of invariant subspaces in Sym^d(V^*) and Sym^d(V) agree.

invariant(s).name: chevalley_restriction_for_adjoint
invariant(s).statement: The invariant ring (Sym(V^*))^G is isomorphic (as a graded algebra) to (Sym(h^*))^W where h is a Cartan subalgebra and W is the Weyl group of G.

invariant(s).name: generator_degrees_for_sl4
invariant(s).statement: For sl_4 (type A_3), (Sym(V^*))^G is a polynomial algebra on three homogeneous generators of degrees 2,3,4, corresponding to the elementary symmetric polynomials e2,e3,e4 in eigenvalues under the trace-zero constraint.

invariant(s).name: graded_dimension_as_monomial_count
invariant(s).statement: If (Sym(V^*))^G ≅ C[f2,f3,f4] with deg(f2)=2, deg(f3)=3, deg(f4)=4, then dim (Sym^d(V^*))^G = #{(a,b,c) in Z_{>=0}^3 : 2a+3b+4c=d}.

invariant(s).name: bridge_interface_invariant
invariant(s).statement: The composite is coupled by the interface variable T: component A uniquely determines T, and component B is defined/solved only after substituting this specific T into its parameter slot.

essential_assumption(s): Weighted degree uses generator degrees exactly 2,3,4.
Polynomial is monic quartic (degree exactly 4) so Vieta/Newton formulas apply directly.
Roots may be complex; symmetric polynomials in roots are still well-defined and equal to coefficient expressions.
Modulo operation is applied to the final integer value of T (after simplification).
Work over C (characteristic 0), so Chevalley restriction applies and invariant rings behave as expected.
The adjoint action of SL_4 on sl_4 is the standard conjugation action on traceless matrices.
Grading is by total polynomial degree, and dim_C of each graded piece is finite.
The bridge quantity T computed from component A is guaranteed to be an integer in the admissible range required by component B (e.g., as an index, bound, or group-action parameter).
uniqueness_certificate(s): Power sums are uniquely determined by coefficients; hence T is unique.
Given T, the Diophantine count U is unique.

T is symmetric in the roots and is therefore a polynomial in the elementary symmetric sums (e1,e2,e3,e4), which are uniquely determined by the coefficients of P. Newton identities compute the required power sums uniquely from (e1,e2,e3,e4), so T (and T mod m) is uniquely determined for each instance.

The graded algebra (Sym(V^*))^G is uniquely determined up to isomorphism by Chevalley restriction and the Lie type A_3, yielding a polynomial ring with generator degrees 2,3,4. Therefore the degree-d dimension is the unique coefficient in the Hilbert series 1/((1-t^2)(1-t^3)(1-t^4)), equivalently a unique finite count of solutions to 2a+3b+4c=d. Hence dim_C(W) is uniquely determined for each d.
(N1 necessity) If T is not computed from component A, component B has multiple admissible outputs across different possible parameter choices; thus the composite is ill-posed without the bridge.
(N2 non-factorization) The final answer is not a trivial Combine(ansA, ansB): the computed T restricts/changes the admissible branch structure inside component B before its output is even defined.

title: Reciprocal quartic triple-square sum -> cubic Newton recurrence partial sum

identity.short_name: Reciprocal quartic triple-square sum -> cubic Newton recurrence partial sum

problem_spec.summary: Composite (CHAIN) family: compute bridge quantity T from component A (Triple products of squared roots via reciprocal Newton sums (quartic)); then use that T as a required parameter in component B (Partial sum of cubic-root power sums modulo 1000) to determine the final output (reduced mod 1000). Instance summary: Compute a reciprocal-quartic symmetric triple-product-of-squares invariant T, then use T as the endpoint in a Newton/Vieta-derived recurrence for cubic-root power sums; output the partial sum mod 1000.
givens: P(x)=x^4-2x^3+x^2-2x+1 is monic; r_1,...,r_4 are its roots.
alpha,beta,gamma are the roots of y^3-3y+1=0; S_n=alpha^n+beta^n+gamma^n.
ask: Compute T=\sum_{i<j<k} r_i^2 r_j^2 r_k^2.
Compute N=\left(\sum_{n=0}^{T} S_n\right) mod 1000.

parameter(s).purpose: pins the reciprocal quartic instance

parameter(s).name: cubic_coeffs
parameter(s).purpose: pins the cubic power-sum recurrence instance

parameter(s).name: degree
parameter(s).purpose: fixes Newton-identity depth and the symmetric-expression arity (triples of 4 roots).

parameter(s).name: coefficients
parameter(s).domain: (a1,a2,a3,a4) \in ℤ^4 for a monic quartic P(x)=x^4+a1 x^3+a2 x^2+a3 x+a4, with a4\neq0
parameter(s).purpose: controls the root symmetric sums; nonzero constant term enables reciprocal-root methods.

parameter(s).name: target_expression
parameter(s).purpose: selects the triple-product-of-squares symmetric polynomial.

parameter(s).name: cubic_coefficients

parameter(s).purpose: determines the symmetric sums and the induced power-sum recurrence

parameter(s).purpose: partial-sum cutoff index (here N=20)

parameter(s).name: m
parameter(s).purpose: modulus for the final reduction (here m=1000)

parameter(s).name: output_statistic
parameter(s).purpose: output is T=\sum_{n=0}^N S_n rather than a single S_N

parameter(s).name: T_bridge
parameter(s).domain: derived integer computed from component A invariants
parameter(s).purpose: interface quantity produced by component A and consumed by component B (non-stapled coupling).

invariant(s).name: reciprocal_root_multiset
invariant(s).statement: Since P is monic with constant term 1, if r is a root then 1/r is a root (with multiplicity).

invariant(s).name: triple_square_to_reciprocal_square
invariant(s).statement: \sum_{i<j<k} r_i^2 r_j^2 r_k^2=(\prod r_i)^2 \sum_i 1/r_i^2.

invariant(s).name: cubic_power_sum_recurrence
invariant(s).statement: From y^3=3y-1, the power sums satisfy S_n=3S_{n-2}-S_{n-3} for n>=3.

invariant(s).name: complementary_product_identity
invariant(s).statement: For roots r1..r4 with product e4=a4, we have \sum_{i<j<k} r_i^2 r_j^2 r_k^2 = e4^2 \sum_{i=1}^4 1/r_i^2.

invariant(s).name: reciprocal_polynomial_roots
invariant(s).statement: If a4\neq0, then s_i=1/r_i are roots of the monic reciprocal polynomial Q(x) = (1/a4) x^4 P(1/x) = x^4 + (a3/a4)x^3 + (a2/a4)x^2 + (a1/a4)x + (1/a4).

invariant(s).name: newton_girard_for_p2
invariant(s).statement: For monic Q(x)=x^4+A1 x^3+A2 x^2+A3 x+A4 with roots s_i and p_k=\sum s_i^k, Newton gives p1=-A1 and p2 = A1^2 - 2A2, uniquely determined by (A1,A2).

invariant(s).name: coefficient_only_closed_form
invariant(s).statement: Combining the reciprocal transform and Newton for p2 yields T = a4^2Â \cdot p2 = a4^2Â \cdot ((a3/a4)^2 - 2(a2/a4)) = a3^2 - 2 a2 a4, so T is a coefficient polynomial independent of a1.

invariant(s).name: root_polynomial_relation
invariant(s).statement: Each root r satisfies r^3 = 3r - 1.

invariant(s).name: induced_linear_recurrence_for_power_sums
invariant(s).statement: For n\geq3, S_n = 3S_{n-2} - S_{n-3}.

invariant(s).name: partial_sum_determinism
invariant(s).statement: Once S_0,...,S_N are determined, T=\sum_{n=0}^N S_n is uniquely determined.

invariant(s).name: bridge_interface_invariant
invariant(s).statement: The composite is coupled by the interface variable T: component A uniquely determines T, and component B is defined/solved only after substituting this specific T into its parameter slot.

essential_assumption(s): All sums are over roots with multiplicity; power sums are well-defined in C.
Constant term a4 \neq 0 so no root is 0 and reciprocals 1/r_i exist.
Roots are counted with multiplicity so symmetric identities apply directly.
Polynomial is monic so Newton-Girard identities use the standard monic form.
The polynomial is monic of degree 3, so every root satisfies the same cubic relation, yielding a linear recurrence for S_n.
Roots are counted with multiplicity (standard for Vieta/Newton sums).
Modular reduction is applied to integer values obtained from the recurrence.
The bridge quantity T computed from component A is guaranteed to be an integer in the admissible range required by component B (e.g., as an index, bound, or group-action parameter).
uniqueness_certificate(s): T is determined uniquely by the quartic coefficients via symmetric polynomials.
Given T, the recurrence/Vieta initial conditions determine the partial sum uniquely; the mod 1000 normalization is deterministic.

The target T is symmetric in the multiset of roots, hence determined uniquely by coefficients. The complementary-product identity reduces T to e4^2 times the power sum p2 of reciprocal roots. Newton-Girard identities compute p2 uniquely from the first two coefficients of the reciprocal polynomial, so T is uniquely determined (equivalently by the closed form T=a3^2-2a2a4).
The recurrence S_n = 3S_{n-2} - S_{n-3} together with initial values S_0=3, S_1=0, S_2=6 uniquely determines S_0,...,S_20, hence uniquely determines T and T mod 1000.
(N1 necessity) If T is not computed from component A, component B has multiple admissible outputs across different possible parameter choices; thus the composite is ill-posed without the bridge.
(N2 non-factorization) The final answer is not a trivial Combine(ansA, ansB): the computed T restricts/changes the admissible branch structure inside component B before its output is even defined.

title: Transaction-determined parameter -> discriminant via factor/resultant chain

identity.short_name: Transaction-determined parameter -> discriminant via factor/resultant chain

subdomain(s): integer equations from transactional balance constraints

problem_spec.summary: Composite (CHAIN) family: compute bridge quantity T from component A (Find red-apple price with Store 3 buying exactly one red (square-first)); then use that T as a required parameter in component B (Discriminant mod 1000 via linear+quadratic factor chain) to determine the final output (reduced mod 1000). Instance summary: Solve a bulk-discount linear pricing system to determine T, then compute the discriminant of a linearquadraticquadratic factorization using product-rule discriminants and resultants, reduced mod 1000.
givens: Pricing equations: 2r+g=17 and r+4g=19.
Store 3 check: (r+5g) - (5^2-5) = 2.
Define T=r and F(x)=(x-T)(x^2+Tx+1)(x^2-x+1).
ask: Compute N = Disc(F) mod 1000.

parameter(s).name: transaction_constants

essential_assumption(s): All prices are integers; the stated equations are consistent.
Listed prices are integers and transactions are executed exactly as described.
In Stores 1 and 2, the final red purchase is a single transaction that spends the entire remaining balance.
In Store 3, the final red purchase buys exactly n3 reds and spends the entire remaining balance.
The discriminant is taken in the standard convention via resultant with the derivative.
Modular output uses the canonical residue in {0,1,...,m-1}.
Computations are in Z[x] and Q[x], using UFD properties as needed.
The bridge quantity T computed from component A is guaranteed to be an integer in the admissible range required by component B (e.g., as an index, bound, or group-action parameter).
uniqueness_certificate(s): The system for (r,g) has a unique solution, so T is unique.
With fixed T, discriminant via factor/resultant identities yields a unique integer, then mod_1000 normalization is deterministic.
Stores 1 and 2 restrict R to a small finite set via case splits (n=1,2 vs n3) and divisibility constraints.
Store 3, with square-first pricing and a fixed final red quantity, yields a single equation in R that selects a unique value from the intersection.

The discriminant is a deterministic integer-valued function of polynomial coefficients, and the factorization/product identities are equalities; therefore Disc(P) mod m is uniquely determined for fixed P and m.
(N1 necessity) If T is not computed from component A, component B has multiple admissible outputs across different possible parameter choices; thus the composite is ill-posed without the bridge.
(N2 non-factorization) The final answer is not a trivial Combine(ansA, ansB): the computed T restricts/changes the admissible branch structure inside component B before its output is even defined.

title: Truncated ring automorphisms -> Burnside orbit count on square-zero elements

identity.short_name: Truncated ring automorphisms -> Burnside orbit count on square-zero elements

subdomain(s): composition of truncated power series over finite fields
ideal-lattice group actions and orbit counting (Burnside)

problem_spec.summary: Composite (CHAIN) family: compute bridge quantity T from component A (Count order-2 automorphisms of F7[x]/(x^6)); then use that T as a required parameter in component B (Count Aut(R)-orbits of ideals with dim_F2(I/2I)=2 in Z4[x]/(x^3,2x)) to determine the final output (reduced mod 1000). Instance summary: Model automorphisms of F7[x]/(x^3) via truncated series x->ax+bx^2, identify the induced square-scaling action on square-zero elements, compute T=|(F7*)^2|, then apply Burnside to count orbits mod 1000.
givens: R=F_7[x]/(x^3). Automorphisms satisfy phi(x)=ax+bx^2 with a in F_7^*, b in F_7.
ask: Compute N = (# Aut(R)-orbits of S) mod 1000.

invariant(s).name: maximal_ideal_and_2_annihilation
invariant(s).statement: The unique maximal ideal is =(2,x) consisting of elements with even constant term, and 2=0. Hence for any proper ideal I⊆, we have 2I=0 so I/2I≅I as F2-vector spaces.

invariant(s).name: ideal_as_T_invariant_subspace
invariant(s).statement: For proper ideals I⊆, the ideal condition is equivalent to I being an F2-subspace of  invariant under multiplication-by-x, i.e. T(I)⊆I where T(y)=xy.

invariant(s).name: automorphisms_determined_by_image_of_x
invariant(s).statement: Any automorphism fixes constants and is determined by xx+x^2+2 with ,F2; thus |Aut(R)|=4. Additionally x^2 is fixed by all automorphisms because (x+x^2+2)^2=x^2.

invariant(s).name: bridge_interface_invariant
invariant(s).statement: The composite is coupled by the interface variable T: component A uniquely determines T, and component B is defined/solved only after substituting this specific T into its parameter slot.

essential_assumption(s): Automorphisms are ring automorphisms fixing F7 pointwise.
Automorphisms are F_7-algebra automorphisms (fix scalars in F_7).
Composition is computed in R, i.e., all series are truncated mod x^6.
Automorphisms preserve 1 and the defining relations x^3=0, 2x=0.
The quotient I/2I is viewed as an F2-vector space via RR/2R.
Proper ideals lie in the maximal ideal  and thus satisfy 2I=0 in this ring.
The bridge quantity T computed from component A is guaranteed to be an integer in the admissible range required by component B (e.g., as an index, bound, or group-action parameter).
uniqueness_certificate(s): The induced action on S is uniquely determined by the coefficient a mod 7; Burnside's lemma yields a unique orbit count.

Each automorphism corresponds to a unique coefficient tuple (a,b,c,d,e); the system f^{2}(x)x (mod x^6) has finitely many solutions in F_7, so |I| is uniquely determined once the coefficient system is solved.

The constrained set  is finite and is completely determined by the invariant-subspace description: dim_F2(I/2I)=2 ⇔ |I|=4 ⇔ I is a 2D T-invariant subspace of  under T(y)=xy. This yields a finite explicit list.

Aut(R) is finite and explicitly parameterized by (,)F2^2. The orbit count is uniquely determined by the action on the explicit list of ideals, equivalently by Burnsides lemma applied to fixed-point counts.
(N1 necessity) If T is not computed from component A, component B has multiple admissible outputs across different possible parameter choices; thus the composite is ill-posed without the bridge.
(N2 non-factorization) The final answer is not a trivial Combine(ansA, ansB): the computed T restricts/changes the admissible branch structure inside component B before its output is even defined.

identity.short_name: Bounded cubic root-filter count -> cubic-root power-sum recurrence at index T

subdomain(s): discriminant criteria for quadratic integer roots and repeated roots

problem_spec.summary: Composite (CHAIN) family: compute bridge quantity T from component A (Count bounded cubics with exactly one integer root among {0,1}); then use that T as a required parameter in component B (Partial sum of cubic-root power sums modulo 1000) to determine the final output (reduced mod 1000). Instance summary: Count bounded integer-coefficient cubics having exactly one root in {0,1} to get T, then evaluate the Newton/Vieta power-sum sequence S_n of a fixed cubic at n=T modulo 1000.
givens: -2 <= a,b,c <= 2 and p(x)=x^3+ax^2+bx+c.
Goodness: exactly one of p(0), p(1) equals 0.
alpha,beta,gamma are roots of y^3-3y+1=0; S_n=alpha^n+beta^n+gamma^n.
ask: Compute T = # {good (a,b,c)}.
Compute N = S_T mod 1000.

identity.short_name: Count bounded cubics with exactly one double root and both roots in (-2,2)

subdomain(s): rational root theorem and Vieta relations
topic_path: counting coefficient triples by multiplicity and root-location constraints

problem_spec.summary: Count monic cubic polynomials with coefficients in a finite integer box that have exactly two distinct real roots (a double root and a simple root), and require both distinct roots to lie in a prescribed open interval. Use the repeated-root criterion to force a rational (hence integer) double root, then apply Vieta to pin down the remaining root and count admissible root pairs.
givens: Requirement: P has exactly two distinct real roots, both lying in (-2,2) (so P has one double real root and one simple real root).
ask: Find the number of such polynomials P.

parameter(s).name: coefficient_box
parameter(s).purpose: finite search space for enumeration/counting.

parameter(s).name: target_interval
parameter(s).purpose: required location of the distinct real roots.

parameter(s).name: multiplicity_pattern
parameter(s).purpose: enforces exactly two distinct roots for a cubic.

invariant(s).name: multiplicity_characterization
invariant(s).statement: A real monic cubic has exactly two distinct real roots iff it factors as (x-r)^2(x-s) with r\neqs real, equivalently iff gcd(P,P′) has degree 1 (or discriminant \Delta=0 but P not a perfect cube).

invariant(s).name: double_root_rational_then_integer
invariant(s).statement: The double root r is a root of gcd(P,P′), hence rational; since P is monic with integer coefficients, the Rational Root Theorem forces r to be an integer dividing c.

invariant(s).name: interval_forces_small_root_set
invariant(s).statement: With r integer and r\in(-2,2), we must have r\in{-1,0,1}. Vieta then gives s=-a-2r, so s is also an integer and s\in(-2,2) forces s\in{-1,0,1}.

invariant(s).name: coefficient_reconstruction_from_roots
invariant(s).statement: If P(x)=(x-r)^2(x-s), then (a,b,c)=(-(2r+s), r^2+2rs, -r^2 s). Thus each admissible ordered pair (r,s) yields exactly one coefficient triple.

essential_assumption(s): The interval is open; roots equal to ±2 are excluded.

title: - Python tooling actually used: optional verification (not required for the hand solution, but used to audit the final integer).

identity.short_name: Coefficient lag autocorrelation via Fourier coefficient and cosine moments

problem_spec.summary: Compute a fixed-lag coefficient autocorrelation of a structured polynomial by rewriting the target as a Laurent coefficient of P(x)P(x^{-1}), converting it to a unit-circle Fourier integral via orthogonality, simplifying |P(e^{iθ})|^2 to a polynomial in cos(2θ), and evaluating the resulting integral using even cosine moments (central binomial coefficients).
givens: a_k is the coefficient of x^k in P(x).
C = Σ_{k} a_k a_{k+4}, summing over k such that k+4 \leq deg(P).
ask: Determine the exact integer value of C.

parameter(s).name: exponent
parameter(s).purpose: controls the power on each quadratic factor, hence the degree and sum length

parameter(s).name: linear_coefficient_u
parameter(s).purpose: sets the conjugate-like quadratic pair (x^2+u x+1),(x^2-u x+1) enabling cosine reduction

parameter(s).name: lag_t
parameter(s).purpose: specifies which coefficient correlation Σ a_k a_{k+t} is extracted as a Fourier coefficient

invariant(s).name: correlation_is_fourier_coefficient
invariant(s).statement: If P(x)=Σ a_k x^k, then Σ_k a_k a_{k+t} = [x^t] P(x)P(x^{-1}) = (1/2\pi)∫_0^{2\pi} |P(e^{iθ})|^2 e^{-itθ} dθ. For real coefficients, this equals (1/2\pi)∫ |P(e^{iθ})|^2 cos(tθ) dθ.

essential_assumption(s): P has real coefficients so P(e^{-iθ}) is the complex conjugate of P(e^{iθ}).
Orthogonality of exponentials on [0,2\pi] is used to identify coefficients via integrals.
Even-power cosine moment formula applies: (1/2\pi)∫_0^{2\pi} cos^{2j}(θ) dθ = C(2j,j)/4^j.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The polynomial P is fixed by the givens, so the coefficient sequence (a_k) is unique. identity.short_name: Engineered remainder \to quadratic probe resultant product

problem_spec.summary: Given a monic cubic P with integer coefficients and roots r_i, define an engineered expression E(x)=xP(x)+Q(x) where Q is a fixed quadratic probe (often x^2+1). Then E(r_i)=Q(r_i) for all roots. The desired product over roots equals Res(P,Q), which can be computed by evaluating P at the roots of Q (a conjugate pair), then reducing modulo 1000.
givens: P(x) is a monic cubic in ℤ[x] with complex roots r_1,r_2,r_3 counted with multiplicity.
Q(x) is a fixed quadratic probe in ℤ[x] (in this instance Q(x)=x^2+1).
E(x) is defined by E(x)=x \cdot P(x)+Q(x) (so deg(E)=4 and E\equivQ (mod P)).
Output is taken modulo m=1000.
ask: Compute T \equiv _{i=1}^3 E(r_i) (mod 1000).

parameter(s).name: cubic_coefficients
parameter(s).domain: monic cubic x^3 + a x^2 + b x + c with a,b,c \in ℤ
parameter(s).purpose: defines the root constraint ring ℤ[x]/(P) and the multiset of roots r_i

parameter(s).name: quadratic_probe
parameter(s).domain: integer-coefficient quadratic Q(x) with deg(Q)=2 (often chosen to have conjugate roots)
parameter(s).purpose: the reduced expression whose values are multiplied over the roots of P

parameter(s).purpose: forces the remainder of E modulo P to be exactly Q, making reduction immediate and structural

invariant(s).statement: Since E(x)=xP(x)+Q(x), we have E(x) \equiv Q(x) (mod P(x)), hence for every root r of P, E(r)=Q(r).

invariant(s).name: resultant_equals_product_over_roots
invariant(s).statement: For monic P, Res(P,Q)=_{P(r)=0} Q(r) (counted with multiplicity), so the target product equals a resultant.

invariant(s).name: integer_valued_symmetry
invariant(s).statement: Res(P,Q) is an integer polynomial in the coefficients of P and Q; therefore the product is an integer.

essential_assumption(s): Fundamental Theorem of Algebra: P has exactly three complex roots counted with multiplicity.
Work in characteristic 0 so resultants and division algorithm behave as usual.
The product over roots uses multiplicity (standard convention), matching the resultant identity.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Because E(x)=xP(x)+Q(x), the product  E(r_i) equals  Q(r_i)=Res(P,Q). The resultant is uniquely determined by the coefficients of P and Q, hence the integer product is unique. Reducing modulo m gives a unique residue in {0,...,m-1}.

identity.short_name: Count palindromic quartics with \sum_{i<j} r_i^2 r_j^2 = 9 in a bounded coefficient box

problem_spec.summary: Consider the 2-parameter palindromic family P_{a,b}(x)=x^4+ax^3+bx^2+ax+1. Using Newton identities, express S=\sum_{i<j} r_i^2 r_j^2 as a closed-form polynomial in (a,b), then count integer pairs (a,b) in a bounded box for which S equals a prescribed target.
givens: P_{a,b}(x) = x^4 + a x^3 + b x^2 + a x + 1 is a monic quartic (a,b \in ℤ).
r1,r2,r3,r4 are the complex roots of P_{a,b}(x)=0, counted with multiplicity.
Define S(a,b)=\sum_{1\leqi<j\leq4} r_i^2 r_j^2.
Constraint box: |a| \leq 5 and |b| \leq 5.
Target value: S(a,b)=9.
ask: Count the integer pairs (a,b) in the box such that S(a,b)=9.

invariant(s).name: palindromic_specialization_formula
invariant(s).statement: For P_{a,b}(x)=x^4+ax^3+bx^2+ax+1, the closed form becomes S(a,b)=b^2-2a^2+2.

invariant(s).name: bounded_integer_count_reduction
invariant(s).statement: With |a|,|b|\leqN, solving S(a,b)=target reduces to a finite search over a-values and checking whether a derived integer expression is a perfect square within the b-bound.

Within the palindromic subfamily this becomes S=b^2-2a^2+2, so S=9 is equivalent to b^2=2a^2+7. The bounded box yields finitely many (a,b) to test, producing a unique count.

identity.short_name: Count F13 degree-8 twisted-reciprocal polynomials with exactly two inversion 2-cycles as roots

subdomain(s): affine-linear constraint counting over finite fields

problem_spec.summary: Over F_13, impose a twisted reciprocal functional symmetry f(x)=x^4 f(1/x) on F_13^* for a monic degree-8 polynomial, fix one coefficient, and require exactly four roots in F_13^* excluding the inversion fixed points ±1. Use inversion-orbit structure to convert "root-orbit conditions into affine-linear constraints in a small parameter family and count solutions.
givens: Work over the finite field F_13.
f(x) \in F_13[x] is monic of degree 8.
Twisted reciprocal symmetry: for all x \in F_13^*, f(x) = x^4 f(1/x).
The coefficient of x^3 in f(x) equals 5.
f has exactly 4 distinct roots in F_13^* and f(1)\neq0, f(-1)\neq0.
ask: Count the number of such polynomials f(x).

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: determines F_q^* cyclic structure and inversion orbits; instance uses q=13.

parameter(s).name: degree
parameter(s).purpose: polynomial degree; instance uses degree=8.

parameter(s).name: twist_exponent_k
parameter(s).purpose: defines twisted reciprocity f(x)=x^k f(1/x) on F_q^*; instance uses k=4.

parameter(s).name: fixed_coefficient
parameter(s).purpose: pins a coefficient in the reduced template; instance fixes a3=5.

parameter(s).name: root_count_target
parameter(s).purpose: target number of distinct roots in F_q^*; instance uses 4.

parameter(s).name: fixed_point_exclusion
parameter(s).purpose: forces roots to come only from inversion 2-cycles, not fixed points.

invariant(s).name: coefficient_template_from_twisted_symmetry
invariant(s).statement: Using x^(q-1)=1 on F_q^* and comparing reduced coefficients modulo x^(q-1)-1, the condition f(x)=x^4 f(1/x) on F_13^* forces a5=a6=a7=0 and a4=a0, a3=a1, leaving the template f(x)=x^8 + a0 x^4 + a1 x^3 + a2 x^2 + a1 x + a0.

invariant(s).name: parameter_reduction_by_fixed_coefficient
invariant(s).statement: Fixing the x^3 coefficient a3=5 forces a1=5, so the family is parameterized by (a0,a2)\inF_13^2.

invariant(s).name: inversion_orbits_in_Fq_star
invariant(s).statement: Inversion partitions F_13^* into fixed points {1,-1} and five 2-cycles: {2,7},{3,9},{4,10},{5,8},{6,11}.

invariant(s).name: root_orbit_closure_under_twisted_symmetry
invariant(s).statement: If r\inF_13^* and f(r)=0, then 0=f(r)=r^4 f(1/r), so f(1/r)=0. Thus roots occur in inversion-orbits.

invariant(s).name: exact_root_count_to_orbit_selection
invariant(s).statement: With fixed points excluded and root_count_target=4, the root set in F_13^* must consist of exactly two inversion 2-cycles (and no other orbit).

invariant(s).name: orbit_root_conditions_are_affine_lines
invariant(s).statement: For each 2-cycle representative r, the condition f(r)=0 is an affine-linear equation in (a0,a2), i.e. a line in F_13^2. Thus "exactly two 2-cycles are roots corresponds to points lying on exactly two of the five orbit-lines and not on the fixed-point root lines.

essential_assumption(s): The symmetry f(x)=x^4 f(1/x) is required only on F_13^*, not as a polynomial identity in F_13[x].
Roots counted are distinct and lie in F_13^* (x=0 excluded).
Fixed-point exclusion f(±1)\neq0 is enforced as part of the specification.

uniqueness_certificate(s).statement: The symmetry and fixed coefficient reduce f to a finite 2-parameter family (a0,a2)\inF_13^2. Each inversion 2-cycle root condition is a single affine-linear constraint in these parameters. Therefore the valid polynomials correspond to a finite, explicitly enumerable set of parameter points, uniquely determining the count.

Because every element of F_13^* lies in exactly one inversion-orbit, verifying that a parameter point lies on exactly two orbit-lines (and on neither fixed-point line) is equivalent to verifying the exact root-orbit pattern; hence correctness is checkable and the answer is unique.

technique(s).name: convert_pointwise_symmetry_to_coefficient_constraints

technique(s).purpose: deduce relations among coefficients using reduction modulo x^(q-1)-1.
outputs: reduced coefficient template with few free parameters

technique(s).purpose: list fixed points and 2-cycles under inversion.
outputs: orbit decomposition used for root counting

technique(s).name: translate_root_conditions_to_affine_linear_constraints
inputs: reduced template and orbit representative r
technique(s).purpose: impose "orbit is a root by evaluating f(r)=0.
outputs: one affine-linear equation (a line) per orbit in parameter space

technique(s).name: intersection_and_parallelism_casework
technique(s).purpose: determine which orbit pairs can co-occur as roots and enumerate their intersections.
outputs: finite list of candidate parameter points

title: - "No real roots" is interpreted as P(x)\neq0 for all real x; for monic quartics this is equivalent to strict positivity.

identity.short_name: Count monic quartics symmetric about 1 with no real roots

problem_spec.summary: Count monic degree-4 integer-coefficient polynomials with bounded coefficients satisfying symmetry P(2-x)=P(x) (symmetry center 1), and having no real zeros. Use the shift x->y+1 to make an even quartic, reduce strict positivity to a quadratic inequality on z=y^2>=0, and count integer parameter pairs under the coefficient bounds.
givens: W = {P in V : P(2-x)=P(x) for all real x}.
Consider monic quartics P(x)=x^4+a_3x^3+a_2x^2+a_1x+a_0 in W with integers a_k and |a_k|<=7.
ask: Determine the number of such polynomials having no real roots.

parameter(s).name: degree
parameter(s).purpose: fixes quartic setting (even-after-shift reduction to y^4+by^2+d)

parameter(s).name: symmetry_center
parameter(s).purpose: determines the translation y=x-1 that makes the polynomial even

parameter(s).name: leading_coefficient
parameter(s).purpose: monic quartic so end behavior is +infinity and "no real roots implies strict positivity

parameter(s).name: coeff_bound
parameter(s).purpose: bounds integer coefficients to make the admissible set finite

invariant(s).name: symmetry_implies_even_after_shift
invariant(s).statement: Let y=x-1 and Q(y)=P(y+1). Then Q is even: Q(-y)=Q(y), so Q(y)=y^4+by^2+d.

invariant(s).name: coefficient_relations_from_center_one_symmetry
invariant(s).statement: For monic quartics with P(2-x)=P(x), necessarily a_3=-4 and a_1=8-2a_2. Equivalently P(x)=(x-1)^4+b(x-1)^2+d with integers b=a_2-6 and d=a_0-a_2+5.

invariant(s).name: positivity_reduction_to_quadratic
invariant(s).statement: With Q(y)=y^4+by^2+d, "no real roots (monic quartic) is equivalent to Q(y)>0 for all y, which is equivalent to z^2+bz+d>0 for all z>=0 via z=y^2.

essential_assumption(s): Coefficients are integers and the polynomial is monic of degree 4.

identity.short_name: SL4 adjoint invariant polynomials (degree 50)

subdomain(s): Chevalley restriction and Weyl group invariants
graded dimension / Hilbert series coefficient extraction

problem_spec.summary: Compute the dimension of the degree-d component of the invariant polynomial ring on the adjoint representation of SL_4(C). Use Chevalley restriction to identify the invariant ring with a Weyl-group invariant polynomial ring, then extract the degree-d dimension by counting monomials of prescribed degrees.
givens: G = SL_4(C).
V = sl_4(C) as the adjoint G-module.
W = (Sym^d(V^*))^G is the space of G-invariant homogeneous polynomial functions on V of degree d.
ask: Determine dim_C(W).

parameter(s).name: polynomial_degree_d
parameter(s).purpose: selects the graded piece of the invariant polynomial ring to be counted

parameter(s).name: lie_type_fixed
parameter(s).purpose: fixes the Weyl group (S_4) and fundamental invariant degrees (2,3,4) for the adjoint action

invariant(s).name: self_duality_of_adjoint
invariant(s).statement: V^* ≅ V as G-modules via the nondegenerate Killing form, so dimensions of invariant subspaces in Sym^d(V^*) and Sym^d(V) agree.

invariant(s).name: chevalley_restriction_for_adjoint
invariant(s).statement: The invariant ring (Sym(V^*))^G is isomorphic (as a graded algebra) to (Sym(h^*))^W where h is a Cartan subalgebra and W is the Weyl group of G.

invariant(s).name: generator_degrees_for_sl4
invariant(s).statement: For sl_4 (type A_3), (Sym(V^*))^G is a polynomial algebra on three homogeneous generators of degrees 2,3,4, corresponding to the elementary symmetric polynomials e2,e3,e4 in eigenvalues under the trace-zero constraint.

invariant(s).name: graded_dimension_as_monomial_count
invariant(s).statement: If (Sym(V^*))^G ≅ C[f2,f3,f4] with deg(f2)=2, deg(f3)=3, deg(f4)=4, then dim (Sym^d(V^*))^G = #{(a,b,c) in Z_{>=0}^3 : 2a+3b+4c=d}.

essential_assumption(s): Work over C (characteristic 0), so Chevalley restriction applies and invariant rings behave as expected.
The adjoint action of SL_4 on sl_4 is the standard conjugation action on traceless matrices.
Grading is by total polynomial degree, and dim_C of each graded piece is finite.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The graded algebra (Sym(V^*))^G is uniquely determined up to isomorphism by Chevalley restriction and the Lie type A_3, yielding a polynomial ring with generator degrees 2,3,4. Therefore the degree-d dimension is the unique coefficient in the Hilbert series 1/((1-t^2)(1-t^3)(1-t^4)), equivalently a unique finite count of solutions to 2a+3b+4c=d. Hence dim_C(W) is uniquely determined for each d.

technique(s).name: invariants_as_trivial_multiplicity
technique(s).purpose: interpret invariant polynomials as the trivial-representation multiplicity in a graded representation

technique(s).name: Chevalley_restriction_identify_invariant_ring
inputs: adjoint action of SL_4 on sl_4
technique(s).purpose: replace G-invariants on V by Weyl-group invariants on a Cartan subalgebra
outputs: invariant ring is a polynomial algebra with known generator degrees (2,3,4)

technique(s).name: Hilbert_series_coefficient_extraction
technique(s).purpose: express dim of the degree-d invariant space as a coefficient / monomial count

technique(s).name: linear_Diophantine_counting
technique(s).purpose: count the number of nonnegative integer solutions by parameterizing one variable and summing
outputs: explicit integer value of dim W

theorem(s).name: Killing_form_self_duality
theorem(s).role: identifies V^* with V for the adjoint representation

theorem(s).name: Chevalley_restriction_theorem
theorem(s).role: (Sym(g^*))^G ≅ (Sym(h^*))^W for the adjoint action

theorem(s).name: fundamental_theorem_of_symmetric_polynomials
theorem(s).role: describes (Sym(C^4)^*)^{S_4} as a polynomial ring on e1,e2,e3,e4, enabling the degree list 1,2,3,4

theorem(s).name: Hilbert_series_of_polynomial_ring
theorem(s).role: coefficient of 1/(1-t^{deg fi}) counts monomials in given total degree

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: invariants_as_fixed_vectors_in_graded_representations
reduction_to_Weyl_group_invariants_and_coefficient_extraction

critical_trick(s): Use Chevalley restriction (Weyl group invariants) to identify the invariant ring for the adjoint action as a polynomial ring.
Convert the dimension question into coefficient extraction in a Hilbert series, i.e. counting monomials of degrees 2,3,4 summing to d.
Count solutions to a linear Diophantine equation to obtain the integer dimension.
