identity.short_name: Pairwise difference fourth powers from quartic roots

problem_spec.summary: Given a monic quartic polynomial with roots r1..r4, compute the symmetric quantity T = sum_{i<j}(r_i - r_j)^4. Express T in terms of power sums p1..p4 by expanding pairwise differences, compute those power sums from coefficients using Newton identities, and report T modulo 1000.
givens: P(x) = x^4 - 5x^3 + x^2 - 8x + 3 is monic with roots r1,r2,r3,r4 in C.
ask: Compute T = sum_{i<j} (r_i - r_j)^4 modulo 1000.

parameter(s).name: quartic_coefficients
parameter(s).domain: Integers (or rationals) defining a monic quartic x^4 - e1 x^3 + e2 x^2 - e3 x + e4.
parameter(s).purpose: Controls the elementary symmetric sums (e1,e2,e3,e4) and hence determines the power sums p_k.

parameter(s).name: target_power
parameter(s).purpose: Specifies the exponent in (r_i - r_j)^4 for the pairwise-difference symmetric sum.

parameter(s).name: modulus
parameter(s).purpose: Controls output normalization by modular reduction.

invariant(s).name: vieta_map
invariant(s).statement: For P(x)=x^4 - e1 x^3 + e2 x^2 - e3 x + e4 with roots r_i, e1 = sum r_i, e2 = sum_{i<j} r_i r_j, e3 = sum_{i<j<k} r_i r_j r_k, e4 = product r_i.

invariant(s).name: newton_degree4
invariant(s).statement: The power sums p_k = sum_i r_i^k satisfy: p1=e1, p2=e1 p1 - 2e2, p3=e1 p2 - e2 p1 + 3e3, p4=e1 p3 - e2 p2 + e3 p1 - 4e4.

invariant(s).name: pairwise_difference_fourth_power_identity
invariant(s).statement: T = sum_{i<j}(r_i - r_j)^4 equals 4p4 - 4p1 p3 + 3p2^2.

essential_assumption(s): Polynomial is monic quartic (degree exactly 4) so Vieta/Newton formulas apply directly.
Roots may be complex; symmetric polynomials in roots are still well-defined and equal to coefficient expressions.
Modulo operation is applied to the final integer value of T (after simplification).
uniqueness_certificate(s): T is symmetric in the roots and is therefore a polynomial in the elementary symmetric sums (e1,e2,e3,e4), which are uniquely determined by the coefficients of P. Newton identities compute the required power sums uniquely from (e1,e2,e3,e4), so T (and T mod m) is uniquely determined for each instance.

technique(s).name: expand_pairwise_difference_power
technique(s).purpose: Expand (r_i - r_j)^4 and sum over pairs to rewrite T in terms of power sums p1..p4.

technique(s).name: compute_power_sums_via_newton
technique(s).purpose: Use Newton identities for degree 4 to compute p1,p2,p3,p4 exactly.

technique(s).name: evaluate_and_reduce_modulo
technique(s).purpose: Evaluate T as an integer and reduce modulo m.

theorem(s).name: Vieta's formulas
theorem(s).role: Relate polynomial coefficients to elementary symmetric sums of roots.

theorem(s).name: Newton's identities (degree 4)
theorem(s).role: Relate power sums p_k to elementary symmetric sums e_i for k up to 4.

theorem(s).name: Symmetry principle
theorem(s).role: Any symmetric polynomial in roots depends only on elementary symmetric polynomials.

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: relationships_between_roots_and_coefficients

critical_trick(s): Expand the pairwise-difference fourth power and reorganize the double sums into p2^2, p1p3, and p4.
Use Newton identities to compute p1..p4 directly from the quartic's coefficients.
Perform modular reduction at the end.

library(s).name: sympy
library(s).reason: Symbolic verification of the derived identity and exact Newton-sum computations; modular reduction.

task(s).name: symbolic_identity_check
task(s).reason: Verify T = 4p4 - 4p1p3 + 3p2^2 holds by symbolic expansion for a generic quartic root tuple.

task(s).name: coefficient_to_T_mod_m
task(s).reason: Compute p1..p4 via Newton identities and evaluate T mod 1000 exactly for the chosen coefficients.

title: x-weighted interlaced rational integral collapses via Newton sum

identity.short_name: x-weighted interlaced rational integral collapses via Newton sum

subdomain(s): change of variables in improper integrals
Viète and Newton identities for root sums
exact evaluation of compactly supported polynomial integrals

problem_spec.summary: For a monic degree-(r+1)/degree-r rational function with strictly interlacing real zeros and poles, evaluate an improper integral with an extra x-factor by splitting into monotone branches and changing variables y=F(x). A Newton-sum/Vieta identity gives Σ x_i(y) x_i'(y) as a simple polynomial in y, collapsing the integral to a fixed compact integral in y. Reduce the resulting rational number modulo 1000.
givens: zeros a = [-7, -2, 3, 10] and poles b = [-5, 1, 6] satisfy strict interlacing a1<b1<a2<b2<a3<b3<a4.
J = ∫_{-∞}^{∞} x \cdot h(F(x)) dx, interpreted as an improper integral over R.
Write J = p/q in lowest terms with p,q>0.
ask: Compute out = (p+q) mod 1000.

parameter(s).name: r
parameter(s).purpose: controls degrees deg(num)=r+1, deg(den)=r (here r=3)

parameter(s).name: zeros_a
parameter(s).domain: strictly increasing real list length r+1
parameter(s).purpose: numerator real roots

parameter(s).name: poles_b
parameter(s).domain: strictly increasing real list length r
parameter(s).purpose: denominator real roots

parameter(s).name: interlacing_pattern
parameter(s).purpose: guarantees monotone branch bijections

parameter(s).name: exponent_e
parameter(s).purpose: exponent in (1-u^2)_+^e (here 5)

parameter(s).name: weight_poly
parameter(s).purpose: extra even weight (here 1+2u^2)

parameter(s).name: x_weight
parameter(s).purpose: integrand includes an extra factor x

parameter(s).name: modulus
parameter(s).purpose: final reduction (here 1000)

invariant(s).name: residue_sign_invariant
invariant(s).statement: With strict interlacing and monic factors, residues α_j at poles b_j satisfy α_j<0. Consequently F'(x)=1+Σ(-α_j)/(x-b_j)^2>0 off the poles.

invariant(s).name: branch_surjectivity
invariant(s).statement: On each pole-separated interval, F is a C^1 increasing bijection onto R, hence has a unique C^1 inverse branch.

invariant(s).name: weighted_inverse_derivative_sum
invariant(s).statement: Let x_i(y) be the inverse branches solving F(x)=y (one per interval). For r=3 (quartic P_y), Σ_i x_i(y) x_i'(y) = y + (Σ a_i - Σ b_j), obtained from Newton sums (p2) and Viète coefficients.

essential_assumption(s): Strict interlacing a1<b1<a2<b2<a3<b3<a4.
Numerator and denominator are monic, so deg(F)=1 and branch limits are ±∞.
Poles are simple (distinct b_j), ensuring inverse branches are smooth.
h(u)=0 for |u|>1, ensuring the integral is finite (compact support after substitution).
uniqueness_certificate(s): Interlacing forces unique monotone inverse branches x_i(y) on each interval. The Newton/Vieta identity determines Σ x_i x_i' uniquely from the coefficients of P_y(x)=N(x)-yD(x). Therefore J reduces uniquely to a fixed integral over y\in[-1,1], giving a unique reduced fraction p/q and hence a unique residue (p+q) mod 1000.

technique(s).name: partial_fractions_and_residue_signs
technique(s).purpose: prove strict monotonicity and branch bijectivity

technique(s).name: branchwise_inverse_substitution
technique(s).purpose: convert ∫ x \cdot h(F(x)) dx into ∫ h(y) Σ x_i(y) x_i'(y) dy

technique(s).name: vieta_newton_weighted_sum
technique(s).purpose: compute Σ x_i x_i' via p2(y)=Σ x_i^2 and Newton sums

technique(s).name: even_polynomial_integration
technique(s).purpose: integrate exactly on [-1,1] using parity and term-by-term integration

technique(s).name: modular_postprocessing
technique(s).purpose: compute (p+q) mod 1000

theorem(s).name: partial_fraction_decomposition
theorem(s).role: represent F as polynomial part plus simple poles

theorem(s).name: change_of_variables_for_diffeomorphisms
theorem(s).role: justify y=F(x) on each monotone branch

theorem(s).name: Vieta_formulas
theorem(s).role: relate coefficients of P_y to e1,e2 of roots

theorem(s).name: Newton_identities
theorem(s).role: p2 = e1^2 - 2e2 for monic polynomials

theorem(s).name: odd_integrand_cancels_on_symmetric_interval
theorem(s).role: ∫_{-1}^1 (odd function) = 0

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: change of variables in integrals
symmetric polynomials (Viète/Newton)

critical_trick(s): Split R into pole-separated monotone branches and substitute y=F(x).
Use Newton sums to compute Σ x_i(y) x_i'(y) as a simple polynomial in y, depending only on Σa_i and Σb_j.
Use evenness to eliminate the odd contribution and integrate a polynomial exactly, then reduce mod 1000.

title: Offset-epicycle simplicity threshold sum (mod 1000)

identity.short_name: Offset-epicycle simplicity threshold sum (mod 1000)

problem_spec.summary: For a two-frequency complex exponential curve with secondary frequency n+2 and discrete amplitude a=k/120, determine which (n,k) yield a simple curve (no multiple points) via a sharp sine-ratio threshold, then compute a weighted sum over all simple instances and reduce modulo 1000.
givens: For integers 0 \leq n \leq 50 and integers -200 \leq k \leq 200, set a = k/120 and m = n+2.
Define z_{n,a}(t) = e^{it} + a e^{imt} for 0 \leq t < 2\pi.
C_{n,a} is "simple" if there do not exist 0 \leq t < u < 2\pi with z_{n,a}(t)=z_{n,a}(u).
Define I(n,k)=1 if C_{n,k/120} is simple; otherwise I(n,k)=0.
ask: Compute out = S mod 1000 (representative in {0,1,...,999}).

parameter(s).name: N_max
parameter(s).purpose: upper limit of n (here 50)

parameter(s).name: K_bound
parameter(s).purpose: symmetric bound for k (here 200)

parameter(s).name: scale
parameter(s).purpose: discretizes a as a=k/scale (here 120)

parameter(s).name: frequency_shift
parameter(s).purpose: secondary frequency is m=n+frequency_shift (here n+2)

parameter(s).name: modulus
parameter(s).purpose: final reduction

invariant(s).name: simplicity_threshold_for_two_frequency_sum
invariant(s).statement: For integer m>=2, the curve t↦e^{it}+a e^{imt} has a multiple point iff |a|>1/m; hence it is simple iff |a|<=1/m.

invariant(s).name: discrete_k_threshold
invariant(s).statement: With a=k/scale and k integer, simplicity is equivalent to |k| <= floor(scale/m).

invariant(s).name: symmetric_k_cancellation
invariant(s).statement: For any integer M>=0, Σ_{k=-M}^{M} k = 0, so Σ_{k=-M}^{M} (n+k) = (2M+1)n.

essential_assumption(s): Multiple points are defined by equality z(t)=z(u) for distinct parameters t<u in [0,2\pi).
Since m=n+2>=2 for all n in range, no degenerate m=0,1 cases occur.
The k-range is wide enough that the active constraint comes from the simplicity threshold (here floor(120/2)=60<200).
uniqueness_certificate(s): The threshold |a|<=1/m uniquely determines I(n,k) for each (n,k) in the finite index set. Therefore S is uniquely determined, and its residue mod 1000 is unique.

technique(s).name: exponential_difference_to_sine
technique(s).purpose: convert the complex equality into a sine-ratio equation in x=(u-t)/2

technique(s).name: sine_ratio_upper_bound
technique(s).purpose: bound |sin(mx)/sin x| by m to obtain the necessary condition |a|>1/m

title: Kernel from Tr(|p| ζ |p|^{-1} ζ) at d=6 and point evaluation

identity.short_name: Kernel from Tr(|p| ζ |p|^{-1} ζ) at d=6 and point evaluation

problem_spec.summary: On H=L^2(R^d), define |p| and a radial multiplier ζ(x)=2(4-d)/|x|^2. For a simple trace expression Tr(|p| ζ |p|^{-1} ζ), insert Fourier and position resolutions to obtain a mixed kernel f(k,q,x,y). Evaluate f on the diagonal (q=k, x=y=x0) at d=6, then apply "inverse then divide by 3" to get an integer output.
givens: H = L^2(R^d, d^dx).
(x_hat_a ψ)(x) = x_a ψ(x), and p_hat_a = -i ∂_a.
abs_p = sqrt(p_hat_a p_hat_a); in Fourier space abs_p acts by multiplication by |k|.
ζ(x)=2(4-d)/|x|^2 and (zeta_hat ψ)(x)=ζ(x)ψ(x).
Fourier convention: ⟨x|k⟩=e^{ik \cdot x}, ∫ d^dk/(2\pi)^d |k⟩⟨k| = I.
Define E = (1/4) Tr[ abs_p  \cdot  zeta_hat  \cdot  abs_p^{-1}  \cdot  zeta_hat ] in the regularized trace sense.
Define f by inserting Fourier/position resolutions so that E = ∬ dx dy ∬ (dk dq)/(2\pi)^{2d} f(k,q,x,y).
Evaluate at d=6, x0=(2,2,2,2,2,2), and substitute q=k, x=y=x0 (with k\neq0).
ask: Compute out = (1/3) \cdot (f(k,k,x0,x0))^{-1} as an exact integer.

parameter(s).name: d
parameter(s).purpose: ambient Euclidean dimension

parameter(s).name: zeta_profile
parameter(s).purpose: fixes the multiplier evaluated at x0

parameter(s).name: operator_expression_template
parameter(s).domain: Tr( abs_p  \cdot  ζ̂  \cdot  abs_p^{-1}  \cdot  ζ̂ ) up to scalar prefactor
parameter(s).purpose: determines the Fourier-symbol kernel f

parameter(s).name: prefactor
parameter(s).purpose: scalar multiplying the trace expression (here 1/4)

parameter(s).name: evaluation_point
parameter(s).purpose: point where the kernel is evaluated (here (2,...,2))

parameter(s).name: postprocess
parameter(s).purpose: apply out=(1/3) \cdot (f_diag)^{-1}

invariant(s).name: abs_p_diagonal_in_fourier
invariant(s).statement: In the Fourier basis, abs_p multiplies by |k| and abs_p^{-1} multiplies by 1/|k| (for k\neq0).

title: Sech-soliton Gamma-limit to floor(exp) then binomial-square mod 1000

identity.short_name: Sech-soliton Gamma-limit to floor(exp) then binomial-square mod 1000

problem_spec.summary: Use the unique positive even L^2 ground state of u''+u^{2n+1}=u to define A_n=∫u_n^2, extract the constant L = lim (n(A_n-1)-ln(n+1)) via Gamma/digamma asymptotics, apply Φ=floor(e^L), then compute a binomial-square expression modulo 1000.
givens: For each integer n>=1, u_n is the unique u\inC^2(R) with u>0, u even, u,u'\inL^2(R), satisfying u''+u^{2n+1}=u.
Define A_n = ∫_{R} u_n(t)^2 dt.
Define L = lim_{n->∞} ( n(A_n-1) - ln(n+1) ).
Define Phi = floor(e^L).
Define R = (Σ_{j=0}^6 binom(6,j) Phi^j)^2 + 41 Phi, and reduce R modulo 1000.
ask: Compute R mod 1000.

parameter(s).name: binomial_degree
parameter(s).purpose: sets the binomial sum Σ_{j=0}^6 C(6,j)Phi^j

parameter(s).name: floor_map
parameter(s).purpose: converts the analytic limit L into an integer

parameter(s).name: modulus
parameter(s).purpose: final modular reduction

parameter(s).name: ode_family
parameter(s).purpose: defines the soliton/ground-state family producing A_n

invariant(s).name: energy_first_integral
invariant(s).statement: Multiplying the ODE by 2u' gives (u')^2 + (1/(n+1))u^{2n+2} - u^2 = C; L^2 decay forces C=0.

invariant(s).name: explicit_sech_profile
invariant(s).statement: The unique positive even L^2 solution is u_n(t)=(n+1)^{1/(2n)} sech(nt)^{1/n}.

invariant(s).name: digamma_constant_limit
invariant(s).statement: Using ψ(1/2)=-γ-2ln2, one gets ln A_n = (ln(n+1)+2ln2)/n + O(1/n^2), hence L = lim (n(A_n-1)-ln(n+1)) = 2 ln 2.

invariant(s).name: floor_exponential_is_integer
invariant(s).statement: Phi=floor(e^L)=floor(4)=4.

essential_assumption(s): The L^2 conditions imply u(t)->0 as |t|->∞, enabling determination of the integration constant.
The limit L exists as a real number (verified via asymptotic expansion).
uniqueness_certificate(s): The IVP determined by evenness and the forced amplitude u(0)=(n+1)^{1/(2n)} has a unique global solution, making A_n unique; the asymptotic expansion fixes L uniquely, and floor(exp(L)) then fixes Phi and R uniquely.

technique(s).name: derive_first_integral_and_initial_amplitude
technique(s).purpose: determine C=0 and u(0)=(n+1)^{1/(2n)}

title: Delta reduction gives Z; compute \sqrt(Z-1) from |∇ψ| at a point

identity.short_name: Delta reduction gives Z; compute \sqrt(Z-1) from |∇ψ| at a point

subdomain(s): partial differential equations (Green functions for Laplacian)
topic_path: delta-function reduction on a defect surface

problem_spec.summary: A delta-supported defect-surface term collapses an integral and yields Z equal to the induced-metric determinant on a graph surface; compute \sqrt(Z-1) at a specified point from a given ψ(x₀,x₁).
givens: A 3D Euclidean Laplacian Green function G0(x,y) and a defect surface Σ given by x₂ = ψ(x₀,x₁).
The delta-supported term F collapses the y-integral so that Z(x_i) = (1/δ(0)) ∬ F(x_i,y_i) dy₀ dy₁ equals det(g_ab(x_i)) after δ(0) cancellation.
ψ(x₀,x₁) = 9 sin(x₀ - 1) + 12 sin(x₁ + 2).
Evaluation point: (x₀,x₁) = (1, -2).
ask: Compute S = \sqrt(Z - 1) at the evaluation point.

parameter(s).name: psi
parameter(s).purpose: defines the defect surface and its induced metric via gradients

parameter(s).name: evaluation_point
parameter(s).purpose: point where Z is evaluated (here (1,-2))

parameter(s).name: amplitude_pair
parameter(s).purpose: supplies explicit gradient values at the evaluation point

parameter(s).name: output_transform
parameter(s).purpose: requests \sqrt(Z-1), i.e. the gradient magnitude

invariant(s).name: delta_pullback_collapse
invariant(s).statement: The F-term reduces to det(g_ab(x)) \cdot δ^(2)(x_i-y_i) \cdot δ(ψ(x)-ψ(y)); integrating over y_i yields det(g_ab(x)) \cdot δ(0), which cancels in Z by definition.

invariant(s).name: induced_metric_determinant
invariant(s).statement: For a graph surface in Euclidean space, g_ab = δ_ab + (∂_aψ)(∂_bψ), hence det(g_ab)=1+|∇ψ|² in (x₀,x₁).

invariant(s).name: nonnegativity_for_sqrt
invariant(s).statement: Z=det(g_ab)\geq1, so Z-1\geq0 and \sqrt(Z-1) is well-defined as the nonnegative root.

essential_assumption(s): The δ(0) renormalization cancels exactly as specified in the definition of Z.
ψ is differentiable so ∇ψ exists at the evaluation point.
The principal (nonnegative) square root is used for \sqrt(Z-1).
uniqueness_certificate(s): After the delta-function reduction, Z is uniquely determined by det(g_ab) at the evaluation point, which is uniquely determined by ∇ψ at that point; hence S=\sqrt(Z-1) is unique.

technique(s).purpose: collapse the y-integral and cancel the δ(0) factor

technique(s).purpose: compute det(g_ab)=1+|∇psi|^2 for a graph surface

technique(s).purpose: obtain |∇ψ|² numerically at the point

title: Distinct real-root count for 4-fold iterate hitting a critical value

identity.short_name: Distinct real-root count for 4-fold iterate hitting a critical value

problem_spec.summary: For a real cubic with two critical points, count distinct real solutions to an iterate equation P^{(k)}(x)=c when c is a critical value. Use monotonicity to determine the number of distinct real preimages of a value (1/2/3 depending on whether it is outside/at/inside the critical-value window), build a preimage-branching recursion that includes boundary nodes, and compute the level-k count.
givens: P(x)=x^3-3x+1 over the reals.
Count distinct real solutions to P^{(k)}(x)=c (multiplicity ignored).
ask: Determine the number of distinct real roots of P^{(4)}(x)=3.

parameter(s).name: k
parameter(s).purpose: iterate depth

parameter(s).name: target_value
parameter(s).purpose: value c whose real preimages under P^{(k)} are counted (here c is a critical value)

parameter(s).name: cubic
parameter(s).purpose: fixed cubic with two critical points at ±1

parameter(s).name: distinctness_convention
parameter(s).purpose: clarifies that repeated roots are counted once

invariant(s).name: critical_points_and_values
invariant(s).statement: P'(x)=3(x-1)(x+1), so critical points are x=-1,1 with critical values P(-1)=3 and P(1)=-1.

invariant(s).name: distinct_preimage_count_by_target_position
invariant(s).statement: For real α, the equation P(x)=α has: (i) 3 distinct real solutions iff -1<α<3, (ii) 1 real solution iff α<-1 or α>3, (iii) 2 distinct real solutions iff α\in{-1,3}.

invariant(s).name: boundary_node_transitions
invariant(s).statement: The boundary targets satisfy: P(x)=3 has distinct solutions x=-1 (double) and x=2; P(x)=-1 has distinct solutions x=1 (double) and x=-2.

invariant(s).name: disjoint_preimage_union
invariant(s).statement: For α1\neqα2, the sets {x:P(x)=α1} and {x:P(x)=α2} are disjoint; hence distinct-root counts add across nodes.

essential_assumption(s): Work over the reals, using monotonicity on (-∞,-1], [-1,1], [1,∞).
Distinct solutions are counted (ignore multiplicity).
Preimage sets for distinct parent values are disjoint, so counting is additive level-by-level.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The critical-value window and boundary behavior uniquely determine the distinct-preimage branching rules. Because preimage sets for distinct parent values are disjoint, the level counts satisfy a deterministic recurrence from a uniquely determined initial level. Therefore the final distinct real-root count is unique.

technique(s).name: critical_value_split_for_cubic_with_boundary
technique(s).purpose: Use critical points/values to determine when P(x)=α has 1,2,3 distinct real solutions, including the boundary cases α\in{-1,3}.

technique(s).name: interval_bracketing_for_preimages
technique(s).purpose: Use monotonicity and IVT to localize the distinct preimages into disjoint intervals, enabling type classification (A vs B) and confirming boundary-node solutions.

technique(s).name: type_based_preimage_tree_with_boundary_nodes
technique(s).purpose: Build a branching process that counts distinct real preimages level-by-level, incorporating special boundary nodes at α=3 and α=-1.

technique(s).name: solve_recurrence_and_sum
technique(s).purpose: compute the level-k total distinct root count

technique(s).name: distinctness_justification_via_disjoint_preimages
technique(s).purpose: prove the recurrence counts distinct roots (no overlaps across different parent values)

theorem(s).role: guarantees existence of roots in sign-change intervals used for localization

theorem(s).name: monotonicity_from_derivative_sign
theorem(s).role: ensures at most one solution per monotone interval, enabling 1/3/2 count rules

theorem(s).name: repeated_root_occurs_at_critical_values
theorem(s).role: boundary targets α\in{-1,3} correspond to double roots at critical points

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: monotonic branches intersect a horizontal line at most once; iterate counts via a branching recurrence

critical_trick(s): Treat the target as a critical value, so the first preimage step has 2 (not 3) distinct branches.
After boundary nodes are resolved, the remaining levels obey the standard B\toA+2B and A\toA branching.
Use disjointness of preimage sets to sum counts without overcounting (distinct-root focus).

title: Laplace ODE \to denominator roots \to set-sum of Re+2Im mod 1000

identity.short_name: Laplace ODE \to denominator roots \to set-sum of Re+2Im mod 1000

problem_spec.summary: Solve a constant-coefficient IVP via Laplace transform to obtain f(s) as a rational function. Extract the denominator polynomial in lowest terms, list its complex roots with multiplicity, map each root to Re(r)+2Im(r), deduplicate to a set, sum, scale, take absolute value, and reduce mod 1000.
givens: y satisfies y''(x)+2y'(x)+y(x)=e^{-3x}\cos(2x) with y(0)=0 and y'(0)=0.
P(s) is the denominator of f(s) in lowest terms.
r_1,...,r_n are the complex roots of P(s) counted with multiplicity.
S={Re(r_i)+2Im(r_i) : i=1,...,n} (set, duplicates removed).
M=|50 * sum_{x in S} x|.
Compute M (mod 1000).
ask: Determine the residue class of M modulo 1000 (representative in {0,...,999}).

parameter(s).name: forcing
parameter(s).purpose: ensures Laplace transform yields shifted rational terms (here e^{-3x}cos(2x))

parameter(s).name: characteristic_coeffs
parameter(s).purpose: determines the homogeneous factor of the denominator (here y''+2y'+y)

parameter(s).name: scaling_factor
parameter(s).purpose: multiplies the set-sum before absolute value and mod reduction (here 50)

parameter(s).name: set_map
parameter(s).purpose: defines the invariant extracted from each root

parameter(s).name: modulus
parameter(s).purpose: final residue computation (here 1000)

invariant(s).name: denominator_from_transfer_and_forcing
invariant(s).statement: For constant-coefficient IVPs solved by Laplace transform, f(s) is rational with denominator given by the characteristic polynomial factors times forcing-shift factors (after simplification).

invariant(s).name: conjugate_pair_re_im_values
invariant(s).statement: Non-real roots occur in conjugate pairs; applying Re(r)+2Im(r) typically yields distinct real values unless a collision occurs, which is removed by set-deduplication.

essential_assumption(s): The denominator is taken after full simplification (lowest terms).
Roots are counted with multiplicity before applying the set map.
Re(z), Im(z) are the usual real and imaginary parts over ℂ.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The IVP uniquely determines y, hence uniquely determines f(s). Lowest-terms reduction fixes P(s) uniquely up to a unit scalar, which does not change its roots. The multiset of roots with multiplicity is unique; applying the map Re+2Im, set-deduplication, summation, scaling, absolute value, and mod reduction yields a unique integer residue class mod 1000.

title: 240^\circ overlap volume from two quadratic caps

identity.short_name: 240^\circ overlap volume from two quadratic caps

subdomain(s): intersection volume of solids of revolution
piecewise integration from min/max of radii

problem_spec.summary: Two planar regions bounded by y=0 and quadratic graphs (both above the x-axis) are each rotated through a fixed angle theta about the x-axis. The intersection of the two resulting partial solids of revolution has volume V, computed via cross-sectional disk sectors whose radius is the pointwise minimum of the two quadratics on the overlap x-interval. A final engineered expression of the form (K*pi)/(m*V) - C is evaluated.
givens: Region I: 0 <= y <= f(x) on an interval [x1,x2], with f a quadratic nonnegative on [x1,x2].
Region II: 0 <= y <= g(x) on an interval [x3,x4], with g a quadratic nonnegative on [x3,x4].
S_I is obtained by rotating Region I about the x-axis through angle theta.
S_II is obtained by rotating Region II about the x-axis through angle theta (same theta and same angular sector).
W = S_I ∩ S_II is the intersection solid and has volume V.
A final expression E = (K*pi)/(m*V) - C is requested (constants K,m,C given in the statement).
ask: Compute the exact value of E.

parameter(s).name: f
parameter(s).purpose: upper boundary above the x-axis for Region I

parameter(s).name: g
parameter(s).purpose: upper boundary above the x-axis for Region II

parameter(s).name: intervals
parameter(s).purpose: controls the overlap interval where both solids exist and where min(f,g) is evaluated

parameter(s).name: theta
parameter(s).purpose: fraction of full revolution for each solid

parameter(s).name: engineered_constants
parameter(s).purpose: chooses K,m,C so E evaluates to a small exact integer after substituting V

invariant(s).name: partial_rotation_scaling
invariant(s).statement: Rotating a planar region about the x-axis through angle theta produces disk-sector (or washer-sector) cross-sections whose area is scaled by factor theta/(2*pi) relative to a full 2*pi revolution.

invariant(s).name: overlap_cross_section_min_radius
invariant(s).statement: On the x-overlap interval [max(x1,x3), min(x2,x4)], the cross-section of the intersection solid W at position x is a disk-sector of radius r(x)=min(f(x), g(x)); outside the overlap interval the cross-section area is 0.

essential_assumption(s): f(x) and g(x) are nonnegative on their stated intervals so radii are valid without absolute values.
The two partial rotations share the same angular sector, so their intersection cross-section uses the same theta/(2*pi) scaling.
The interval overlap is nonempty; otherwise V=0 is still uniquely determined.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Given explicit quadratics f and g and explicit intervals, the overlap interval is fixed and the breakpoint set where f(x)=g(x) is finite and computable. This yields a unique piecewise-polynomial radius r(x)=min(f(x),g(x)), hence a unique volume V from a definite integral of a fully determined area function. Substituting V into E gives a unique real number; exact simplification yields a unique integer output.

technique(s).purpose: confirm the radii are nonnegative and determine the effective x-interval where both solids exist

technique(s).name: locate_switch_points
technique(s).purpose: solve f(x)=g(x) to determine subintervals on which min(f,g) is represented by a single quadratic

technique(s).name: build_piecewise_min_area_and_integrate
technique(s).purpose: build the scaled cross-sectional area A(x) using r(x)=min(f(x),g(x)) and integrate to obtain V

technique(s).name: evaluate_engineered_expression
technique(s).purpose: compute E = (K*pi)/(m*V) - C exactly

theorem(s).role: V = ∫ A(x) dx for solids with known cross-sectional area A(x)

theorem(s).name: partial_rotation_factor
theorem(s).role: A_theta(x) = (theta/(2*pi)) * A_full(x)

theorem(s).name: polynomial_integration
theorem(s).role: exact antiderivatives for polynomials (hence for squared quadratics)

critical_trick(s): Model the overlap (intersection) of two partial solids as a disk-sector with radius min(f(x),g(x)) at each x.
Find the unique switch point solving f(x)=g(x) to split the integral into polynomial pieces.
Use engineered constants so (K*pi)/(m*V) - C simplifies to an integer.

title: Rectangular grid lasers; parity forces a walk; count configs mod 1000

identity.short_name: Rectangular grid lasers; parity forces a walk; count configs mod 1000

problem_spec.summary: On a W×H rectangular grid of unit cells, each interior lattice junction emits exactly one directed unit laser segment to a neighboring junction. A move between adjacent cells is allowed iff their common side contains no laser. A Beautiful Walk is a path of adjacent cells from the southwest cell to the northeast cell using only allowed moves. For a specific rectangle (W,H), count configurations admitting at least one Beautiful Walk and reduce mod 1000.
givens: The grid is [0,W]×[0,H] with (W,H)=(7,5), tiled into W \cdot H unit square cabins.
Interior junctions are (a,b) with 1\leqa\leqW-1 and 1\leqb\leqH-1; each chooses exactly one directed unit segment to one of its four neighbors.
Boundary junctions emit no lasers. Laser segments may overlap.
A move between adjacent cabins is allowed iff their shared side contains no laser segment (either direction).
A Beautiful Walk starts at the southwest cabin and ends at the northeast cabin.
K is the number of configurations admitting at least one Beautiful Walk.
ask: Compute K mod 1000.

parameter(s).name: width_W
parameter(s).purpose: number of unit cabins horizontally (here 7)

parameter(s).name: height_H
parameter(s).purpose: number of unit cabins vertically (here 5)

parameter(s).domain: each interior junction chooses exactly 1 of 4 directions
parameter(s).purpose: defines configuration space size 4^{(W-1)(H-1)}

parameter(s).name: blocking_rule
parameter(s).domain: a shared side is blocked iff any laser segment lies along it
parameter(s).purpose: induces allowed adjacency among cabins

parameter(s).name: endpoints
parameter(s).purpose: start and finish cabins

parameter(s).name: modulus
parameter(s).purpose: final reduction (here 1000)

invariant(s).statement: There are (W-1)(H-1) interior junctions, each choosing one of 4 directions independently, so total configurations are 4^{(W-1)(H-1)}.

invariant(s).name: boundary_chain_parity_obstruction
invariant(s).statement: If the northeast cabin is not reachable from the southwest cabin using allowed moves, the interface between reachable and non-reachable cabins (shared sides) forms a set of grid segments whose interior junction degrees are even; thus it contains an open boundary-to-boundary chain.

invariant(s).name: directed_chain_impossibility
invariant(s).statement: A boundary-to-boundary chain of grid segments each containing a laser is impossible: boundary junctions have outdegree 0, and outdegree 1 at interior junctions forces all lasers along the chain to orient backward, eventually requiring a boundary junction to emit.

invariant(s).name: always_reachable
invariant(s).statement: Since non-reachability would imply an impossible boundary-to-boundary laser chain, the northeast cabin is always reachable; therefore every configuration admits a Beautiful Walk.

essential_assumption(s): Only interior junctions emit lasers; boundary junctions emit none.
Overlaps are allowed; any presence blocks a side.
Reachability is via 4-neighbor cabin adjacency only.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Prove every configuration admits a Beautiful Walk, so K equals the full configuration count 4^{(W-1)(H-1)}. Then K mod 1000 is uniquely determined by modular arithmetic (e.g., CRT).

technique(s).purpose: propagate forced laser orientations along the chain to contradict boundary outdegree 0

technique(s).name: conclude_all_configs_good
technique(s).purpose: conclude the target is always reachable, so K equals total configurations

theorem(s).name: CRT_mod_1000
theorem(s).role: combine congruences mod 8 and mod 125 into a unique mod 1000 residue

Therefore all configurations work, so K is the full count 4^{(W-1)(H-1)}, then reduce modulo 1000 via CRT.

title: Two-stage cube-face blocking with one swap edge; corner probability

problem_spec.summary: A cube-face 3×3 blocking puzzle with a single special edge where row/column continuation swaps (with a reversal). Four initial marks induce blocked lines. title: Maximize |(A+B) ∩ (A∪B)| when A,B partition {1..2n} inside {1..3n}

identity.short_name: Maximize |(A+B) ∩ (A∪B)| when A,B partition {1..2n} inside {1..3n}

problem_spec.summary: Choose disjoint A,B of size n from {1..3n} with the extra constraint that A∪B is exactly {1..2n}. Let E=A+B be the cross-sumset. Maximize how many elements of the partition set {1..2n} appear as cross-sums. Prove a sharp upper bound by a forcing-chain (induction) that would otherwise require too many elements in one set, and match it by an explicit construction covering an entire interval of sums.
givens: n = 100 (general n\geq3 used in derivation).
A,B ⊂ U are disjoint, |A|=|B|=n, and A ∪ B = {1,2,...,2n}.
E = {a+b : a\inA, b\inB}.
ask: Determine max |E ∩ (A ∪ B)| and evaluate it for n=100.

parameter(s).name: n
parameter(s).purpose: size of each set and scale of the labeled universe.

parameter(s).name: universe_multiplier
parameter(s).purpose: labels live in {1..3n} but only {1..2n} are occupied by A∪B.

parameter(s).name: occupied_prefix_constraint
parameter(s).purpose: forces A,B to be a partition of the first 2n labels (a stronger constraint than "any disjoint A,B").

parameter(s).name: target_functional
parameter(s).purpose: objective is the number of occupied labels representable as a cross-sum.

invariant(s).name: unavoidable_small_sum_exclusions
invariant(s).statement: 1 ∉ A+B always. Also 2 ∉ A+B because it would require 1+1 with 1 in both sets, impossible under disjointness.

invariant(s).name: forcing_chain_when_trying_to_cover_all_small_sums
invariant(s).statement: If 1\inA and 2\inB, then requiring 4\inA+B forces 3\inB; requiring 5\inA+B forces 4\inB; and in general, if {2,...,k}⊂B and k+2\inA+B then k+1\inB. Attempting to cover all sums 3..2n would force B to contain {2..2n-1}, contradicting |B|=n for n\geq3.

essential_assumption(s): A and B are disjoint subsets of {1,...,3n} of equal size n.
A ∪ B is fixed to be exactly the prefix {1,...,2n}.
E is the cross-sumset (only sums a+b with a\inA, b\inB).

uniqueness_certificate(s).statement: The forcing-chain argument shows max |E∩(A∪B)| \leq 2n-3 for all admissible partitions (n\geq3). A concrete construction achieves 2n-3, so the maximum is uniquely determined as 2n-3; for n=100 this equals 197.

technique(s).name: derive_basic_obstructions
technique(s).purpose: show certain small labels (1 and 2) can never be in the cross-sumset.

technique(s).name: forcing_chain_induction
technique(s).purpose: propagate forced membership of successive integers into one color class when attempting full coverage.

title: Cycle lengths enclosing exactly 4 interior vertices in side-5 triangular grid (sum)

identity.short_name: Cycle lengths enclosing exactly 4 interior vertices in side-5 triangular grid (sum)

problem_spec.summary: A robot moves between adjacent unit-triangle cells inside a side-5 triangular grid, forming a simple closed path (no backtrack, all visited cells distinct). The bounded region contains a specified number m of interior grid-vertices (where 6 unit triangles meet). Using duality and a perimeter formula k=6m-2e for the boundary length, enumerate the achievable cycle lengths for fixed m and compute their sum.
givens: T is an equilateral triangle of side length 5 partitioned into unit equilateral triangle cells.
Two cells are adjacent iff they share a common edge.
The robot moves between adjacent cells and never immediately backtracks.
A closed path of length k visits exactly k distinct cells (so it is a simple cycle in the adjacency graph).
Interior grid-vertices are vertices of the subdivision not on the boundary of T (six unit triangles meet there).
The region bounded by the cycle contains exactly m=4 interior grid-vertices.
ask: Let S be the set of all k for which such a cycle exists. Compute Σ_{k\inS} k and output it as a three-digit zero-padded number.

parameter(s).name: side_length
parameter(s).purpose: size of the triangular grid (here 5)

parameter(s).name: move_rule
parameter(s).purpose: forbids immediate reversal; with distinct-visit condition yields simple cycles

parameter(s).name: cycle_measure
parameter(s).purpose: identifies simple cycles in the cell adjacency graph

parameter(s).name: enclosed_interior_vertex_count
parameter(s).purpose: fixes m, the number of interior grid-vertices inside the cycle (here 4)

invariant(s).name: dual_graph_bipartite
invariant(s).statement: The cell adjacency graph is bipartite by triangle orientation (up vs down), so every cycle has even length.

invariant(s).name: interior_vertex_hex_face_correspondence
invariant(s).statement: Each interior grid-vertex corresponds to a hexagonal face in the dual graph (six unit triangles surround it). A simple cycle bounds a connected union of these faces.

invariant(s).name: fixed_m_perimeter_formula
invariant(s).statement: If the bounded region contains m interior vertices and the induced adjacency among them has e shared edges, then the boundary cycle length is k = 6m - 2e (each shared edge removes two boundary edges).

invariant(s).name: finite_internal_graph_for_side5
invariant(s).statement: For side_length=5, the interior-vertex adjacency graph has exactly 6 vertices and 9 edges, so for fixed m=4, the possible e values are finite and can be enumerated.

essential_assumption(s): A closed path visiting exactly k distinct cells is a simple cycle in the planar dual graph.
The region bounded by a simple cycle corresponds to a connected union of hexagonal faces (interior vertices).
The perimeter identity k=6m-2e applies to this dual-face union.
uniqueness_certificate(s): For fixed side_length=5 and m=4, every valid cycle length must equal 6m-2e for some connected 4-vertex subgraph of the fixed 6-vertex interior graph, and each achievable e yields an achievable cycle. Enumerating the finite set of possible e values uniquely determines S and hence Σ_{k\inS} k.

technique(s).name: parity_and_cycle_model_in_dual_graph
technique(s).purpose: convert robot motion to cycles in a bipartite graph and restrict k to even values

technique(s).name: internal_vertex_perimeter_model
technique(s).purpose: relate cycle length k to m and internal adjacency edge count e via k=6m-2e

technique(s).name: enumerate_connected_internal_subgraphs_fixed_m
technique(s).purpose: list possible e values for connected m-vertex subgraphs, hence possible k values

technique(s).name: compute_sum_and_format
technique(s).purpose: compute Σ_{k\inS} k and zero-pad to three digits

theorem(s).name: bipartite_graph_cycle_parity
theorem(s).role: all cycles in a bipartite graph have even length

theorem(s).name: planar_boundary_count_for_union_of_hex_faces
theorem(s).role: boundary length = 6m - 2e for a connected union of m hexagons with e shared edges

theorem(s).name: connected_subgraph_edge_bounds
theorem(s).role: a connected graph on m vertices has at least m-1 edges

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: translate constrained motion to planar cycles, then compute possible perimeters via a finite internal-structure count.

critical_trick(s): Use the bipartite nature of the dual adjacency graph to constrain cycle lengths to be even.
Encode the interior of a cycle by the induced adjacency graph on enclosed interior vertices and use k=6m-2e.
For side_length=5 and fixed m=4, enumerate possible edge counts e in the finite 6-vertex internal graph to obtain S and its sum.

library(s).name: itertools
library(s).reason: enumerate 4-vertex subsets and count edges

library(s).name: networkx
library(s).reason: optional connectedness checks for induced subgraphs

task(s).reason: encode the 6 interior vertices and 9 adjacency edges

task(s).name: enumerate_connected_size4_subgraphs
task(s).reason: compute attainable e values and thus attainable k values for m=4

task(s).name: compute_sum_and_zero_pad
task(s).reason: sum k values and format as three digits

title: Sum of squares of achievable cycle lengths in a side-4 triangular grid

identity.short_name: Sum of squares of achievable cycle lengths in a side-4 triangular grid

problem_spec.summary: In a side-4 triangular grid of unit triangles, robot closed paths with no immediate backtrack and "length = number of distinct visited cells" correspond to simple cycles in the dual adjacency graph. Use bipartite parity and a dual-face enclosure/perimeter model (bounded faces correspond to interior grid vertices) to enumerate achievable cycle lengths, then compute the sum of their squares.
givens: T is an equilateral triangle of side length 4 partitioned into unit equilateral triangle cells.
Two cells are adjacent iff they share an edge; G is the resulting adjacency (dual) graph.
A robot moves between adjacent cells and never moves to the cell it occupied immediately before.
For integer k, we ask whether there exists a closed path of length k visiting exactly k distinct cells.
ask: Let S be the set of achievable k. Compute out = Σ_{k\inS} k^2 and output it as a three-digit zero-padded integer.

parameter(s).name: side_length
parameter(s).purpose: fixes a small finite dual-face configuration enabling complete by-hand enumeration

parameter(s).name: move_rule
parameter(s).purpose: rules out immediate reversals; with distinct-vertex requirement this encodes simple-cycle behavior

parameter(s).name: aggregate_function
parameter(s).purpose: changes the postprocessing from Σk to Σk^2, altering the final output while preserving the same structural enumeration

invariant(s).name: dual_graph_bipartite
invariant(s).statement: The dual adjacency graph is bipartite by triangle orientation (up vs down), so every cycle length is even.

invariant(s).name: bounded_faces_correspond_to_interior_vertices
invariant(s).statement: In a planar triangulation, bounded faces of the dual correspond to interior vertices of the primal. For side length 4, there are exactly 3 interior vertices, hence exactly 3 bounded dual faces, each a 6-cycle.

invariant(s).name: perimeter_of_union_of_bounded_faces
invariant(s).statement: Any simple cycle is the boundary of the union of bounded faces it encloses. If m faces are enclosed and e dual edges are shared between enclosed faces, then the boundary length is k = 6m - 2e.

title: Permanent of a 4x4 linear mod matrix

identity.short_name: Permanent of a 4x4 linear mod matrix

problem_spec.summary: Compute the permanent of a 4×4 matrix whose entries are given by a linear formula modulo 7, then reduce the result modulo 1000.
givens: M is 4×4 with indices i,j \in {1,2,3,4}.
M_{ij} = (i + 2j) mod 7, interpreted in {0,1,2,3,4,5,6}.
ask: Compute perm(M) mod 1000.

parameter(s).name: n
parameter(s).purpose: matrix size

parameter(s).name: entry_modulus
parameter(s).purpose: modulus defining entries

parameter(s).name: alpha
parameter(s).purpose: coefficient of i in entry definition

parameter(s).name: beta
parameter(s).purpose: coefficient of j in entry definition

parameter(s).name: output_modulus
parameter(s).purpose: final reduction modulus

invariant(s).name: deterministic_entry_rule
invariant(s).statement: For fixed parameters, each entry is uniquely M_{ij}=(alpha \cdot i+beta \cdot j) mod entry_modulus.

invariant(s).name: permanent_definition
invariant(s).statement: perm(M)=Σ_{\pi\inS_n} Π_{i=1}^n M_{i,\pi(i)}.

essential_assumption(s): Indices are 1-based: i,j \in {1,...,n}.

title: Sum of mixed-boundary Pascal-grid entries for n=27 mod 1000

identity.short_name: Sum of mixed-boundary Pascal-grid entries for n=27 mod 1000

problem_spec.summary: Define an n×n grid by the Pascal recurrence a(i,j)=a(i-1,j)+a(i,j-1) with a mixed boundary: top row is 1..n while the left column is constant 1. Find S(n)=sum of all entries and compute S(27) mod 1000.
givens: n is a positive integer (instance uses n=27).
Top row: a(1,j)=j for j=1..n.
Left column: a(i,1)=1 for i=1..n.
Interior recurrence: a(i,j)=a(i-1,j)+a(i,j-1) for i,j>=2.
S(n)=sum_{i=1}^n sum_{j=1}^n a(i,j).
ask: Compute S(27) mod 1000.

parameter(s).name: n
parameter(s).purpose: grid size

parameter(s).name: boundary_profile
parameter(s).purpose: initializes the recurrence on the top/left borders

parameter(s).name: recurrence
parameter(s).purpose: Pascal-type interior rule

parameter(s).name: modulus
parameter(s).purpose: final reduction (here 1000)

invariant(s).name: pascal_grid_binomial_closed_form
invariant(s).statement: With top row a(1,j)=j and left column a(i,1)=1, the unique solution is a(i,j)=C(i+j-1,i)=C(i+j-1,j-1), since it matches the boundary and satisfies Pascal's identity.

invariant(s).name: iterated_hockey_stick_for_total_sum
invariant(s).statement: S(n)=\sum_{i=1}^n\sum_{j=1}^n C(i+j-1,i) reduces via two hockey-stick applications to S(n)=C(2n+1,n)-(n+1).

invariant(s).name: uniqueness_from_boundary_and_recurrence
invariant(s).statement: The boundary values and recurrence determine all interior entries uniquely by forward filling, hence S(n) is uniquely determined.

essential_assumption(s): Binomial coefficients use the standard integer definition and standard identities.
The recurrence is applied over integers (not modulo) before the final modular reduction.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The recurrence with the specified boundary conditions has a unique solution array. The closed form gives a unique integer S(n), and reducing mod 1000 produces a unique residue.

technique(s).name: derive_closed_form_entry_via_pascal_identity
technique(s).purpose: guess and verify a binomial closed form for a(i,j) using boundary matching and Pascal's identity

technique(s).name: evaluate_total_sum_by_double_hockey_stick
technique(s).purpose: evaluate S(n) by summing over j then over i using hockey-stick identities

technique(s).name: substitute_and_reduce_mod
technique(s).purpose: compute S(27) from the closed form and reduce to a residue mod 1000

theorem(s).name: Pascal_identity
theorem(s).role: C(m,r)=C(m-1,r)+C(m-1,r-1) validates the recurrence for binomial closed forms

theorem(s).name: hockey_stick_identity
theorem(s).role: \sum_{t=r}^M C(t,r)=C(M+1,r+1) evaluates binomial sums

theorem(s).name: uniqueness_of_grid_recurrence_solution
theorem(s).role: boundary plus local recurrence determines the grid uniquely

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: interpret recurrence values through binomial identities, then evaluate totals by standard summation identities

critical_trick(s): Recognize the mixed boundary forces the simple binomial form a(i,j)=C(i+j-1,i).
Use hockey-stick twice to collapse the double sum to S(n)=C(2n+1,n)-(n+1).
Compute the final residue modulo 1000.

library(s).reason: provides exact integer binomial coefficients via comb for large values

library(s).name: itertools
library(s).reason: optional dynamic-programming verification of the recurrence table for n=27

task(s).reason: compute comb(55,27) exactly and reduce mod 1000 to get the final residue

task(s).name: dynamic_program_check_mod_1000
task(s).reason: compute the grid values mod 1000 by recurrence and sum them to confirm the same residue

title: Max K guaranteed coverable by 43 north/south canals in a 2025×2025 grid

identity.short_name: Max K guaranteed coverable by 43 north/south canals in a 2025×2025 grid

subdomain(s): Mirsky theorem and greedy chain removal

problem_spec.summary: In an N×N grid, canals are monotone lattice paths of two types: northbound (E/N from SW to NE) and southbound (E/S from NW to SE). For a fixed budget t of canals, determine the largest K such that any placement of K marked cells can be covered by t canals. Prove sufficiency via greedy longest-chain removal plus Mirsky's theorem, and prove necessity by constructing separated antichain blocks that force t+1 canals.
givens: The grid is N×N with N=2025.
Allowed canals are complete lattice paths along grid edges:

title: Minimal time adversary can block when avoidance starts at step 0

identity.short_name: Minimal time adversary can block when avoidance starts at step 0

problem_spec.summary: On a finite grid, a walker makes unit taxicab steps with a no-backtrack constraint. An adversary walks as well, and at each step n the pair {b_n,b_{n+1}} is forbidden for both endpoints of the walker's step n (including n=0). Determine the minimal time t for which the adversary can preselect a path b_1,...,b_t that makes any length-t walker path impossible.
givens: G = {0,1,2,3}^2 with taxicab distance d((x,y),(x',y'))=|x-x'|+|y-y'|.
Walker start: a_0=(0,0).
Adversary start: b_0=(0,3).
Walker dynamics: for all n>=0, d(a_{n+1},a_n)=1 and no-backtrack a_{n+2}\neqa_n.
Adversary dynamics: for all n>=0, d(b_{n+1},b_n)=1.
Blocked pair: B_n={b_n,b_{n+1}}.
Avoidance rule: for each step n=0,1,...,t-1, both endpoints satisfy a_n∉B_n and a_{n+1}∉B_n.
ask: Find the minimal t such that some adversary choice of (b_n) makes it impossible for any (a_n) to satisfy all constraints through step t-1; if none exists, output 999.

parameter(s).name: grid_size
parameter(s).purpose: defines G={0,...,m-1}^2

parameter(s).name: start_a0
parameter(s).purpose: walker start

parameter(s).name: start_b0
parameter(s).purpose: adversary start

parameter(s).name: forbidden_block_size
parameter(s).purpose: each B_n is two adjacent points

parameter(s).name: avoidance_start_step
parameter(s).purpose: avoidance applies beginning at step 0 (key mutation from the source family)

parameter(s).name: no_backtrack
parameter(s).purpose: restricts walk dynamics

invariant(s).name: local_avoidance_constraint
invariant(s).statement: For each step n=0..t-1, the walker's endpoints must lie in G\B_n, i.e., B_n deletes up to two vertices for that step.

invariant(s).name: finite_state_reduction
invariant(s).statement: The walker's feasible state at step n can be represented by the directed edge (a_{n-1},a_n), since the next move is any neighbor of a_n except a_{n-1}, subject to avoidance constraints.

invariant(s).name: manhattan_reachability_bound
invariant(s).statement: After k adversary moves, b_k lies within taxicab distance k of b_0, so every point in B_n lies within distance \leq n+1 of b_0. This yields "safe" cells that cannot appear in early blocked pairs.

invariant(s).name: adversary_threshold_definition
invariant(s).statement: Minimal t is characterized by existence of a b-sequence killing all a-paths by time t and nonexistence for t-1.

essential_assumption(s): The adversary's sequence (b_n) is fixed in advance (adversarial existential quantifier over sequences).
Avoidance is enforced for steps n=0..t-1 (includes the first step).
No-backtrack is strict: immediate reversal a_{n+2}=a_n is forbidden.
uniqueness_certificate(s): An explicit adversary path that blocks all walker paths by time t gives an upper bound on the minimal t. A single explicit walker path that succeeds up to time t-1 for every admissible adversary path gives a lower bound. Matching bounds uniquely determine the minimal t.

technique(s).name: universal_safe_path_for_lower_bound
technique(s).purpose: build a fixed walker path that avoids all possible early blocked pairs using distance-to-b0 arguments

technique(s).name: explicit_adversary_construction_for_failure
technique(s).purpose: provide a concrete adversary path whose induced blocked pairs force a contradiction for any walker path

technique(s).name: local_forcing_case_analysis
technique(s).purpose: prune walker possibilities step-by-step until a forced forbidden endpoint occurs

technique(s).name: combine_bounds
technique(s).purpose: conclude the exact minimal t

theorem(s).name: grid_graph_walk_model
theorem(s).role: Manhattan-distance-1 steps are edges in the grid graph

theorem(s).name: memory_1_walk_constraint
theorem(s).role: no-backtrack makes the walker's state a directed edge

theorem(s).name: manhattan_ball_reachability
theorem(s).role: after k unit steps, a point is within distance k of the start

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: view the process as a finite-state reachability game and use distance constraints to find safe trajectories.

critical_trick(s): Use a "safe-zone" distance argument to produce a universal walker path surviving 4 steps (lower bound).
Give a concrete adversary path whose moving blocked pair forces the walker into a unique continuation and then a forbidden endpoint (upper bound).

title: Expected misread error under 4-hour window on a 12-hour clock with identical hands

identity.short_name: Expected misread error under 4-hour window on a 12-hour clock with identical hands

subdomain(s): fractional part constraints on real variables

problem_spec.summary: Ambiguous readings with indistinguishable clock hands are characterized by a fractional-part system that reduces to discrete integer-part parameters. Under a bounded-error window, count all admissible ambiguous configurations, compute the expected misread error, and output the sum of numerator and denominator of the reduced fraction.
givens: The clock is a 12-hour cycle and is perfect, but the hour and minute hands are indistinguishable.
A valid reading (h,m) with 0<=h,m<12 satisfies h = floor(h) + m/12.
A configuration {h,m} is ambiguous if both (h,m) and (m,h) satisfy the valid-reading constraint and h != m.
Only configurations with |h-m| <= 4 are considered.
Choose uniformly among all such configurations; the error is |h-m|.
ask: Compute the expected error E. Write E=a/b in lowest terms with a,b positive integers and output a+b.

parameter(s).name: cycle_length
parameter(s).purpose: hour-cycle length for the clock model

parameter(s).name: accuracy_window
parameter(s).purpose: bound on |h-m| (here 4)

parameter(s).name: selection_rule
parameter(s).purpose: choose uniformly among admissible ambiguous configurations

invariant(s).name: valid_time_pair_constraint
invariant(s).statement: Valid readings satisfy h = floor(h) + m/12 (equivalently m = 12*{h}).

invariant(s).name: ambiguity_via_swap
invariant(s).statement: Ambiguity occurs when both (h,m) and (m,h) are valid readings, giving a coupled fractional-part system.

invariant(s).name: discrete_error_offsets
invariant(s).statement: Writing i=floor(h), j=floor(m), solutions satisfy h-m = 12(i-j)/13, so errors are discrete multiples of 12/13.

invariant(s).name: window_bounds_integer_parameter
invariant(s).statement: The constraint |h-m|<=4 becomes |i-j|<=4. Excluding h=m removes |i-j|=0.

invariant(s).name: multiplicity_by_diagonal_count
invariant(s).statement: For fixed n=|i-j|, the number of unordered configurations is 12-n (count of integer pairs with i>j and i-j=n).

essential_assumption(s): Times are modeled on [0,12) with ordinary absolute difference |h-m| (no circular wrap-around).
Ambiguity excludes the trivial case h=m.
The admissible ambiguous configurations form a finite set, enabling a uniform choice and exact expectation.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The fractional-part system reduces to integer-part parameters (i,j) and forces the error |h-m|=12|i-j|/13. The window bounds |i-j| and yields finitely many cases with uniquely determined multiplicities. Therefore the expected error is a unique rational number; its reduced form (a,b) is unique, hence a+b is unique.

technique(s).name: set_up_fractional_part_equations
technique(s).purpose: translate "both readings valid" into equations involving floors (integer parts)

technique(s).name: derive_discrete_offsets
technique(s).purpose: eliminate h,m to obtain the discrete error formula |h-m|=12|i-j|/13

technique(s).name: apply_window_and_count_multiplicities
technique(s).purpose: bound |i-j| and count how many configurations realize each offset n via diagonal counting

technique(s).name: compute_expected_error_and_finalize
technique(s).purpose: compute E as a weighted average, reduce to a/b, and output a+b

theorem(s).name: decomposition_by_integer_parts
theorem(s).role: floor decomposition turns the fractional-part constraints into linear equations on integer-part regions

theorem(s).name: integer_bound_from_inequality
theorem(s).role: bounding |12n/13|<=4 yields n<=4

theorem(s).name: uniqueness_of_reduced_fraction
theorem(s).role: gcd normalization makes a/b unique

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: reduce a continuous-looking ambiguity to discrete integer parameters and then compute an exact expected value by counting

critical_trick(s): Convert the swap-ambiguity constraint into a linear system indexed by integer parts i=floor(h), j=floor(m).
Derive the discrete error law |h-m|=12|i-j|/13 and use the window to restrict |i-j|.
Count configurations for each offset by counting lattice points on diagonals, then compute the uniform expected error.

task(s).reason: loop over i,j in 0..11, construct (h,m), filter |h-m|<=4 and h!=m, and compute the same expectation as a check

task(s).name: reduce_and_sum

title: Probability an ambiguous 12-hour configuration has error \leq5 (output A+B)

identity.short_name: Probability an ambiguous 12-hour configuration has error \leq5 (output A+B)

subdomain(s): fractional part constraints on real variables
counting on finite sets and reduced fractions

problem_spec.summary: On a 12-hour clock with indistinguishable hour/minute hands, ambiguous configurations are intersections of the validity curve m=12{h} with its swap. Decompose by integer parts to obtain a discrete family indexed by n=|a-b|, count how many configurations have error E=|h-m| at most a given window, and compute the resulting probability and the sum of numerator and denominator.
givens: The clock is accurate, but the hour and minute hands are indistinguishable.
A valid ordered reading (h,m) with 0<=h,m<12 satisfies m = 12*{h}.
A configuration is ambiguous if both (h,m) and (m,h) are valid and h\neqm.
Define the ambiguity error E=|h-m|.
Choose an ambiguous configuration uniformly at random from all ambiguous configurations in a 12-hour cycle.
Consider the window condition E<=5.
ask: Let p be the probability that E<=5. Write p=A/B in lowest terms and output out=A+B.

parameter(s).name: cycle_length
parameter(s).purpose: hour-cycle length for the clock model

parameter(s).name: error_window
parameter(s).purpose: threshold on ambiguity error (here 5)

parameter(s).name: selection_model
parameter(s).purpose: defines the probability space (uniform over all ambiguous configurations)

parameter(s).name: output_map
parameter(s).purpose: postprocess p=A/B to A+B

invariant(s).name: valid_time_pair_constraint
invariant(s).statement: A valid ordered clock reading satisfies m = 12{h} with 0<=h,m<12.

invariant(s).name: ambiguity_via_swap
invariant(s).statement: An ambiguous configuration satisfies both m=12{h} and h=12{m}, with h\neqm.

invariant(s).name: discrete_error_levels
invariant(s).statement: Writing a=floor(h), b=floor(m), ambiguity solutions satisfy E=|h-m|=12|a-b|/13, so E takes discrete values indexed by n=|a-b|.

invariant(s).name: configuration_count_by_difference
invariant(s).statement: For n=1,...,11, the number of ambiguous configurations with |a-b|=n is 12-n, and the total number of ambiguous configurations is C(12,2)=66.

invariant(s).name: probability_is_favorable_over_total
invariant(s).statement: Under uniform selection over ambiguous configurations, p = (# configurations with E<=window) / 66.

essential_assumption(s): Times are modeled continuously within one 12-hour cycle (0<=h,m<12).
Ambiguous configurations exclude the trivial case h=m.
The probability space is uniform over the finite set of ambiguous configurations.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Decomposing by integer parts a=floor(h), b=floor(m) yields a unique solution (h,m) for each unordered pair {a,b} with a\neqb, and the error E depends only on n=|a-b|. The window E<=5 uniquely determines which n are favorable, and counting such pairs produces a unique reduced probability p=A/B and hence a unique out=A+B.

title: Even Betti sum via Alexander duality for a complement in R^3

identity.short_name: Even Betti sum via Alexander duality for a complement in R^3

subdomain(s): singular homology with coefficients in Q

problem_spec.summary: Given semialgebraic subsets of R^3 consisting of two disjoint spheres and an affine line tangent to one sphere, form their union U and study the complement S = R^3 \ U. Use one-point compactification to pass to S^3 and form A = closure(U) in S^3. Identify A up to homotopy as a disjoint union of a wedge S^2 ∨ S^1 and a sphere S^2, compute H_*(A;Q), then apply Alexander duality to obtain Betti numbers of S. The asked output is the even Betti sum b0(S)+b2(S).
givens: S1 is a sphere in R^3 given by an explicit quadratic equation.
S2 is a sphere in R^3 given by an explicit quadratic equation.
L is an affine line in R^3 given by two independent linear equations.
S1 and S2 are disjoint; L intersects S1 in exactly one point (tangency) and is disjoint from S2.
U = S1 ∪ S2 ∪ L and S = R^3 \ U.
Betti numbers b_k(S) are taken with rational coefficients.
ask: Compute b_0(S) + b_2(S).

parameter(s).name: semialgebraic_pieces
parameter(s).purpose: defines the union U whose complement is studied

parameter(s).name: intersection_pattern
parameter(s).domain: two disjoint spheres; one affine line tangent to exactly one sphere and disjoint from the other
parameter(s).purpose: forces a wedge-plus-disjoint-sphere homotopy model for the compactified closure A

parameter(s).name: coefficient_field
parameter(s).purpose: determines Betti numbers as vector-space dimensions (this instance uses Q)

parameter(s).name: output_linear_form
parameter(s).purpose: specifies which linear combination of Betti numbers is returned

invariant(s).name: compactification_equivalence
invariant(s).statement: For semialgebraic U ⊂ R^3, letting A be the closure of U in the one-point compactification S^3, the complement S = R^3 \ U is homotopy equivalent to S^3 \ A.

invariant(s).name: compactification_of_affine_line
invariant(s).statement: If L ⊂ R^3 is an affine line (homeomorphic to R), then its closure in S^3 is homeomorphic to S^1.

invariant(s).name: alexander_duality_relation
invariant(s).statement: Reduced cohomology satisfies  \tilde H^i(S^3 \ A; k) ≅ \tilde H_{2-i}(A; k)  for i=0,1,2 over a field k.

essential_assumption(s): The given sets are semialgebraic (locally contractible), so Alexander duality applies to A ⊂ S^3.
The intersection pattern (disjointness and single-point tangency) holds; otherwise the homotopy type of A may change.
Coefficients are taken in a field (this instance uses Q), so Betti numbers are dimensions of vector spaces.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The explicit defining equations uniquely determine the intersection graph: S1 ∩ S2 = ∅, L ∩ S1 is one point, and L ∩ S2 = ∅. Under one-point compactification, L closes to S^1, so A is homotopy equivalent to (S^2 ∨ S^1) ⊔ S^2. This homotopy type uniquely determines H_*(A) over Q, and Alexander duality uniquely determines b0(S) and b2(S), hence b0(S)+b2(S) is uniquely determined.

technique(s).name: compactify_and_replace_complement
technique(s).purpose: replace S = R^3 \ U by S^3 \ A via one-point compactification

technique(s).name: analyze_intersection_pattern
technique(s).purpose: solve intersection conditions to determine the intersection graph of the pieces and their closures

technique(s).name: model_closure_as_wedge_and_disjoint_union
technique(s).purpose: identify A up to homotopy using wedge (contractible intersection) and disjoint-union decomposition

technique(s).name: compute_homology_from_models
technique(s).purpose: compute H_*(A) using standard homology of S^1, S^2, and behavior under wedge/disjoint unions

technique(s).name: apply_Alexander_duality_and_extract_Betti
technique(s).purpose: apply Alexander duality and universal coefficients (over Q) to obtain Betti numbers of S

technique(s).name: evaluate_requested_linear_combination
technique(s).purpose: compute out = b0 + b2

theorem(s).name: Alexander_duality_in_S3
theorem(s).role: relates \tilde H^i(S^3\A) to \tilde H_{2-i}(A)

theorem(s).name: Mayer_Vietoris
theorem(s).role: computes homology of unions from homology of parts and intersections (used to justify wedge modeling)

theorem(s).name: wedge_from_contractible_intersection
theorem(s).role: if X and Y are path-connected and X∩Y is contractible, then X∪Y ≃ X∨Y

theorem(s).name: homology_of_spheres_and_circles
theorem(s).role: provides H_*(S^1;k) and H_*(S^2;k) to compute H_*(A)

theorem(s).name: universal_coefficients_over_Q
theorem(s).role: ensures ranks of H^i and H_i agree over Q

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: compute topology of a complement by dualizing the homology of the compactified obstacle set

critical_trick(s): Pass from R^3 to S^3 via one-point compactification so the obstacle becomes compact.
Recognize an affine line compactifies to S^1 and a one-point intersection with a sphere yields a wedge S^2 ∨ S^1.
Use Alexander duality to read b0 and b2 of the complement from \tilde H2(A) and \tilde H0(A).

title: Equal chords and tangent-chord angles force a fixed sec^2 value

identity.short_name: Equal chords and tangent-chord angles force a fixed sec^2 value

subdomain(s): circle geometry on the unit circle
topic_path: Tangent and chord constraints to trig value

problem_spec.summary: A tangent line to the unit circle fixes the tangency point, hence fixes a horizontal chord through it. An equal-chord condition fixes the sine of a certain inscribed angle. Two tangent-defined angles at the tangency point are converted via the tangent-chord theorem to inscribed angles whose sum is that same inscribed angle (mod 180^\circ). Evaluate a scaled sec^2 exactly.
givens: \omega is the unit circle x^2+y^2=1.
\ell: 3x+4y=5 is tangent to \omega at A3.
A2 is the second intersection of \omega with the horizontal line through A3.
A1,A4 are distinct points on \omega such that |A1A4|=|A2A3| and A1,A2,A3,A4 are in counterclockwise order.
B1,B2 are points on \ell with A3 between them.
θ=∠B1A3A4 and φ=∠A1A3B2 are taken as acute tangent-chord angles at A3.
ask: Compute E = 16 \cdot sec^2(θ+φ).

parameter(s).name: tangent_line_coeffs
parameter(s).domain: line ax+by=c with c/\sqrt(a^2+b^2)=1 (tangent to unit circle)
parameter(s).purpose: fixes the tangency point A3 and thus the reference chord length

parameter(s).name: horizontal_chord_choice
parameter(s).purpose: canonically defines A2 from A3

title: Classify N/P/I for axis-placed points with two slope constraints

identity.short_name: Classify N/P/I for axis-placed points with two slope constraints

subdomain(s): coordinate geometry on axes and quadrants

problem_spec.summary: Points A,D lie on the x-axis and points B,C lie on the y-axis, with the origin between each pair. Two positive-slope constraints (AC and BD) force a small finite set of sign patterns. Five geometric propositions (slope sign, convexity, sign-contrast impossibility, concyclicity, and axis-tangent circle existence) are classified as necessarily true/possible/impossible. The output encodes the counts by 100 \cdot C(N)+10 \cdot C(P)+C(I).
givens: A=(a,0), D=(d,0) lie on the x-axis with a\neq0, d\neq0 and the origin lies strictly between them (ad<0).
B=(0,b), C=(0,c) lie on the y-axis with b\neq0, c\neq0 and the origin lies strictly between them (bc<0).
slope(AC)>0 and slope(BD)>0.
Five propositions (P1)-(P5) as stated in the Problem are to be classified as N/P/I.
Output code is out = 100 \cdot C(N)+10 \cdot C(P)+C(I).
ask: Compute the 3-digit code out.

parameter(s).name: axis_assignment
parameter(s).purpose: fixes which points lie on which axes (breaks the "either axis" symmetry).

parameter(s).name: between_origin_constraint
parameter(s).purpose: forces opposite rays for each paired axis.

parameter(s).name: slope_constraints
parameter(s).purpose: reduces admissible configurations to two sign-pattern families.

parameter(s).name: proposition_list
parameter(s).purpose: targets for N/P/I classification.

parameter(s).name: encoding_rule
parameter(s).purpose: maps counts to a 3-digit output.

invariant(s).name: forced_sign_relations_from_slopes
invariant(s).statement: With A=(a,0), B=(0,b), C=(0,c), D=(d,0), the conditions slope(AC)>0 and slope(BD)>0 imply sign(b)=sign(a) and sign(c)=sign(d). Together with ad<0 and bc<0, this leaves exactly two global sign patterns related by (x,y)↦(-x,-y).

invariant(s).name: slope_AB_and_slope_CD_always_negative
invariant(s).statement: Under the forced sign relations, slope(AB)=-b/a<0 and slope(CD)=-c/d<0 in all admissible configurations.

invariant(s).name: concyclicity_condition_for_axis_points
invariant(s).statement: Points (a,0),(d,0),(0,b),(0,c) are concyclic iff ad = bc (equivalently, the constant term w in x^2+y^2+ux+vy+w=0 matches from x-axis and y-axis pairs).

invariant(s).name: axis_tangent_circle_axis_intersections
invariant(s).statement: A circle tangent to both coordinate axes meets each axis at exactly one point (the tangency point). Hence it passes through A=(a,0) and B=(0,b) iff |a|=|b| (and signs match the quadrant of the center).

invariant(s).name: convexity_by_axis_ray_order
invariant(s).statement: With A and B on rays of the same sign and C and D on rays of the opposite sign, the cyclic order A-B-D-C produces a simple quadrilateral; cross-product signs are consistent, so it is convex.

title: Tan of ∠DPM in a square from DM=3MF under equal-offset construction

identity.short_name: Tan of ∠DPM in a square from DM=3MF under equal-offset construction

problem_spec.summary: In a square, choose points on adjacent sides with equal offsets, drop a perpendicular to a connecting line, take a midpoint, impose a distance ratio constraint, and determine tan of a fixed angle at the midpoint.
givens: ABCD is a square.
E lies on segment AB and F lies on segment BC.
AE = CF.
M lies on line CE and BM ⟂ CE (M is the perpendicular foot from B to CE).
P is the midpoint of EF.
The metric constraint DM = 3 \cdot MF holds.
ask: Let theta = ∠DPM. Write tan(theta) = p/q in lowest terms with p,q positive integers. Compute p+q.

parameter(s).name: side_length
parameter(s).purpose: scale of the square (problem is scale-invariant)

parameter(s).name: offset
parameter(s).purpose: sets AE=t and CF=t (positions of E and F)

parameter(s).name: ratio_constraint
parameter(s).purpose: selects a specific configuration and forces a fixed tangent value

invariant(s).name: complement_equality_on_sides
invariant(s).statement: From AB=BC and AE=CF, we get BE=BF.

invariant(s).name: perpendicular_foot_uniqueness
invariant(s).statement: The perpendicular from B to CE meets CE at a unique point M.

invariant(s).name: tangent_equals_ratio
invariant(s).statement: In this configuration, tan(∠DPM) = DM/MF (derivable via coordinates).

essential_assumption(s): E and F lie on the closed segments AB and BC, and 0 < AE < AB (nondegenerate).
The condition DM = 3 \cdot MF is consistent with the configuration (it is, yielding a valid interior t).
uniqueness_certificate(s): After scaling the square, the configuration is parametrized by t=AE=CF. The constructions of M and P are unique. The identity tan(∠DPM)=DM/MF holds for all such t, so imposing DM=3 \cdot MF forces tan(∠DPM)=3 uniquely. Reduced p/q is unique, hence p+q is unique.

title: Probability of always-true statements in a triangle/circle/reflection configuration (9 cards)

identity.short_name: Probability of always-true statements in a triangle/circle/reflection configuration (9 cards)

problem_spec.summary: In an acute triangle, a line through the circumcenter defines points on two sides and a circle through a vertex. A free point on the third side defines a second intersection point with that circle. A reflection across a fixed side creates perpendicularity/midpoint invariants. Among a finite list of candidate statements, determine which are universally true, then compute a uniform probability and an encoded integer output.
givens: ABC is an acute triangle with circumcenter O.
A line \ell through O meets AB and AC at interior points D and E.
\omega is the circumcircle of triangle ADE.
K is chosen on open segment BC with K not on AO, and line AK meets \omega again at L\neqA.
M is the reflection of O across line AC.
N = OM ∩ AC.
F is the foot from O to line AB.
There are 9 fixed candidate statements; one is chosen uniformly at random; success means it is true for every admissible (\ell,K).
ask: Compute p=q/r in lowest terms and output 100q+r.

parameter(s).name: triangle_shape
parameter(s).purpose: ensures standard circumcenter/foot behavior and avoids degeneracy.

parameter(s).name: line_through_circumcenter
parameter(s).purpose: determines D,E and the circle \omega.

parameter(s).name: point_on_BC
parameter(s).purpose: determines L as second intersection of AK with \omega.

parameter(s).name: statement_set
parameter(s).purpose: determines which claims are tested for universality and the probability denominator.

invariant(s).name: collinearity_from_intersection_definition
invariant(s).statement: D and E lie on \ell and \ell passes through O, so D,O,E are always collinear.

invariant(s).name: concyclicity_from_circle_definition
invariant(s).statement: \omega is the circumcircle of A,D,E and L lies on \omega, so A,D,E,L are always concyclic.

invariant(s).name: collinearity_AKL
invariant(s).statement: L is defined as the second intersection of line AK with \omega, hence A,K,L are always collinear.

invariant(s).name: reflection_ac_perpendicular_bisector
invariant(s).statement: Reflecting O across AC makes AC the perpendicular bisector of OM; thus OM ⟂ AC and N=OM∩AC is the midpoint of OM.

invariant(s).name: universality_is_boolean_per_statement
invariant(s).statement: Each statement is either true for all admissible (\ell,K) or not; the count of universally true statements is well-defined.

essential_assumption(s): D and E are interior points so \omega is a nondegenerate circle.
K is chosen so that L exists as a distinct second intersection (AK not tangent to \omega at A).
Triangle is acute so circumcenter and perpendicular feet behave in the usual Euclidean way.

uniqueness_certificate(s).statement: The set of universally true statements is determined by proving a subset holds for all configurations and giving a single admissible counterexample for each remaining statement. Therefore the number of universally true statements, the probability p, and the encoded output 100q+r are unique.

technique(s).name: prove_universal_statements_by_unpacking_definitions
inputs: incidence definitions of D,E,\omega,L and reflection definition of M
technique(s).purpose: certify which statements are tautologically or structurally always true.

technique(s).name: prove_reflection_claims
inputs: M as reflection of O across AC
technique(s).purpose: derive OM ⟂ AC and midpoint property for N.

technique(s).name: disprove_nonuniversal_statements_by_coordinate_counterexample
inputs: freedom to choose an acute triangle and admissible \ell,K
technique(s).purpose: exhibit one explicit configuration where remaining statements fail.

technique(s).name: compute_probability_from_count
inputs: number of universally true statements and total statements
technique(s).purpose: compute p=q/r and output 100q+r.

theorem(s).name: Reflection across a line gives perpendicular bisector
theorem(s).role: If M is the reflection of O across line AC, then AC is the perpendicular bisector of OM.

theorem(s).name: Circle through three noncollinear points is unique
theorem(s).role: Justifies defining \omega as circumcircle of triangle ADE.

theorem(s).name: Tangency criterion (radius ⟂ tangent)
theorem(s).role: A line is tangent to \omega at L iff it is perpendicular to the radius from center(\omega) to L.

theorem(s).name: Concyclicity check via circle equation / equal power
theorem(s).role: Supports coordinate verification that four points are not concyclic in a counterexample.

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Use rigid definitions and reflection invariants to prove universality; use a single explicit counterexample to refute non-universal claims.

critical_trick(s): Identify a core set of statements that are always true purely from definitions (collinearity/concyclicity) and reflection invariants.
Use one explicit coordinate configuration to falsify all remaining candidate statements at once.
Convert the count into a uniform probability and compute 100q+r.

title: Count parameter components giving exactly 3 bounded regions (circle + diameter + moving line)

identity.short_name: Count parameter components giving exactly 3 bounded regions (circle + diameter + moving line)

problem_spec.summary: A circle (given as two semicircle graphs), a fixed vertical diameter, and a translated diagonal line y=x+a define a planar subdivision; count the number of connected parameter-sets for which the bounded region count equals a target value.
givens: Curves are graphs of f(x)=sqrt(4-x^2) and g(x)=-sqrt(4-x^2), each defined on the real domain [-2,2].
The fixed vertical line is ell: x=0.
The moving line is p_a: y = x + a for real parameter a.
A "bounded region" is a connected bounded face of the planar subdivision induced by these curves/lines.
ask: Determine the number of disjoint (connected) subsets of real a-values for which the arrangement has exactly 3 bounded regions (pad to 3 digits).

parameter(s).name: a
parameter(s).purpose: translates the moving line y=x+a and changes the chord it cuts in the disk

parameter(s).purpose: radius of the circle given by semicircle graphs (here radius=2)

parameter(s).purpose: x-coordinate of the fixed vertical line (here 0)

parameter(s).purpose: defines the arrangement whose bounded faces are counted

invariant(s).name: local_constancy_away_from_events
invariant(s).statement: The bounded region-count is constant on any open interval of a with no topological events (no tangency, no intersection creation/annihilation, no endpoint degeneracy).

invariant(s).name: critical_event_types
invariant(s).statement: Region-count can change only when the moving line is tangent to the circle, passes through an existing vertex/intersection point (e.g., circle ∩ fixed line), or hits a curve endpoint (x=±radius for the semicircle graphs).

invariant(s).name: bounded_regions_live_inside_closed_curve
invariant(s).statement: Since the only closed curve is the circle (f∪g), all bounded regions lie inside the disk it encloses; lines alone cannot create bounded faces.

essential_assumption(s): Curves are considered with their real domains, and regions are counted only when fully enclosed.
Generic a values avoid degeneracies; critical a values are isolated.
The circle is treated as the union of the semicircle graphs, including their endpoints.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Once all critical a values are identified and ordered, the region-count on each interval is fixed; hence the number of disjoint solution-sets is uniquely determined by checking a representative a in each interval and handling isolated critical cases.
