title: Convex rounded-square room visible area equals total area; \pi\to1; mod 1000

identity.short_name: Convex rounded-square room visible area equals total area; \pi\to1; mod 1000

subdomain(s): planar geometry with rounded corners (offset/Minkowski sums)
area and perimeter decomposition with circular arcs

problem_spec.summary: A convex planar "rounded square" region R is formed as the distance-\leqr neighborhood of a filled square, then extruded to a height H. Observers are placed inside the convex prism, implying every surface point is visible. Compute the total surface area in closed form (involving \pi), apply the replacement rule \pi\to1, and reduce mod 1000.
givens: R is the set of points within distance r=2 of Q (i.e., R = Q ⊕ disk(2)).
The room is the extrusion R×[0,H] with H=11, including floor, ceiling, and vertical walls over ∂R.
Observers are at O1=(0,0,0) and O2=(4,0,0).
A surface point is visible if the segment to O1 or O2 lies in the room (endpoints allowed).
After computing A_vis exactly, replace \pi by 1 to obtain integer S, then output S mod 1000.
ask: Determine S mod 1000.

title: Ray-intersection-defined region (4 hits): area and normalized arctan/\sqrt2 expression

identity.short_name: Ray-intersection-defined region (4 hits): area and normalized arctan/\sqrt2 expression

subdomain(s): area computation by decomposition and integration
Boundary graphs on t>0 are f(t)=1/2, g(t)=1/(1+9t^2), h(t)=2, together with the vertical line x=1.
A point (x,y) is in R if:
Let A be the area of R.
Compute the integer E = 2 / ( A - (1/3) \cdot arctan(1/2) + \sqrt2/24 ).
Intersections are counted as distinct geometric points (no multiplicity).
Rays have strictly positive slope and extend for all s\geq0.
Measure-zero degeneracies (a ray passing through an intersection point of boundary curves) do not affect the region's area and can be avoided since the condition is "there exists a ray".

The monotone-intersection bound restricts each boundary piece to at most one hit, so the only viable way to reach 4 intersections is to hit every piece; this yields a unique inequality description of R, hence A and E are uniquely determined.
monotonicity of f,g,h and positive slope of the ray
intersection with u occurs iff start point lies below u at x
viable intersection pattern and base constraints
piecewise top boundary via min-switch point
Intersection monotonicity reduces a ray-existence condition to explicit inequalities; area follows from a definite integral.

Use monotonicity to prove each boundary piece is intersected at most once, so the "4 intersections" condition forces the ray to hit every piece, turning the existential ray condition into a simple "below all graphs" inequality.
Identify the min-switch point (where the active top boundary changes) and compute the area by a rectangle plus an arctan integral.
Python is not required for the analytic derivation (all steps are elementary monotonicity + one standard arctan integral), but it is useful for verification.
Symbolically integrate 1/(1+9x^2), evaluate at endpoints, and simplify the final expression for E.
Numerically approximate A and E as a sanity check (E should be extremely close to 12).

title: Mean squared edge length after iterative face drilling (n=4)

identity.short_name: Mean squared edge length after iterative face drilling (n=4)

subdomain(s): counting edges created by local modifications

problem_spec.summary: Starting from a cube, iteratively remove disjoint subcubes from specially-scaled hole-free faces. Track removable-face count and the induced recurrences for the number of edges and the sum of squared edge lengths. Compute the mean squared edge length at stage 4 and output the reduced numerator mod 1000.
givens: P0 is a cube of side length s>0.
For each n>=1, each removable face F of P_{n-1} is a hole-free square parallel to a face of P0 with side length f_n = (2^n n!)/(2n)! * s.
From every such face F, remove n^2 disjoint subcubes of side length \ell_n = (2^n n!)/(2n+1)! * s, arranged in an n×n grid with gaps so removals do not touch existing edges/vertices.
E_n is the number of edges of P_n.
Q_n is the sum of squares of lengths of edges of P_n.
M_n = Q_n/E_n is the average squared edge length.
For n=4, write M_4=(p/q)s^2 in lowest terms.
ask: Compute out = p mod 1000.

parameter(s).name: target_stage
parameter(s).purpose: stage at which the mean-squared edge length is evaluated (instance uses 4)

parameter(s).name: base_side_length
parameter(s).purpose: overall geometric scale

parameter(s).name: face_scale_formula
parameter(s).purpose: identifies which faces are removable at stage n

parameter(s).name: subcube_scale_formula
parameter(s).purpose: edge length of newly created cavity edges at stage n

parameter(s).name: moment_order
parameter(s).purpose: specifies that Q_n is a second moment (sum of squares of edge lengths)

parameter(s).name: modulus
parameter(s).purpose: output modulus (instance uses 1000)

invariant(s).name: removable_faces_become_nonremovable
invariant(s).statement: Removing subcubes from a removable face introduces holes, so that face is no longer removable afterward.

invariant(s).name: per_removal_new_faces
invariant(s).statement: Each subcube removal creates exactly 5 new hole-free square faces (the cavity walls excluding the removed patch), all parallel to faces of P0.

invariant(s).name: next_stage_face_size_matches_subcube_size
invariant(s).statement: f_{n+1} = \ell_n, since (2^{n+1}(n+1)!)/(2n+2)! = (2^n n!)/(2n+1)!. Thus the new faces created at stage n have exactly the removable-face size for stage n+1.

invariant(s).name: edge_count_increment
invariant(s).statement: Each subcube removal creates exactly 12 new edges, and disjointness prevents any edge splitting/merging.

invariant(s).name: squared_length_increment
invariant(s).statement: Each new edge has length \ell_n, so each removal increases Q by 12 \cdot \ell_n^2; existing edges keep their lengths.

essential_assumption(s): Removals are pairwise disjoint and do not touch existing edges/vertices, so counts and length-moments add linearly.
Each removed subcube produces a cavity with exactly 5 new faces and 12 new edges of length \ell_n.
Edge lengths are measured in Euclidean 3-space; edges are maximal line segments where two faces meet.
The construction rules uniquely determine which faces are removable at each stage via f_n.

uniqueness_certificate(s).statement: With base values R_0=6, E_0=12, Q_0=12s^2, the deterministic per-removal increments imply unique recurrences: R_n=5n^2R_{n-1}, E_n=E_{n-1}+12n^2R_{n-1}, Q_n=Q_{n-1}+12n^2R_{n-1}\ell_n^2. Therefore M_4=Q_4/E_4=(p/q)s^2 is uniquely determined, and so is p mod 1000.

technique(s).name: define_state_variables
technique(s).purpose: introduce R_n, E_n, Q_n (second-moment) to summarize the evolving polyhedron

technique(s).name: derive_recurrences_from_local_change
technique(s).purpose: convert local geometry changes into global recurrences for R_n,E_n,Q_n

technique(s).name: factorial_ratio_simplification
technique(s).purpose: simplify \ell_n values and show f_{n+1}=\ell_n for stage compatibility

technique(s).name: iterate_to_target_stage
technique(s).purpose: compute R_n,E_n,Q_n for n up to the target stage

technique(s).name: reduce_fraction_and_extract_numerator_mod
technique(s).purpose: reduce M_4 coefficient to lowest terms and compute p mod 1000

theorem(s).name: linearity_of_counts_under_disjoint_local_modifications
theorem(s).role: disjoint removals imply additive updates for counts and moment sums

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Translate an iterative geometric construction into exact recurrences for combinatorial and metric invariants.

critical_trick(s): Add a second-moment state variable Q_n (sum of squared edge lengths) in addition to edge count E_n.
Use the per-removal "12 new edges of length \ell_n" rule to update Q_n by 12\ell_n^2 per removal.
Evaluate recurrences up to n=4, reduce M_4=(p/q)s^2, then compute p mod 1000.

library(s).reason: lightweight exact rational computation (Fractions) as an alternative to sympy

task(s).reason: iterate the recurrences exactly and simplify to lowest terms

task(s).name: extract_numerator_mod_1000
task(s).reason: compute p % 1000 after fraction reduction

title: Count 2D totally isotropic subspaces for a rank-4 symmetric form on F3^5

identity.short_name: Count 2D totally isotropic subspaces for a rank-4 symmetric form on F3^5

subdomain(s): incidence double counting in finite geometry

problem_spec.summary: In a finite-dimensional vector space over F3 equipped with a symmetric bilinear form having a 1D radical, count the 2D subspaces on which the restricted form is identically zero (totally isotropic). Reduce by splitting off the radical, classify subspaces by intersection with the radical, and count isotropic lines and isotropic planes in the nondegenerate quotient, then lift by graphs of linear maps.
givens: Work over the finite field F_3.
B:V×V\toF_3 is symmetric bilinear with B(x,y)=x1y1+x2y2+x3y3+x4y4 in coordinates (x1,...,x5).
T is the set of 2D subspaces W⊂V such that B(w,w')=0 for all w,w'\inW (totally isotropic).
ask: Find |T|.

parameter(s).name: field_size
parameter(s).purpose: determines finite-field counts and isotropy behavior (here q=3).

parameter(s).name: ambient_dimension
parameter(s).purpose: dimension of V (here 5).

parameter(s).name: form_rank
parameter(s).purpose: rank of the symmetric bilinear form (here 4), hence radical dimension 1.

parameter(s).name: target_subspace_dimension
parameter(s).purpose: dimension of W (here 2).

parameter(s).name: target_property
parameter(s).purpose: selects the subspace property B|_W\equiv0.

invariant(s).name: radical_structure
invariant(s).statement: The radical R={v: B(v, \cdot )=0} is 1D, spanned by e5, and V decomposes as V0⊕R with V0=span(e1,e2,e3,e4).

invariant(s).name: projection_preserves_pairings
invariant(s).statement: Writing w=u+r with u\inV0, r\inR, we have B(w,w')=B0(u,u') where B0=B|V0, since R is radical.

invariant(s).name: case_split_by_intersection_with_radical
invariant(s).statement: For dim(W)=2 and dim(R)=1, either W contains R (W∩R\neq0) or W avoids R (W∩R=0). The cases are disjoint.

invariant(s).name: case_A_bijection_to_isotropic_lines
invariant(s).statement: If R⊂W then W=R⊕\ell for a unique line \ell⊂V0, and W is totally isotropic iff \ell is B0-isotropic.

invariant(s).name: case_B_reduction_and_lift
invariant(s).statement: If W∩R=0 then U=P(W)⊂V0 is 2D and W is the graph of a unique linear map φ:U\toR. Moreover B|W\equiv0 iff B0|U\equiv0.

invariant(s).name: lift_count
invariant(s).statement: For each 2D U⊂V0, the distinct graphs W over U are in bijection with Hom(U,R), giving q^{dim(U) \cdot dim(R)}=3^2 lifts.

essential_assumption(s): Totally isotropic means B(w,w')=0 for all w,w' in W (restriction is the zero bilinear form).
Counting is over linear subspaces of V (not affine planes).
All linear algebra and orthogonality are over F_3.

uniqueness_certificate(s).statement: The radical split V=V0⊕R and the case split (R⊂W vs W∩R=0) are canonical and disjoint. In Case A, W↔\ell is bijective; in Case B, W↔(U,φ) is bijective with |Hom(U,R)| fixed. Therefore |T| is uniquely determined by counts of isotropic lines and isotropic planes in (V0,B0).

The count of isotropic planes in (V0,B0) is uniquely determined by incidence double counting between isotropic lines and totally isotropic planes, once the number of isotropic lines and the number of planes through a line are computed explicitly. Hence the final total is uniquely forced.

technique(s).name: identify_radical_and_split_space
technique(s).purpose: find the radical and reduce computations to a nondegenerate 4D part plus a 1D radical.
outputs: V=V0⊕R with R=⟨e5⟩ and B0 nondegenerate on V0

technique(s).name: reduce_isotropy_via_projection_and_case_split
technique(s).purpose: classify totally isotropic W by whether it contains R or is a graph over a subspace U⊂V0.
outputs: Case A counted by isotropic lines in V0; Case B reduced to totally isotropic planes in V0

technique(s).name: count_isotropic_lines_in_V0_by_coordinate_enumeration
technique(s).purpose: count isotropic lines using the "exactly three nonzero coordinates" criterion.

technique(s).name: count_totally_isotropic_planes_in_V0_by_incidence
inputs: set of isotropic lines; structure of lines inside a 2D plane
technique(s).purpose: compute

technique(s).name: count_lifts_via_linear_maps
technique(s).purpose: count graphs W over U by Hom(U,R).

technique(s).name: sum_disjoint_cases
inputs: counts from Case A and Case B
technique(s).purpose: produce final |T|.

title: Trace of adj(A^2)+adj(A^5) from char poly

identity.short_name: Trace of adj(A^2)+adj(A^5) from char poly

problem_spec.summary: Given the characteristic polynomial of an invertible 4×4 matrix A, compute the trace of a linear combination of adjugates of powers of A. Reduce the computation to negative power sums of eigenvalues, obtain the characteristic polynomial of A^{-1} by reciprocal transform, and evaluate the needed power sums using Newton identities.
givens: A is a 4x4 real matrix.
B = adj(A^2) + adj(A^5).
ask: Compute tr(B).

parameter(s).name: n
parameter(s).purpose: matrix size (here n=4)

parameter(s).name: exponent_list
parameter(s).purpose: exponents in the linear combination of adjugate powers (here [2,5])

parameter(s).name: charpoly_coeffs
parameter(s).domain: coefficients (a1,...,an) of a monic polynomial P(λ)=λ^n + a1 λ^{n-1} + ... + an with an != 0
parameter(s).purpose: fixes the eigenvalue multiset and thus all symmetric power sums

parameter(s).name: linear_combination_weights
parameter(s).purpose: coefficients in B = Σ w_i adj(A^{m_i}) (here weights are all 1)

invariant(s).name: invertibility_from_constant_term
invariant(s).statement: det(A)=(-1)^n*(constant term of P). Since det(A)\neq0, A and all A^m are invertible.

invariant(s).name: adjugate_inverse_identity
invariant(s).statement: For invertible M, adj(M)=det(M) \cdot M^{-1}.

invariant(s).name: trace_linearity
invariant(s).statement: tr(Σ_i M_i)=Σ_i tr(M_i).

invariant(s).name: reciprocal_polynomial_for_inverse
invariant(s).statement: If P(λ)=det(λI-A) is monic with constant term a_n, then the characteristic polynomial of A^{-1} is (1/a_n) \cdot x^n P(1/x); its roots are ρ_i=1/λ_i.

essential_assumption(s): det(A)\neq0 so A^{-1} and A^{-m} exist.
trace equals sum of eigenvalues with algebraic multiplicity.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The characteristic polynomial uniquely determines the eigenvalue multiset {λ_i}. Then each term tr(adj(A^{m_i})) = det(A)^{m_i} \cdot Σ λ_i^{-m_i} is a symmetric rational function of {λ_i}, hence uniquely determined by the coefficients of P. Newton identities on the monic characteristic polynomial of A^{-1} compute Σ (1/λ_i)^{m_i} uniquely, so tr(B) is unique.

technique(s).name: adjugate_to_inverse_reduction
technique(s).purpose: rewrite tr(adj(A^{m})) as det(A)^m \cdot tr(A^{-m}) and use trace linearity for sums

technique(s).name: reciprocal_polynomial
technique(s).purpose: obtain the monic characteristic polynomial of A^{-1} whose roots are ρ_i=1/λ_i

technique(s).name: newton_sums_for_power_sum
technique(s).purpose: compute required power sums s_m = Σ ρ_i^m via Newton identities and recurrence

technique(s).name: substitution_back_to_trace
technique(s).purpose: assemble tr(B) from the computed power sums

theorem(s).name: adjugate_identity
theorem(s).role: For invertible M, adj(M)=det(M) \cdot M^{-1}.

theorem(s).name: characteristic_polynomial_of_inverse
theorem(s).role: P_{A^{-1}}(x) = (1/a_n) x^n P_A(1/x) for monic P_A with constant term a_n.

theorem(s).name: newton_identities
theorem(s).role: Relate power sums of roots to coefficients of a monic polynomial.

theorem(s).name: trace_equals_sum_of_eigenvalues
theorem(s).role: Trace of a matrix equals the sum of its eigenvalues with algebraic multiplicity.

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: symmetric_polynomials_det_trace_eigenvalues

critical_trick(s): Use adj(A^m)=det(A^m)A^{-m} and trace linearity to reduce to negative power sums of eigenvalues.
Compute the power sums of eigenvalues of A^{-1} using the reciprocal characteristic polynomial and Newton identities.

library(s).reason: symbolic Newton sums / discriminant-free verification with a companion matrix

task(s).name: compute_power_sums_via_newton
task(s).reason: mechanize Newton identities to compute s2 and s5 exactly

task(s).reason: build a companion matrix for P and confirm tr(adj(A^2)+adj(A^5)) equals the derived value

title: Eigenvalue power ratio for an integral/series-defined 2x2 operator

identity.short_name: Eigenvalue power ratio for an integral/series-defined 2x2 operator

subdomain(s): linear operators on finite-dimensional complex vector spaces

problem_spec.summary: A 2x2 complex matrix is defined by evaluating an integral and a geometric series. Select the eigenvalue with positive imaginary part, raise it to a fixed power, form Re/Im as a reduced rational, then output (p+q) mod 1000.
givens: T is represented on C^2 by the matrix M = [[ ∫_0^1 4x dx,   -\sum_{n=1}^∞ (1/2)^n ], [  \sum_{n=1}^∞ (1/2)^n,  ∫_0^1 4x dx ]].
lambda is the eigenvalue of M with Im(lambda) > 0.
Omega = lambda^10.
Psi = Re(Omega) / Im(Omega).
ask: Write Psi in lowest terms as p/q with p,q in Z and q>0, then compute (p+q) mod 1000.

parameter(s).name: entry_definitions
parameter(s).purpose: defines numeric constants used as matrix entries

parameter(s).name: exponent
parameter(s).purpose: power applied to the selected eigenvalue (here 10)

parameter(s).name: selection_rule
parameter(s).purpose: makes the eigenvalue choice unique when eigenvalues are non-real conjugates

parameter(s).name: modulus
parameter(s).purpose: final modular reduction of p+q (here 1000)

invariant(s).name: conjugate_eigenpair
invariant(s).statement: If M has non-real eigenvalues and real characteristic polynomial coefficients, they occur as a conjugate pair; Im(lambda)>0 selects one uniquely.

invariant(s).name: ratio_as_cotangent
invariant(s).statement: Writing lambda = r e^{i theta}, Omega = r^{exponent} e^{i exponent theta}, so Psi = Re(Omega)/Im(Omega) = cot(exponent theta) when Im(Omega) != 0.

invariant(s).name: special_form_rotation_scaling
invariant(s).statement: A matrix of the form [[a,-b],[b,a]] has eigenvalues a±bi, simplifying eigenvalue selection.

essential_assumption(s): The integral and series converge and define finite matrix entries.
The chosen eigenvalue satisfies Im(lambda)>0 and is non-real.
Im(Omega) != 0 so Psi is defined.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The evaluated constants uniquely determine M. The eigenvalue with Im(lambda)>0 is unique. Omega and Psi are uniquely determined, and the reduced fraction p/q is unique by gcd normalization, giving a unique residue (p+q) mod 1000.

technique(s).name: constant_evaluation_for_matrix_entries
technique(s).purpose: compute explicit numeric entries of M from an integral and a geometric series

technique(s).name: eigenvalue_from_characteristic_polynomial
technique(s).purpose: compute eigenvalues and apply Im(lambda)>0 selection

technique(s).name: polar_form_power_and_ratio
technique(s).purpose: compute Omega=lambda^exponent and Psi=Re(Omega)/Im(Omega) (using exact complex powering)

technique(s).name: reduce_fraction_and_apply_modulus
technique(s).purpose: write Psi as p/q in lowest terms and compute (p+q) mod modulus

theorem(s).role: characteristic polynomial roots determine eigenvalues

theorem(s).role: real-coefficient characteristic polynomial yields conjugate eigenpair when non-real

theorem(s).name: de_moivre_and_polar_form
theorem(s).role: compute lambda^n via magnitude/argument or exact complex arithmetic

theorem(s).name: gcd_uniqueness_of_reduced_fraction
theorem(s).role: ensures unique p/q normalization

critical_trick(s): Evaluate the integral and series to get a concrete matrix of the form [[a,-b],[b,a]].
Select the eigenvalue by Im(lambda)>0 to avoid ambiguity.
Compute lambda^10 exactly (e.g., repeated squaring), then form Psi=Re/Im and reduce.
Output (p+q) mod 1000.

title: Rank-2 nilpotent 4x4 F2 matrices: probability of being square-zero (output B-A)

identity.short_name: Rank-2 nilpotent 4x4 F2 matrices: probability of being square-zero (output B-A)

subdomain(s): conjugacy classes and centralizers in GL_n(F_q)
Work over the finite field 𝔽2 (q=2).
Consider all 4×4 nilpotent matrices X over 𝔽2 with rank(X)=2.
Define S = {X \\in M4(𝔽2) : X is nilpotent and rank(X)=2}.
Let P(E)=A/B in lowest terms with A>0. Compute B-A.
Similarity is taken over 𝔽2 via conjugation by GL4(𝔽2).
Nilpotent" means some power is zero (equivalently X^4=0 for 4×4 matrices).
The event X^2=0 is interpreted as square-zero over 𝔽2.

Rank 2 forces a finite set of Jordan partitions, and square-zero further selects a subfamily; each partition determines a unique similarity class size via orbit-stabilizer, so the probability and B-A are uniquely determined.
admissible partitions and the square-zero condition
name: Jordan classification of nilpotent matrices over a field
name: Rank formula from Jordan blocks
name: Square-zero criterion from Jordan blocks
name: Basic probability on uniform finite spaces
Classification up to similarity reduces counting to finitely many types; probability reduces to a ratio of counts.
Use rank = n - (#blocks) to reduce rank-2 nilpotents to partitions (3,1) and (2,2).
Use "square-zero ⇔ blocks of size \leq2" to isolate (2,2) as the favorable type.
Compute each class size via orbit-stabilizer using centralizer sizes in GL4(𝔽2), then form and reduce the ratio.
Python is not required for the derivation (all counts follow from Jordan-type filtering and explicit centralizer counts), but it is useful for independent verification.
implement matrix multiplication mod 2; compute ranks via Gaussian elimination mod 2.

title: Count order-8 elements in GL2(F3) with trace 1

identity.short_name: Count order-8 elements in GL2(F3) with trace 1

problem_spec.summary: Count invertible 2×2 matrices over F_q with prescribed exact order and prescribed trace. Use semisimplicity (when order is coprime to char), exclude split characteristic polynomials, pin down the unique irreducible quadratic characteristic polynomial from trace and determinant, prove the order via Cayley-Hamilton, and count matrices via centralizer-based conjugacy class size.
givens: q = 3 (field F_3).
Group is GL_2(F_3).
Target order m = 8.
Trace constraint tr(A) = t = 1 in F_3.
ask: Compute N = #{A in GL_2(F_3) : ord(A)=8 and tr(A)=1}.

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: choose the finite field size F_q.

parameter(s).name: m
parameter(s).purpose: target group element order.

parameter(s).name: trace_value
parameter(s).purpose: restrict to elements with a prescribed trace in F_q.

invariant(s).name: semisimple_condition
invariant(s).statement: If gcd(m, char(F_q))=1 and ord(A)=m, then A is semisimple, so its structure is controlled by characteristic/minimal polynomial and eigenvalues in a splitting field.

invariant(s).name: split_case_exclusion
invariant(s).statement: If χ_A splits over F_q, eigenvalues lie in F_q^× of order dividing q-1; hence ord(A) divides q-1. When m ∤ (q-1), any element of exact order m must have irreducible quadratic characteristic polynomial.

invariant(s).name: trace_det_charpoly
invariant(s).statement: For 2×2 matrices: χ_A(x)=x^2-(tr A)x+det(A). Fixing trace fixes the linear coefficient.

invariant(s).name: determinant_forced_by_order8_over_F3
invariant(s).statement: For irreducible quadratic type in GL_2(F_3), det(A) equals the norm from F_9 to F_3 of an eigenvalue λ. If ord(A)=8, then λ has order 8 in F_9^×, so λ^4=-1=2, hence det(A)=2.

invariant(s).name: order_from_cayley_hamilton
invariant(s).statement: If χ_A(x)=x^2-tx+d, then Cayley-Hamilton gives A^2=tA-dI. For (t,d)=(1,2) in F_3, this implies A^4=2I and A^8=I, so ord(A)=8.

invariant(s).name: centralizer_size_irreducible_quadratic
invariant(s).statement: If χ_A is irreducible quadratic, then C_{GL_2(F_q)}(A) ≅ (F_{q^2})^×, so |C|=q^2-1.

essential_assumption(s): Matrices are invertible (work in GL_2).
gcd(m, char(F_q))=1 (here gcd(8,3)=1) so semisimple classification applies cleanly.
For degree-2 irreducible characteristic polynomial, the centralizer is a field-multiplicative group of size q^2-1.

uniqueness_certificate(s).statement: Under gcd(m,p)=1, elements of exact order m are semisimple and partition by characteristic polynomial type (split vs irreducible). The trace constraint fixes the linear coefficient of χ_A; the order constraint forces det(A) (hence the constant term) and thus pins χ_A uniquely. A fixed irreducible quadratic χ_A determines exactly one conjugacy class in GL_2(F_q), whose size is |GL_2(F_q)|/(q^2-1). Therefore the final count N is unique.

technique(s).name: split_vs_irreducible_casework
technique(s).purpose: exclude split-eigenvalue cases when m does not divide q-1.
outputs: reduction to irreducible quadratic characteristic polynomial

technique(s).name: trace_det_to_charpoly
inputs: trace constraint and determinant implications of order
technique(s).purpose: deduce the unique candidate characteristic polynomial χ_A(x)=x^2-tx+d.

technique(s).name: irreducibility_test_discriminant
technique(s).purpose: confirm χ_A is irreducible over the base field.

technique(s).name: order_from_cayley_hamilton
technique(s).purpose: compute a small power of A and deduce ord(A)=m.

title: Count GL8(F2) elements with (x+1)^2 and irreducible-cubic primary data

identity.short_name: Count GL8(F2) elements with (x+1)^2 and irreducible-cubic primary data

subdomain(s): finite fields and linear groups GL(n,q)

problem_spec.summary: Choose T uniformly from GL_8(F2). Impose constraints on chi_T, mu_T, and dim ker(T-I) that force a unique coprime primary decomposition: a 2D unipotent (x+1)-part and a 6D semisimple part for an irreducible cubic q. Count the resulting single conjugacy class via centralizer size and reduce the count modulo 1000 using factor cancellation.
givens: V is an 8-dimensional vector space over F2.
Choose T uniformly at random from GL(V).
q(x)=x^3+x+1 in F2[x] (irreducible over F2).
Event X: chi_T(x)=(x+1)^2 q(x)^2, mu_T(x)=(x+1)^2 q(x), and dim ker(T-I)=1.
Define M = |GL(V)| \cdot P(X).
ask: Compute M mod 1000.

parameter(s).name: field_size
parameter(s).purpose: base finite field

parameter(s).name: dimension
parameter(s).purpose: dimension of V (here n=8)

parameter(s).name: irreducible_factor
parameter(s).purpose: nonsplit primary component (here q(x)=x^3+x+1)

parameter(s).name: charpoly_exponents
parameter(s).purpose: specifies chi_T factorization ((x+1)^2 and q^2)

parameter(s).name: minpoly_exponents
parameter(s).purpose: specifies mu_T factorization ((x+1)^2 and q^1)

parameter(s).name: kernel_constraint
parameter(s).purpose: fixes dim ker(T-I) on the (x+1)-primary part (here 1)

parameter(s).name: output_modulus
parameter(s).purpose: compute M modulo 1000

invariant(s).name: primary_decomposition
invariant(s).statement: Since (x+1) and q(x) are coprime, V decomposes as a T-stable direct sum of the (x+1)-primary component (dimension 2) and the q-primary component (dimension 6).

invariant(s).name: q_primary_is_semisimple_over_F8
invariant(s).statement: The q-primary part has chi=q^2 and mu=q, hence is semisimple with invariant factors q and q. Equivalently it is a 2-dimensional vector space over F8 on which T acts as multiplication by a fixed element of F8 with minimal polynomial q.

invariant(s).name: (x+1)_primary_partition_forced
invariant(s).statement: On the (x+1)-primary part (dimension 2), mu=(x+1)^2 forces the unique Jordan partition (2), hence dim ker(T-I)=1.

invariant(s).name: centralizer_product
invariant(s).statement: For coprime primary components, the centralizer in GL_8(F2) factors as the product of the centralizers on each component.

invariant(s).name: conjugacy_class_count_equals_class_size
invariant(s).statement: If the invariants determine a single similarity class, then the number of such T equals |GL_8(F2)| / |C(T)|.

essential_assumption(s): The given chi_T, mu_T, and kernel condition determine a unique similarity class over F2.
Standard centralizer facts apply for a 2×2 unipotent Jordan block over F2 and for the semisimple q-part via F8-linearity.
Modular reduction may use cancellation of factors without needing full expansion into decimal form.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The coprime factorization of chi_T forces a unique primary decomposition. The (x+1)-part is uniquely forced to Jordan type (2) by mu_T and dimension, and the q-part is uniquely semisimple with invariant factors (q,q). Hence T is unique up to similarity, so the count equals one conjugacy class size, uniquely determined by the centralizer size.

title: Count 3x3 F3 matrices with forced Jordan type and two fixed invariant lines

identity.short_name: Count 3x3 F3 matrices with forced Jordan type and two fixed invariant lines

subdomain(s): minimal polynomial and Jordan canonical form
conjugacy classes, centralizers, and flag transitivity
topic_path: Jordan form with conjugacy and flag counting

problem_spec.summary: Count 3×3 matrices over F3 satisfying an annihilating polynomial that restricts eigenvalues to {0,1}, plus trace and rank conditions that force a unique Jordan type. Impose simultaneously a fixed 0-kernel line and a fixed 1-eigenspace line, and count by combining orbit-stabilizer with transitivity on ordered pairs of lines (flags).
givens: Work in M_3(F_3) (q=3).
Matrix polynomial constraint: A^4 + A^3 + A^2 = 0 (equivalently A^2(A-I)^2=0 in F_3).
Trace constraint: tr(A)=2 in F_3.
Rank constraints: rank(A)=2 and rank(A-I)=2.
Fixed-line constraints: v0=(1,1,1)^T satisfies Av0=0; v1=(1,0,0)^T satisfies Av1=v1.
ask: Count the number of matrices A satisfying all constraints.

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: finite-field arithmetic and group sizes; instance uses q=3.

parameter(s).name: n
parameter(s).purpose: matrix size; instance uses n=3.

parameter(s).name: matrix_polynomial
parameter(s).purpose: bounds the minimal polynomial and restricts eigenvalues/Jordan block sizes.

parameter(s).name: trace_value
parameter(s).purpose: fixes eigenvalue multiplicities among the allowed eigenvalues.

parameter(s).name: rank_constraints
parameter(s).purpose: forces the geometric multiplicities and selects a unique Jordan type.

parameter(s).name: fixed_line_pair
parameter(s).purpose: simultaneously fixes ker(A) and ker(A-I) lines via membership constraints on v0 and v1.

invariant(s).name: eigenvalue_restriction_from_annihilator
invariant(s).statement: Over F_3, x^4+x^3+x^2 = x^2(x-1)^2, so the minimal polynomial divides x^2(x-1)^2. Hence eigenvalues lie in {0,1} and Jordan blocks at each eigenvalue have size \leq2.

invariant(s).name: multiplicities_from_trace
invariant(s).statement: With eigenvalues in {0,1} and n=3, tr(A)=2 forces algebraic multiplicities (n0,n1)=(1,2).

invariant(s).name: jordan_type_forced_by_ranks
invariant(s).statement: rank(A)=2 gives dim ker(A)=1, so the 0-part is a single 1×1 block. rank(A-I)=2 gives dim ker(A-I)=1 while n1=2, forcing a single size-2 Jordan block at eigenvalue 1. Thus A is similar to J=diag(0,J2(1)), uniquely.

invariant(s).name: distinct_line_pair_invariant
invariant(s).statement: dim ker(A)=dim ker(A-I)=1 and eigenvalues are distinct, so ker(A) and ker(A-I) are distinct lines, giving an ordered pair in the flag set Ω={(L0,L1): L0,L1 lines, L0\neqL1}.

invariant(s).name: fixed_line_pair_realization
invariant(s).statement: Since the kernels are 1D, Av0=0 forces ker(A)=<v0> and Av1=v1 forces ker(A-I)=<v1>, determining the ordered pair (ker(A),ker(A-I)) uniquely.

essential_assumption(s): All computations are in F_3 and ranks/kernels are taken over F_3.
Similarity is via GL_3(F_3), preserving trace, rank, kernels, and the polynomial identity.
v0 and v1 are nonzero and define distinct 1D subspaces.

uniqueness_certificate(s).statement: Constraints (polynomial + trace + ranks) force A into a unique GL_3(F_3)-conjugacy class (Jordan type J=diag(0,J2(1))). Therefore counting reduces to counting elements of that orbit satisfying a line-pair condition.

The map f(A)=(ker(A),ker(A-I)) is GL_3(F_3)-equivariant from Orb(J) onto the transitive G-set of ordered pairs of distinct lines Ω. Hence every fiber has the same size, so the count for a fixed ordered pair is |Orb(J)|/|Ω|, uniquely determined by orbit-stabilizer and the known size of Ω.

technique(s).purpose: restrict eigenvalues and Jordan block sizes.

technique(s).name: trace_to_multiplicity
technique(s).purpose: determine eigenvalue multiplicities.

technique(s).name: rank_constraints_to_jordan_selection
technique(s).purpose: force the unique Jordan type.

technique(s).name: conjugacy_class_count
technique(s).purpose: compute |Orb(J)| = |GL_n(F_q)|/|Cent(J)|.

identity.short_name: Count F3 3x3 matrices with fixed nilpotent Jordan chain

problem_spec.summary: Count 3x3 matrices over a finite field satisfying a matrix polynomial identity and trace/rank constraints that force a unique Jordan form, with extra linear constraints fixing both a kernel generator and a specific generalized eigenvector mapping to it.
givens: Work in M_3(𝔽_3).
Matrix constraint: A^3 = A^2 (equivalently A^2(A-I)=0).
Trace constraint: tr(A)=1 in 𝔽_3.
Rank constraint: rank(A)=2.
Chain constraints: Av=0 and Aw=v for v=(1,1,1)^T, w=(1,0,0)^T.
ask: Count the number of matrices A satisfying all constraints.

invariant(s).name: eigenvalue_set_from_polynomial
invariant(s).statement: From A^2(A-I)=0, the minimal polynomial divides x^2(x-1), so eigenvalues lie in {0,1} and 0-block sizes are \leq2.

invariant(s).name: multiplicities_from_trace
invariant(s).statement: With eigenvalues in {0,1} and n=3, tr(A)=1 forces algebraic multiplicities (n_1,n_0)=(1,2).

invariant(s).name: non_diagonalizable_forced_by_rank
invariant(s).statement: With (n_1,n_0)=(1,2), diagonalizability would give rank 1; rank(A)=2 forces a size-2 Jordan block at eigenvalue 0.

invariant(s).name: chain_is_fixed
invariant(s).statement: If dim ker(A)=1 and Av=0, then ker(A)=⟨v⟩; adding Aw=v fixes the length-2 Jordan chain (w\tov\to0) inside the generalized 0-eigenspace.

essential_assumption(s): All arithmetic and ranks are over 𝔽_3.
The vectors v and w are linearly independent (w ∉ ⟨v⟩), ensuring the chain constraint is consistent with rank(A)=2.
Similarity is via GL_3(𝔽_3), preserving the polynomial identity, rank, and trace.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The constraints A^3=A^2, tr(A)=1, and rank(A)=2 force a unique Jordan form up to similarity: diag(1,J_2(0)).
The constraints Av=0 and Aw=v fix the nilpotent Jordan chain; remaining freedom is exactly the choice of the 1-eigenline complementary to span(v,w), yielding a finite, uniquely determined count.

problem_spec.summary: Count invertible 3×3 matrices over F2 satisfying a polynomial identity by restricting eigenvalues/Jordan types and summing the sizes of the corresponding conjugacy classes.
givens: Work in V = M_3(F_2).
Let G = GL_3(F_2).
Define S = {A in V : A^4 + A^2 = 0}.
We seek matrices A in S ∩ G (equivalently det(A)=1).
ask: Determine the number of such matrices.

parameter(s).name: q

invariant(s).name: minimal_polynomial_divides_annihilator

invariant(s).name: factorization_in_F2
invariant(s).statement: Over F2, x^4+x^2 = x^2(x+1)^2, so eigenvalues lie in {0,1} and Jordan block sizes at each eigenvalue are \leq2.

invariant(s).name: invertible_eliminates_zero_eigenvalue
invariant(s).statement: If det(A)\neq0 then 0 is not an eigenvalue of A, so under the spectrum restriction {0,1} all eigenvalues must be 1.

invariant(s).name: conjugacy_invariance
invariant(s).statement: The conditions p(A)=0 and det(A)=1 are conjugacy-invariant, enabling counting by similarity types.

essential_assumption(s): Computations are over F2; Jordan reasoning uses that eigenvalues 0,1 lie in F2.
Similarity is via GL_3(F2).
uniqueness_certificate(s): The polynomial constraint plus invertibility forces a finite list of similarity types (here exactly two).
The count is uniquely the sum of the sizes of the corresponding conjugacy classes, computed via orbit-stabilizer and centralizers.

title: Count rank-2 square-zero elements in the centralizer of J(2,2) over F2

identity.short_name: Count rank-2 square-zero elements in the centralizer of J(2,2) over F2

problem_spec.summary: Given a fixed nilpotent Jordan matrix J of partition (2,2) over F2, describe its centralizer via a similarity that puts J into a 2-step nilpotent block form, then count the elements in the centralizer that are square-zero (B^2=0) and have rank exactly 2.
givens: Work over the finite field 𝔽₂ (q=2).
J \in M₄(𝔽₂) is the nilpotent Jordan matrix with partition (2,2) (two 2×2 Jordan blocks for eigenvalue 0).
C(J) = {B \in M₄(𝔽₂) : BJ = JB}.
𝒮 = {B \in C(J) : B² = 0}.
ask: Count matrices B \in 𝒮 such that rank(B) = 2.

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: sets finite-field arithmetic; instance fixes q=2.

parameter(s).name: block_partition
parameter(s).purpose: fixes the Jordan type of J and hence the commutant structure.

parameter(s).name: nilpotency_constraint
parameter(s).purpose: selects the polynomial identity constraint B^2=0 inside the centralizer.

parameter(s).name: target_rank
parameter(s).purpose: selects the rank stratum to be counted; instance uses target_rank=2.

invariant(s).name: commutation_constraint
invariant(s).statement: BJ=JB constrains B to lie in the centralizer C(J).

invariant(s).name: permutation_similarity_normal_form_for_J
invariant(s).statement: A fixed permutation similarity P puts J into block form J̄ = P⁻¹JP = [[0,I₂],[0,0]]. Similarity preserves rank and identities such as B²=0.

invariant(s).name: centralizer_canonical_block_form
invariant(s).statement: In the J̄-basis, B̄ = P⁻¹BP commutes with J̄ iff it has the unique form B̄ = [[M,N],[0,M]] with M,N \in M₂(𝔽_q) free.

invariant(s).name: square_zero_reduction_to_M_and_anticommutator
invariant(s).statement: For B̄=[[M,N],[0,M]], we have B̄²=[[M², MN+NM],[0,M²]]. Thus B̄²=0 iff M²=0 and MN+NM=0.

invariant(s).name: rank_bound_for_square_zero
invariant(s).statement: If B̄²=0 on a 4-dimensional space, then im(B̄) ⊆ ker(B̄), hence rank(B̄) \leq 2.

essential_assumption(s): J is exactly the nilpotent Jordan form of partition (2,2) over the stated field.
All computations (commutation, squaring, rank) are over the same field (here 𝔽₂).
Similarity transforms used are invertible, so rank and the constraint B²=0 are preserved.

uniqueness_certificate(s).statement: The permutation similarity P yields a bijection between C(J) and matrices B̄=[[M,N],[0,M]] with M,N \in M₂(𝔽₂). The constraint B²=0 is equivalent to the finite system (M²=0, MN+NM=0). The rank constraint rank(B)=2 then partitions into disjoint cases M=0 and M\neq0, each with a finite, explicitly enumerable number of solutions. Hence the total count is uniquely determined.
Because B̄²=0 implies rank(B̄)\leq2, proving rank(B̄)\geq2 in the M\neq0 case forces rank(B̄)=2 automatically, eliminating ambiguity from higher-rank possibilities.

technique(s).name: centralizer_structure_via_permutation_similarity
technique(s).purpose: put J into the 2-step nilpotent block form J̄=[[0,I],[0,0]] and solve BJ=JB in blocks.

technique(s).name: compute_square_of_block_matrix
technique(s).purpose: translate B²=0 into algebraic constraints on M and N.

technique(s).name: classify_square_zero_2x2_over_F2
technique(s).purpose: enumerate/count all M with M²=0.
outputs: M=0 or one of 3 nonzero rank-1 solutions

technique(s).name: solve_anticommutator_linear_system
inputs: fixed nonzero M with M²=0, unknown N
technique(s).purpose: count N satisfying MN+NM=0 (linear constraints in entries of N once M is fixed).
outputs: exactly 4 admissible N per nonzero M (in the representative M=J₂ basis)

technique(s).name: rank_control_via_square_zero_inclusion
technique(s).purpose: bound rank(B̄)\leq2 using im(B̄)⊆ker(B̄), then force rank=2 by exhibiting two independent images.

technique(s).name: finite_case_counting
technique(s).purpose: sum contributions from each finite case to obtain the final count.

theorem(s).name: Similarity preserves rank and polynomial identities
theorem(s).role: Justifies replacing (J,B) with (J̄,B̄) under permutation similarity.

theorem(s).name: Image-kernel inclusion for square-zero maps
theorem(s).role: If T²=0 then im(T)⊆ker(T), implying rank(T)\leqdim/2.

theorem(s).name: Rank-1 factorization uvᵀ
theorem(s).role: Any rank-1 matrix can be written as uvᵀ; squaring gives (uvᵀ)² = u(vᵀu)vᵀ, enabling the M²=0 count.

title: Count rank-2 square-zero elements in the centralizer of J(2,2) over F2

identity.short_name: Count rank-2 square-zero elements in the centralizer of J(2,2) over F2

problem_spec.summary: For the nilpotent Jordan matrix J of type (2,2) over F2, describe the centralizer and count commuting matrices that are square-zero with a prescribed rank.
givens: Work over the finite field $\mathbb F_2$ (so $q=2$).
Let $J_2=\begin{pmatrix}0&1\\0&0\end{pmatrix}$ and $J=J_2\oplus J_2\in M_4(\mathbb F_2)$ (nilpotent Jordan type $(2,2)$).
The centralizer is $C(J)=\{B\in M_4(\mathbb F_2): BJ=JB\}$.
Define $\mathcal Z=\{B\in C(J): B^2=0\}$ (square-zero cone inside the centralizer).
ask: Count matrices $B\in\mathcal Z$ such that $\operatorname{rank}(B)=2$.

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: defines the finite-field arithmetic (here fixed to q=2 in the instance)

parameter(s).name: block_partition
parameter(s).purpose: fixes the Jordan type of J and hence the structure of C(J)

parameter(s).name: nilpotency_degree
parameter(s).purpose: imposes the polynomial constraint $B^2=0$ (square-zero condition)

parameter(s).name: target_rank
parameter(s).purpose: selects the rank stratum to count within $\mathcal Z$

invariant(s).name: commutation_constraint
invariant(s).statement: $BJ=JB$ forces $B$ to have a constrained $2\times2$ block-Toeplitz form relative to the Jordan block decomposition.

invariant(s).name: centralizer_block_form
invariant(s).statement: With $J=J_2\oplus J_2$, any $B\in C(J)$ can be represented by $2\times2$ blocks $B_{ij}$ that each commute with $J_2$, hence each $B_{ij}$ is upper-triangular Toeplitz over the field.

invariant(s).name: similarity_reduction
invariant(s).statement: A fixed permutation similarity transforms $B$ to $\bar B=\begin{pmatrix}M&N\\0&M\end{pmatrix}$ with $M,N\in M_2(\mathbb F_q)$, preserving rank and polynomial identities such as $B^2=0$.

invariant(s).name: square_zero_reduction_to_MN
invariant(s).statement: For $\bar B=\begin{pmatrix}M&N\\0&M\end{pmatrix}$, $\bar B^2=\begin{pmatrix}M^2&MN+NM\\0&M^2\end{pmatrix}$; hence $\bar B^2=0$ iff $M^2=0$ and $MN+NM=0$ (over $\mathbb F_2$ this is $MN=NM$).

essential_assumption(s): $J$ is exactly the nilpotent Jordan form with partition $(2,2)$ over the stated field.
Rank and the equation $B^2=0$ are computed over the same field (here $\mathbb F_2$).
Similarity transforms used are invertible, so rank and the condition $B^2=0$ are preserved.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The map $C(J)\to M_2(\mathbb F_2)\times M_2(\mathbb F_2)$ induced by the permutation similarity is a bijection.

The constraints $B^2=0$ and $\operatorname{rank}(B)=2$ translate to explicit conditions on $(M,N)$ that split into disjoint cases ($M=0$ vs $M\neq0$), each with a fixed finite count; therefore the final count is uniquely determined.

technique(s).name: centralizer_structure_via_commutation
inputs: Jordan block $J_2$ and the equation $BJ=JB$ in $2\times2$ block form
technique(s).purpose: constrain the form of elements of $C(J)$
outputs: each $2\times2$ block commuting with $J_2$ is upper-triangular Toeplitz; overall centralizer parameterized by 8 scalars

technique(s).name: similarity_to_separate_diagonal_offdiagonal
technique(s).purpose: convert the centralizer into a simpler canonical form for counting
outputs: representation $\bar B=\begin{pmatrix}M&N\\0&M\end{pmatrix}$ with $M,N$ arbitrary in $M_2(\mathbb F_2)$

technique(s).name: square_zero_condition_in_block_form
technique(s).purpose: translate the square-zero constraint $B^2=0$ into conditions on $M$ and $N$

technique(s).name: classify_square_zero_2x2_over_F2
technique(s).purpose: enumerate all possibilities for $M$ with $M^2=0$
outputs: $M=0$ or one of 3 nonzero rank-1 nilpotents

technique(s).name: rank_count_by_cases_with_commuting_N
inputs: fixed $M$ and the set of $N$ commuting with $M$
technique(s).purpose: count $N$ yielding $\operatorname{rank}(\bar B)=\text{target_rank}$
outputs: if $M=0$: $N$ must be invertible (6 choices); if $M\neq0$ nilpotent: $N$ must lie in $\mathrm{Cent}(M)$ (4 choices) and then rank is automatically 2

theorem(s).name: Similarity preserves rank and polynomial identities
theorem(s).role: justify replacing $B$ by $\bar B$ for both rank and $B^2=0$

theorem(s).name: Centralizer of a $2\times2$ Jordan block
theorem(s).role: $\mathrm{Cent}(J_2)$ consists of upper-triangular Toeplitz matrices; used both for describing $C(J)$ and for counting $\mathrm{Cent}(M)$ when $M$ is nonzero nilpotent

theorem(s).name: Nonzero nilpotent $2\times2$ matrices have rank 1 and square to zero
theorem(s).role: simplifies the classification of $M^2=0$ over $\mathbb F_2$

theorem(s).name: Row-reduction over $\mathbb F_2$
theorem(s).role: compute rank of a small explicit $4\times4$ matrix in the canonical case

critical_trick(s): Use commutation with $J_2$ to force Toeplitz block structure for $C(J)$.
Apply a permutation similarity to obtain $\bar B=\begin{pmatrix}M&N\\0&M\end{pmatrix}$ where $M,N$ are free $2\times2$ matrices.
Compute $\bar B^2$ and exploit characteristic 2 to reduce $MN+NM=0$ to $MN=NM$.
Split into $M=0$ and $M\neq0$; count $N$ in each case and evaluate rank.

title: Count rank-2 symmetric idempotents with zero diagonal in M4(F2)

identity.short_name: Count rank-2 symmetric idempotents with zero diagonal in M4(F2)

subdomain(s): symmetric bilinear forms and orthogonality over F2

problem_spec.summary: Count 4×4 matrices over F2 that are idempotent and symmetric of rank 2, with the additional constraint that all diagonal entries are zero. Interpret A as an orthogonal projection with respect to the standard dot product. Show the diagonal constraint forces the image plane to lie in the even-weight hyperplane, then count the nondegenerate 2-planes in that hyperplane by excluding those containing the radical line.
givens: Work in M4(F2) with the standard dot product on V = F2^4.
Constraints: A^2 = A, A = A^T, rank(A) = 2.
Additional constraint: all diagonal entries of A are zero (a11=a22=a33=a44=0).
ask: Determine Q = #{A in M4(F2) : A^2=A, A=A^T, rank(A)=2, diag(A)=0}.

parameter(s).name: n
parameter(s).purpose: ambient dimension (this instance uses n=4)

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: base field size (this family targets q=2)

parameter(s).name: r
parameter(s).purpose: target rank of the idempotent (this instance uses r=2)

parameter(s).name: diagonal_constraint
parameter(s).purpose: adds a parity/isotropy constraint via the diagonal of A

parameter(s).name: symmetry_form
parameter(s).purpose: defines the meaning of "symmetric" and orthogonality

invariant(s).name: idempotent_is_projection
invariant(s).statement: A^2=A implies V = Im(A) ⊕ Ker(A), and A acts as identity on Im(A) and zero on Ker(A).

invariant(s).name: symmetry_implies_orthogonality
invariant(s).statement: A=A^T (self-adjoint) implies Im(A) ⟂ Ker(A) for the standard dot product. With dim(Im)=r and dim(Ker)=n-r, this forces Ker(A)=Im(A)^⊥ whenever Im(A) is nondegenerate.

invariant(s).name: diagonal_zero_forces_even_weight_image_for_rank2
invariant(s).statement: For standard basis vectors e_i, the diagonal entry a_ii equals <Ae_i,e_i> = <Ae_i,Ae_i>. If diag(A)=0 then each Ae_i is isotropic, so Im(A) is spanned by isotropic vectors. In rank 2 over F2 this implies <w,w>=0 for all w in Im(A), i.e. Im(A) lies in the even-weight hyperplane E.

invariant(s).name: even_weight_hyperplane_has_radical_line
invariant(s).statement: In V=F2^4, E={x: x1+x2+x3+x4=0} is 3-dimensional and the restriction of dot to E has radical spanned by r=(1,1,1,1). A 2-plane W ⊆ E is nondegenerate iff r ∉ W.

essential_assumption(s): The bilinear form used for symmetry is the standard dot product on F2^4.
For rank 2, admissible images W must be nondegenerate so that V = W ⊕ W^⊥ and the orthogonal projection is defined.
The diagonal constraint is interpreted in the fixed standard basis (so "diag(A)=0" is a concrete algebraic constraint).
uniqueness_certificate(s): Map A ↦ W=Im(A). For symmetric idempotents of rank 2, Ker(A)=W^⊥ and A is uniquely the projection onto W along W^⊥. The added constraint diag(A)=0 is equivalent to W ⊆ E (even-weight hyperplane). Thus Q equals the number of 2-planes W ⊆ E avoiding the radical line of dot|_E, a finite count, so Q is uniquely determined.

technique(s).name: reduce_matrix_count_to_subspace_count
technique(s).purpose: replace counting matrices by counting admissible image planes W with kernel forced to be W^⊥

technique(s).name: translate_diagonal_constraint_to_image_property
technique(s).purpose: convert the diagonal constraint into an isotropy/parity constraint on Im(A)

technique(s).name: compute_radical_of_even_weight_subspace
technique(s).purpose: identify the radical line of the restricted form on E to characterize degeneracy of planes in E

technique(s).name: count_planes_avoiding_radical
technique(s).purpose: count 2-planes in a 3D F2-space not containing a fixed line via Gaussian binomial coefficients

technique(s).name: reconstruct_projection_and_conclude
technique(s).purpose: use uniqueness of the orthogonal projection to equate plane_count with the number of matrices

theorem(s).name: projection_decomposition
theorem(s).role: idempotent \Rightarrow V decomposes as image ⊕ kernel

theorem(s).name: self_adjoint_image_kernel_orthogonality
theorem(s).role: symmetric operator \Rightarrow Im(A) ⟂ Ker(A), hence Ker(A)=Im(A)^⊥ in the rank-2 nondegenerate case

theorem(s).name: diagonal_pairing_identity
theorem(s).role: for the standard dot product, a_ii = <Ae_i,e_i>, and for self-adjoint idempotents also equals <Ae_i,Ae_i>

theorem(s).name: Gaussian_binomial_subspace_counting
theorem(s).role: counts k-dimensional subspaces of an n-dimensional F2-space, and counts those containing a fixed subspace

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: classify linear operators by invariant subspaces (image/kernel) and count admissible subspaces under extra constraints

critical_trick(s): Convert diagonal constraints into isotropy constraints on images of basis vectors via the dot product.
Reduce counting matrices to counting 2-planes inside the even-weight hyperplane and exclude planes containing the radical line.
Use the uniqueness of the orthogonal projection once W and W^⊥ are fixed.

title: dim(ker(ad_{J^2}^8) ∩ im(ad_{J^2})) on 20x20 matrices

identity.short_name: dim(ker(ad_{J^2}^8) ∩ im(ad_{J^2})) on 20x20 matrices

problem_spec.summary: Let J be the n×n Jordan shift, take N=J^d, and consider the commutator operator T=ad_N on V=M_n(C). Compute dim(ker(T^k) ∩ im(T)) by (i) finding the Jordan type of N=J^d, (ii) reducing T via block decomposition to a direct sum of smaller commutator maps, and (iii) summing the blockwise contributions min(k,b-1).
givens: J is the 20×20 Jordan-shift matrix with J_{i,i+1}=1 and other entries 0.
d = 2 and N = J^d = J^2.
T(M)=NM-MN (commutator operator ad_N).
S = ker(T^k) ∩ im(T).
ask: Compute dimS = dim_C(S).

parameter(s).name: n
parameter(s).purpose: ambient matrix size (here 20).

parameter(s).name: d
parameter(s).purpose: power of the Jordan shift used to define N=J^d (here d=2), changing the Jordan type of N.

parameter(s).name: k
parameter(s).purpose: kernel power in ker(T^k) (here 8).

invariant(s).name: Jordan_type_of_J_power
invariant(s).statement: For J the size-n Jordan shift, N=J^d splits C^n into d invariant chains indexed by residues mod d, giving Jordan blocks whose sizes differ by at most 1. For (n,d)=(20,2), N has Jordan type (10,10), so N is similar to diag(J10,J10).

invariant(s).name: blockwise_commutator_direct_sum
invariant(s).statement: If N=diag(N1,N2) is block diagonal, then ad_N on M_{n1+n2} acts blockwise: ad_N([[A,B],[C,D]]) = [[ad_{N1}(A), N1B-BN2],[N2C-CN1, ad_{N2}(D)]]. When N1=N2, all four blocks are governed by the same commutator operator, so ad_N is a direct sum of four copies.

invariant(s).name: Jordan_blocks_of_ad_regular_nilpotent_size_m
invariant(s).statement: For a single Jordan block J_m in M_m(C), ad_{J_m} on M_m(C) is nilpotent with Jordan-block sizes 1,3,5,...,2m-1 (each once).

invariant(s).name: intersection_dim_on_single_Jordan_block
invariant(s).statement: For a nilpotent Jordan block of size b, dim(ker(N^k) ∩ im(N)) = min(k, b-1).

essential_assumption(s): Work over an algebraically closed field of characteristic 0 (C) so Jordan decomposition and dimension arguments apply cleanly.
J is the standard Jordan shift (one Jordan block) so its powers have the described chain decomposition.
Dimensions are computed in the full matrix space M_n(C), and all decompositions are linear direct sums.

uniqueness_certificate(s).statement: The Jordan type of N=J^d is uniquely determined by (n,d); for (20,2) it is (10,10). This forces a block-diagonal similarity N ~ diag(J10,J10) and hence a canonical decomposition ad_N ≅ ad_{J10}^{⊕4}. The Jordan-block multiset of ad_{J10} is uniquely {1,3,...,19}, and the contribution of each block to dim(ker(T^k)∩im(T)) is uniquely min(k,b-1). Therefore dimS is a uniquely determined integer.

technique(s).name: parity_chain_similarity_for_J_squared
technique(s).purpose: compute the Jordan type of N=J^2 and exhibit an explicit basis giving N ~ diag(J10,J10).
outputs: block diagonal form of N with two size-10 Jordan blocks

technique(s).name: block_matrix_decomposition_of_commutator
inputs: N in block diagonal form and block decomposition of M_20
technique(s).purpose: reduce ad_N to a direct sum of smaller commutator maps on 10×10 blocks.

technique(s).name: invoke_ad_regular_nilpotent_Jordan_spectrum
technique(s).purpose: identify Jordan-block sizes 1,3,...,19 for the smaller commutator operator.

technique(s).name: compute_intersection_dim_blockwise
technique(s).purpose: compute dim(ker(T0^k)∩im(T0)) as Σ min(k,b-1) over blocks.

technique(s).name: scale_by_direct_sum_multiplicity
technique(s).purpose: multiply the per-copy dimension by 4 to obtain dimS.

theorem(s).name: Similarity preserves kernel/image dimensions
theorem(s).role: permits replacing N by a similar block diagonal form.

theorem(s).name: Jordan-block description of powers of a Jordan shift
theorem(s).role: justifies N=J^2 has Jordan type (10,10) via invariant parity chains.

theorem(s).name: Jordan block kernel-image fact
theorem(s).role: on a size-b nilpotent Jordan block, dim(ker(N^k) ∩ im(N)) = min(k,b-1).

theorem(s).name: Regular nilpotent adjoint Jordan spectrum
theorem(s).role: ad_{J_m} on M_m(C) has Jordan blocks 1,3,5,...,2m-1.

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Decompose the space into invariant direct sums so kernels/images and dimensions add blockwise.

critical_trick(s): Replace N=J^2 by a similar block diagonal matrix diag(J10,J10) using the parity-chain basis.
Observe that commutation with a block diagonal matrix acts blockwise, giving ad_N as a 4-fold direct sum.
Reduce the dimension computation to the known regular-nilpotent ad spectrum in size 10 and a blockwise min-sum.

title: Commuting square-zero 3x3 matrices over F2

identity.short_name: Commuting square-zero 3x3 matrices over F2

topic_path: Counting commuting nilpotent matrices via Jordan form

problem_spec.summary: Count ordered pairs (A,B) of 3x3 matrices over F2 such that A^2=B^2=0 and AB=BA.
givens: Work in M_3(F_2).
AB = BA.
ask: Determine the number N of ordered pairs (A,B) satisfying the conditions.

parameter(s).name: n
parameter(s).purpose: matrix size (here n=3)

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: base field size (here q=2)

parameter(s).name: k
parameter(s).purpose: nilpotency exponent constraint A^k=0, B^k=0 (here k=2)

invariant(s).name: square_zero_similarity_partition
invariant(s).statement: A^2=0 implies all Jordan blocks have size \leq2. In n=3 this forces A to be either 0 (partition (1,1,1)) or of Jordan type (2,1) (rank 1).

invariant(s).name: centralizer_invariance_under_conjugation
invariant(s).statement: If A and A' are similar, conjugation gives a bijection between {B : AB=BA and B^2=0} and {B' : A'B'=B'A' and (B')^2=0}. Thus the commuting square-zero count depends only on the similarity type of A.

invariant(s).name: rank_one_outer_product_characterization_over_F2
invariant(s).statement: Over F2, every nonzero rank-1 matrix is uniquely u w^T with u,w \neq 0, and (u w^T)^2=0 iff w^T u = 0.

essential_assumption(s): Jordan classification applies over F2 for nilpotent operators.
Over F2, scalar rescaling is trivial, making rank-1 outer-product representations unique.
Conjugation by GL_3(F2) preserves commuting relations and the equation X^2=0.

uniqueness_certificate(s).name: finite_check_certificate
uniqueness_certificate(s).statement: The set M_3(F2)^2 is finite, so the number of solutions is a uniquely defined integer.

uniqueness_certificate(s).name: conjugacy_invariance_certificate
uniqueness_certificate(s).statement: All nonzero square-zero matrices have the single Jordan type (2,1); conjugation invariance implies a constant number of commuting square-zero partners for each such matrix. Hence the total count is uniquely determined from one representative computation.

technique(s).name: classify_square_zero_by_Jordan_type
technique(s).purpose: reduce possible similarity types to 0 and Jordan type (2,1)
outputs: dichotomy A=0 or A similar to E_12

technique(s).name: count_nonzero_square_zero_via_rank1_outer_product
technique(s).purpose: count nonzero A with A^2=0 by counting pairs (u,w) with w^T u = 0
outputs: number of nonzero square-zero matrices equals 21; total square-zero equals 22

technique(s).name: conjugacy_invariance_reduce_to_representative
inputs: similarity class of A of type (2,1)
technique(s).purpose: reduce counting commuting B to a single canonical representative

technique(s).name: compute_centralizer_and_square_zero_subset
technique(s).purpose: compute the centralizer Z(A0) and count square-zero elements inside it
outputs: Z(A0) has form [[a,b,c],[0,a,0],[0,d,e]]; square-zero elements count is 6

technique(s).name: sum_cases
technique(s).purpose: assemble total ordered-pair count

theorem(s).name: Jordan Canonical Form for nilpotent operators
theorem(s).role: identifies square-zero matrices with partitions having all parts \leq2

theorem(s).name: conjugation transports centralizers
theorem(s).role: justifies invariance of the commuting-square-zero count across a similarity class

theorem(s).name: rank-1 outer product identity
theorem(s).role: (u w^T)^2 = u (w^T u) w^T, giving the condition for square-zero

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: classification up to similarity + counting via invariants

critical_trick(s): Reduce A^2=0 to the two similarity types 0 and (2,1).
Count nonzero square-zero matrices via rank-1 outer products u w^T with w^T u = 0.
Use conjugacy invariance to compute commuting square-zero B by analyzing one representative's centralizer.

library(s).reason: brute-force enumeration of all 3x3 matrices over F2 for verification

library(s).name: numpy
library(s).reason: fast matrix multiplication mod 2 for brute-force verification

task(s).reason: enumerate all 512 matrices A,B, filter A^2=B^2=0 and AB=BA, and confirm N=148

task(s).name: verify_centralizer_count
task(s).reason: for a representative A0, enumerate all B with B^2=0 and BA0=A0B to confirm the value 6

title: Count trace-1 idempotent 2x2 matrices over Z/20Z

identity.short_name: Count trace-1 idempotent 2x2 matrices over Z/20Z

subdomain(s): Chinese Remainder Theorem for matrix rings
lifting solutions modulo prime powers (nilpotent ideals)

problem_spec.summary: Count idempotent matrices in M2(Z/nZ) satisfying a specified trace congruence. Use CRT to split n into coprime factors and multiply the counts from each factor ring. Over the field factor, use rank/trace classification. Over the prime-power factor, lift idempotents through a square-zero ideal and track the trace of lifts.
givens: E is idempotent if E^2 = E in R.
Trace condition: tr(E) \equiv 1 (mod 20).
ask: Compute the total number N of idempotent matrices E in M2(Z/20Z) with tr(E) \equiv 1 (mod 20).

parameter(s).name: modulus_n
parameter(s).purpose: determines the base ring Z/nZ (here 20)

parameter(s).name: factorization
parameter(s).purpose: enables CRT decomposition (here 20=4 \cdot 5)

parameter(s).name: matrix_size
parameter(s).purpose: size of matrices in M_k(Z/nZ)

parameter(s).name: trace_target
parameter(s).purpose: specified trace congruence (here 1 mod 20)

invariant(s).name: CRT_matrix_ring_product
invariant(s).statement: If n=ab with gcd(a,b)=1 then Z/nZ ≅ Z/aZ × Z/bZ, hence M2(Z/nZ) ≅ M2(Z/aZ) × M2(Z/bZ). Idempotents correspond componentwise, so counts multiply.

invariant(s).name: trace_splits_under_CRT
invariant(s).statement: Under CRT, tr(E) mod n corresponds to (tr(E_a) mod a, tr(E_b) mod b). Thus tr(E)\equivt (mod n) is equivalent to the pair of congruences tr(E_a)\equivt (mod a) and tr(E_b)\equivt (mod b).

invariant(s).name: field_idempotent_rank_trace
invariant(s).statement: Over a field F_q, idempotent matrices have eigenvalues in {0,1}; hence trace equals rank. For 2×2, trace 1 ⇔ rank 1.

invariant(s).name: lifting_through_square_zero_ideal
invariant(s).statement: For the reduction M2(Z/4Z)\toM2(F2), the kernel ideal I=2 \cdot M2(Z/4Z) satisfies I^2=0. Idempotent lifting reduces to a linear equation in the correction term, and the number of lifts equals the size of a kernel of a linear map depending only on the mod-2 idempotent class.

essential_assumption(s): Use CRT with coprime factors 4 and 5: Z/20Z ≅ Z/4Z × Z/5Z.
In the lifting step from mod 2 to mod 4, the kernel ideal squares to 0 inside M2(Z/4Z).
Over fields, similarity classification by rank applies to idempotents and trace equals rank.
uniqueness_certificate(s): CRT gives a bijection between idempotents in M2(Z/20Z) and pairs of idempotents in the two factors, and the trace congruence splits componentwise. Hence the total count is the product of two uniquely determined factor counts. The field-factor count is uniquely determined by rank/trace classification, and the prime-power factor count is uniquely determined by the kernel size of the linearized lift map (a conjugacy invariant), giving a unique integer N.

technique(s).name: split_modulus_by_CRT
technique(s).purpose: reduce counting in M2(Z/20Z) to independent counts in M2(Z/4Z) and M2(Z/5Z)

technique(s).name: count_trace_one_idempotents_over_field
technique(s).purpose: count 2×2 idempotents over F_q with trace 1 (rank-1 projections)

technique(s).name: lift_idempotents_mod2_to_mod4_with_trace
technique(s).purpose: lift rank-1 idempotents through a square-zero ideal and show all lifts have trace 1 mod 4

technique(s).name: multiply_factor_counts
technique(s).purpose: obtain the final count in M2(Z/20Z)

theorem(s).name: Chinese_Remainder_Theorem
theorem(s).role: ring decomposition for coprime moduli and product behavior of idempotents and traces

theorem(s).name: idempotents_over_a_field_are_projections
theorem(s).role: similarity classes determined by rank; trace equals rank

theorem(s).name: linearization_of_idempotent_lift_in_square_zero_extension
theorem(s).role: for I^2=0, (e+x)^2=e+x becomes a linear equation in x, so lift counts are kernel sizes

theorem(s).name: conjugacy_invariance_of_kernel_size
theorem(s).role: kernel size of the lift-linear map depends only on the conjugacy class of the mod-p idempotent

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: reduce ring counting by CRT, then classify idempotents by rank/trace and lift through nilpotent kernels

critical_trick(s): Use CRT: M2(Z/20Z) ≅ M2(Z/4Z)×M2(F5), and split the trace condition accordingly.
Over F5, trace-1 idempotents are exactly rank-1 projections; count them via ordered complementary lines.
Over Z/4Z, reduce mod 2 and lift rank-1 idempotents through a square-zero ideal; compute kernel size on a representative.

library(s).name: itertools
library(s).reason: brute-force enumeration in M2(Z/20Z) for verification (20^4 = 160000)

library(s).name: math
library(s).reason: basic arithmetic and counting checks

task(s).name: brute_force_verify_mod20_trace1
task(s).reason: enumerate all 2×2 matrices over Z/20Z and count those with E^2=E and tr(E)=1

task(s).name: verify_CRT_factor_counts_with_trace
task(s).reason: count trace-1 idempotents mod 4 and mod 5 independently and confirm product equals mod 20 count

task(s).name: verify_lift_kernel_sizes
task(s).reason: for each mod-2 rank-1 idempotent, solve the linearized lift equation and confirm 4 lifts with trace 1 mod 4

title: Count bounded integer 2x2 matrices with exp(\piM)=-I (mod 1000)

identity.short_name: Count bounded integer 2x2 matrices with exp(\piM)=-I (mod 1000)

subdomain(s): counting via divisor pairs and sum-of-two-squares

problem_spec.summary: Count integer 2×2 matrices with bounded entries for which every solution of u'(t)=Mu(t) is \pi-antiperiodic (u(t+\pi)=-u(t)), equivalently the monodromy satisfies e^{\piM}=-I, and report the count modulo 1000.
givens: B=10 is a fixed bound on entries.
M is a 2×2 integer matrix with |entries(M)| \leq B.
u'(t)=Mu(t) is the associated linear ODE system on ℝ^2.
Antiperiodicity requirement: for all solutions u, u(t+\pi)=-u(t) for all real t.
ask: Compute N mod 1000, where N is the number of such \pi-antiperiodic matrices M.

parameter(s).name: B
parameter(s).purpose: controls the finite search box for integer matrix entries (here 10)

parameter(s).name: half_period
parameter(s).purpose: fixes the antiperiodicity constraint e^{\piM}=-I

parameter(s).name: dimension_n
parameter(s).purpose: enables 2×2 trace/determinant simplifications

parameter(s).name: modulus
parameter(s).purpose: postprocesses the exact count into a residue class

invariant(s).name: antimonodromy_minus_identity
invariant(s).statement: every solution is \pi-antiperiodic iff e^{\piM}=-I

invariant(s).name: spectral_constraint_odd_imag
invariant(s).statement: if e^{\piM}=-I then every eigenvalue λ satisfies e^{\piλ}=-1, hence λ\in i(2ℤ+1)

invariant(s).name: traceless_scalar_square_form
invariant(s).statement: in 2×2, eigenvalues {im,-im} with odd m force tr(M)=0 and M^2=-m^2 I

invariant(s).name: traceless_parametrization
invariant(s).statement: tr(M)=0 implies M=[[a,b],[c,-a]] with a,b,c\inℤ and bounds inherited from |entries|\leqB

invariant(s).name: scalar_square_equation
invariant(s).statement: for M=[[a,b],[c,-a]], one has M^2=(a^2+bc)I, so M^2=-m^2 I iff bc=-(a^2+m^2)

essential_assumption(s): M has integer entries and satisfies |entries(M)| \leq B.
Antiperiodicity is required for every solution (not just existence of one).
Standard matrix exponential solution theory for constant-coefficient linear systems applies.
uniqueness_certificate(s): finite-check certificate: the candidate set is finite (size (2B+1)^4), so N is uniquely determined
structural certificate: e^{\piM}=-I forces tr(M)=0 and M^2=-m^2I with odd m\geq1, reducing to bc=-(a^2+m^2)
postprocessing certificate: N mod 1000 is a deterministic function of the unique integer N

technique(s).name: fundamental_matrix_monodromy
technique(s).purpose: convert "all solutions are \pi-antiperiodic" into a matrix identity on e^{\piM}

technique(s).name: spectral_mapping_for_exponential
technique(s).purpose: force eigenvalues into i(2ℤ+1) and deduce trace zero in 2×2

technique(s).name: 2x2_traceless_parametrization
technique(s).purpose: reduce to M=[[a,b],[c,-a]]

technique(s).name: compute_square_as_scalar_identity
technique(s).purpose: derive M^2=(a^2+bc)I and translate M^2=-m^2I into bc=-(a^2+m^2)

technique(s).name: divisor_pair_count_under_bounds
technique(s).purpose: count (b,c) solving bc=-K with bounded entries via divisor pairs

technique(s).name: sum_over_K_with_representation_multiplicity
technique(s).purpose: aggregate contributions over admissible K

technique(s).name: modular_postprocessing
technique(s).purpose: output N mod 1000

theorem(s).name: matrix_exponential_solution_formula
theorem(s).role: solutions satisfy u(t)=e^{tM}u(0), so \pi-antiperiodicity for all solutions is e^{\piM}=-I

theorem(s).name: spectral_mapping_theorem_for_matrix_exponential
theorem(s).role: eigenvalues of e^{\piM} are e^{\piλ}, forcing e^{\piλ}=-1

theorem(s).name: conjugate_pair_eigenvalues_for_real_matrices
theorem(s).role: nonreal eigenvalues occur in conjugate pairs, giving {im,-im} in 2×2

theorem(s).name: cayley_hamilton_specialization_2x2_traceless
theorem(s).role: in the traceless case, M^2=-det(M)I, consistent with M^2=(a^2+bc)I

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: eigenvalues_control_matrix_functions and reduce bounded counting to finite Diophantine constraints

critical_trick(s): Replace "all solutions \pi-antiperiodic" by the antimonodromy identity e^{\piM}=-I.
Use spectral mapping to force eigenvalues to be odd imaginary integers, hence tr(M)=0 and M^2=-m^2I with odd m.
Convert M^2=-m^2I to bc=-(a^2+m^2) under traceless parametrization, then count via bounded divisor pairs and sum-of-two-squares.

library(s).reason: brute-force enumeration of bounded integer matrices or (a,b,c,m) tuples for verification

title: Matrix-polynomial invariants with defined eigenvalue symbols and mod-1000 output

identity.short_name: Matrix-polynomial invariants with defined eigenvalue symbols and mod-1000 output

subdomain(s): traces and determinants as eigenvalue invariants

problem_spec.summary: Given a fixed 4x4 integer matrix A, define matrices B,C,D through explicit matrix polynomials, with an integer parameter Phi defined by a floor involving det(B), tr(C), and det(A). Define mu_i, sigma_i, rho_i as the eigenvalues of B,C,D (with algebraic multiplicity), and compute an aggregate S built from sums of these eigenvalues (and squares), then output S mod 1000.
givens: Let I=I_4.
C = B^2 - tr(B) \cdot I.
Phi = floor(det(B)/(tr(C)+det(A))).
D = A^3 - Phi A^2 + Phi^2 A - Phi^3 I.
mu_1..mu_4 are the eigenvalues of B (algebraic multiplicity).
sigma_1..sigma_4 are the eigenvalues of C (algebraic multiplicity).
rho_1..rho_4 are the eigenvalues of D (algebraic multiplicity).
ask: Compute S = (sum_{i=1}^4 mu_i^2) + (sum_{i=1}^4 sigma_i) + (sum_{i=1}^4 rho_i) + det(A) (mod 1000), reported as a three-digit string.

parameter(s).name: dimension
parameter(s).purpose: fixes trace(I)=4 and the number of eigenvalues counted

parameter(s).name: A_entries
parameter(s).purpose: specifies the concrete matrix A

parameter(s).name: poly_B
parameter(s).purpose: defines B=p(A)

parameter(s).name: poly_C
parameter(s).purpose: defines C and links its trace to tr(B^2) and tr(B)

parameter(s).name: floor_rule_for_Phi
parameter(s).purpose: defines an integer parameter from invariants

parameter(s).name: poly_D
parameter(s).purpose: defines D and hence rho_i

parameter(s).name: modulus
parameter(s).purpose: output reduction

invariant(s).name: eigenvalue_symbol_definition
invariant(s).statement: The symbols mu_i, sigma_i, rho_i denote the eigenvalues of B, C, D respectively, each counted with algebraic multiplicity (so sums over i equal traces).

invariant(s).name: spectral_mapping_for_matrix_polynomials
invariant(s).statement: If lambda_i are eigenvalues of A, then mu_i=p(lambda_i) are eigenvalues of B=p(A), and rho_i=lambda_i^3-Phi lambda_i^2+Phi^2 lambda_i-Phi^3 are eigenvalues of D.

invariant(s).name: sigma_sum_equals_trace_C
invariant(s).statement: sum_{i=1}^4 sigma_i = tr(C) = tr(B^2)-4 tr(B).

title: Rank and kernel of the 5x5 (i-j)^3 skew-symmetric Toeplitz matrix

identity.short_name: Rank and kernel of the 5x5 (i-j)^3 skew-symmetric Toeplitz matrix

topic_path: rank and nullspace of structured matrices

problem_spec.summary: Determine the rank and a nullspace generator for a fixed finite skew-symmetric Toeplitz matrix whose entries are an odd power of the signed index difference (i-j)^p. Use skew-symmetry to bound rank, a nonzero 4x4 minor to certify rank, and a finite-difference identity to construct a kernel vector.
givens: A is a 5x5 matrix with entries A_{ij} = (i-j)^3 for i,j in {1,2,3,4,5}.
Computations are over a field of characteristic 0 (e.g., ℚ or ℝ).
ask: Compute rank(A) and exhibit a nonzero vector v with Av=0.

parameter(s).name: n
parameter(s).purpose: matrix dimension (here n=5)

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: odd exponent in signed distance power (i-j)^p (here p=3)

parameter(s).name: distance_type
parameter(s).purpose: uses (i-j) rather than |i-j|, forcing skew-symmetry when p is odd

parameter(s).name: base_field_characteristic
parameter(s).purpose: ensures skew-symmetric rank parity and Pfaffian determinant identities hold

invariant(s).name: toeplitz_structure
invariant(s).statement: A_{ij} depends only on (i-j), so A is Toeplitz.

invariant(s).name: skew_symmetry_for_odd_p
invariant(s).statement: For odd p, (i-j)^p = -(j-i)^p, hence A^T = -A.

invariant(s).name: even_rank_constraint
invariant(s).statement: Over characteristic \neq2, any skew-symmetric matrix has even rank; in odd dimension its determinant is 0.

invariant(s).name: polynomial_sequence_rows
invariant(s).statement: For fixed i, the row entries A_{i,j}=(i-j)^3 form a degree-3 polynomial sequence in j.

invariant(s).name: finite_difference_annihilator_vector
invariant(s).statement: The 4th forward difference of any degree \leq3 polynomial is 0, yielding a kernel vector v=(1,-4,6,-4,1)^T satisfying Av=0 for n=5.

essential_assumption(s): Indices i,j range over consecutive integers 1..n.
Rank and nullspace are computed over a field with characteristic not equal to 2 (here characteristic 0).

uniqueness_certificate(s).statement: Skew-symmetry in odd dimension forces rank(A) \leq n-1, and a nonzero (n-1)x(n-1) minor certifies rank(A) \geq n-1. Together these force rank(A)=n-1 uniquely (here rank=4).
Once rank(A)=4 for a 5x5 matrix, nullity(A)=1 by rank-nullity, so any nonzero kernel vector spans ker(A), giving a unique kernel line.

technique(s).name: explicit_matrix_expansion
technique(s).purpose: write A as an explicit integer matrix.

technique(s).name: skew_symmetry_rank_parity_bound
technique(s).purpose: constrain possible ranks (even rank; odd-size determinant 0).

technique(s).name: nonzero_minor_certificate_via_pfaffian
technique(s).purpose: certify rank(A) \geq 4 by showing det(minor) \neq 0 using Pfaffian^2 formula.

technique(s).name: conclude_rank_from_bounds
technique(s).purpose: pin rank exactly.

technique(s).name: finite_difference_kernel_construction
technique(s).purpose: build a null vector using vanishing 4th finite differences for cubics.

technique(s).name: rank_nullity_finalize_kernel
inputs: rank(A) and a nonzero kernel vector
technique(s).purpose: deduce ker(A) is 1D and identify its span.

theorem(s).name: Elementary row operations preserve rank
theorem(s).role: Justifies rank certification via minors or row-reduction.

theorem(s).name: Skew-symmetric rank parity and odd-size determinant
theorem(s).role: Over char \neq2, skew-symmetric matrices have even rank; odd dimension implies determinant 0.

theorem(s).name: Pfaffian determinant identity (4x4 case)
theorem(s).role: For 4x4 skew-symmetric matrices, det = (pfaffian)^2.

theorem(s).name: Finite difference vanishing
theorem(s).role: The k-th forward difference of a polynomial of degree <k is identically 0.

theorem(s).name: Rank-Nullity Theorem
theorem(s).role: nullity = n - rank, so a nonzero null vector spans when nullity=1.

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Linear independence and rank via certified minors.
Polynomial sequences annihilated by finite differences.

critical_trick(s): Use skew-symmetry (odd size) to restrict rank to \leq4.
Prove rank\geq4 by a nonzero 4×4 principal minor, computed cleanly via a Pfaffian.
Construct a kernel vector using the vanishing 4th finite difference for cubic sequences.

library(s).reason: optional floating-point cross-check of rank via SVD (not required for exactness).

task(s).reason: compute rank(A) and a basis for ker(A) exactly.

task(s).name: minor_determinant_check
task(s).reason: verify det of the 4x4 principal minor is nonzero.

title: Echo Ruler Weak Quartet Extremal Mini Staircase Skew Combiner

identity.short_name: Max-probe sum extraction \to weak-quartet extremal (N as digit-sum) \to mini staircase bindings

subdomain(s): adversarial identification via discrete distance oracle
rook placements / bipartite matchings on Ferrers (staircase) boards
M is the minimum number of probes required to determine (u,v,w) in the worst case.
Recorded replies (from using M probes): R(8,8,8)=32, R(2,3,5)=4, R(2,3,3)=0.
Friendship relation is a simple undirected graph G on N vertices.
Weak quartet: a 4-vertex subset inducing exactly one friendship edge.
Q(N): maximum possible number of weak quartets over all graphs on N vertices.
k: number of connected components (cliques) in an extremal configuration attaining Q(N).
Binding of size r: set of r disjoint Warden-Seal pairs (a matching).
Legality constraint: Wi may only bind to Sj with j \leq 2i-1.
A(7,r): number of legal bindings of size r.
Define r := M + w - k and V := A(7,r) - Q(N).
Oracle replies are exact and deterministic functions of (u,v,w) and the probe.
All variables u,v,w,a,b,c are integers; absolute value parity property is used.
The auditor's goal for M is worst-case guaranteed identification over all 9^3 triples.
Friendship graph is simple undirected (no loops, no multi-edges).
A weak quartet is defined via the induced subgraph on exactly four distinct vertices.
Bindings are matchings: Warden-Seal pairs are disjoint in both wardens and seals.
Legality constraint uses the total order on seals and the inequality j \leq 2i-1.
Composite parameter r must satisfy 0 \leq r \leq 7 for A(7,r) to be nonzero.
V is an integer difference of uniquely determined integers A(7,r) and Q(N), hence unique.
Linear system for u,v,w with a unique solution
Graph objective: count of 4-sets with exactly one edge
Extremal graphs are disjoint unions of cliques
Objective q as a function of clique sizes
Optimal sizes differ by \leq 1 for fixed k
Small-N comparison for N derived from digits
Ferrers board with row i length 2i-1
Use parity/range to get a sharp information lower bound M\geq3 and read off M=3 from the 3-probe log.
Exploit a zero-reply probe to force all three absolute-value terms to vanish, giving exact pairwise sums and an immediate unique solve for (u,v,w).
Encode N as the digit-sum u+v+w to push the extremal weak-quartet stage into a small-N regime where balanced clique partitions can be compared directly.
Alter the stage-coupling to r = M + w - k so that the staircase matching parameter is small and A(7,r) remains tractable.
Apply the rook-number closed form A(n,r)=C(n,r)^2 r! to finish in closed form.
