title: 5-step unipotent pulse with 6 random sign schedules

identity.short_name: 5-step unipotent pulse with 6 random sign schedules

problem_spec.summary: A T-step affine recursion in R^n is driven by a unipotent matrix exp(kA) where A is a nilpotent Jordan block. A random sign schedule is chosen uniformly from a fixed finite set. Closed-cycle existence reduces to a single scalar closure constraint on the last coordinate, because Im(I-exp(TkA)) = Im(A) for k\neq0. The probability that a schedule is admissible is therefore determined by matching a schedule-dependent signed sum to a polynomial d(k). Enforcing probability 1/2 forces exactly half the schedules, yielding one scalar equation in d(k), hence a quadratic in k with two real roots. Vieta's formulas then produce the required modular output.
givens: A is the 5x5 nilpotent Jordan block with ones on the superdiagonal (A^5=0).
The sign schedule epsilon = (ε0,ε1,ε2,ε3,ε4) is chosen uniformly from a fixed set Σ of 6 sign 5-tuples.
d_k is the quadratic polynomial d(k)=k^2-3k+1.
For t=0,1,2,3,4 the recursion is x_{t+1} = B_k x_t + (0,0,0,0,d_k+ε_t)^T in R^5.
A schedule is admissible at k if there exists x0 with x5=x0 (closed 5-pulse).
Constraint: k \neq 0.
Promise: exactly two real values k_1<k_2 satisfy Prob(k)=1/2.
ask: Determine k_1, k_2 from Prob(k)=1/2 and compute:

title: Random 3x3 ODE + polynomial-sum divisibility (n=7 variant)

identity.short_name: Random 3x3 ODE + polynomial-sum divisibility (n=7 variant)

subdomain(s): linear ODE systems and matrix exponentials
polynomial evaluation sums and modular divisibility

problem_spec.summary: Choose discrete random data S (defining a companion-like 3x3 matrix), a nonzero binary initial vector v for X'=A_S X, and a subset T defining a cubic q_T. The event requires a Wronskian-type determinant det[X,X',X''] to be nonzero for all t\geq0 and a separate polynomial-sum invariant Θ_T to be divisible by 6. Compute P(E) exactly and output a three-digit residue derived from the reduced fraction.
givens: S is chosen uniformly among 3-subsets of {1,2,3,4,5,6,7}, written S={s1<s2<s3}.
A_S is the 3x3 real matrix with top two rows [[0,1,0],[0,0,1]] and bottom row [(-1)^{s1}, s2-4, (-1)^{s3}].
v is chosen uniformly from {0,1}^3 \ {0}.
X(t) solves X'(t)=A_S X(t), X(0)=v.
T is chosen uniformly among 3-subsets of {0,1,2,3,4,5,6}.
q_T(z)=\prod_{t in T}(z-t) in Z[z].
Theta_T=\sum_{k=0}^4 q_T(k).
Event E: (i) W(t)\neq0 for all t\geq0, and (ii) 6 | Theta_T.
ask: Compute P(E)=m/n in lowest terms and output the zero-padded three-digit string for ((m+2n) mod 1000).

parameter(s).name: universe_size
parameter(s).purpose: size of the base set for S and T (here 7)

parameter(s).name: subset_size_S
parameter(s).purpose: size of S defining A_S (here 3)

parameter(s).name: matrix_template
parameter(s).purpose: maps S to A_S via parity signs and a centered shift beta=s2-4

parameter(s).name: initial_vector_distribution
parameter(s).purpose: chooses v uniformly from {0,1}^3\{0}

parameter(s).name: subset_size_T
parameter(s).purpose: size of T defining q_T (here 3)

parameter(s).name: evaluation_points
parameter(s).purpose: k=0..4 used in Theta_T

parameter(s).name: divisibility_modulus
parameter(s).purpose: modulus 6 for Theta_T condition

parameter(s).name: output_modulus
parameter(s).purpose: 1000 for final reduction

parameter(s).name: output_linear_form
parameter(s).purpose: postprocessing uses the linear form m+2n before reducing mod 1000

invariant(s).name: wronskian_reduction_to_static_determinant
invariant(s).statement: For X'=A_S X with X(t)=e^{tA_S}v, we have X^{(k)}(t)=A_S^k X(t), hence W(t)=det[X,AX,A^2X]=det(e^{tA_S}) \cdot det[v,Av,A^2v]=e^{t \cdot tr(A_S)} \cdot Delta_S(v). Therefore W(t)\neq0 for all t\geq0 iff Delta_S(v)\neq0.

invariant(s).name: event_independence_factorization
invariant(s).statement: (S,v) are independent of T, and the W-condition depends only on (S,v) while the Theta-condition depends only on T, so P(E)=P(Delta_S(v)\neq0) \cdot P(6|Theta_T).

invariant(s).name: theta_sum_closed_form
invariant(s).statement: Writing q_T(z)=z^3-s1 z^2+s2 z-s3, we have Theta_T=\sum_{k=0}^4 q_T(k)=100-30s1+10s2-5s3.

invariant(s).name: theta_mod6_structure
invariant(s).statement: From Theta_T=100-30s1+10s2-5s3, the mod-2 condition reduces to s3 even (i.e., T contains an even element). Mod 3, Theta_T \equiv 1+s2+s3, which depends only on the residues of elements of T modulo 3.

essential_assumption(s): The ODE solution exists uniquely for all t\geq0 (true for constant-coefficient linear systems).
Determinant and trace identities over reals apply: det(e^{tA})=e^{t tr(A)}.
Uniform distributions on S, v, and T are independent.
Theta_T is interpreted as an integer; divisibility is in Z.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The finite sample spaces have fixed sizes: |C(7,3)|=35 for S and T, and 7 for v. The W-condition reduces deterministically to Delta_S(v)\neq0, so counting good pairs (S,v) gives a unique rational. Separately, counting T with 6|Theta_T gives a unique rational. Independence fixes P(E) uniquely; lowest-terms reduction fixes (m,n) uniquely and thus the three-digit residue of (m+2n) mod 1000.

technique(s).name: wronskian_to_initial_data_determinant
technique(s).purpose: replace the infinite-time condition W(t)\neq0 for all t\geq0 by the finite check Delta_S(v)\neq0

technique(s).name: discrete_parameterization_of_A_S
technique(s).purpose: encode A_S by (alpha,beta,gamma) with alpha=(-1)^{s1}, beta=s2-4, gamma=(-1)^{s3}

technique(s).name: enumerate_zero_locus_over_binary_vectors
technique(s).purpose: evaluate Delta_S(v) on the 7 nonzero binary vectors and count vanishing cases

technique(s).name: expand_theta_via_symmetric_sums
technique(s).purpose: express Theta_T via symmetric sums and precomputed power sums over k=0..4, then reduce mod 6

technique(s).name: classify_T_by_residues_mod3
technique(s).purpose: count T satisfying the mod-3 component by residue-class patterns, then combine with the parity constraint

technique(s).name: multiply_probabilities_and_postprocess
technique(s).purpose: compute P(E), reduce to m/n, then output zero-padded ((m+2n) mod 1000)

theorem(s).role: X(t)=e^{tA}v for constant A

theorem(s).name: derivative_power_relation
theorem(s).role: X^{(k)}=A^k X for X'=AX

theorem(s).name: determinant_multiplicativity_and_trace_exponential
theorem(s).role: det(e^{tA})=e^{t tr(A)} and det(e^{tA}B)=det(e^{tA})det(B)

theorem(s).name: polynomial_from_roots_expansion
theorem(s).role: q_T(z)=\prod(z-t)=z^3-s1 z^2+s2 z-s3

theorem(s).name: CRT_style_mod6_check
theorem(s).role: 6|Theta ⇔ (2|Theta and 3|Theta)

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: reduce an analytic condition to a finite algebraic determinant, then do exact finite counting

critical_trick(s): Use W(t)=e^{t tr(A_S)}\Delta_S(v) to turn "for all t" into an algebraic determinant condition at t=0.
Count (S,v) by classifying A_S via discrete parameters (α,β,γ) and checking \Delta_S(v) on the 7 binary vectors.
Compute Θ_T mod 6 via symmetric-sum expansion and residue-pattern counting modulo 3, with a simple parity exclusion.
Multiply the independent probabilities, reduce to lowest terms, then compute the required three-digit residue of (m+2n) mod 1000.

title: Count integer (a,b) with P(1)=0 and exactly one root in (0,1)

identity.short_name: Count integer (a,b) with P(1)=0 and exactly one root in (0,1)

subdomain(s): polynomial real-root counting with parameter constraints
topic_path: parameter counting via convexity and anchored roots

problem_spec.summary: With integer parameters (a,b) in a box and constrained by P(1)=0, determine when a quintic has exactly one real root in (0,1) using a convex auxiliary function and a derivative-sign criterion, then count all lattice points satisfying the conditions.
givens: Integers a,b satisfy |a| \leq 6 and |b| \leq 6.
Anchoring constraint: P(1)=0 (equivalently a+b=-2).
The target interval is (0,1).
ask: Count ordered pairs (a,b) satisfying the givens such that P(x)=0 has exactly one real root in (0,1).

parameter(s).name: a
parameter(s).purpose: controls the cubic-term contribution and (via H'(1)=a+1) the location of the convex minimum relative to x=1

parameter(s).name: b
parameter(s).purpose: controls the vertical shift of the auxiliary convex function H

parameter(s).name: anchored_root_point
parameter(s).purpose: imposes the affine constraint P(1)=0, i.e. forces the line b=-a-2 in the (a,b)-lattice

parameter(s).name: interval
parameter(s).purpose: interval in which the unique root is required

parameter(s).name: transform_choice
parameter(s).purpose: converts root counting for P on (0,∞) to convex analysis for H

invariant(s).name: root_equivalence
invariant(s).statement: For x>0, P(x)=0 iff H(x)=P(x)/x^2=0.

invariant(s).name: strict_convexity
invariant(s).statement: H''(x)=6x+6/x^4>0 for x>0, so H is strictly convex on (0,∞).

invariant(s).name: anchored_value_at_one
invariant(s).statement: P(1)=a+b+2; the constraint P(1)=0 enforces H(1)=0.

invariant(s).name: derivative_at_one
invariant(s).statement: H'(1)=a+1, so the sign of a+1 determines whether the minimizer of H lies left or right of 1.

invariant(s).name: derivative_sign_criterion_for_root_left_of_one
invariant(s).statement: Under H(1)=0 and strict convexity, H has a (necessarily unique) root in (0,1) iff H'(1)>0.

essential_assumption(s): Consider x>0 when dividing by x^2.
Real analysis facts (strict convexity, monotonicity of H', IVT) apply on (0,∞).
The constraint P(1)=0 is enforced exactly in integers (a,b).
uniqueness_certificate(s): The condition P(1)=0 forces b=-a-2, reducing the parameter search to a finite set of integers a.
Strict convexity of H gives a rigid equivalence: existence of a root in (0,1) is determined solely by the sign of H'(1)=a+1.
Therefore the set of admissible (a,b) is uniquely determined by finitely many inequalities and has a unique count.

technique(s).name: convex_helper_function
technique(s).purpose: produce a strictly convex function whose zeros correspond to positive zeros of P

technique(s).name: anchored_root_reduction
technique(s).purpose: reduce the 2D lattice search to an affine line b=-a-2

technique(s).name: derivative_sign_classification_at_anchor
inputs: strict convexity of H and the anchored condition H(1)=0
technique(s).purpose: decide whether an additional root lies in (0,1) via the sign of H'(1)
outputs: criterion H has a root in (0,1) ⇔ H'(1)>0 ⇔ a\geq0 (for integer a)

technique(s).name: lattice_count_on_affine_constraint
technique(s).purpose: count admissible integer pairs

theorem(s).name: Intermediate Value Theorem
theorem(s).role: certify existence of a root in (0,1) from a sign change between 0+ and the minimum point

theorem(s).name: Strict convexity implies monotone derivative and at most two zeros
theorem(s).role: guarantees uniqueness of the minimizer and bounds the number of positive roots

theorem(s).name: Convex anchored-zero derivative criterion
theorem(s).role: for strictly convex H with H(1)=0, a root in (0,1) exists iff H'(1)>0

theorem(s).name: basic lattice counting on a line segment in a box
theorem(s).role: count integer points satisfying b=-a-2 with bound constraints and a-inequality

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Convexity and monotonicity of derivatives control root existence; counting reduces to integer points on an affine constraint.

critical_trick(s): Divide by x^2 to obtain a strictly convex auxiliary function H on (0,∞).
Use the anchoring constraint P(1)=0 (so H(1)=0) and strict convexity to reduce root existence in (0,1) to the sign of H'(1)=a+1.
Count integer a satisfying the resulting inequalities and bounds for b=-a-2.

title: Minimize complement-of-cycle edge sum under universal cycle constraint

identity.short_name: Minimize complement-of-cycle edge sum under universal cycle constraint

subdomain(s): quadratic forms from graph adjacency (cycle graph)
extremal pair-sum minimization on subgraphs of K_n
Hamiltonian cycle decompositions of complete graphs

problem_spec.summary: For odd n, impose a universal lower bound on the cyclic adjacent-product sum across all permutations via the quadratic form u^T(M+M^T)u. Minimize a modified complete-graph pair-sum objective that excludes the edges of one fixed Hamilton cycle C (the base cycle), i.e. Phi = Psi - u^T(M+M^T)u. Use a Hamilton decomposition of K_n that includes C to express Phi/2 as a sum of (n-3)/2 Hamilton cycle weights, apply the per-cycle bound, and construct a two-level vector making every Hamilton cycle weight constant to attain the bound.
givens: n is an odd integer (instance: n=101).
M is the n×n cyclic shift matrix, so M+M^T is the adjacency matrix of the cycle C_n.
For permutation matrix P, Q_P(v)=v^T P^T(M+M^T)Pv.
v is admissible if min_P Q_P(v) >= -C (instance: C=14).
Psi(v)=(sum_i v_i)^2 - sum_i v_i^2.
Define Phi(v)=Psi(v) - v^T(M+M^T)v (equivalently, twice the total weight on edges of K_n excluding the base cycle edges).
ask: Compute | min Phi(v) | over all admissible v.

parameter(s).name: n
parameter(s).purpose: dimension; ensures existence of a Hamilton decomposition of K_n

parameter(s).name: constraint_level_C
parameter(s).purpose: lower bound on min_P Q_P(v)

parameter(s).name: base_graph
parameter(s).purpose: M+M^T encodes adjacency on the cycle used in the permutation-min constraint

parameter(s).name: distinguished_hamilton_cycle
parameter(s).domain: a fixed Hamilton cycle C in K_n (typically the base cycle (1,2,...,n,1))
parameter(s).purpose: determines which K_n edges are excluded in the objective Phi

invariant(s).name: quadratic_form_as_cycle_edge_sum
invariant(s).statement: For u=Pv, Q_P(v)=u^T(M+M^T)u equals 2*sum_{i mod n} u_i u_{i+1}, i.e., twice the weighted edge sum along the Hamilton cycle order given by u.

invariant(s).name: admissibility_is_universal_cycle_bound
invariant(s).statement: min_P Q_P(v) >= -C is equivalent to: for every permutation u of v, sum_{i mod n} u_i u_{i+1} >= -C/2.

invariant(s).name: Psi_is_complete_graph_pair_sum
invariant(s).statement: Psi(v) = (sum v_i)^2 - sum v_i^2 = 2*sum_{1<=i<j<=n} v_i v_j, i.e., twice the total edge weight of K_n with weights v_i v_j.

invariant(s).name: Phi_is_complement_of_distinguished_cycle
invariant(s).statement: v^T(M+M^T)v = 2*sum_{edges of the distinguished cycle C} v_i v_j. Therefore Phi(v)=Psi(v)-v^T(M+M^T)v equals 2*(total edge weight on K_n \ C).

invariant(s).name: hamilton_decomposition_including_fixed_cycle
invariant(s).statement: For odd n, K_n decomposes into (n-1)/2 edge-disjoint Hamilton cycles. By relabeling vertices, we may assume the decomposition contains the distinguished Hamilton cycle C. Hence K_n \ C decomposes into (n-3)/2 Hamilton cycles.

essential_assumption(s): n is odd so a Hamilton decomposition of K_n exists (Walecki).
The admissibility constraint uses min over all permutation matrices P (equivalently, all Hamilton cycle orderings of the multiset of entries).
v is allowed to be any real vector (not restricted to integers).
uniqueness_certificate(s): Lower bound: write Phi/2 as the sum of (n-3)/2 Hamilton cycle edge sums via a Hamilton decomposition of K_n that includes the distinguished cycle. Each such Hamilton cycle sum equals sum u_i u_{i+1} for some permutation u of v, hence is >= -C/2 by admissibility. Therefore Phi >= -(n-3)C/2. Attainment: choose a two-level vector with one entry a and the remaining entries b so that every Hamilton cycle sum is the same constant 2ab+(n-2)b^2 = -C/2, forcing equality in all cycle bounds simultaneously. Hence min Phi is uniquely -(n-3)C/2 and |min Phi| is uniquely (n-3)C/2.

technique(s).name: convert_constraint_to_cycle_sums
technique(s).purpose: translate the permutation-min quadratic constraint into a uniform lower bound on cyclic adjacent products
outputs: inequality sum u_i u_{i+1} >= -C/2 for all permutations u

technique(s).name: express_objective_as_complete_graph_minus_cycle_weight
technique(s).purpose: reinterpret Phi as twice the total edge weight of K_n with one Hamilton cycle removed
outputs: Phi/2 equals total weight on K_n \ C

technique(s).name: apply_hamilton_decomposition_with_distinguished_cycle
technique(s).purpose: decompose K_n as C plus (n-3)/2 other Hamilton cycles so K_n \ C is covered by cycles
outputs: representation of Phi/2 as a sum over (n-3)/2 Hamilton cycle weights

technique(s).name: aggregate_bounds
technique(s).purpose: obtain the sharp global lower bound on Phi

technique(s).name: extremal_vector_construction_two_level
technique(s).purpose: build v with one value a and n-1 values b making every Hamilton cycle sum constant and equal to -C/2

technique(s).name: conclude_min_and_absolute_value
technique(s).purpose: conclude the unique minimum and output its absolute value

theorem(s).name: Walecki_hamilton_decomposition
theorem(s).role: for odd n, K_n decomposes into (n-1)/2 edge-disjoint Hamilton cycles

theorem(s).name: relabeling_preserves_decomposition
theorem(s).role: applying a vertex permutation to a Hamilton decomposition yields another decomposition, allowing inclusion of a chosen Hamilton cycle

theorem(s).name: adjacency_matrix_quadratic_form_identity
theorem(s).role: u^T(M+M^T)u equals twice the cyclic adjacent-product sum along the Hamilton order

theorem(s).name: algebraic_identity_for_Psi
theorem(s).role: Psi(v) = (sum v_i)^2 - sum v_i^2 = 2*sum_{i<j} v_i v_j

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: transform a permutation-min constraint into a uniform Hamilton-cycle inequality and globalize via Hamilton decompositions

critical_trick(s): Interpret the constraint as a bound on every Hamilton cycle edge sum after permutation.
Rewrite the objective as the complete-graph edge sum with one Hamilton cycle removed, and decompose the remaining edges into Hamilton cycles.
Use a two-level vector with one special entry to make every Hamilton cycle sum constant, achieving equality.

title: AM-GM beta; power-law trajectory; mod 29 lattice congruence; compute alpha*^(alpha*-1)

identity.short_name: AM-GM beta; power-law trajectory; mod 29 lattice congruence; compute alpha*^(alpha*-1)

problem_spec.summary: Compute β as the minimum of a symmetric radical expression via AM-GM. Use β inside a separable ODE dy/dx = α (y-β)/(x-β) with a β-dependent initial point to obtain an explicit power-law curve Γ_α. Parameterize integer lattice points on Γ_α, select the two with smallest x-coordinate above a threshold, impose a congruence mod 29 on m^2+n^2 for those points, find the least α satisfying it, then output α^(α-1).
givens: Define F(x,y,z) = (sqrt(x/y)+sqrt(y/z)+sqrt(z/x))^2 for x,y,z>0.
Let beta be the minimum value of F over R_{>0}^3.
For each positive integer alpha, consider the ODE (for x != beta):

title: Blow-up limit L*=1; rational-phase trig selector; weighted sum mod 1000

identity.short_name: Blow-up limit L*=1; rational-phase trig selector; weighted sum mod 1000

subdomain(s): singular first-order ODE (Riccati-type) and analytic germs
periodic trigonometric selectors reducible to congruences
CRT-style residue intersection and block summation

problem_spec.summary: A singular Riccati ODE with a specified real-analytic germ at 0 blows up in finite positive time. A normalized blow-up limit L* is extracted and inserted into trigonometric inequalities whose phases are rational multiples of \pi, converting the selector to congruence conditions. The resulting weighted sum up to N is computed by one-period residue enumeration and block summation, then reduced mod 1000.
givens: ODE: x f'(x) = x + f(x)^2.
f is real-analytic near 0 with f(0)=0 and f'(0)=1.
Let beta be the right endpoint of maximal existence on (0,beta) (beta is finite).
Define L_star = lim_{x->beta^-} ((beta-x)/beta) f(x).
Define R = sum_{n=1}^{2025} n * 1_{ { cos(pi*n/7 + pi*L_star) > 0 and sin(pi*n/9 - (pi/2)*L_star) < 0 } }.
Output requested is R mod 1000.
ask: Compute R modulo 1000.

parameter(s).name: N
parameter(s).purpose: upper limit of the weighted sum (here 2025)

parameter(s).name: trig_denominators
parameter(s).purpose: sets periods 14 and 18, hence joint period 126

parameter(s).name: phase_recipe
parameter(s).purpose: ensures trig sign tests reduce to congruences once L_star is known

parameter(s).name: output_modulus
parameter(s).purpose: reduce final integer modulo 1000

invariant(s).name: analytic_uniqueness_via_series_recursion
invariant(s).statement: Writing f(x)=sum a_n x^n with a_0=0,a_1=1 and matching coefficients in x f'=x+f^2 yields a unique recursion for a_n (n>=2), hence a unique analytic germ at 0.

invariant(s).name: finite_positive_blow_up_time
invariant(s).statement: For x>0, f'(x)=1+f(x)^2/x>0 and comparison f' >= f^2/x forces a finite blow-up time beta<infty.

invariant(s).name: normalized_blowup_limit_universal
invariant(s).statement: As x->beta^-, f(x)->+infty and f'/f^2 = 1/x + 1/f^2 -> 1/beta, implying 1/f(x) ~ (beta-x)/beta and therefore L_star = 1.

invariant(s).name: congruence_reduction_of_trig_signs
invariant(s).statement: With phases pi*L_star and (pi/2)*L_star, and with L_star=1, the sign conditions become cos(pi*n/7)<0 and cos(pi*n/9)>0, depending only on n mod 14 and mod 18 respectively.

invariant(s).name: periodic_selector_joint_period
invariant(s).statement: The joint selector is periodic in n with period lcm(14,18)=126, enabling block summation up to N.

essential_assumption(s): The analytic solution through x=0 is selected by f(0)=0,f'(0)=1.
Continuation is valid for x>0 as long as f stays finite.
Strict trig inequalities are used; the chosen phases avoid zero cases for integer n.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The series recursion determines f uniquely near 0; maximal continuation determines beta uniquely. The normalization identity forces L_star=1 uniquely. Substituting L_star fixes the congruence-based selector uniquely on a finite period, so the resulting weighted sum and its residue mod 1000 are unique.

technique(s).name: series_solution_and_recursion
technique(s).purpose: certify local analytic existence/uniqueness and fix the germ at 0

technique(s).name: finite_time_blow_up_via_comparison
technique(s).purpose: prove beta is finite

technique(s).name: normalized_blowup_asymptotic
technique(s).purpose: compute L_star without solving for beta explicitly

technique(s).name: trig_signs_to_congruences
technique(s).purpose: convert trig inequalities into residue classes mod 14 and 18

technique(s).name: CRT_and_one_period_enumeration
technique(s).purpose: find the valid residues mod 126 and compute their count and sum

technique(s).name: block_sum_and_mod
technique(s).purpose: compute the weighted sum up to N by full periods plus remainder, then reduce mod 1000

theorem(s).name: analytic_existence_uniqueness_for_ODE
theorem(s).role: supports uniqueness of the analytic germ fixed by recursion

theorem(s).name: ODE_continuation_theorem
theorem(s).role: rules out finite beta with finite f(beta^-)

theorem(s).name: asymptotic_from_ratio_limit
theorem(s).role: if f'/f^2 -> 1/beta and f->infty then (beta-x)f/beta -> 1

theorem(s).name: trigonometric_periodicity
theorem(s).role: cos(pi*(n+14)/7)=cos(pi*n/7), cos(pi*(n+18)/9)=cos(pi*n/9)

theorem(s).name: chinese_remainder_theorem_with_gcd_2
theorem(s).role: solvability and uniqueness mod 126 for congruences mod 14 and 18

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: comparison inequality for blow-up
periodic reduction to finite residue enumeration

critical_trick(s): Extract L_star=1 from universal blow-up normalization.
Choose phases as rational multiples of \pi so trig sign tests become pure congruences.
Use one-period enumeration plus block summation to compute the weighted sum and reduce mod 1000.

library(s).reason: optional numerical verification of L_star=1 and trig-sign checks

library(s).reason: optional enumeration of residues mod 126 and block-sum verification

task(s).reason: enumerate n=1..126 and confirm the 32 valid residues

task(s).name: verify_block_sum

task(s).reason: reduce the final integer to its residue mod 1000

title: Gaussian norm coupling with prime-square quadratic obstruction (Hensel lifting)

identity.short_name: Gaussian norm coupling with prime-square quadratic obstruction (Hensel lifting)

problem_spec.summary: A prime p is encoded via its unique Gaussian norm representation p=x^2+y^2, yielding a canonical residue t \equiv x \cdot y^{-1} (mod p) with t^2 \equiv -1 (mod p). This t drives a quadratic congruence modulo q^2 in a forced double-root regime q|D. The root count is decided by a valuation/Hensel obstruction for square roots modulo a prime square, and the final output is a linear form reduced mod 1000.
givens: Let p=1001093 and q=38711 be distinct odd primes with p\equiv1 (mod 4).
Let (x,y) be the unique integers with x>y>0 and x^2+y^2=p.
Let delta=2.
Define t to be the least positive residue of x \cdot y^{-1} (mod p).
Define f(n) \equiv n^2 + t n + (t+delta) (mod q^2).
Assume the double-root condition q | (t^2 - 4(t+delta)).
ask: Let M be the number of n in {0,1,...,q^2-1} such that f(n)\equiv0 (mod q^2). Compute the unique A in {0,1,...,999} with A \equiv 2x + 7y + 9M + delta (mod 1000). (Normalization: reduction modulo 1000.)

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).domain: odd prime p with p\equiv1 (mod 4) and p>10^6
parameter(s).purpose: guarantees a nontrivial Gaussian norm representation p=x^2+y^2 and a canonical square root of -1 modulo p derived from (x,y)

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).domain: odd prime q with q>10^4 and q\neqp
parameter(s).purpose: provides the prime-square modulus q^2 where lifting behavior is nontrivial and brute-force search over residues modulo q^2 is infeasible by hand

parameter(s).name: delta
parameter(s).purpose: coupling knob changing the discriminant D=t^2-4(t+delta) and hence the lifting obstruction regime

parameter(s).name: normalization_rule
parameter(s).purpose: fixes unit/conjugation symmetries so (x,y) and the induced residue t are uniquely defined

invariant(s).name: unique_gaussian_norm_representation
invariant(s).statement: For prime p\equiv1 (mod 4), there exists a Gaussian integer \pi=x+iy with N(\pi)=p, giving p=x^2+y^2; multiplication by units and conjugation only changes signs and swaps x,y, so the rule x>y>0 selects a unique pair (x,y).

invariant(s).name: t_is_sqrt_minus_one_mod_p
invariant(s).statement: With t \equiv x \cdot y^{-1} (mod p), one has t^2 \equiv -1 (mod p).

invariant(s).name: completion_of_square_mod_q2
invariant(s).statement: For f(n)=n^2+tn+(t+delta), the identity 4f(n)=(2n+t)^2-D holds with D=t^2-4(t+delta); hence f(n)\equiv0 (mod q^2) iff (2n+t)^2\equivD (mod q^2).

invariant(s).name: prime_square_sqrt_obstruction
invariant(s).statement: If q is an odd prime and q|D but q^2∤D, then D is not a square modulo q^2, so the congruence z^2\equivD (mod q^2) has no solutions.

essential_assumption(s): The condition p\equiv1 (mod 4) is structural: it ensures the existence (and normalized uniqueness) of p=x^2+y^2 in Z[i].
The normalization rule x>y>0 is essential to make (x,y) and the induced residue t \equiv x \cdot y^{-1} (mod p) unique.
The modulus is q^2 with q odd, so 2 and 4 are invertible modulo q^2 and completion-of-square is valid.
The double-root condition q | D forces the prime-square lifting regime; without it the congruence would be controlled by the usual discriminant/Legendre-symbol behavior modulo q instead.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The normalization rule gives a unique (x,y), hence a unique t; then D=t^2-4(t+delta) is fixed.

In the double-root regime, M equals the number of square roots of D modulo q^2 (via completion-of-square). By the prime-square obstruction, either M=0 (if q^2∤D) or M=q (if q^2|D), so M is uniquely determined by v_q(D).
A is uniquely determined by a single congruence modulo 1000 together with the range 0\leqA<1000.

technique(s).name: unique_factorization_Zi_optional
technique(s).purpose: justify existence and normalized uniqueness of the representation p=x^2+y^2
outputs: coprime integers (x,y) with x>y>0 and x^2+y^2=p

technique(s).name: descent_via_norms_optional
inputs: a square root u of -1 modulo p
technique(s).purpose: run Euclidean descent (equivalently, the Gaussian Euclidean algorithm for gcd(p,u+i)) to extract an explicit representation p=x^2+y^2

technique(s).name: roots_of_unity_filter_light
technique(s).purpose: quotient out symmetries and enforce the normalization rule x>y>0

technique(s).name: norm_to_modular_interface
technique(s).purpose: produce the canonical residue t \equiv x \cdot y^{-1} (mod p) satisfying t^2\equiv-1 (mod p)

technique(s).name: completion_square_prime_power
technique(s).purpose: rewrite f(n)\equiv0 (mod q^2) as a square-root congruence (2n+t)^2\equivD (mod q^2)
outputs: reduction of root counting to square roots of the discriminant D modulo q^2

technique(s).name: hensel_square_root_lifting
technique(s).purpose: use a Hensel-lifting/valuation argument to decide whether D is a square modulo q^2 in the singular case q|D
outputs: determination of M (either 0 or q) and thus the final residue A mod 1000

technique(s).name: modular_reduction_output
technique(s).purpose: apply the required normalization A mod 1000

theorem(s).name: sum_of_two_squares_criterion
theorem(s).role: characterize primes p\equiv1 (mod 4) as exactly those representable as x^2+y^2, equivalently those splitting in Z[i]

theorem(s).name: Hensel_lemma
theorem(s).role: lifting principle for solutions modulo prime powers; used here via the singular square-root/valuation obstruction at modulus q^2

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Norm maps in quadratic integer rings and lifting behavior modulo prime powers constrain congruence solvability.

critical_trick(s): Extract the normalized Gaussian norm representation p=x^2+y^2 via the Gaussian Euclidean algorithm applied to gcd(p,u+i) for an explicit u^2\equiv-1 (mod p).
Convert the quadratic congruence modulo q^2 into a square-root problem for the discriminant using completion of the square.
Apply a Hensel-lifting/valuation obstruction: if v_q(D)=1 then D cannot be a square modulo q^2, forcing M=0.

library(s).name: math
library(s).reason: integer arithmetic (isqrt) and fast modular computations

library(s).name: sympy
library(s).reason: primality testing, modular inverses, modular square roots for composite moduli, and symbolic canonicalization for novelty auditing

task(s).name: primary_structural_check
task(s).reason:
Correctness checks aligned with the intended math path:
- Verified p=1001093 and q=38711 are primes, p\equiv1 (mod 4), and q is odd.
- Verified 769*p - 1 = 27746^2, hence u=27746 satisfies u^2\equiv-1 (mod p).
- Verified x=902, y=433 satisfy x^2+y^2=p and x>y>0, then computed t \equiv x \cdot y^{-1} (mod p) and confirmed t=27746.
- Computed D=t^2-4(t+delta) with delta=2 and verified q | D and q^2 ∤ D.
- Used sqrt_mod to confirm D has no square root modulo q^2, hence M=0, then computed A\equiv2x+7y+9M+delta (mod 1000) giving A=837.
Preflight attempts (cheap strategies) to demonstrate nontriviality:
Attempt A: Naively guessing that a double root modulo q always lifts to q roots modulo q^2 would give M=q and hence A\equiv236 (mod 1000), which mismatches the true A=837.
Attempt B: A bounded scan over 0\leqn\leq5000 finds no solution to f(n)\equiv0 (mod q^2), so small-range searching does not shortcut the prime-square obstruction.
Attempt C: Working only modulo q forces a double root but leaves two candidates M\in{0,q}, hence leaves multiple possible outputs in {0,...,999} (here 837 vs 236).

task(s).name: secondary_independent_check
task(s).reason:
Independent check by enumerating all potential lifts of the unique root modulo q:
- Computed r \equiv -t \cdot 2^{-1} (mod q) as the (double) root modulo q.
- Checked all q candidates n=r+kq (k=0,...,q-1) modulo q^2 and found none satisfy f(n)\equiv0 (mod q^2), confirming M=0 and A=837.

task(s).name: novelty_equivalence_audit
task(s).reason:
Canonicalized parent and daughter constraint signatures with sympy:
- Polynomials f(n,t,delta)=n^2+tn+(t+delta) and discriminant D=t^2-4(t+delta) were expanded and converted to Poly in (n,t,delta) with lex order; normalization enforced leading coefficient 1.
- "Tried symmetry transforms: identity; t\\to-t; n\\to-n-t; and the combined transform."
- The signatures cannot match because the parent family is a modulus-q (prime) root-count problem under q∤D, while the daughter is a modulus-q^2 (prime-square) root-count problem in the singular regime q|D; the modulus exponent and discriminant divisibility condition differ under all tested transforms.

title: Gaussian/Eisenstein integer norms (sum of two squares / $x^2+xy+y^2$)

identity.short_name: Gaussian/Eisenstein integer norms (sum of two squares / $x^2+xy+y^2$)

problem_spec.summary: Use norms in $\mathbb{Z}[i]$ (and optionally $\mathbb{Z}[\omega]$) to represent integers/primes by quadratic forms, e.g. $p=x^2+y^2$ or $p=x^2+xy+y^2$, with a normalization that makes the representation unique.
givens: Either: $p$ is a prime with $p\equiv 1\pmod 4$ (Gaussian mode) or $p\equiv 1\pmod 3$ (Eisenstein mode), ensuring representability.
Normalization convention: output integers $(x,y)$ with $x\ge y\ge 0$ and the chosen quadratic form equals $p$ (or $n$).
ask: Produce the normalized representation $(x,y)$ such that $p=x^2+y^2$ (Gaussian mode) or $p=x^2+xy+y^2$ (Eisenstein mode).

parameter(s).name: mode
parameter(s).purpose: selects Gaussian vs. Eisenstein quadratic form

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: input prime guaranteed to be representable by the chosen norm form

parameter(s).name: normalization_rule
parameter(s).purpose: fixes sign/order symmetries to ensure uniqueness of output

invariant(s).name: norm_multiplicativity
invariant(s).statement: Norms satisfy $N(zw)=N(z)N(w)$ in $\mathbb{Z}[i]$ / $\mathbb{Z}[\omega]$, enabling descent and factorization arguments.

invariant(s).name: unique_factorization_controls_representations
invariant(s).statement: In the relevant ring (UFD in this olympiad-light setting), a split prime $p$ factors as $p=\pi\bar\pi$; the norm equation $N(\pi)=p$ yields a representation of $p$ by the norm form, unique up to units and conjugation.

essential_assumption(s): Representability requires a splitting condition on $p$ (e.g., $p\equiv 1\pmod 4$ for $x^2+y^2$). This congruence condition is structural and must be part of the instance domain.
Uniqueness of the *normalized* pair $(x,y)$ uses the fact that all representations differ only by unit multiples and swapping $x,y$; the normalization rule fixes these symmetries.
uniqueness_certificate(s): In Gaussian mode, for prime $p\equiv 1\pmod 4$ there are exactly $8$ representations $(\pm x,\pm y)$ and swapped; imposing $x\ge y\ge 0$ selects exactly one. Analogously for Eisenstein mode with its unit group symmetry.

technique(s).name: unique_factorization_Zi_optional
technique(s).purpose: factor $p$ in $\mathbb{Z}[i]$ and extract a norm representation

technique(s).name: descent_via_norms_optional
technique(s).purpose: perform Euclidean descent on norms to obtain an exact representation $p=x^2+y^2$

technique(s).name: roots_of_unity_filter_light
technique(s).purpose: classify representations up to multiplication by units
outputs: normalization reduction to a unique representative

theorem(s).name: sum_of_two_squares_criterion
theorem(s).role: characterize when an integer (or prime) is representable as a sum of two squares

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: A representation like $x^2+y^2=n$ is a norm equation in a quadratic integer ring; multiplicativity of norms turns arithmetic questions into factorization questions.

critical_trick(s): Translate the quadratic form equation into a norm equation and use (light) unique factorization / norm descent to produce a representation.
Impose a normalization (e.g., $x\ge y\ge 0$) to obtain a unique reported pair.

title: Gaussian/Eisenstein integer norms (sum of two squares / $x^2+xy+y^2$) - Buffed Seed

identity.short_name: Gaussian/Eisenstein integer norms (sum of two squares / $x^2+xy+y^2$) - Buffed Seed

problem_spec.summary: Couple a canonical Gaussian-norm representation p=x^2+y^2 with a forced quadratic-residue computation modulo a second prime. The normalized norm representation produces a distinguished intermediate residue t; a q-periodic quadratic sequence built from t has a root-count governed by a residue symbol whose evaluation requires reciprocity. The final reported output is an explicit integer expression reduced modulo 1000.
givens: Let p=1000249 and q=100003 be distinct odd primes with p\equiv 1\pmod 4 and q\equiv 3\pmod 4.
Let (x,y) be the unique integers with x>y>0 and x^2+y^2=p.
Let mode=coupling_delta_1 so \delta=1.
Define t to be the least positive residue of x\cdot y^{-1}\pmod p.
Define (a_n) by a_n\equiv n^2+t\,n+(t+\delta)\pmod q.
Non-degeneracy: q mid(t^2-4(t+\delta)).
ask: Compute A\in{0,1,\dots,999} such that A\equiv x+2y+5N+\delta\pmod{1000}, where N is the number of n\in{0,1,\dots,q-1} with a_n\equiv 0\pmod q.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).domain: odd prime p with p\equiv 1\pmod 4 and p>10^6
parameter(s).purpose: ensures a nontrivial Gaussian norm representation and blocks brute-force search for (x,y) as a solution method

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).domain: odd prime q with q\equiv 3\pmod 4 and 10^5<q<p
parameter(s).purpose: auxiliary modulus forcing a nontrivial residue-symbol evaluation (reciprocity regime) rather than enumeration over all residues

parameter(s).name: mode
parameter(s).purpose: structural coupling knob selecting \delta\in{1,2} and changing the discriminant class to be analyzed modulo q

parameter(s).name: normalization_rule
parameter(s).purpose: fixes the unit/conjugation and ordering symmetries so (x,y) (and hence t) is unique

invariant(s).name: norm_multiplicativity
invariant(s).statement: Norms satisfy N(zw)=N(z)N(w) in \mathbb{Z}[i], enabling descent/factorization arguments that produce p=x^2+y^2.

title: Gaussian/Eisenstein integer norms (sum of two squares / $x^2+xy+y^2$) - Buffed Seed

identity.short_name: Gaussian/Eisenstein integer norms (sum of two squares / $x^2+xy+y^2$) - Buffed Seed

problem_spec.summary: Couple a canonical Gaussian-norm representation $p=x^2+y^2$ with a forced quadratic-residue computation modulo a second prime. The normalized norm representation produces a distinguished intermediate residue $t$; a $q$-periodic quadratic sequence built from $t$ has a root-count governed by a residue symbol whose evaluation requires reciprocity. The final reported output is an explicit integer expression reduced modulo $1000$.
givens: Let $p$ and $q$ be distinct odd primes with $p\equiv 1\pmod 4$, $q\equiv 3\pmod 4$, and $10^5<q<p$ and $p>10^6$.
Let $(x,y)$ be the unique integers with $x>y>0$ and $x^2+y^2=p$.
Define $\delta$ by the structural parameter $\mathrm{mode}$: if $\mathrm{mode}=\texttt{coupling_delta_1}$ then $\delta=1$, and if $\mathrm{mode}=\texttt{coupling_delta_2}$ then $\delta=2$.
Define $t$ to be the least positive residue of $x\cdot y^{-1}\pmod p$, i.e. $1\le t\le p-1$ and $t\equiv x\,y^{-1}\pmod p$.
Define the $q$-periodic sequence $(a_n)_{n\ge 0}$ by $a_n\equiv n^2+t\,n+(t+\delta)\pmod q$.
Non-degeneracy: $q\nmid\bigl(t^2-4(t+\delta)\bigr).
ask: Stage 1 (norm interface): Using the normalized Gaussian-norm representation $p=x^2+y^2$, justify that $t$ is well-defined and show that $N:=|\{0\le n\le q-1:\ a_n\equiv 0\pmod q\}|$ is determined by the quadratic-residue symbol of the discriminant $D:=t^2-4(t+\delta)$ modulo $q$.
Stage 2 (reciprocity finish): Evaluate $N$ (hence $A\equiv x+2y+5N+\delta\pmod{1000}$) by computing the relevant residue symbol via reciprocity. Report the unique integer $A\in\{0,1,\dots,999\}$.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: ensures a nontrivial Gaussian norm representation and blocks brute-force search for $(x,y)$ as a solution method

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: auxiliary modulus forcing a nontrivial residue-symbol evaluation (reciprocity regime) rather than enumeration over all residues

parameter(s).name: mode
parameter(s).purpose: structural coupling knob selecting $\delta\in\{1,2\}$ and changing the discriminant class to be analyzed modulo $q$

parameter(s).name: normalization_rule
parameter(s).purpose: fixes the unit/conjugation and ordering symmetries so $(x,y)$ (and hence $t$) is unique

invariant(s).name: norm_multiplicativity
invariant(s).statement: Norms satisfy $N(zw)=N(z)N(w)$ in $\mathbb{Z}[i]$, enabling descent/factorization arguments that produce $p=x^2+y^2$.

invariant(s).name: unique_factorization_controls_representations
invariant(s).statement: For $p\equiv 1\pmod 4$, the prime splits in $\mathbb{Z}[i]$ as $p=\pi\bar\pi$; the norm equation $N(\pi)=p$ yields $p=x^2+y^2$, and the normalization rule selects a unique $(x,y)$ among unit/conjugation and swap symmetries.

invariant(s).name: anti_triviality_distinct_prime_classes
invariant(s).statement: The instance requires distinct odd primes with $p\equiv 1\pmod 4$, $q\equiv 3\pmod 4$, and $10^5<q<p$, excluding degenerate small-modulus or equal-prime cases.

invariant(s).name: coprime_and_opposite_parity_in_representation
invariant(s).statement: In the unique representation $p=x^2+y^2$ with $x>y>0$, necessarily $\gcd(x,y)=1$ and $x,y$ have opposite parity.

invariant(s).name: t_is_canonical_sqrt_minus_one_mod_p
invariant(s).statement: With $t\equiv x\,y^{-1}\pmod p$, one has $t^2\equiv -1\pmod p$; the normalization of $(x,y)$ induces a unique residue class $t$.

invariant(s).name: discriminant_nonzero_mod_q
invariant(s).statement: Let $\delta$ be determined by $\mathrm{mode}$ and set $D:=t^2-4(t+\delta)$. The admissible domain enforces $D\not\equiv 0\pmod q$.

invariant(s).name: quadratic_root_count_via_legendre_symbol
invariant(s).statement: For $f(n)=n^2+t\,n+(t+\delta)$ modulo $q$ with discriminant $D=t^2-4(t+\delta)$, the number $N$ of roots in $\{0,1,\dots,q-1\}$ satisfies $N=1+\left(\frac{D}{q}\right)$; under $D\not\equiv 0\pmod q$, this forces $N\in\{0,2\}$.

essential_assumption(s): The splitting condition $p\equiv 1\pmod 4$ is structural: it guarantees existence of a Gaussian integer $\pi=x+iy$ with $N(\pi)=p$ and supports the normalization-based uniqueness of $(x,y)$.

The normalization rule ($x>y>0$) is essential: without it, the induced residue $t\equiv x\,y^{-1}\pmod p$ is ambiguous up to unit/conjugation and swap symmetries, making the downstream discriminant modulo $q$ ill-defined.

One-step template exclusion: the intended solution must (i) extract the canonical $t$ from the norm representation in $\mathbb{Z}[i]$ and then (ii) evaluate the Legendre symbol controlling $N$ using quadratic reciprocity; the parameter ranges are chosen to preclude brute-force enumeration over residues modulo $q$ as a valid solution strategy.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The normalization rule selects a unique $(x,y)$ with $x^2+y^2=p$, hence a unique residue class $t\equiv x\,y^{-1}\pmod p$ and a unique discriminant $D=t^2-4(t+\delta)$ modulo $q$ for each admissible $(p,q,\mathrm{mode})$.

The non-degeneracy invariant $D\not\equiv 0\pmod q$ forces the root-count $N$ of $n^2+t\,n+(t+\delta)\equiv 0\pmod q$ to be exactly $N=1+\left(\frac{D}{q}\right)\in\{0,2\}$, uniquely determined by the Legendre symbol.
The final reported value $A\equiv x+2y+5N+\delta\pmod{1000}$ is therefore uniquely determined; the normalization rule "mod $1000$" fixes a unique integer output in $\{0,1,\dots,999\}$.

technique(s).name: unique_factorization_Zi_optional
technique(s).purpose: factor $p$ in $\mathbb{Z}[i]$ and extract the normalized norm representation $p=x^2+y^2$

technique(s).name: descent_via_norms_optional
technique(s).purpose: perform Euclidean descent on norms to obtain an exact normalized representation $p=x^2+y^2$

technique(s).name: roots_of_unity_filter_light
technique(s).purpose: classify representations up to multiplication by units and conjugation and enforce the normalization rule

technique(s).name: norm_to_modular_interface
technique(s).purpose: derive the canonical residue $t\equiv x\,y^{-1}\pmod p$ (a canonical square root of $-1$ modulo $p$) and pass it to the discriminant $D=t^2-4(t+\delta)$ to interface the Gaussian-norm regime with quadratic-residue analysis modulo $q$
outputs: the integer $t$ and the discriminant $D$ modulo $q$

technique(s).name: quadratic_reciprocity_legendre_symbol_compute
technique(s).purpose: evaluate the Legendre symbol $\left(\frac{D}{q}\right)$ controlling $N$ (hence the final answer), explicitly coupling the norm-derived $t$ with reciprocity-based symbol computation
outputs: the value of $\left(\frac{D}{q}\right)$ and thus $N$ and $A \bmod 1000$

theorem(s).name: sum_of_two_squares_criterion
theorem(s).role: characterize representability of primes as $x^2+y^2$ (equivalently splitting in $\mathbb{Z}[i]$)

theorem(s).name: quadratic_reciprocity
theorem(s).role: compute the needed Legendre symbol(s) modulo $q$ from congruence data and reduced symbols

theorem(s).name: quadratic_congruence_root_count
theorem(s).role: for $f(n)=n^2+bn+c$ modulo an odd prime, the number of roots is $1+\left(\frac{b^2-4c}{q}\right)$

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: A norm equation in a quadratic integer ring turns arithmetic conditions on primes into structured algebra; Legendre symbols encode solvability of quadratic congruences, and reciprocity provides a deterministic evaluation path.

critical_trick(s): Translate $p=x^2+y^2$ into a Gaussian norm equation and enforce a normalization to make $(x,y)$ unique.
Use the normalized $(x,y)$ to define a canonical residue $t\equiv x\,y^{-1}\pmod p$, then pass this interface into a discriminant whose Legendre symbol modulo $q$ controls the root count $N$.
Evaluate the controlling Legendre symbol via quadratic reciprocity, and finish by reducing the derived expression modulo $1000$ to obtain the unique output in $\{0,\dots,999\}$.

task(s).name: secondary_independent_check

task(s).name: search_representation_small_primes

title: Arithmetic functions from prime exponents ($\varphi,\sigma,\tau$; multiplicativity)

identity.short_name: Arithmetic functions from prime exponents ($\varphi,\sigma,\tau$; multiplicativity)

problem_spec.summary: Given an integer $n$ (typically via its prime factorization), compute standard arithmetic functions such as Euler totient $\varphi(n)$, divisor count $\tau(n)$, and sum of divisors $\sigma(n)$, and optionally solve inverse/characterization constraints involving these functions.
givens: $n\in\mathbb{N}$, $n\ge 1$
The instance may provide the prime factorization of $n$ or keep $n$ small enough for factorization to be feasible.
ask: Output the triple $(\varphi(n),\sigma(n),\tau(n))$. (Variants may additionally impose constraints such as $\varphi(n)=m$ or $\sigma(n)=\tau(n)$ and ask to recover $n$.)

parameter(s).name: n
parameter(s).purpose: primary input integer

parameter(s).name: input_mode
parameter(s).purpose: controls whether the task is forward evaluation or an inverse characterization under constraints

parameter(s).name: constraint_profile
parameter(s).purpose: in inverse mode, specifies the system used to recover $n$

invariant(s).name: multiplicativity_prime_power_reduction
invariant(s).statement: For multiplicative functions (e.g., $\varphi,\sigma,\tau$), values on general $n$ factor as a product of values on prime powers in the factorization $n=\prod p_i^{e_i}$.

invariant(s).name: parity_constraints
invariant(s).statement: Parity of $\varphi(n),\sigma(n),\tau(n)$ is largely controlled by the presence/absence of odd prime factors and exponent parity (useful in inverse constraints).

essential_assumption(s): In inverse mode, the constraint_profile must be strong enough to ensure a unique $n$ in the declared search domain; otherwise the family should be instantiated in forward-evaluation mode.
When $n$ is given without factorization, instances should keep $n$ within a factorable range for verification tasks.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Forward mode: $(\varphi(n),\sigma(n),\tau(n))$ are deterministic functions of $n$, hence unique. In inverse mode: uniqueness is certified by proving the constraint_profile forces exactly one admissible prime exponent vector (hence exactly one $n$).

technique(s).name: multiplicative_functions
technique(s).purpose: reduce computation of $\varphi,\sigma,\tau$ to prime power cases

technique(s).name: phi_totient
technique(s).purpose: compute $\varphi(n)=n\prod_{p\mid n}(1-1/p)$

technique(s).name: sigma_sum_divisors
technique(s).purpose: compute $\sigma(n)=\prod_i \frac{p_i^{e_i+1}-1}{p_i-1}$

technique(s).name: tau_divisor_count
technique(s).purpose: compute $\tau(n)=\prod_i (e_i+1)$

technique(s).name: parity_of_arith_functions
technique(s).purpose: quickly rule out or pin down factorization shapes in inverse problems

theorem(s).name: fundamental_theorem_arithmetic
theorem(s).role: ensures the prime exponent vector uniquely determines $n$ and makes multiplicative formulas well-defined

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Divisors of $n$ correspond to choices of exponents in its prime factorization; arithmetic functions over divisors factor cleanly across primes.

critical_trick(s): Factor $n$ and reduce to prime powers; apply closed formulas for $\varphi(p^e), \sigma(p^e), \tau(p^e)$ and multiply.
For inverse constraints, use parity/size inequalities to restrict possible exponent vectors and then solve finitely.

task(s).name: secondary_independent_check
task(s).reason: Cross-check the main result via an alternative computation (e.g., brute force for small bounds, or an independent formula such as factorization vs. valuation, or CRT reconstruction vs. direct search).

task(s).name: inverse_mode_bruteforce_small_domain
task(s).reason: When instantiated in inverse mode with a small bound, brute force all $n$ in the domain and confirm uniqueness of the solution.

title: Arithmetic functions from prime exponents ($\varphi,\sigma,\tau$; multiplicativity) - Buffed Seed

identity.short_name: Arithmetic functions from prime exponents ($\varphi,\sigma,\tau$; multiplicativity) - Buffed Seed

problem_spec.summary: Let $n=p^kq$ with $p<q$ distinct odd primes and $k\ge 2$. Impose coupled $p$-adic and $q$-adic constraints so that $\sigma(n)$ shares a controlled nontrivial gcd with $n$, placing a linear congruence in the non-invertible regime. The task is to solve the resulting congruence class and then select a uniquely determined least positive representative by enforcing an exact $q$-adic valuation condition, with the final reported answer taken modulo $1000$.
givens: $p,q$ are distinct odd primes with $p<q$, and $k\in\mathbb{Z}$ with $k\ge 2$.
Define $n=p^kq$, and let $\varphi(n),\sigma(n),\tau(n)$ denote Euler's totient, the sum of positive divisors, and the number of positive divisors of $n$.
Valuation hardening: $v_p(q+1)=1$ and $v_p(k+1)=1$ (equivalently $p\mid(q+1)$, $p^2\nmid(q+1)$ and $p\mid(k+1)$, $p^2\nmid(k+1)$).
Nondegeneracy: $q\nmid\sigma(p^k)$ and $q<p^k$ (so $q^2<n$).
Consider the congruence $\sigma(n)\,x\equiv q\,\tau(n)\pmod{n}$ and restrict attention to solutions $x$ with $v_q(x)=1$.
ask:
Let $p<q$ be distinct odd primes and let $k\ge 2$ be an integer. Set $n=p^kq$ and write $\varphi(n),\sigma(n),\tau(n)$ for Euler's totient, the sum of divisors, and the divisor-count function of $n$. Assume the admissibility conditions


v_p(q+1)=1,\qquad v_p(k+1)=1,\qquad q\nmid\sigma(p^k),\qquad q<p^k.


Consider the congruence


\sigma(n)\,x\equiv q\,\tau(n)\pmod{n}.


Among all integers $x$ satisfying this congruence with $v_q(x)=1$ (i.e., $q\mid x$ but $q^2\nmid x$), let $x_0$ be the least positive such solution.
(a) Prove that such an $x_0$ exists and is unique.
(b) Determine the value of $x_0\bmod 1000$ (report the final answer modulo $1000$).

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: prime controlling the repeated prime-power part $p^k$ of the structured modulus

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: second prime factor of the structured modulus; also used for the forced $q$-adic condition on solutions

parameter(s).name: k
parameter(s).purpose: exponent shaping both the arithmetic functions and the solvability regime of the congruence

invariant(s).name: structured_modulus_shape
invariant(s).statement: For every admissible instance, $n=p^kq$ with $p,q$ distinct odd primes, $p<q$, and $k\ge 2$; in particular $n$ is odd and composite.

invariant(s).name: controlled_gcd_via_p_adic_divisibility
invariant(s).statement: Since $p\nmid\sigma(p^k)$, one has $v_p(\sigma(n))=v_p(q+1)=1$; together with $q\nmid\sigma(p^k)$ this forces $\gcd(\sigma(n),n)=p$, so $\sigma(n)$ is not invertible modulo $n$.

invariant(s).name: solvability_nontriviality
invariant(s).statement: The condition $v_p(k+1)=1$ implies $p\mid\tau(n)=2(k+1)$, hence $p\mid q\,\tau(n)$ while $p^2\nmid q\,\tau(n)$; therefore the congruence $\sigma(n)x\equiv q\tau(n)\pmod n$ is solvable but remains in a genuinely non-coprime (gcd-reduction) regime.

invariant(s).name: q_adic_filter_is_essential
invariant(s).statement: Because $q\nmid\sigma(n)$, reducing the congruence modulo $q$ forces $x\equiv 0\pmod q$, so every solution is divisible by $q$. The bound $q<p^k$ ensures $q^2<n$, making the extra restriction $v_q(x)=1$ nonvacuous and enabling a unique least positive admissible solution.

essential_assumption(s): Admissible instances are restricted to the non-invertible congruence regime $\gcd(\sigma(n),n)=p>1$; thus one cannot solve by a single modular inverse step modulo $n$, and gcd-reduction is mandatory.

The valuation constraints and the $q$-adic filter $v_q(x)=1$ are part of the task definition; without them the set of admissible solutions may be empty or the extremal quantity $x_0$ may not be well-defined.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Let $d=\gcd(\sigma(n),n)$. Under the stated invariants one has $d=p$ and $d\mid q\tau(n)$, so the congruence has solutions and reduces (after dividing by $p$) to a congruence with coprime coefficient modulo $n/p$, yielding a unique residue class modulo $n/p$. All solutions modulo $n$ are obtained by adding multiples of $n/p$. Since $q\mid (n/p)$ but $q^2\nmid (n/p)$ and $q^2<n$, this solution class contains admissible elements with $v_q(x)=1$, and there is a unique least positive such element $x_0$. The reported output is the canonical representative $x_0\bmod 1000\in\{0,1,\dots,999\}$.

technique(s).name: multiplicative_functions
technique(s).purpose: express $\varphi(n),\sigma(n),\tau(n)$ via closed prime-power formulas and separate $p$-parts and $q$-parts

technique(s).name: sigma_sum_divisors
technique(s).purpose: compute $\sigma(n)=\sigma(p^k)(q+1)$ and translate $v_p(q+1)=1$ and $q\nmid\sigma(p^k)$ into the exact gcd statement $\gcd(\sigma(n),n)=p$
outputs: a controlled nontrivial gcd $\gcd(\sigma(n),n)$ determined by $p$-adic divisibility

technique(s).name: tau_divisor_count
technique(s).purpose: use $\tau(n)=2(k+1)$ and the constraint $v_p(k+1)=1$ to guarantee the solvability condition $\gcd(\sigma(n),n)\mid q\tau(n)$ in the non-coprime regime
outputs: verification that the linear congruence has solutions

technique(s).name: linear_congruence_gcd_reduction_and_inverse
inputs: congruence $\sigma(n)x\equiv q\tau(n)\pmod n$ and the computed gcd $\gcd(\sigma(n),n)=p$
technique(s).purpose: apply the solvability criterion and reduce to a coprime congruence modulo $n/p$; the $p$-adic constraints are used to pin down the gcd exactly, forcing interaction between linear congruence theory (LIN) and prime-exponent/divisibility control (VP)
outputs: a unique residue class $x\equiv x_*\pmod{n/p}$ and the full solution set modulo $n$

technique(s).name: q_adic_extremal_selection
technique(s).purpose: use that $q\mid(n/p)$ but $q^2\nmid(n/p)$ together with $q^2<n$ to show admissible solutions exist and to extract the unique least positive one

theorem(s).name: fundamental_theorem_arithmetic
theorem(s).role: ensures the prime exponent data $(p,k,q)$ determines $n$ and supports multiplicative evaluations

theorem(s).name: prime_power_closed_forms_for_phi_sigma_tau
theorem(s).role: provides $\varphi(p^e)=p^{e-1}(p-1)$, $\sigma(p^e)=\frac{p^{e+1}-1}{p-1}$, and $\tau(p^e)=e+1$

theorem(s).name: linear_congruence_solvability_and_solution_structure
theorem(s).role: if $ax\equiv b\pmod m$ and $d=\gcd(a,m)$, then solutions exist iff $d\mid b$; in that case solutions form a residue class modulo $m/d$

theorem(s).name: Bezout_identity_extended_euclid
theorem(s).role: constructs inverses modulo $m/d$ after gcd-reduction

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Prime factorization reduces divisor-based arithmetic functions to prime-power computations; congruences reduce via gcd and are controlled by prime-power divisibility.

critical_trick(s): Compute $\sigma(n)=\sigma(p^k)(q+1)$ and $\tau(n)=2(k+1)$ using multiplicativity, then use the $p$-adic conditions to force $\gcd(\sigma(n),n)=p$ and guarantee solvability.
Reduce the non-coprime congruence by dividing out the gcd, solve the reduced coprime congruence modulo $n/p$, and lift to all solutions modulo $n$.
Exploit that $q\mid(n/p)$ but $q^2\nmid(n/p)$ and $q^2<n$ to enforce the filter $v_q(x)=1$ and select the unique least positive admissible solution.

library(s).reason: gcd and integer arithmetic; valuation by repeated division.

invariant(s).statement: The condition $v_p(k+1)=1$ implies $p\mid\tau(n)=2(k+1)$, hence $p\mid q\,\tau(n)$ while $p^2\nmid q\,\tau(n)$; therefore the congruence $\sigma(n)x\equiv q\tau(n)\pmod n$ is solvable but remains in a genuinely non-coprime (gcd-reduction) regime.

invariant(s).name: q_adic_filter_is_essential
invariant(s).statement: Because $q\nmid\sigma(n)$, reducing the congruence modulo $q$ forces $x\equiv 0\pmod q$, so every solution is divisible by $q$. The bound $q<p^k$ ensures $q^2<n$, making the extra restriction $v_q(x)=1$ nonvacuous and enabling a unique least positive admissible solution.

essential_assumption(s): Admissible instances are restricted to the non-invertible congruence regime $\gcd(\sigma(n),n)=p>1$; thus one cannot solve by a single modular inverse step modulo $n$, and gcd-reduction is mandatory.

The valuation constraints and the $q$-adic filter $v_q(x)=1$ are part of the task definition; without them the set of admissible solutions may be empty or the extremal quantity $x_0$ may not be well-defined.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Let $d=\gcd(\sigma(n),n)$. Under the stated invariants one has $d=p$ and $d\mid q\tau(n)$, so the congruence has solutions and reduces (after dividing by $p$) to a congruence with coprime coefficient modulo $n/p$, yielding a unique residue class modulo $n/p$. All solutions modulo $n$ are obtained by adding multiples of $n/p$. Since $q\mid (n/p)$ but $q^2\nmid (n/p)$ and $q^2<n$, this solution class contains admissible elements with $v_q(x)=1$, and there is a unique least positive such element $x_0$. The reported output is the canonical representative $x_0\bmod 1000\in\{0,1,\dots,999\}$.

technique(s).name: multiplicative_functions
technique(s).purpose: express $\varphi(n),\sigma(n),\tau(n)$ via closed prime-power formulas and separate $p$-parts and $q$-parts

technique(s).name: sigma_sum_divisors
technique(s).purpose: compute $\sigma(n)=\sigma(p^k)(q+1)$ and translate $v_p(q+1)=1$ and $q\nmid\sigma(p^k)$ into the exact gcd statement $\gcd(\sigma(n),n)=p$
outputs: a controlled nontrivial gcd $\gcd(\sigma(n),n)$ determined by $p$-adic divisibility

technique(s).name: tau_divisor_count
technique(s).purpose: use $\tau(n)=2(k+1)$ and the constraint $v_p(k+1)=1$ to guarantee the solvability condition $\gcd(\sigma(n),n)\mid q\tau(n)$ in the non-coprime regime
outputs: verification that the linear congruence has solutions

technique(s).name: linear_congruence_gcd_reduction_and_inverse
inputs: congruence $\sigma(n)x\equiv q\tau(n)\pmod n$ and the computed gcd $\gcd(\sigma(n),n)=p$
technique(s).purpose: apply the solvability criterion and reduce to a coprime congruence modulo $n/p$; the $p$-adic constraints are used to pin down the gcd exactly, forcing interaction between linear congruence theory (LIN) and prime-exponent/divisibility control (VP)
outputs: a unique residue class $x\equiv x_*\pmod{n/p}$ and the full solution set modulo $n$

technique(s).name: q_adic_extremal_selection
technique(s).purpose: use that $q\mid(n/p)$ but $q^2\nmid(n/p)$ together with $q^2<n$ to show admissible solutions exist and to extract the unique least positive one

theorem(s).name: fundamental_theorem_arithmetic
theorem(s).role: ensures the prime exponent data $(p,k,q)$ determines $n$ and supports multiplicative evaluations

theorem(s).name: prime_power_closed_forms_for_phi_sigma_tau
theorem(s).role: provides $\varphi(p^e)=p^{e-1}(p-1)$, $\sigma(p^e)=\frac{p^{e+1}-1}{p-1}$, and $\tau(p^e)=e+1$

theorem(s).name: linear_congruence_solvability_and_solution_structure
theorem(s).role: if $ax\equiv b\pmod m$ and $d=\gcd(a,m)$, then solutions exist iff $d\mid b$; in that case solutions form a residue class modulo $m/d$

theorem(s).name: Bezout_identity_extended_euclid
theorem(s).role: constructs inverses modulo $m/d$ after gcd-reduction

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Prime factorization reduces divisor-based arithmetic functions to prime-power computations; congruences reduce via gcd and are controlled by prime-power divisibility.

critical_trick(s): Compute $\sigma(n)=\sigma(p^k)(q+1)$ and $\tau(n)=2(k+1)$ using multiplicativity, then use the $p$-adic conditions to force $\gcd(\sigma(n),n)=p$ and guarantee solvability.
Reduce the non-coprime congruence by dividing out the gcd, solve the reduced coprime congruence modulo $n/p$ via Bezout, and lift to all solutions modulo $n$.
Exploit that $q\mid(n/p)$ but $q^2\nmid(n/p)$ and $q^2<n$ to enforce the filter $v_q(x)=1$ and select the unique least positive admissible solution.

title: Dirichlet convolution and Mobius inversion on arithmetic functions - Buffed Seed

identity.short_name: Dirichlet convolution and Mobius inversion on arithmetic functions - Buffed Seed

problem_spec.summary:
Let $p<q$ be odd primes and $k\ge 2$, and set $n_0:=p^k q$. Fix an integer $A$ with $2\le A\le pq-2$ such that $\gcd(A+q-1,p)=1$ and $\gcd(A-1,q)=1$. Define an arithmetic function $F$ on the divisor lattice of $n_0$ by

F(n)=\begin{cases} n + A\,p^{v_p(n)}, & q\mid n,\\ n, & q\nmid n,\end{cases}\qquad (n\mid n_0),

where $v_p(\cdot)$ is the $p$-adic valuation. Suppose $f$ is an arithmetic function on divisors of $n_0$ satisfying $F(n)=\sum_{d\mid n} f(d)$ for every $n\mid n_0$, and write $t:=v_p(f(n_0))$. Consider the residue-class set $S:=\{x\ (\bmod\ n_0): f(n_0)x\equiv p^t\ (\bmod\ n_0)\}.
givens: $p$ and $q$ are distinct odd primes with $p<q$, and $k$ is an integer with $k\ge 2$
$n_0 = p^k q$
$A$ is an integer with $2\le A\le pq-2$, $\gcd(A+q-1,p)=1$, and $\gcd(A-1,q)=1$
For every divisor $n\mid n_0$, define $F(n)=n + A p^{v_p(n)}$ if $q\mid n$ and $F(n)=n$ if $q\nmid n$
There exists an arithmetic function $f$ on divisors of $n_0$ such that for every $n\mid n_0$ we have $F(n)=\sum_{d\mid n} f(d)$ (equivalently, $F = f * 1$ on the divisor lattice of $n_0$)
Define $t:=v_p(f(n_0))$ and $S:=\{x\ (\bmod\ n_0): f(n_0)x\equiv p^t\ (\bmod\ n_0)\}$
ask: (Stage 1 - inversion to a valuation artifact) Use Mobius inversion on the divisor lattice of $n_0$ to express $f(n_0)$ in terms of $F(n_0)$, $F(n_0/p)$, $F(n_0/q)$, $F(n_0/(pq))$, and determine $t=v_p(f(n_0))$.

(Stage 2 - linear congruence controlled by $t$) Represent $S$ by its least residues in $\{0,1,\dots,n_0-1\}$ and compute $\sum_{x\in S} x$ modulo $1000$. The required output is this sum reduced mod $1000$.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: prime base for the prime-power factor p^k in the structured modulus n_0

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: second prime factor of n_0; enforces a nontrivial composite modulus of the form p^k q

parameter(s).name: k
parameter(s).purpose: exponent controlling the prime-power component p^k; forces a nontrivial p-adic valuation regime (t>0) and a multi-solution linear congruence

parameter(s).name: A
parameter(s).domain: integer A with 2<=A<=pq-2, gcd(A+q-1,p)=1, and gcd(A-1,q)=1
parameter(s).purpose: structural twist in F that prevents collapsing to a one-step solve and guarantees invertibility after valuation reduction

invariant(s).name: convolution_associativity
invariant(s).statement: Dirichlet convolution (restricted to arithmetic functions on divisors of n_0) is associative and commutative; inversion is performed inside this algebra.

title: Dirichlet convolution and Möbius inversion on arithmetic functions

identity.short_name: Dirichlet convolution and Möbius inversion on arithmetic functions

problem_spec.summary: Work with arithmetic functions under Dirichlet (divisor-sum) convolution. Given a relation such as $F = f * 1$ (i.e., $F(n)=\sum_{d\mid n} f(d)$), recover $f$ using Möbius inversion and evaluate $f(n_0)$ for a specified input $n_0$.
givens: $F:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{Z}$ is explicitly specified (by formula or by values on the divisor lattice of $n_0$)
There exists an arithmetic function $f$ with $F = f * 1$, i.e. $F(n)=\sum_{d\mid n} f(d)$ for all $n$ in the relevant domain
$n_0\in\mathbb{N}$ is the evaluation point
ask: Compute $f(n_0)$ (and in variants: compute $f$ on all divisors of $n_0$) using Möbius inversion / convolution algebra.

parameter(s).name: n_0
parameter(s).purpose: evaluation point; determines the divisor poset involved

parameter(s).name: F_spec
parameter(s).purpose: encodes the "known" function to be inverted

parameter(s).name: relation_mode
parameter(s).purpose: selects the convolution equation to invert

invariant(s).name: convolution_associativity
invariant(s).statement: Dirichlet convolution is associative and commutative; inversion is performed inside this algebra when the convolving function is invertible.

invariant(s).name: mobius_inverse_of_1
invariant(s).statement: The Möbius function $\mu$ is the Dirichlet inverse of the constant-1 function: $\mu * 1 = \varepsilon$ (identity at $n=1$).

essential_assumption(s): The relation_mode must specify a convolution equation where the right-hand convolving function is invertible in the Dirichlet convolution algebra (e.g., $1$ with inverse $\mu$).
The instance supplies enough information about $F$ on the divisor lattice of $n_0$ to compute the divisor sums.
uniqueness_certificate(s): If $F=f*1$, then convolving both sides with $\mu$ yields $f=F*\mu$, so $f(n_0)=\sum_{d\mid n_0} \mu(d)F(n_0/d)$; the right-hand side is uniquely determined, hence the output is unique.

technique(s).name: Dirichlet_convolution_optional
technique(s).purpose: encode divisor-sum identities as algebraic products

technique(s).name: mobius_inversion_optional
technique(s).purpose: invert the divisor-sum transform

technique(s).name: mu_mobius_optional
technique(s).purpose: provide the inverse weights $\mu(d)$

technique(s).name: multiplicative_functions
technique(s).purpose: (optional) evaluate divisor sums efficiently by multiplicativity when $F$ is multiplicative

theorem(s).name: exact_division_arguments
theorem(s).role: implement divisor-sum expansions by enumerating divisors and checking $d\mid n$

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Divisor-sum transforms form a convolution algebra; Möbius inversion is inclusion–exclusion on the divisor lattice.

critical_trick(s): Rewrite the given relation as a Dirichlet convolution equation and multiply by the Dirichlet inverse (often $\mu$).
If $n_0$ is large, exploit multiplicativity to factor the divisor sum into a product over prime powers.

title: Dirichlet convolution and Möbius inversion on arithmetic functions - Buffed Seed

identity.short_name: Dirichlet convolution and Möbius inversion on arithmetic functions - Buffed Seed

problem_spec.summary:
Let $p<q$ be odd primes and $k\ge 2$, and set $n_0:=p^k q$. Fix an integer $A$ with $2\le A\le pq-2$ such that $\gcd(A+q-1,p)=1$ and $\gcd(A-1,q)=1$. Define an arithmetic function $F$ on the divisor lattice of $n_0$ by

F(n)=\begin{cases} n + A\,p^{v_p(n)}, & q\mid n,\\ n, & q\nmid n,\end{cases}\qquad (n\mid n_0),

where $v_p(\cdot)$ is the $p$-adic valuation. Suppose $f$ is an arithmetic function on divisors of $n_0$ satisfying $F(n)=\sum_{d\mid n} f(d)$ for every $n\mid n_0$, and write $t:=v_p(f(n_0))$. Consider the residue-class set $S:=\{x\ (\bmod\ n_0): f(n_0)x\equiv p^t\ (\bmod\ n_0)\}$.
givens: $p$ and $q$ are distinct odd primes with $p<q$, and $k$ is an integer with $k\ge 2$
$n_0 = p^k q$
$A$ is an integer with $2\le A\le pq-2$, $\gcd(A+q-1,p)=1$, and $\gcd(A-1,q)=1$
For every divisor $n\mid n_0$, define $F(n)=n + A p^{v_p(n)}$ if $q\mid n$ and $F(n)=n$ if $q\nmid n$
There exists an arithmetic function $f$ on divisors of $n_0$ such that for every $n\mid n_0$ we have $F(n)=\sum_{d\mid n} f(d)$ (equivalently, $F = f * 1$ on the divisor lattice of $n_0$)
Define $t:=v_p(f(n_0))$ and $S:=\{x\ (\bmod\ n_0): f(n_0)x\equiv p^t\ (\bmod\ n_0)\}$
ask: (Stage 1 - inversion to a valuation artifact) Use Möbius inversion on the divisor lattice of $n_0$ to express $f(n_0)$ in terms of $F(n_0)$, $F(n_0/p)$, $F(n_0/q)$, $F(n_0/(pq))$, and determine $t=v_p(f(n_0))$.

(Stage 2 - linear congruence controlled by $t$) Represent $S$ by its least residues in $\{0,1,\dots,n_0-1\}$ and compute $\sum_{x\in S} x$ modulo $1000$. The required output is this sum reduced mod $1000$.

parameter(s).purpose: prime base for the prime-power factor p^k in the structured modulus n_0

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: second prime factor of n_0; enforces a nontrivial composite modulus of the form p^k q

parameter(s).name: k
parameter(s).purpose: exponent controlling the prime-power component p^k; forces a nontrivial p-adic valuation regime (t>0) and a multi-solution linear congruence

parameter(s).name: A
parameter(s).domain: integer A with 2\leqA\leqpq-2, gcd(A+q-1,p)=1, and gcd(A-1,q)=1

invariant(s).name: convolution_associativity
invariant(s).statement: Dirichlet convolution (restricted to arithmetic functions on divisors of $n_0$) is associative and commutative; inversion is performed inside this algebra.

invariant(s).name: mobius_inverse_of_1
invariant(s).statement: The Möbius function $\mu$ is the Dirichlet inverse of the constant-1 function: $\mu * 1 = \varepsilon$ (identity at $n=1$).

invariant(s).name: structured_modulus_LIN
invariant(s).statement: The modulus/evaluation point has the forced structured form $n_0=p^k q$ with $p,q$ odd primes, $q\ne p$, and $k\ge 2$ (so $n_0$ is composite and has a nontrivial prime-power factor).

invariant(s).name: squarefree_reduction_for_f_n0
invariant(s).statement: Because $\mu(d)=0$ for non-squarefree $d$, the value $f(n_0)$ is determined by the four-term inclusion-exclusion: $f(n_0)=F(n_0)-F(n_0/p)-F(n_0/q)+F(n_0/(pq))$.

invariant(s).name: anti_triviality_valuation_window_VP
invariant(s).statement: The guards $\gcd(A+q-1,p)=1$ and $\gcd(A-1,q)=1$ force $\gcd((p-1)(q-1+A),pq)=1$, hence $f(n_0)=p^{k-1}(p-1)(q-1+A)$ satisfies $1\le v_p(f(n_0))=k-1<k$ and $v_q(f(n_0))=0$.

invariant(s).name: anti_triviality_noninvertible_but_solvable_congruence
invariant(s).statement: With $t=v_p(f(n_0))=k-1$, one has $\gcd(f(n_0),n_0)=p^t$ (neither $1$ nor $n_0$), so the congruence $f(n_0)x\equiv p^t\pmod{n_0}$ has multiple solutions and requires valuation-guided reduction to a coprime modulus.

essential_assumption(s): The convolution equation is of the invertible form $F=f*1$ on the divisor lattice of $n_0$, so that convolving with $\mu$ yields $f=F*\mu$.
The instance supplies enough information about $F$ on the divisor lattice of $n_0$ to compute the divisor sums.

Non-template guard: the construction enforces $\gcd(f(n_0),n_0)=p^{k-1}$ with $k\ge 2$, so Stage 2 cannot be solved by a single modular inverse step (since $f(n_0)$ is not invertible modulo $n_0$); one must compute the exact $p$-adic valuation $t$ and reduce the linear congruence accordingly.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Uniqueness of $f(n_0)$ and $t$: since $\mu*1=\varepsilon$, the solution $f$ on $n\mid n_0$ is unique and $f(n_0)=\sum_{d\mid n_0} \mu(d)F(n_0/d)$ is uniquely determined; then $t=v_p(f(n_0))$ is uniquely determined by prime-power valuation.

Uniqueness of the reported output and mod-$1000$ normalization: the congruence $f(n_0)x\equiv p^t\pmod{n_0}$ reduces (by dividing by $p^t$ and using $\gcd(f(n_0)/p^t,\, n_0/p^t)=1$ from the invariants) to a unique residue class modulo $n_0/p^t=pq$. Hence $S$ consists of exactly $p^t$ residues $x=x_0+(pq)j$ for $j=0,\dots,p^t-1$, making $\sum_{x\in S}x$ uniquely determined; the final answer is this integer reduced modulo $1000$, yielding a unique value in $\{0,1,\dots,999\}$.

Linear congruences reduce by the gcd; p-adic valuation computes that gcd when the modulus has prime-power structure.

Compute t=v_p(f(n_0)) and certify v_q(f(n_0))=0 from the parameter guards, so gcd(f(n_0),n_0)=p^t is a proper nontrivial divisor.
Divide the congruence f(n_0)x\equivp^t (mod n_0) by p^t to obtain an invertible coefficient modulo pq, then parametrize all solutions and sum them before reducing mod 1000.

title: Mobius inversion on a triple-prime modulus with dual valuation extraction, 2-adic LTE order control, prime-power order lifting, and Hensel square-root counting

identity.short_name: Mobius inversion on a triple-prime modulus with dual valuation extraction, 2-adic LTE order control, prime-power order lifting, and Hensel square-root counting

problem_spec.summary: On the divisor lattice of $n_0=2^a p^k q^2$, define a valuation-sensitive function $F$ with three additive twists: one active on multiples of $2$, one on multiples of $p$, and one on multiples of $q$. Recover $f(n_0)$ uniquely by Möbius inversion, extract both $t_2=v_2(f(n_0))$ and $t_p=v_p(f(n_0))$ to form the unit $M=f(n_0)/(2^{t_2}p^{t_p})$. Then compute (i) $r=\operatorname{ord}_{n_0}(M)$ using $2$-adic LTE for the $2^a$ component, LTE-based order lifting for odd prime powers, and CRT-lcm recombination, and (ii) the root count $s$ for $x^2\equiv M$ on the odd part using quadratic reciprocity, Hensel lifting, and CRT counting. Output $r+s$.
givens: $p$ and $q$ are distinct odd primes; $a\ge 3$ and $k\ge 2$
$A,B,C$ are integers with $\gcd(A,q)=1$ and $\gcd(B,p)=1$
For each divisor $n\mid n_0$ with $u=v_2(n)$, $i=v_p(n)$, $j=v_q(n)$, define $F(n)=n+C\mathbf{1}_{u\ge 1}\cdot n/2 + B\mathbf{1}_{i\ge 1}\cdot n/p + A\mathbf{1}_{j\ge 1}\cdot n/q$
There exists an arithmetic function $f$ on divisors of $n_0$ such that $F(n)=\sum_{d\mid n} f(d)$ for all $n\mid n_0$
Let $r=\operatorname{ord}_{n_0}(M)$ and $s=\#\{x\bmod (p^k q^2):x^2\equiv M \pmod{p^k q^2}\}$
ask: Compute $f(n_0)$ and determine $t_2$, $t_p$, and $M$
Determine $r=\operatorname{ord}_{n_0}(M)$ and $s$, then output $r+s$

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: base odd prime for the $p^k$ component and $p$-adic lifting

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: base odd prime for the $q^2$ component and residue/order computations

parameter(s).name: a
parameter(s).purpose: depth of the $2$-power modulus $2^a$ introducing a 2-adic component in CRT

parameter(s).name: k
parameter(s).purpose: depth of the prime-power modulus $p^k$

parameter(s).name: A
parameter(s).purpose: $q$-side twist coefficient in $F$

parameter(s).name: B
parameter(s).purpose: $p$-side twist coefficient in $F$

parameter(s).name: C
parameter(s).purpose: $2$-side twist coefficient in $F$

invariant(s).name: convolution_encoding
invariant(s).statement: $F(n)=\sum_{d\mid n} f(d)$ is equivalent to $F=f*\mathbf{1}$ under Dirichlet convolution on divisors of $n_0$

invariant(s).name: mobius_inverse_of_1
invariant(s).statement: $\mu*\mathbf{1}=\varepsilon$ on the divisor lattice, so $f$ is uniquely determined by $f=F*\mu$

invariant(s).name: squarefree_reduction_for_2a_p_k_q2
invariant(s).statement: For $n_0=2^a p^k q^2$, Möbius inversion collapses to the 8-term inclusion-exclusion over squarefree $d\mid 2pq$

invariant(s).name: full_support_evaluation
invariant(s).statement: If $a\ge 3$ and $k\ge 2$ (and $q^2$ fixed), every $n_0/d$ for squarefree $d\mid 2pq$ remains divisible by $2,p,q$, so the full three-twist formula for $F(n_0/d)$ applies uniformly

title: Mobius inversion to a quadratic congruence solved by Hensel lifting and CRT

identity.short_name: Mobius inversion to a quadratic congruence solved by Hensel lifting and CRT

problem_spec.summary: On the divisor lattice of $n_0=p^k q^2$, define a valuation-aware twist $F$ that adds $A p^{v_p(n)} q^{v_q(n)-1}$ on multiples of $q$. Recover $f(n_0)$ by Mobius inversion, extract $t=v_p(f(n_0))$, set $M=f(n_0)/p^t$, and solve the quadratic congruence $x^2\equiv M\pmod{n_0}$ using Hensel lifting on $p^k$ and $q^2$ and CRT. Then select the canonical half-set $S_{<}$ and sum it modulo $1000$.
givens: $p$ and $q$ are distinct odd primes with $p<q$, and $k$ is an integer with $k\ge 3$
$A$ is an integer with $2\le A\le pq-2$, $\gcd(A,q)=1$, and $\gcd(q+A,pq)=1$
For every divisor $n\mid n_0$, define $F(n)=n$ if $q\nmid n$, and $F(n)=n + A p^{v_p(n)} q^{v_q(n)-1}$ if $q\mid n$
There exists an arithmetic function $f$ on divisors of $n_0$ such that for every $n\mid n_0$ we have $F(n)=\sum_{d\mid n} f(d)$ (equivalently, $F = f * 1$ on the divisor lattice of $n_0$)
Define $t:=v_p(f(n_0))$ and $M:=f(n_0)/p^t$
Define $S:=\{x\ (\bmod\ n_0): x^2\equiv M\ (\bmod\ n_0)\}$ and $S_<:=\{x\in S:0\le x< n_0/2\}$ using least residues
ask: (Stage 1 - inversion to a structured residue) Use Mobius inversion on the divisor lattice of $n_0=p^k q^2$ to compute $f(n_0)$, then determine $t=v_p(f(n_0))$ and $M=f(n_0)/p^t$.

(Stage 2 - quadratic congruence via prime-power lifting) Solve $x^2\equiv M\pmod{n_0}$ by lifting roots modulo $p$ and $q$ to $p^k$ and $q^2$ via Hensel's lemma and combining via CRT. Form $S_<$ and compute $\sum_{x\in S_<}x$ modulo $1000$.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: prime base for the prime-power component p^k in n_0; enables nontrivial Hensel lifting modulo p^k

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: second prime base; appears as a square factor q^2 in n_0 to force a second lifting regime

parameter(s).name: k
parameter(s).purpose: exponent controlling the depth of the p-adic lifting stage

parameter(s).name: A
parameter(s).domain: integer A with 2<=A<=pq-2, gcd(A,q)=1, gcd(q+A,pq)=1, and M=(p-1)(q-1)(q+A) a quadratic residue mod both p and q
parameter(s).purpose: twist parameter shaping f(n_0) and ensuring solvability of x^2 equiv M

invariant(s).name: convolution_associativity
