title: Mobius inversion with a two-sided valuation twist feeding an LTE plus CRT multiplicative-order computation

identity.short_name: Mobius inversion with a two-sided valuation twist feeding an LTE plus CRT multiplicative-order computation

problem_spec.summary: On the divisor lattice of $n_0=p^k q^2$, define a valuation-sensitive function $F$ that adds one twist on multiples of $q$ and a second twist on multiples of $p$. Recover $f(n_0)$ uniquely via Mobius inversion, extract the $p$-adic factor $t=v_p(f(n_0))$ and $M=f(n_0)/p^t$, then compute the multiplicative order of $M$ modulo $n_0$ by combining LTE-based order lifting on $p^k$ and $q^2$ with a CRT-lcm recombination.
givens: $p$ and $q$ are distinct odd primes and $k\ge 3$
$A$ and $B$ are integers with $\mathrm{gcd}(A,q)=1$ and $\mathrm{gcd}(B,p)=1$

For each divisor $n\mid n_0$ with $i=v_p(n)$ and $j=v_q(n)$, define $F$ by the rules: $F(1)=1$; if $j=0$ and $i\ge 1$ then $F(p^i)=p^i+Bp^{i-1}$; if $i=0$ and $j\ge 1$ then $F(q^j)=q^j+Aq^{j-1}$; if $i\ge 1$ and $j\ge 1$ then $F(p^i q^j)=p^i q^j + A p^i q^{j-1} + B p^{i-1} q^j$
There exists an arithmetic function $f$ on divisors of $n_0$ such that for every $n\mid n_0$, $F(n)=\sum_{d\mid n} f(d)$
Define $t=v_p(f(n_0))$ and $M=f(n_0)/p^t$
ask: Compute $f(n_0)$ and determine $t$ and $M$
Determine the least positive integer $r$ such that $M^r\equiv 1(\mathrm{mod}\ n_0)$

parameter(s).purpose: base prime for the $p^k$ component and $p$-adic order lifting

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: base prime for the $q^2$ component and $q$-adic order lifting

parameter(s).name: k
parameter(s).purpose: depth of the prime-power modulus $p^k$

parameter(s).name: A
parameter(s).purpose: $q$-side valuation twist in $F$

parameter(s).name: B
parameter(s).purpose: $p$-side valuation twist in $F$

invariant(s).name: convolution_encoding
invariant(s).statement: $F(n)=\sum_{d\mid n} f(d)$ is equivalent to $F=f*\mathbf{1}$ under Dirichlet convolution on divisors of $n_0$

invariant(s).name: mobius_inverse_of_1
invariant(s).statement: $\mu*\mathbf{1}=\varepsilon$ on the divisor lattice, so $f$ is uniquely determined by $f=F*\mu$

invariant(s).name: LTE_valuation_growth
invariant(s).statement: For an odd prime $\ell$ with $\ell\mid (a-1)$, LTE gives $v_\ell(a^{\ell^m}-1)=v_\ell(a-1)+m$

invariant(s).statement: If $\mathrm{gcd}(m,n)=1$ and $\mathrm{gcd}(a,mn)=1$ then $\mathrm{ord}_{mn}(a)=\mathrm{lcm}(\mathrm{ord}_m(a),\mathrm{ord}_n(a))$

essential_assumption(s): Mobius inversion applies on the divisor lattice, so $F=f*\mathbf{1}$ implies $f=F*\mu$
The computed $M$ is coprime to $n_0$, so its multiplicative order modulo $n_0$ is defined
$p$ and $q$ are odd primes so LTE applies in the form used
uniqueness_certificate(s): Mobius inversion uniquely determines $f(n_0)$ and therefore uniquely determines $t$ and $M$
Lemma 2 fixes the local orders from $v_p(M-1)$ and $v_q(M-1)$, and Lemma 3 then fixes the global order $r$ uniquely

title: Totient-sigma congruence with Hensel/2-adic/order-coupled square filter

identity.short_name: Totient-sigma congruence with Hensel/2-adic/order-coupled square filter

problem_spec.summary: Let $n=p^kqr$ with distinct odd primes $p<q<r$ and $k\ge 2$. Solve the congruence $\varphi(n)x\equiv p^{k-1}qr\,\sigma(n)\pmod n$ via gcd-reduction and CRT, then impose a square filter $x/(qr)=y^2$ together with three coupled constraints on $y$: a Hensel-lifted quadratic condition modulo $p^2$, a fixed 2-adic square class modulo $2^a$, and an exact multiplicative order condition modulo $q$. These constraints force a unique least positive admissible $x_0$, and the output is $x_0\bmod 999$.
givens: $p,q,r$ are distinct odd primes with $p<q<r$, and $k$ is an integer with $k\ge 2$.
Define $n=p^kqr$. Let $\varphi(n)$ be Euler's totient function and $\sigma(n)$ the sum of positive divisors of $n$.
Nondegeneracy guards: $p\nmid(q-1)(r-1)$ and $q\nmid(r-1)$.
Fix integers $a\ge 3$, $u$ such that $u$ is a quadratic residue modulo $2^a$.
Fix an integer $c$ such that $y^2\equiv c\pmod p$ has a solution $y_0\not\equiv 0\pmod p$ (so it Hensel-lifts uniquely to modulo $p^2$).
Fix $d$ with $d\mid(q-1)$.
Consider the congruence $\varphi(n)x\equiv p^{k-1}qr\,\sigma(n)\pmod n$ together with filters $v_q(x)=v_r(x)=1$ and $x/(qr)=y^2$ for some integer $y>0$.
Coupled constraints on $y$: $y^2\equiv c\pmod{p^2}$, $y^2\equiv u\pmod{2^a}$, and $\operatorname{ord}_q(y)=d$.
Let $x_0$ be the least positive $x$ satisfying all conditions.
ask: Determine the unique integer $N\in\{0,1,\dots,998\}$ such that $N\equiv x_0\pmod{999}$.

parameter(s).name: $p$
parameter(s).purpose: prime-power component $p^k$ creating the gcd bottleneck

parameter(s).name: $k$
parameter(s).purpose: controls the size of the forced gcd-reduction

parameter(s).name: $q$
parameter(s).purpose: prime factor forced into all solutions and the modulus for the order constraint

parameter(s).name: $r$
parameter(s).purpose: second forced prime factor and valuation constraint at $r$

parameter(s).name: $c$
parameter(s).purpose: target for the Hensel-lifted square condition $y^2\equiv c\pmod{p^2}$

parameter(s).name: $a$
parameter(s).purpose: exponent defining the 2-adic modulus $2^a$

parameter(s).name: $u$
parameter(s).domain: odd integer that is a square modulo $2^a$
parameter(s).purpose: 2-adic square-class target $y^2\equiv u\pmod{2^a}$

parameter(s).name: $d$
parameter(s).purpose: prescribed multiplicative order $\operatorname{ord}_q(y)=d$

invariant(s).name: structured_modulus_shape
invariant(s).statement: $n=p^kqr$ with $p,q,r$ distinct odd primes and $k\ge 2$.

invariant(s).name: totient_gcd_bottleneck
invariant(s).statement: Under $p\nmid(q-1)(r-1)$ and $q\nmid(r-1)$, $\gcd(\varphi(n),n)=p^{k-1}$.

invariant(s).name: modified_rhs_solvability
invariant(s).statement: With right-hand side $p^{k-1}qr\,\sigma(n)$, the divisibility condition $\gcd(\varphi(n),n)\mid p^{k-1}qr\,\sigma(n)$ is automatic.

invariant(s).name: crt_forces_qr_divisibility
invariant(s).statement: After gcd-reduction to modulus $n/p^{k-1}=pqr$, the reduced congruence forces $x\equiv 0\pmod q$ and $x\equiv 0\pmod r$, hence $qr\mid x$.

invariant(s).name: hensel_lift_square_constraint
invariant(s).statement: If $y^2\equiv c\pmod p$ has a solution $y_0\not\equiv 0\pmod p$, then $y^2\equiv c\pmod{p^2}$ has a unique lift in each of the two classes $y\equiv \pm y_0\pmod p$.

invariant(s).name: two_adic_square_class_constraint
invariant(s).statement: For $a\ge 3$, the constraint $y^2\equiv u\pmod{2^a}$ restricts $y$ to specific residue classes modulo $2^a$.

invariant(s).name: order_filter_constraint
invariant(s).statement: The constraint $\operatorname{ord}_q(y)=d$ restricts $y\bmod q$ to units of exact order $d$ in $(\mathbb{Z}/q\mathbb{Z})^\times$.

invariant(s).name: coupled_crt_on_y
invariant(s).statement: The constraints on $y$ modulo $p^2$, $2^a$, and $q$ combine (CRT + order filtering) to a finite set of residue classes for $y$ modulo $p^2\cdot 2^a\cdot q$, from which the least positive admissible $y$ is determined.

essential_assumption(s): $k\ge 2$ so $\gcd(\varphi(n),n)>1$ and gcd-reduction is nontrivial.
Guards $p\nmid(q-1)(r-1)$ and $q\nmid(r-1)$ so the reduced coefficient is invertible modulo $pqr$.
$c$ admits a simple-root Hensel lift from modulo $p$ to modulo $p^2$.
$u$ is a quadratic residue modulo $2^a$ so the 2-adic constraint is consistent.
$d\mid(q-1)$ so $\operatorname{ord}_q(y)=d$ is meaningful for $\gcd(y,q)=1$.
The coupled constraints admit at least one solution, and the task selects the least positive one.
uniqueness_certificate(s):
Let $d_0=\gcd(\varphi(n),n)=p^{k-1}$. The congruence reduces to modulus $n/d_0=pqr$, and under the guards the reduced coefficient is coprime to $pqr$, hence the reduced congruence has a unique residue class; in particular it forces $qr\mid x$.
Writing $x=qr\,y^2$ (square filter) and imposing the constraints $y^2\equiv c\pmod{p^2}$ (Hensel-lifted), $y^2\equiv u\pmod{2^a}$ (2-adic square class), and $\operatorname{ord}_q(y)=d$ (order filter) restrict $y$ to finitely many residue classes modulo $p^2\cdot 2^a\cdot q$.
The least positive representative among these classes is unique, hence yields a unique least admissible $y$ and therefore a unique least admissible $x_0=qr\,y^2$. The normalized answer $N=x_0\bmod 999$ in $\{0,\dots,998\}$ is then unique.

outputs: unique reduced class and full solution set

technique(s).purpose: force $q\mid x$ and $r\mid x$ and obtain the full solution progression

title: Totient-sigma congruence with prime-power gcd and CRT selection

identity.short_name: Totient-sigma congruence with prime-power gcd and CRT selection

problem_spec.summary: For n = p^k q with odd primes, force a non-invertible linear congruence via gcd(varphi(n),n)=p^{k-1} under a nondegeneracy guard, ensure solvability by requiring p^{k-1} | (q+1), reduce modulo pq, solve the reduced class via CRT, then apply an exact q-adic valuation filter v_q(x)=1 to select a unique least positive solution and report it modulo 1000.
givens: p,q are distinct odd primes with p<q, and k is an integer with k>=2.
Define n=p^k q, and let varphi(n) and sigma(n) denote Euler's totient and the sum of positive divisors of n.
Admissibility: p^{k-1} | (q+1) and p does not divide (q-1).
Consider the congruence varphi(n) x \equiv q sigma(n) (mod n) and restrict attention to solutions x with v_q(x)=1.
ask:
Let p<q be distinct odd primes and let k>=2 be an integer. Set n=p^k q. Assume p^{k-1} | (q+1) and p does not divide (q-1).
Consider the congruence varphi(n) x \equiv q sigma(n) (mod n).
Among all integers x satisfying this congruence with v_q(x)=1 (i.e., q | x but q^2 does not divide x), let x0 be the least positive such solution.
Prove that x0 exists and is unique, and report the normalized value N in {0,1,...,999} such that N \equiv x0 (mod 1000).

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: repeated prime-power component p^k controlling the gcd regime via gcd(varphi(n),n)=p^{k-1} under the guard p does not divide (q-1)

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: second prime factor and the prime defining the valuation filter v_q(x)=1

parameter(s).name: k
parameter(s).purpose: exponent creating a nontrivial prime-power gcd and the reduction modulus pq

invariant(s).name: structured_modulus_shape
invariant(s).statement: For every admissible instance, n=p^k q with p,q distinct odd primes and k>=2.

invariant(s).name: prime_power_gcd_for_totient_under_guard
invariant(s).statement: If p does not divide (q-1) then gcd(varphi(p^k q), p^k q)=p^{k-1}, so varphi(n) is non-invertible modulo n whenever k>=2.

invariant(s).name: solvability_forced_by_q_plus_1
invariant(s).statement: Since sigma(p^k q)=sigma(p^k)(q+1) and p does not divide sigma(p^k), the condition p^{k-1} | (q+1) forces p^{k-1} | q sigma(n), guaranteeing solvability of varphi(n)x \equiv q sigma(n) (mod n) in the non-coprime regime.

invariant(s).name: reduced_modulus_is_pq_and_crt_applicable
invariant(s).statement: After dividing by d=p^{k-1}, the modulus becomes n/d=pq. The guard p does not divide (q-1) ensures the reduced coefficient (p-1)(q-1) is coprime to pq, so the reduced class is unique and can be solved by CRT on mod p and mod q.

invariant(s).name: q_adic_filter_nonvacuous
invariant(s).statement: Reducing the reduced congruence modulo q forces x \equiv 0 (mod q), so every solution is a multiple of q. Since pq is divisible by q but not by q^2, the filter v_q(x)=1 is nonvacuous and supports a unique least positive admissible solution.

essential_assumption(s): The congruence is intentionally in a non-invertible regime because gcd(varphi(n),n)=p^{k-1}>1 for k>=2; gcd-reduction is mandatory.
The divisibility guard p^{k-1} | (q+1) is required so that gcd(varphi(n),n) divides q sigma(n) and solutions exist.
The nondegeneracy guard p does not divide (q-1) is required so the reduced coefficient is invertible modulo pq, making the reduced solution class unique.
The valuation filter v_q(x)=1 is part of the task definition and is used to select a unique least positive representative from the full solution set.
uniqueness_certificate(s):
Let d=gcd(varphi(n),n)=p^{k-1} (under the guard p does not divide (q-1)). The condition p^{k-1} | (q+1) ensures d | q sigma(n), so the congruence is solvable and reduces to a coprime congruence modulo n/d=pq.
This reduced congruence has exactly one residue class modulo pq, computable by CRT. Lifting back yields all solutions x=x_*+t(pq).
The reduced congruence forces q | x_*, hence q | x for all solutions; since pq is divisible by q but not by q^2, there exist solutions with v_q(x)=1, and the monotone shift by pq yields a unique least positive such solution x0.
The reported output is x0 mod 1000 in {0,...,999}.

technique(s).purpose: determine d=gcd(varphi(n),n) and verify the solvability divisibility d | q sigma(n)
outputs: a forced nontrivial gcd and solvability in the non-invertible regime

technique(s).name: linear_congruence_gcd_reduction_and_inverse
inputs: congruence varphi(n)x \equiv q sigma(n) (mod n) and d=gcd(varphi(n),n)
technique(s).purpose: reduce the congruence modulo n/d and obtain a unique reduced residue class when the reduced coefficient is coprime to n/d
outputs: unique reduced residue class modulo pq and full lifted solution set

technique(s).name: Chinese_remainder_theorem
inputs: reduced congruence modulo pq and its reductions modulo p and q
technique(s).purpose: solve the reduced residue class by splitting into mod-p and mod-q constraints and recombining uniquely

technique(s).name: q_adic_extremal_selection
inputs: lifted solution set x=x_*+t(pq) and valuation constraint v_q(x)=1
technique(s).purpose: classify which t are admissible and select the unique least positive admissible solution

technique(s).name: modular_normalization_mod_1000
technique(s).purpose: output canonical N in {0,...,999} with N \equiv x0 (mod 1000)

theorem(s).role: justifies prime-power decomposition and multiplicativity setup

theorem(s).name: prime_power_closed_forms_for_phi_sigma_tau
theorem(s).role: provides varphi(p^e)=p^{e-1}(p-1) and sigma(p^e)=(p^{e+1}-1)/(p-1)

theorem(s).name: linear_congruence_solvability_and_solution_structure
theorem(s).role: if ax \equiv b (mod m) and d=gcd(a,m), then solutions exist iff d | b; then solutions form one class modulo m/d

theorem(s).name: Bezout_identity_extended_euclid
theorem(s).role: guarantees modular inverses after gcd-reduction when the reduced coefficient is coprime to the reduced modulus

theorem(s).name: Chinese_remainder_theorem
theorem(s).role: recombines congruences modulo coprime moduli to a unique class modulo their product

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Prime factorization plus modular reduction: arithmetic functions reduce to prime-power evaluations, then congruences reduce via gcd and CRT.

critical_trick(s): Replace the parent-family sigma/tau forcing bottleneck with a prime-power totient gcd bottleneck: use d=gcd(varphi(n),n)=p^{k-1}, then enforce p^{k-1} | (q+1) so d | q sigma(n) and gcd-reduction is solvable.
After reduction, solve the unique class modulo pq using a CRT-driven split into mod-p and mod-q constraints.
Use the exact valuation filter v_q(x)=1 on the lifted arithmetic progression x=x_*+t(pq) to select the unique least positive admissible solution.

task(s).name: primary_structural_check
task(s).reason:
Correctness checks with domain-agnostic preflight attempts:
- recompute n, varphi(n), sigma(n); confirm d=gcd(varphi(n),n) and that d | q sigma(n) so the congruence is solvable;
- perform gcd-reduction to modulus n/d and solve the reduced congruence, then lift and apply the v_q(x)=1 filter to obtain x0 and x0 mod 1000.
Preflight Attempt A: naive guess x=q; verify it does NOT satisfy the original congruence (explicit nonzero remainder).
Preflight Attempt B: bounded search over admissible x <= 1500; confirm no solution appears, so x0 is not a tiny-range artifact.
Preflight Attempt C: using only partial reductions (e.g. modulo q alone, or modulo 3 alone) leaves multiple candidates for N in {0,...,999}; full gcd-reduction + CRT + valuation selection is necessary.

task(s).name: secondary_independent_check
task(s).reason:
Independent validation by direct search:
enumerate x=q*m in increasing order with q does not divide m (so v_q(x)=1) and directly test varphi(n)x \equiv q sigma(n) (mod n); confirm the first admissible hit equals x0 and matches x0 mod 1000.

task(s).name: novelty_equivalence_audit
task(s).reason:
Canonicalize and compare the parent and daughter congruence constraints with sympy:
represent each by (A,B,M) with Ax \equiv B (mod M); compute a canonicalized triple using expand+simplify+factor+cancel+together+collect in x, normalize by dividing common integer gcds, and attempt deterministic transforms (identity and p<->q swap where meaningful). Reject if canonicalized triples match under allowed transforms.

title: ```text

identity.short_name: Totient-sigma congruence with CRT and square-filter minimization

problem_spec.summary:
Work with $n=p^kqr$ where $p,q,r$ are distinct odd primes and $k\ge 2$.
Use $\gcd(\varphi(n),n)=p^{k-1}$ (under nondegeneracy guards) to force a mandatory gcd-reduction of a linear congruence.
After reduction to modulus $pqr$, CRT forces $qr\mid x$.
Add filters $v_q(x)=v_r(x)=1$ and $x/(qr)$ is a perfect square, turning the lifted solution progression into $y^2=t_0+pt$ and selecting the unique least positive admissible solution by an extremal square-in-progression argument.
Output the result modulo $999$.
givens: $p,q,r$ are distinct odd primes with $p<q<r$, and $k$ is an integer with $k\ge 2$.
Define $n=p^kqr$, and let $\varphi(n)$ and $\sigma(n)$ denote Euler's totient and the sum of divisors of $n$.
Nondegeneracy guards: $p\nmid (q-1)(r-1)$ and $q\nmid (r-1)$.
Solvability guard: $p^{k-1}\mid (q+1)(r+1)$.
Consider the congruence $\varphi(n)x\equiv (qr)\sigma(n)\pmod n$ with filters $v_q(x)=v_r(x)=1$ and $x/(qr)$ a perfect square.
ask:
Let $n=p^kqr$ satisfy the givens.
Among all integers $x$ satisfying $\varphi(n)x\equiv (qr)\sigma(n)\pmod n$ with $v_q(x)=v_r(x)=1$ and $x/(qr)$ a perfect square, let $x_0$ be the least positive such $x$.
Determine the unique integer $N\in\{0,1,\dots,998\}$ such that $N\equiv x_0\pmod{999}$.

parameter(s).name: $p$
parameter(s).purpose: prime-power component $p^k$ producing the nontrivial gcd $\gcd(\varphi(n),n)=p^{k-1}$

parameter(s).name: $k$
parameter(s).purpose: exponent controlling the gcd bottleneck and forcing gcd-reduction

parameter(s).name: $q$
parameter(s).purpose: prime factor forced into all solutions and used in the valuation constraint $v_q(x)=1$

parameter(s).name: $r$
parameter(s).purpose: second forced prime factor and valuation constraint $v_r(x)=1$

invariant(s).name: structured_modulus_shape
invariant(s).statement: $n=p^kqr$ with $p,q,r$ distinct odd primes and $k\ge 2$.

invariant(s).name: totient_gcd_bottleneck
invariant(s).statement: Under the guards $p\nmid (q-1)(r-1)$ and $q\nmid (r-1)$, $\gcd(\varphi(n),n)=p^{k-1}$.

invariant(s).name: sigma_factorization_and_solvability
invariant(s).statement: $\sigma(n)=\sigma(p^k)(q+1)(r+1)$ and $p\nmid \sigma(p^k)$, so the guard $p^{k-1}\mid (q+1)(r+1)$ implies $p^{k-1}\mid (qr)\sigma(n)$.

invariant(s).name: crt_forces_qr_divisibility
invariant(s).statement: After gcd-reduction to modulus $n/p^{k-1}=pqr$, the reduced congruence implies $x\equiv 0\pmod q$ and $x\equiv 0\pmod r$, hence $qr\mid x$ for every solution.

invariant(s).name: square_filter_progression
invariant(s).statement: Writing solutions as $x=qr(t_0+pt)$, the filter $x/(qr)$ is a perfect square becomes $y^2=t_0+pt$, and the least admissible $y$ forces a unique least positive $x_0$.

essential_assumption(s): $k\ge 2$ so that $\gcd(\varphi(n),n)>1$ and gcd-reduction is nontrivial.
Solvability guard $p^{k-1}\mid (q+1)(r+1)$ ensures $\gcd(\varphi(n),n)\mid (qr)\sigma(n)$.
Nondegeneracy guards ensure the reduced coefficient is invertible modulo $pqr$, giving a unique reduced residue class.
Filters $v_q(x)=v_r(x)=1$ and $x/(qr)$ a perfect square are part of the task and are needed to select a unique least positive solution.
uniqueness_certificate(s):
Let $d=\gcd(\varphi(n),n)=p^{k-1}$. Under the solvability guard, the congruence reduces to one congruence modulo $n/d=pqr$.
Under the nondegeneracy guards, the reduced coefficient is coprime to $pqr$, so the reduced congruence has a unique residue class, computable by CRT; in particular it forces $qr\mid x$.
Lifting yields $x=qr(t_0+pt)$. The square filter forces $y^2=t_0+pt$, and the least positive root $y_0$ of $y^2\equiv t_0\pmod p$ yields the unique least positive admissible $x_0=qr\,y_0^2$.
The valuation constraints enforce $\gcd(y_0,qr)=1$, so $v_q(x_0)=v_r(x_0)=1$.

title: Capstone: build $N$ by CRT then compute $v_p(N^t\!\pm\!1)$

identity.short_name: Capstone: build $N$ by CRT then compute $v_p(N^t\!\pm\!1)$

problem_spec.summary: Capstone CHAIN: (1) Use CRT on prime-power constraints to construct a canonical integer $T$ (least nonnegative solution). (2) Feed $T$ into valuation/exponent-lifting (LTE/Hensel-light) to compute an exact $p$-adic valuation of $T^t\pm 1$ or to certify a desired prime-power divisibility.
givens: A finite set of congruences specifying $T$ modulo pairwise coprime prime powers, typically of the form $T\equiv 1\pmod{p_i^{\alpha_i}}$ and/or $T\equiv -1\pmod{q_j^{\beta_j}}$.
A query prime $p$ (often among the $p_i$) and exponent $t\in\mathbb{N}$ for which $v=v_p(T^t\pm 1)$ is requested.
ask: Let $T$ be the least nonnegative solution of the congruence system (CRT). Compute $v=v_p(T^t\pm 1)$ exactly.

parameter(s).name: prime_power_constraints
parameter(s).domain: finite list of congruences modulo pairwise coprime prime powers
parameter(s).purpose: defines the CRT construction stage

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: the valuation prime queried in stage (2)

parameter(s).name: t
parameter(s).purpose: exponent in $T^t\pm 1$

parameter(s).name: sign_variant
parameter(s).purpose: selects $T^t-1$ or $T^t+1$ in the valuation stage

invariant(s).name: interface_T
invariant(s).statement: Stage (1) outputs the canonical integer $T$ (least nonnegative CRT solution). Stage (2) consumes this same $T$ to evaluate $v_p(T^t\pm 1)$.

invariant(s).name: valuation_controlled_by_local_congruence
invariant(s).statement: If $T\equiv 1\pmod{p^{\alpha}}$ then $v_p(T^t-1)$ can be computed via LTE as $\alpha + v_p(t)$ under standard admissibility conditions (with analogous statements for $T\equiv -1$ and the plus case).

essential_assumption(s): The congruence moduli are pairwise coprime (or otherwise satisfy full CRT compatibility) so that a unique class modulo $M$ exists.
LTE/Hensel-light steps require admissibility (e.g., $p\mid (T\mp 1)$ and $p\nmid T$ for odd $p$); instances must ensure the correct branch conditions.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Global uniqueness is chained: CRT gives a unique residue class for $T$ modulo $M$, and choosing the least nonnegative representative fixes $T$ uniquely. Then the $p$-adic valuation $v_p(T^t\pm 1)$ is uniquely defined. Thus the composed output is globally unique and not the result of two unrelated subtasks.

technique(s).purpose: construct $T$ with prescribed local behavior at several primes simultaneously

technique(s).name: v_p_constraints_on_congruences

technique(s).purpose: lift valuation from $T\mp 1$ to $T^t\mp 1$

theorem(s).name: lifting_exponents_LTE
theorem(s).role: core valuation-lifting principle used in stage (2)

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Local conditions modulo prime powers can be glued by CRT into a single integer; valuations then read off exactly how strongly those local conditions persist under exponentiation.

critical_trick(s): Define the interface integer $T$ by CRT so that $T\equiv \pm 1$ modulo specified prime powers.
Apply LTE to convert $v_p(T\mp 1)$ into $v_p(T^t\mp 1)$, keeping careful track of the correct sign and the $p=2$ parity branch if needed.

title: Capstone: build $N$ by CRT then compute $v_p(N^t\!\pm\!1)$ - Buffed Seed

identity.short_name: Capstone: build $N$ by CRT then compute $v_p(N^t\!\pm\!1)$ - Buffed Seed

problem_spec.summary: Two-stage chain (divisibility wrapper): an integer $T$ is constructed as the least nonnegative solution of a CRT system of pairwise coprime prime-power congruences that forces prescribed local $p$-adic and $q$-adic behavior of $T\!\pm\!1$ at two distinct odd primes. From these forced local valuations, an exponent $t$ is defined by a minimal simultaneous prime-power divisibility requirement on $T^t-1$ and $T^t+1$. Finally, a normalized integer is extracted by reducing a derived expression modulo $1000$.
givens: Let $p,q$ be distinct odd primes with $p,q\not\in\{2,5\}$, and let $\alpha,\beta$ be integers with $\alpha,\beta\ge 1$.
Define $M=p^{\alpha+1}q^{\beta+1}$ and let $T$ be the least nonnegative integer satisfying the CRT system $T\equiv 1+p^{\alpha}\pmod{p^{\alpha+1}}$ and $T\equiv -1+q^{\beta}\pmod{q^{\beta+1}}$.
(These congruences force $v_p(T-1)=\alpha$ and $v_q(T+1)=\beta$ exactly, while keeping $p\nmid T$ and $q\nmid T$.)
ask: (Stage 1: determine the interface exponent.) Let $t$ be the smallest positive integer such that both $p^{2\alpha}\mid (T^t-1)$ and $q^{2\beta}\mid (T^t+1)$ hold. Prove that such a $t$ exists and is uniquely determined by the givens.

(Stage 2: compute a normalized residue.) Define

A \equiv \frac{T^t-1}{p^{2\alpha}} + \frac{T^t+1}{q^{2\beta}} \pmod{1000},

where the divisions are in the integers. Output the unique integer representative of $A$ in $\{0,1,\dots,999\}$.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: one prime defining the CRT modulus and the $p$-adic valuation constraints

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: second prime defining the CRT modulus and the $q$-adic valuation constraints

parameter(s).name: alpha
parameter(s).purpose: forces the exact local condition $v_p(T-1)=\alpha$ via a prime-power congruence

parameter(s).name: beta
parameter(s).purpose: forces the exact local condition $v_q(T+1)=\beta$ via a prime-power congruence

parameter(s).name: prime_power_constraints
parameter(s).domain: CRT system on pairwise coprime moduli containing at least the two prime powers $p^{\alpha+1}$ and $q^{\beta+1}$ (so at least one modulus is a nontrivial prime power with exponent $\ge 2$)
parameter(s).purpose: structural descriptor: ensures the construction stage is genuinely CRT over non-prime moduli (not merely mod primes)

invariant(s).name: interface_T_to_t_chain
invariant(s).statement: Stage (1) fixes a unique integer $T\in[0,M)$ by CRT; Stage (1) then defines $t$ by a minimal simultaneous divisibility requirement on $T^t\!\pm\!1$; Stage (2) computes the final residue from this same $(T,t)$ pair.

invariant(s).name: nontrivial_CRT_profile
invariant(s).statement: The CRT moduli include the pairwise coprime prime powers $p^{\alpha+1}$ and $q^{\beta+1}$ with $p\ne q$ and $\alpha,\beta\ge 1$, so at least one modulus has exponent $\ge 2$ and the construction is not a one-modulus or prime-modulus triviality.

invariant(s).name: exact_local_valuations
invariant(s).statement: The congruences $T\equiv 1+p^{\alpha}\pmod{p^{\alpha+1}}$ and $T\equiv -1+q^{\beta}\pmod{q^{\beta+1}}$ imply $v_p(T-1)=\alpha$ and $v_q(T+1)=\beta$ (not merely lower bounds), and also $p\nmid T$, $q\nmid T$.

invariant(s).name: LTE_admissibility_guard
invariant(s).statement: Because $p,q$ are odd and do not divide $T$, LTE applies in the forms $v_p(T^t-1)=v_p(T-1)+v_p(t)$ and (for odd $t$) $v_q(T^t+1)=v_q(T+1)+v_q(t)$; the minimality of $t$ forces the required parity and exactness.

essential_assumption(s): The CRT moduli are pairwise coprime and include at least two distinct odd prime powers, so the congruence system defines a unique residue class modulo $M=p^{\alpha+1}q^{\beta+1}$ and the least nonnegative representative $T$ is well-defined.

LTE steps are used only under admissibility: $p,q$ are odd, $p\mid (T-1)$, $q\mid (T+1)$, and $p,q\nmid T$; the congruences are required to force these conditions and to avoid the $p=2$ special branches.

The exponent $t$ is *not* given as a parameter; it is defined via a minimal coupled divisibility condition at two different primes. This blocks a one-shot 'apply LTE with a supplied exponent' template and forces the CRT\tovaluation interface reasoning before any reduction modulo $1000$.
uniqueness_certificate(s): CRT gives a unique residue class for $T$ modulo $M=p^{\alpha+1}q^{\beta+1}$, and choosing the least nonnegative representative fixes $T$ uniquely.

Given this $T$, LTE converts the divisibility thresholds $p^{2\alpha}\mid (T^t-1)$ and $q^{2\beta}\mid (T^t+1)$ into precise constraints on $v_p(t)$, $v_q(t)$ and the parity of $t$, so the minimal admissible $t$ is unique.

With $(T,t)$ unique, the integer expression defining $A$ is unique; the required normalization 'reduce modulo $1000$ and take the representative in $\{0,\dots,999\}$' then yields a single evaluation target.

technique(s).name: local_global_CRT_to_construct_numbers
technique(s).purpose: construct the unique interface integer $T$ whose local behaviors at $p$ and $q$ are simultaneously enforced
outputs: canonical $T$ in $[0,M)$ and exact residues modulo each prime power

technique(s).name: v_p_constraints_on_congruences
technique(s).purpose: read off exact local valuations $v_p(T-1)=\alpha$ and $v_q(T+1)=\beta$ needed for admissible exponent lifting
outputs: exact baseline valuations and coprimality conditions $p,q\nmid T$

technique(s).name: CRT_to_LTE_interface_minimality
inputs: target divisibility thresholds at two primes
technique(s).purpose: use the CRT-forced baseline valuations as the input for LTE, then solve the resulting valuation inequalities to identify the unique minimal exponent $t$
outputs: unique minimal $t$ satisfying both prime-power divisibility conditions

theorem(s).role: since $p,q\not\in\{2,5\}$, the integers $p^{2\alpha}$ and $q^{2\beta}$ are invertible modulo $1000$, allowing quotient residues to be computed mod $1000$

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Local congruence information at prime powers determines exact $p$-adic valuations; combining these local constraints via CRT enables valuation-lifting (LTE) to control global divisibility patterns.

critical_trick(s): Use CRT to pin down a canonical integer $T$ whose residues force *exact* values of $v_p(T-1)$ and $v_q(T+1)$ (not just lower bounds).

Feed these exact baseline valuations into LTE to translate the coupled divisibility thresholds into constraints on $v_p(t)$, $v_q(t)$ and the parity of $t$, thereby identifying the unique minimal exponent $t$.
Reduce the final integer expression modulo $1000$ by replacing integer quotients with modular multiplications using inverses (valid because $p,q\not\in\{2,5\}$).

title: Capstone: build $T$ by CRT then compute $v_p(T^t\!\pm\!1)$ - Realized Instance

identity.short_name: Capstone: build $T$ by CRT then compute $v_p(T^t\!\pm\!1)$ - Realized Instance

problem_spec.summary: Two-stage chain (divisibility wrapper): an integer $T$ is constructed as the least nonnegative solution of a CRT system of pairwise coprime prime-power congruences that forces prescribed local $p$-adic and $q$-adic behavior of $T\!\pm\!1$ at two distinct odd primes. From these forced local valuations, an exponent $t$ is defined by a minimal simultaneous prime-power divisibility requirement on $T^t-1$ and $T^t+1$. Finally, a normalized integer is extracted by reducing a derived expression modulo $1000$.
givens: Let $p,q$ be distinct odd primes with $p,q\not\in\{2,5\}$, and let $\alpha,\beta$ be integers with $\alpha,\beta\ge 1$.
Define $M=p^{\alpha+1}q^{\beta+1}$ and let $T$ be the least nonnegative integer satisfying the CRT system $T\equiv 1+p^{\alpha}\pmod{p^{\alpha+1}}$ and $T\equiv -1+q^{\beta}\pmod{q^{\beta+1}}$.
(These congruences force $v_p(T-1)=\alpha$ and $v_q(T+1)=\beta$ exactly, while keeping $p\nmid T$ and $q\nmid T$.)
ask: (Stage 1: determine the interface exponent.) Let $t$ be the smallest positive integer such that both $p^{2\alpha}\mid (T^t-1)$ and $q^{2\beta}\mid (T^t+1)$ hold. Prove that such a $t$ exists and is uniquely determined by the givens.

(Stage 2: compute a normalized residue.) Define

A \equiv \frac{T^t-1}{p^{2\alpha}} + \frac{T^t+1}{q^{2\beta}} \pmod{1000},

where the divisions are in the integers. Output the unique integer representative of $A$ in $\{0,1,\dots,999\}$.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: one prime defining the CRT modulus and the $p$-adic valuation constraints

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: second prime defining the CRT modulus and the $q$-adic valuation constraints

parameter(s).name: alpha
parameter(s).purpose: forces the exact local condition $v_p(T-1)=\alpha$ via a prime-power congruence

parameter(s).name: beta
parameter(s).purpose: forces the exact local condition $v_q(T+1)=\beta$ via a prime-power congruence

parameter(s).name: prime_power_constraints
parameter(s).domain: CRT system on pairwise coprime moduli containing at least the two prime powers $p^{\alpha+1}$ and $q^{\beta+1}$ (so at least one modulus is a nontrivial prime power with exponent $\ge 2$)
parameter(s).purpose: structural descriptor ensuring CRT is over prime powers (not merely primes)

invariant(s).name: interface_T_to_t_chain
invariant(s).statement: Stage (1) fixes a unique integer $T\in[0,M)$ by CRT; Stage (1) then defines $t$ by a minimal simultaneous divisibility requirement on $T^t\!\pm\!1$; Stage (2) computes the final residue from this same $(T,t)$ pair.

invariant(s).name: nontrivial_CRT_profile
invariant(s).statement: The CRT moduli include the pairwise coprime prime powers $p^{\alpha+1}$ and $q^{\beta+1}$ with $p\ne q$ and $\alpha,\beta\ge 1$, so at least one modulus has exponent $\ge 2$.

invariant(s).name: exact_local_valuations
invariant(s).statement: The congruences force $v_p(T-1)=\alpha$ and $v_q(T+1)=\beta$ exactly (and $p,q\nmid T$), providing admissibility for LTE.

invariant(s).name: LTE_admissibility_guard
invariant(s).statement: For odd primes $p,q$ not dividing $T$, LTE gives $v_p(T^t-1)=v_p(T-1)+v_p(t)$ and (for odd $t$) $v_q(T^t+1)=v_q(T+1)+v_q(t)$; minimality of $t$ forces the required parity and exactness.

essential_assumption(s): The CRT moduli are coprime and include at least two distinct odd prime powers, so $T$ is well-defined as the least nonnegative solution.
LTE is applied only under admissibility: $p,q$ are odd, $p\mid (T-1)$, $q\mid (T+1)$, and $p,q\nmid T$.
The exponent $t$ is defined by a coupled minimal divisibility condition at two primes, preventing one-shot completion from a single congruence.
uniqueness_certificate(s): CRT yields a unique $T$ modulo $M=p^{\alpha+1}q^{\beta+1}$ and the least nonnegative representative fixes $T$ uniquely.
LTE turns the divisibility thresholds into constraints on $v_p(t)$, $v_q(t)$ and the parity of $t$, forcing a unique minimal $t$.
With $(T,t)$ unique, the normalized residue $A\bmod 1000$ is uniquely determined.

technique(s).name: local_global_CRT_to_construct_numbers
technique(s).purpose: construct the unique interface integer $T$ whose local behaviors at $p$ and $q$ are simultaneously enforced
outputs: canonical $T$ in $[0,M)$ and exact residues modulo each prime power

technique(s).name: v_p_constraints_on_congruences
technique(s).purpose: read off exact local valuations $v_p(T-1)=\alpha$ and $v_q(T+1)=\beta$ and coprimality $p,q\nmid T$
outputs: baseline valuations and admissibility conditions for LTE

technique(s).name: CRT_to_LTE_interface_minimality
inputs: target divisibility thresholds at two primes
technique(s).purpose: convert the coupled divisibility problem into a minimal-exponent valuation problem
outputs: unique minimal $t$ satisfying both prime-power conditions

technique(s).name: LTE_difference_and_sum_of_powers
technique(s).purpose: compute $v_p(T^t-1)$ and $v_q(T^t+1)$ and certify integrality of the final expression

technique(s).name: mod_1000_reduction_with_inverses
technique(s).purpose: compute quotient residues mod $1000$ via modular inverses and finish the evaluation

theorem(s).name: Chinese_remainder
theorem(s).role: existence and uniqueness of the CRT solution for $T$

theorem(s).name: lifting_exponents_LTE
theorem(s).role: valuation lifting for $T^t-1$ and $T^t+1$ under admissibility and parity constraints

theorem(s).name: existence_of_modular_inverse
theorem(s).role: invertibility of $p^{2\alpha}$ and $q^{2\beta}$ modulo $1000$ (since $p,q\not\in\{2,5\}$)

theorem(s).name: Euler_theorem_exponent_cycle_mod_125
theorem(s).role: (metadata completion) reduce exponents when computing $T^t\bmod 125$ since $\varphi(125)=100$

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Local prime-power congruences determine exact $p$-adic valuations, which then control global divisibility via LTE.

critical_trick(s): Use CRT to force exact valuations of $T-1$ and $T+1$ at two primes.
Use LTE to translate prime-power divisibility thresholds into a minimal-exponent valuation constraint on $t$ (including an odd/even branch).
Compute the final residue mod $1000$ using modular inverses and exponent cycles mod $8$ and $125$.

task(s).name: primary_structural_check
task(s).reason:
Correctness checks:
- Compute (T, M) by CRT and verify 0<=T<M and both given congruences.
- Verify exact valuations v_3(T-1)=3 and v_11(T+1)=3.
- Verify t=3^3*11^3 satisfies 3^6|(T^t-1) and 11^6|(T^t+1).
- Verify minimality of t by brute iteration of powers modulo 3^6 and 11^6 up to 100000.
NT5 preflight attempts (cheap strategies that fail):
- "Small-mod-only: enforce only the weaker conditions 3^3|(T^t-1) and 11^3|(T^t+1); the minimal exponent is t_weak=1, but it fails the required 3^6 and 11^6 thresholds."
- "Bounded scan: brute search for t<=5000 satisfying both strong conditions returns no solution."
- "Bounded scan on T: brute search for a CRT solution with T<=50000 returns none."
- "Template probes: A is not equal to T mod 1000, t mod 1000, (T+t) mod 1000, 0, or 500."
NT6 collision resistance:
- Wrong-branch exponent t_wrong=3t (still admissible but not minimal) produces A_wrong=702 != 742.

task(s).name: secondary_independent_check
task(s).reason:
Independent computation of A:
- Compute r1 = T^t mod (1000*3^6) and r2 = T^t mod (1000*11^6).
- Recover q1 = (T^t-1)/3^6 mod 1000 as ((r1-1) mod (1000*3^6))/3^6, and similarly q2 = (T^t+1)/11^6 mod 1000.
- Confirm (q1+q2) mod 1000 equals the modular-inverse computation.

title: Capstone: reciprocity decides representability, norms construct it

identity.short_name: Capstone: reciprocity decides representability, norms construct it

problem_spec.summary: Capstone CHAIN: (1) Use Legendre symbols and quadratic reciprocity to decide a local representability condition (e.g., whether $-1$ is a quadratic residue mod $p$). This produces an interface quantity $T$. (2) Conditional on $T=1$, construct a normalized integer solution of a norm equation such as $x^2+y^2=p$ via Gaussian integer descent / quadratic form methods.
givens: $p$ is an odd prime
Goal norm equation is fixed by mode (default: $x^2+y^2=p$) with normalization $x\ge y\ge 0$.
ask: Compute $T=(\!-1/p)$ using quadratic reciprocity and supplementary laws. If $T=1$, output the unique normalized pair $(x,y)$ with $x^2+y^2=p$. (Instances are chosen so that $T=1$ holds; otherwise the output is undefined and the instance is outside the family domain.)

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: prime whose representability is tested and then represented

parameter(s).name: mode
parameter(s).purpose: fixes the norm form (default: Gaussian norm)

parameter(s).name: normalization_rule
parameter(s).purpose: removes unit/order symmetries to make the representation unique

parameter(s).name: CRT_optional_glue_switch
parameter(s).purpose: optional: first construct a prime with desired residue class conditions using CRT, then apply the chain

invariant(s).name: interface_T
invariant(s).statement: Stage (1) outputs $T=(\!-1/p)\in\{\pm 1\}$, which decides whether $p$ splits in $\mathbb{Z}[i]$ and hence whether the norm equation $x^2+y^2=p$ is solvable.

invariant(s).name: norm_solution_uses_T
invariant(s).statement: Stage (2) (Gaussian descent / quadratic forms) is invoked only when $T=1$; it constructs $(x,y)$ from the splitting information guaranteed by $T=1$.

essential_assumption(s): Instances must satisfy the representability condition (e.g., $p\equiv 1\pmod 4$, equivalently $(\!-1/p)=1$) so that a solution exists.
Uniqueness relies on a normalization rule fixing sign/unit symmetries (e.g., $x\ge y\ge 0$).

If CRT_optional_glue is used, the constructed prime must meet both primality and residue constraints; the CRT stage outputs a candidate progression, and primality is ensured by the instance design (olympiad-style).
uniqueness_certificate(s): The coupling is global: $T$ computed in stage (1) determines solvability and is required for stage (2). When $T=1$, the norm method yields representations unique up to units; normalization fixes a single pair. Thus the final output is uniquely determined by the chain, not by independent subtasks.

technique(s).name: Legendre_symbol
technique(s).purpose: define $T=(\!-1/p)$ as the interface quantity

technique(s).name: quadratic_reciprocity
technique(s).purpose: evaluate the symbol using congruence class data

technique(s).name: Gaussian_integer_descent_optional
inputs: a nontrivial square root of $-1$ modulo $p$
technique(s).purpose: convert local data into an exact representation $p=x^2+y^2$ by descent on norms

technique(s).name: norm_equation_solving
technique(s).purpose: solve $N(z)=p$ in $\mathbb{Z}[i]$ and extract $(x,y)$

theorem(s).name: sum_of_two_squares_criterion
theorem(s).role: links $T=(\!-1/p)$ to representability as $x^2+y^2$

theorem(s).name: quadratic_reciprocity
theorem(s).role: computes $T$ efficiently and provides the local obstruction when $T=-1$

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Local quadratic-residue data determines splitting of primes in quadratic integer rings; norms then turn splitting into explicit representations.

critical_trick(s): Compute the interface symbol $T=(\!-1/p)$ using reciprocity (essentially $p\bmod 4$).
When $T=1$, use a square root of $-1$ modulo $p$ and run Gaussian descent (or UFD factorization) to produce $x^2+y^2=p$, then normalize.

title: Capstone: reciprocity decides representability, norms construct it - Buffed Seed

identity.short_name: Capstone: reciprocity decides representability, norms construct it - Buffed Seed

title: CRT exponent targets with 2-adic and Hensel-lifted quartic residue coupling

identity.short_name: CRT exponent targets with 2-adic and Hensel-lifted quartic residue coupling

problem_spec.summary: Construct a unit A by CRT modulo 2^m p^k q^2 ell from residues A \equiv u (mod 2^m), A \equiv 1+p (mod p^k), A \equiv g (mod q^2), A \equiv h (mod ell). Define a 2-adic target D \equiv u^t (mod 2^m), a Hensel-lifted quartic target B (the unique lift to q^2 of a chosen square root of -1 mod q), and an auxiliary prime target C \equiv h^{(ell-1)/2} (mod ell). Translate A^n congruences into a coupled exponent system with non-coprime moduli, solve by gcd reduction / sequential CRT, then normalize n0 modulo 999.
givens: $p,q,\ell$ are pairwise distinct odd primes, $k \ge 3$, and $q \equiv 1 \pmod 4$.
$u$ is odd with $\operatorname{ord}_{2^m}(u)=2^{m-2}$.
$g$ is a primitive root modulo $q^2$, hence $\operatorname{ord}_{q^2}(g)=q(q-1)$.
$h$ is a primitive root modulo $\ell$, hence $\operatorname{ord}_{\ell}(h)=\ell-1$.
$t$ is an integer with $0 \le t < 2^{m-2}$.
$x_0$ is an integer with $x_0^2 \equiv -1 \pmod q$ and $q \nmid x_0$.
$D$ is the least nonnegative integer with $D \equiv u^{t} \pmod{2^m}$.
$B$ is the least nonnegative integer with $B \equiv x_0 \pmod q$ and $B^2 \equiv -1 \pmod{q^2}$.
$C$ is the least nonnegative integer with $C \equiv h^{(\ell-1)/2} \pmod{\ell}$.
$A$ is the least nonnegative integer with $A \equiv u \pmod{2^m}$, $A \equiv 1+p \pmod{p^k}$, $A \equiv g \pmod{q^2}$, and $A \equiv h \pmod{\ell}$.
ask: Determine the unique integer $N$ with $0 \le N < 999$ and $N \equiv n_0 \pmod{999}$, where $n_0$ is the least positive $n$ such that $A^n \equiv D \pmod{2^m}$, $A^n \equiv 1 \pmod{p^k}$, $A^n \equiv B \pmod{q^2}$, and $A^n \equiv C \pmod{\ell}$.

parameter(s).name: m
parameter(s).purpose: supplies a 2-adic modulus $2^m$ whose unit group has maximal cyclic component of size $2^{m-2}$

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: supplies the odd prime-power modulus $p^k$ and enables an LTE-based order computation for $1+p$

parameter(s).name: k
parameter(s).purpose: sets the odd prime-power depth and forces the exponent kernel $p^{k-1}$

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: supplies the prime-square modulus $q^2$ and allows a Hensel-lifted square root of $-1$

parameter(s).name: ell
parameter(s).purpose: supplies an auxiliary prime modulus creating a fourth exponent congruence modulo $\ell-1$

parameter(s).name: u
parameter(s).purpose: makes the 2-adic power constraint translate to the linear congruence $n \equiv t \pmod{2^{m-2}}$

parameter(s).name: g
parameter(s).purpose: rigidifies exponents modulo $q^2$ and enables order-$4$ target classification

parameter(s).name: h
parameter(s).purpose: rigidifies exponents modulo $\ell$

parameter(s).name: t
parameter(s).purpose: target exponent for the 2-adic constraint $A^n \equiv u^t \pmod{2^m}$

parameter(s).name: x0
parameter(s).domain: integer root of $x^2 \equiv -1 \pmod q$ with $q \nmid x_0$
parameter(s).purpose: selects which of the two order-$4$ lifts is used in the $q^2$ constraint

invariant(s).name: CRT_synthesized_unit
invariant(s).statement: $M=2^m p^k q^2 \ell$ has pairwise coprime factors, so CRT gives a unique $A \pmod M$ from the four local residues.

invariant(s).name: LTE_order_of_1_plus_p
invariant(s).statement: For odd prime $p$, $v_p((1+p)^n-1)=1+v_p(n)$ and $\operatorname{ord}_{p^k}(1+p)=p^{k-1}$.

invariant(s).name: two_adic_max_order_invariant
invariant(s).statement: If $\operatorname{ord}_{2^m}(u)=2^{m-2}$, then $u^n \equiv u^t \pmod{2^m}$ iff $n \equiv t \pmod{2^{m-2}}$.

invariant(s).name: hensel_quartic_lift_invariant
invariant(s).statement: If $x_0^2 \equiv -1 \pmod q$ and $q \nmid x_0$, then there is a unique $B \pmod{q^2}$ with $B \equiv x_0 \pmod q$ and $B^2 \equiv -1 \pmod{q^2}$; this $B$ has order $4$ modulo $q^2$.

invariant(s).name: coupling_gcd_compatibility
invariant(s).statement: The exponent system requires compatibility modulo shared gcds when combining $2^{m-2}$, $q(q-1)$, and $\ell-1$ via gcd reduction.

essential_assumption(s): $u$ must have maximal order $2^{m-2}$ modulo $2^m$ so the 2-adic condition becomes a single linear congruence in $n$.
$q \equiv 1 \pmod 4$ and $q \nmid x_0$ are required so $x^2 \equiv -1 \pmod q$ has a simple root that lifts uniquely to $q^2$.
$g$ being a primitive root modulo $q^2$ is required to avoid discrete logarithm ambiguity and to classify the order-$4$ target exponent uniquely once $x_0$ is fixed.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The four power congruences translate to the coupled exponent system $n \equiv t \pmod{2^{m-2}}$, $n \equiv 0 \pmod{p^{k-1}}$, $n \equiv r \pmod{q(q-1)}$, $n \equiv (\ell-1)/2 \pmod{\ell-1}$, where $r$ is uniquely forced by the chosen Hensel lift (pinned by $x_0$). Solving by gcd reduction and sequential CRT yields a single residue class, hence a unique least positive $n_0$ and a unique $N \equiv n_0 \pmod{999}$ with $0 \le N < 999$.

technique(s).purpose: construct the unique CRT-synthesized unit $A$ modulo $2^m p^k q^2 \ell$

technique(s).name: LTE_valuation
technique(s).purpose: compute $v_p((1+p)^n-1)$ and force $\operatorname{ord}_{p^k}(1+p)=p^{k-1}$

technique(s).name: two_adic_LTE
technique(s).purpose: compute 2-adic valuations and orders modulo $2^m$
outputs: determine $\operatorname{ord}_{2^m}(u)$ and translate $u^n \equiv u^t$ into $n \equiv t \pmod{2^{m-2}}$

technique(s).name: quadratic_reciprocity
technique(s).purpose: guarantee solvability of $x^2 \equiv -1 \pmod q$ when $q \equiv 1 \pmod 4$
outputs: existence of $x_0$ needed for Hensel lifting

technique(s).name: Hensel_lifting
technique(s).purpose: lift $x_0$ uniquely to $B \pmod{q^2}$ solving $B^2 \equiv -1 \pmod{q^2}$

technique(s).name: primitive_root_mod_prime_power
technique(s).purpose: rigidify exponents modulo $q^2$ and classify order-$4$ elements

technique(s).name: primitive_root_usage
technique(s).purpose: translate $A^n \equiv h^{(\ell-1)/2}$ into an exponent congruence modulo $\ell-1$

technique(s).name: gcd_based_reduction
technique(s).purpose: reduce and solve intermediate linear congruences by dividing by gcd and inverting

technique(s).name: generalized_CRT_sequential
inputs: progression for $n$ and remaining exponent congruences
technique(s).purpose: sequentially incorporate constraints to reach a single residue class

technique(s).name: modular_exponentiation
technique(s).purpose: compute $n_0 \bmod 999$

theorem(s).name: Chinese_remainder_theorem_coprime_moduli
theorem(s).role: existence and uniqueness of $A$ modulo $2^m p^k q^2 \ell$

theorem(s).name: lifting_the_exponent_LTE_odd_prime
theorem(s).role: valuation formula $v_p((1+p)^n-1)=v_p(1+p-1)+v_p(n)$ for odd prime $p$

theorem(s).name: lifting_the_exponent_LTE_p_equals_2
theorem(s).role: valuation formula $v_2(a^n-b^n)=v_2(a-b)+v_2(a+b)+v_2(n)-1$ for odd $a,b$ and even $n$

theorem(s).name: quadratic_reciprocity_supplement_minus_one
theorem(s).role: criterion $\left(\frac{-1}{q}\right)=(-1)^{(q-1)/2}$

theorem(s).name: Hensel_lemma_simple_root
theorem(s).role: unique lifting of a simple root modulo $q$ to modulo $q^2$

theorem(s).name: Euler_totient_prime_power_formula
theorem(s).role: $\varphi(q^2)=q(q-1)$

theorem(s).name: cyclic_group_order_formula
theorem(s).role: order of $g^a$ in a cyclic group is $m/\gcd(m,a)$

theorem(s).role: solvability and reduction of $ax \equiv b \pmod m$

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Equality of powers in a finite cyclic group is equivalent to congruence of exponents modulo the group order.

critical_trick(s): Convert each power condition on $A^n$ into a linear congruence on $n$ using LTE, 2-adic LTE, primitive-root rigidity, and an order-$4$ Hensel-lifted target.
Solve the coupled exponent system by gcd reduction and sequential CRT, then take the least positive solution and normalize modulo $999$.

title: CRT exponent targets with a prime square and an auxiliary prime

identity.short_name: CRT exponent targets with a prime square and an auxiliary prime

problem_spec.summary: Build $A$ by CRT modulo $p^k q^2 \ell$ with $A \equiv 1+p \pmod{p^k}$, $A \equiv g \pmod{q^2}$, and $A \equiv h \pmod{\ell}$. Convert the three conditions $A^n \equiv 1 \pmod{p^k}$, $A^n \equiv g^r \pmod{q^2}$, $A^n \equiv h^s \pmod{\ell}$ into a coupled exponent system and solve it by gcd reduction and modular inversion.
givens: $p,q,\ell$ are pairwise distinct odd primes with $q>3$.
$k$ is an integer with $k \ge 3$.
$g$ is a primitive root modulo $q^2$, so $\operatorname{ord}_{q^2}(g)=\varphi(q^2)=q(q-1)$.
$h$ is a primitive root modulo $\ell$, so $\operatorname{ord}_{\ell}(h)=\ell-1$.
$r$ is an integer with $0<r<q(q-1)$ and $\gcd(p^{k-1},q(q-1)) \mid r$.
$s$ is an integer with $0<s<\ell-1$ such that the combined exponent system is consistent.
$B$ is the least nonnegative integer with $B \equiv g^r \pmod{q^2}$.
$C$ is the least nonnegative integer with $C \equiv h^s \pmod{\ell}$.
$M:=p^k q^2 \ell$.
$A$ is the least nonnegative integer with $A \equiv 1+p \pmod{p^k}$, $A \equiv g \pmod{q^2}$, and $A \equiv h \pmod{\ell}$.
ask: Determine the unique integer $N$ with $0 \le N < 1000$ and $N \equiv n_0 \pmod{1000}$, where $n_0$ is the least positive integer $n$ such that $A^n \equiv 1 \pmod{p^k}$, $A^n \equiv B \pmod{q^2}$, and $A^n \equiv C \pmod{\ell}$.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: supplies the prime-power modulus $p^k$ and the LTE order of $1+p$ modulo $p^k$

parameter(s).name: k
parameter(s).purpose: forces a nontrivial prime power modulus and yields the exponent kernel $p^{k-1}$

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: supplies the prime square modulus $q^2$ with unit group order $q(q-1)$

parameter(s).name: ell
parameter(s).purpose: adds an auxiliary prime modulus enforcing a third exponent congruence modulo $\ell-1$

parameter(s).name: g
parameter(s).purpose: rigidifies exponents modulo $q^2$ by turning $A^n \equiv g^r$ into $n \equiv r \pmod{q(q-1)}$

parameter(s).name: h
parameter(s).purpose: rigidifies exponents modulo $\ell$ by turning $A^n \equiv h^s$ into $n \equiv s \pmod{\ell-1}$

parameter(s).name: r
parameter(s).purpose: target exponent for the $q^2$ constraint and solvability guard against the $p^{k-1}$ kernel

parameter(s).name: s
parameter(s).domain: integer $s$ with $0<s<\ell-1$ consistent with the combined system
parameter(s).purpose: target exponent for the auxiliary prime constraint

invariant(s).name: CRT_synthesized_unit
invariant(s).statement: $M=p^k q^2 \ell$ with pairwise coprime factors, so CRT gives a unique $A \pmod M$ from local residues.

invariant(s).name: LTE_order_of_1_plus_p
invariant(s).statement: For odd prime $p$, $v_p((1+p)^n-1)=1+v_p(n)$ and $\operatorname{ord}_{p^k}(1+p)=p^{k-1}$.

invariant(s).name: prime_square_generator_rigidity
invariant(s).statement: If $\operatorname{ord}_{q^2}(g)=q(q-1)$, then $g^n \equiv g^r \pmod{q^2}$ if and only if $n \equiv r \pmod{q(q-1)}$.

invariant(s).name: auxiliary_prime_generator_rigidity
invariant(s).statement: If $\operatorname{ord}_{\ell}(h)=\ell-1$, then $h^n \equiv h^s \pmod{\ell}$ if and only if $n \equiv s \pmod{\ell-1}$.

essential_assumption(s): The condition that $g$ is a primitive root modulo $q^2$ is required to avoid discrete logarithm ambiguity modulo $q^2$.
The divisibility condition $\gcd(p^{k-1},q(q-1)) \mid r$ is required for solvability of $n \equiv 0 \pmod{p^{k-1}}$ and $n \equiv r \pmod{q(q-1)}$.
The choice of $s$ must be consistent with the arithmetic progression forced by the first two congruences.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The three congruences on $A^n$ translate to the exponent system $n \equiv 0 \pmod{p^{k-1}}$, $n \equiv r \pmod{q(q-1)}$, $n \equiv s \pmod{\ell-1}$. The first two determine a unique arithmetic progression by gcd reduction, and the third becomes a linear congruence in the progression parameter. This yields exactly one arithmetic progression of solutions, hence a unique least positive $n_0$ and a unique $N \equiv n_0 \pmod{1000}$ with $0 \le N < 1000$.

critical_trick(s): Use LTE to compute $\operatorname{ord}_{p^k}(1+p)=p^{k-1}$.
Use primitive roots modulo $q^2$ and $\ell$ to convert power congruences into linear exponent congruences.
Solve the exponent system by gcd reduction and a final linear congruence in a progression parameter.

title: Chinese remainder synthesis (construct a unique residue class)

identity.short_name: Chinese remainder synthesis (construct a unique residue class)

problem_spec.summary: Given a system of congruences $x\equiv r_i\pmod{m_i}$ with compatibility conditions, construct the unique solution modulo $M=\mathrm{lcm}(m_1,\dots,m_k)$ and report the least nonnegative representative.
givens: $k\in\mathbb{N},\ k\ge 2$
$m_i\in\mathbb{N},\ m_i\ge 2$ for all $i$
Either: (standard CRT) $\gcd(m_i,m_j)=1$ for $i\neq j$, or: (optional) the non-coprime consistency condition $r_i\equiv r_j\pmod{\gcd(m_i,m_j)}$ holds for all $i,j$.
ask: Construct $x\in\{0,1,\dots,M-1\}$ such that $x\equiv r_i\pmod{m_i}$ for all $i$, where $M=\mathrm{lcm}(m_1,\dots,m_k)$.

parameter(s).name: k
parameter(s).purpose: controls number of congruences

parameter(s).name: m_vector
parameter(s).purpose: sets moduli and determines $M$

parameter(s).name: r_vector
parameter(s).purpose: sets target residues

parameter(s).name: coprime_mode
parameter(s).purpose: switches between classical CRT and the consistent non-coprime extension

invariant(s).name: compatibility_invariant
invariant(s).statement: Compatibility is either automatic (pairwise coprime moduli) or enforced by $r_i\equiv r_j\ (\mathrm{mod}\ \gcd(m_i,m_j))$ (non-coprime mode).

invariant(s).name: uniqueness_mod_M
invariant(s).statement: Any two solutions differ by a multiple of $M=\mathrm{lcm}(m_1,\dots,m_k)$.

essential_assumption(s): In non-coprime mode, the instance must satisfy the standard CRT consistency conditions; otherwise no solution exists.
The reported answer is the least nonnegative representative modulo $M$ (structural normalization).
uniqueness_certificate(s): CRT gives existence and uniqueness of the residue class modulo $M$ under the stated compatibility. Selecting the least nonnegative representative makes the reported integer unique.

technique(s).name: Chinese_remainder
technique(s).purpose: turn local congruence constraints into one global congruence class modulo $M$

technique(s).name: constructive_CRT
technique(s).purpose: explicitly build $x=\sum r_i M_i (M_i^{-1}\bmod m_i)$
outputs: concrete integer representative of the CRT class

technique(s).name: pigeonhole_on_residues
technique(s).purpose: (optional) prove existence of an integer with a prescribed residue pattern by counting residue classes
outputs: existence certificate for at least one admissible residue class

technique(s).name: construct_counterexample
inputs: target modular pattern forcing a contradiction to a naive claim
technique(s).purpose: (optional) use CRT to build an integer violating a universal statement

theorem(s).name: simultaneous_congruences
theorem(s).role: frame the problem as solving a simultaneous congruence system

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: A congruence constraint is a statement about membership in an arithmetic progression; CRT intersects progressions into a single progression.

critical_trick(s): Reduce the system to one modulus $M$ and construct a solution using modular inverses of $M_i=M/m_i$ modulo $m_i$.
When moduli are not coprime, first verify pairwise consistency $r_i\equiv r_j\pmod{\gcd(m_i,m_j)}$.

title: Chinese remainder synthesis (construct a unique residue class) - Buffed Seed

identity.short_name: Chinese remainder synthesis (construct a unique residue class) - Buffed Seed

problem_spec.summary: Construct a CRT-synthesized unit modulo a mixed modulus containing a nontrivial prime power, then determine the least positive exponent that forces a prescribed local power-pattern; this requires translating power congruences into exponent congruences via multiplicative orders and then recombining them by CRT, with the final answer reported modulo 1000.
givens: $p,q$ are distinct odd primes with $q>3$.
$k\in\mathbb{Z}$ with $k\ge 3$.
$\gcd(p,q-1)=1$ (equivalently $\gcd(p^{k-1},q-1)=1$).
$g$ is a primitive root modulo $q$, i.e. $\operatorname{ord}_q(g)=q-1$.
Define the mixed modulus $M:=p^k q$.
Let $A$ be the unique residue class modulo $M$ such that $A\equiv 1+p\pmod{p^k}$ and $A\equiv g\pmod q$, and take $A\in\{0,1,\dots,M-1\}$ to be its least nonnegative representative.

title: Chinese remainder synthesis (construct a unique residue class) - Buffed Seed

identity.short_name: Chinese remainder synthesis (construct a unique residue class) - Buffed Seed

problem_spec.summary: Construct a CRT-synthesized unit modulo a mixed modulus containing a nontrivial prime power, then determine the least positive exponent that forces a prescribed local power-pattern; this requires translating power congruences into exponent congruences via multiplicative orders and then recombining them by CRT, with the final answer reported modulo 1000.
givens: $p,q$ are distinct odd primes with $q>3$.
$k\in\mathbb{Z}$ with $k\ge 3$.
$\gcd(p,q-1)=1$ (equivalently $\gcd(p^{k-1},q-1)=1$).
$g$ is a primitive root modulo $q$, i.e. $\operatorname{ord}_q(g)=q-1$.
Define the mixed modulus $M:=p^k q$.
Let $A$ be the unique residue class modulo $M$ such that $A\equiv 1+p\pmod{p^k}$ and $A\equiv g\pmod q$, and take $A\in\{0,1,\dots,M-1\}$ to be its least nonnegative representative.
ask:
Let \(A\) be the least nonnegative integer with \(A\equiv 1+p\pmod{p^k}\) and \(A\equiv g\pmod q\), where \(g\) is a primitive root modulo \(q\), and set \(M=p^kq\).
Define \(S=\{n\in\mathbb Z_{>0} : A^n\equiv 1\pmod{p^k}\ 	ext{and}\ A^n\equiv -1\pmod q\}\).
Prove \(S
eqarnothing\), let \(n_0=\min S\), and report \(n_0  mod 1000\) (an integer in \(\{0,1,\dots,999\}\)).

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: supplies the prime-power factor $p^k$ and the nontrivial order constraint via $1+p$ modulo $p^k$

parameter(s).name: k
parameter(s).purpose: forces a nontrivial prime power modulus and ensures the exponent modulus $p^{k-1}$ is itself a genuine prime power with $\ge 9$

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: ensures a cyclic unit group of size $q-1$ that is coprime to $p^{k-1}$, enabling a clean CRT step on exponent congruences

parameter(s).name: g
parameter(s).purpose: fixes a generator modulo $q$ so that the condition $A^n\equiv -1\pmod q$ becomes a rigid congruence condition on $n$

invariant(s).name: structural_CRT_modulus
invariant(s).statement: The modulus has the structured form $M=p^kq$ with $k\ge 3$ and $p,q$ distinct odd primes, so $\gcd(p^k,q)=1$ and at least one local modulus is a nontrivial prime power (not all moduli are prime).

invariant(s).name: anti_trivial_nontrivial_orders
invariant(s).statement: Since $k\ge 3$ and $q>3$, both $p^{k-1}>1$ and $q-1>2$, so neither local power condition collapses to a tautology and the extremal value $n_0$ cannot be $1$.

invariant(s).name: order_of_one_plus_p_mod_p_k
invariant(s).statement: In $(\mathbb{Z}/p^k\mathbb{Z})^\times$, the element $1+p$ has exact multiplicative order $p^{k-1}$.

invariant(s).name: primitive_root_half_turn
invariant(s).statement: Since $\operatorname{ord}_q(g)=q-1$ and $q$ is odd, $g^{(q-1)/2}\equiv -1\pmod q$, and $g^n\equiv -1\pmod q\iff n\equiv (q-1)/2\pmod{q-1}$.

invariant(s).name: coprime_exponent_moduli_for_CRT
invariant(s).statement: $\gcd(p^{k-1},q-1)=1$, hence the simultaneous congruences $n\equiv 0\pmod{p^{k-1}}$ and $n\equiv (q-1)/2\pmod{q-1}$ admit a unique solution class modulo $p^{k-1}(q-1)$.

invariant(s).name: unit_invertibility
invariant(s).statement: $\gcd(A,M)=1$ (since $A\equiv 1+p\not\equiv 0\pmod p$ and $A\equiv g\not\equiv 0\pmod q$), so multiplicative orders and exponent reductions are well-defined.

essential_assumption(s): The modulus interface is structurally constrained: $M=p^kq$ with $k\ge 3$ and $\gcd(p^k,q)=1$, so the CRT synthesis of $A$ is deterministic and includes a genuine prime-power factor.

The task cannot be solved by a single CRT reconstruction step on $A$; one must first use multiplicative-order reasoning to translate the power constraints on $A^n$ into congruences on the exponent $n$, and only then apply CRT to those exponent congruences to obtain the extremal value $n_0$.
Anti-triviality guards $k\ge 3$ and $q>3$ ensure both exponent moduli $p^{k-1}$ and $q-1$ exceed $2$, ruling out degenerate cases such as order $=1$ or $-1\equiv 1$.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Using $\operatorname{ord}_{p^k}(A)=p^{k-1}$ (since $A\equiv 1+p\pmod{p^k}$), we have $A^n\equiv 1\pmod{p^k}\iff n\equiv 0\pmod{p^{k-1}}$. Using $\operatorname{ord}_q(A)=q-1$ (since $A\equiv g\pmod q$ with $g$ primitive), we have $A^n\equiv -1\pmod q\iff n\equiv (q-1)/2\pmod{q-1}$. Because $\gcd(p^{k-1},q-1)=1$, CRT yields a unique residue class $n\pmod{p^{k-1}(q-1)}$ satisfying both; therefore $S$ is exactly one arithmetic progression and has a unique smallest positive element $n_0$. The reported output is the canonical residue $n_0\bmod 1000\in\{0,1,\dots,999\}$, which is unique because $n_0$ itself is unique.

technique(s).name: Chinese_remainder
technique(s).purpose: synthesize the global unit $A\pmod{p^kq}$ from its prescribed local residues
outputs: a unique residue class $A\pmod M$ and its least nonnegative representative

technique(s).name: order_translation
technique(s).purpose: translate power congruences for $A^n$ into congruences on the exponent $n$ (bridge from ORDER to CRT)

technique(s).name: CRT_on_order_constraints
technique(s).purpose: combine ORDER-derived exponent constraints into a single congruence class and extract the least positive representative (interaction kernel: ORDER + CRT)
outputs: unique class $n\pmod{p^{k-1}(q-1)}$ and the extremal value $n_0=\min S$

theorem(s).name: Chinese_remainder_theorem_coprime_moduli
theorem(s).role: guarantees existence/uniqueness of $A\pmod{p^kq}$ from its residues modulo $p^k$ and $q$, and guarantees existence/uniqueness of the exponent class modulo $p^{k-1}(q-1)$

theorem(s).name: order_of_1_plus_p_mod_prime_power
theorem(s).role: gives $\operatorname{ord}_{p^k}(1+p)=p^{k-1}$ (typically proved by binomial expansion / lifting arguments)

theorem(s).name: primitive_root_mod_prime
theorem(s).role: ensures $(\mathbb{Z}/q\mathbb{Z})^\times$ is cyclic and that $\operatorname{ord}_q(g)=q-1$ makes $-1$ the half-power $g^{(q-1)/2}$

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: A congruence constraint is a statement about membership in an arithmetic progression; CRT intersects progressions into a single progression.
In a finite cyclic group, a power equation $u^n=v$ becomes a congruence condition on $n$ once the order structure is identified.

critical_trick(s): Use the known exact order $\operatorname{ord}_{p^k}(1+p)=p^{k-1}$ to rewrite $A^n\equiv 1\pmod{p^k}$ as the exponent constraint $n\equiv 0\pmod{p^{k-1}}$.
Use that $A\equiv g\pmod q$ with $g$ primitive to rewrite $A^n\equiv -1\pmod q$ as $n\equiv (q-1)/2\pmod{q-1}$.
Apply CRT to these ORDER-derived exponent congruences (made possible by $\gcd(p^{k-1},q-1)=1$), then take the least positive representative and finally normalize modulo $1000$.

title: Linear congruence with split valuations, order lifted to $q^2$, and Hensel square-root coupling

identity.short_name: Linear congruence with split valuations, order lifted to $q^2$, and Hensel square-root coupling

problem_spec.summary: Solve a coupled linear congruence $ax\equiv b\pmod{n}$ with $n=p^kq$ where $A=(a^{q-1}-1)/p^k$ and $C=(a^{p-1}-1)/q$ are defined in an exact-valuation regime forced by LTE. Compute $x$ by determining $x\bmod q$ and $x\bmod p^k$, recombining by CRT, then compute $t=\operatorname{ord}_{q^2}(x)$ by lifting the order from $q$ to $q^2$. Finally compute a uniquely selected Hensel-lifted square root $y$ of $x+(p-1)$ modulo $p^k$, and output the normalized residue $N\equiv x+t+y\pmod{999}$.
givens: $p$ and $q$ are distinct odd primes, $k\ge 5$, and $n=p^kq$.
$a$ is an integer with $\gcd(a,pq)=1$, $v_p(a-1)\ge 2$, and $v_q(a-1)=1$.
$A=(a^{q-1}-1)/p^k$ and $C=(a^{p-1}-1)/q$ are integers (forced by the valuation constraints).
$x$ is the least nonnegative solution to $ax\equiv b\pmod n$.
$t=\operatorname{ord}_{q^2}(x)$.
$y$ is the unique integer with $0\le y<p^k$ satisfying $y^2\equiv x+(p-1)\pmod{p^k}$ and $y\equiv 1\pmod p$.
$N$ is the unique integer with $0\le N<999$ and $N\equiv x+t+y\pmod{999}$.
ask: Determine $N$.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: base prime for the $p$-power modulus and $p$-adic expansions

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: prime controlling the $q$ and $q^2$ computations

parameter(s).name: k
parameter(s).purpose: size of the $p$-power modulus $p^k$ and the coupling exponent $p^{k-4}$

parameter(s).name: a
parameter(s).purpose: near-$1$ unit enabling LTE, binomial truncation, and controlled residue computations

invariant(s).name: structured_modulus
invariant(s).statement: $n=p^kq$ with $p,q$ distinct odd primes and $k\ge 5$.

invariant(s).name: LTE_integrality_A
invariant(s).statement: LTE gives $v_p(a^{q-1}-1)\ge k$, hence $A=(a^{q-1}-1)/p^k\in\mathbb{Z}$.

invariant(s).name: LTE_integrality_C
invariant(s).statement: LTE gives $v_q(a^{p-1}-1)=1$, hence $C=(a^{p-1}-1)/q\in\mathbb{Z}$ and $q\nmid C$.

invariant(s).name: shifted_coupling_term
invariant(s).statement: $b=A+p^{k-4}C$ forces simultaneous computation modulo $q$ and $p^k$.

invariant(s).name: order_lift_gate_q2
invariant(s).statement: If $d=\operatorname{ord}_q(x)$ then $\operatorname{ord}_{q^2}(x)\in\{d,qd\}$ and equals $qd$ iff $x^d\not\equiv 1\pmod{q^2}$.

invariant(s).name: hensel_square_shift_selector
invariant(s).statement: The constraints $y^2\equiv x+(p-1)\pmod{p^k}$ and $y\equiv 1\pmod p$ uniquely determine $y$ by Hensel lifting in the simple-root regime.

essential_assumption(s): $\gcd(a,n)=1$ so the linear congruence has a unique solution class modulo $n$.
$y\equiv 1\pmod p$ selects the unique Hensel lift among the two square roots.
The reported output is the canonical residue $N$ with $0\le N<999$ and $N\equiv x+t+y\pmod{999}$.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Since $\gcd(a,n)=1$, the congruence $ax\equiv b\pmod n$ has a unique solution class modulo $n$, and the normalization $0\le x<n$ fixes a unique $x$. The order $t=\operatorname{ord}_{q^2}(x)$ is uniquely defined because $\gcd(x,q)=1$, and the order-lifting gate $x^{\operatorname{ord}_q(x)}\not\equiv 1\pmod{q^2}$ forces $t=q\operatorname{ord}_q(x)$. The auxiliary variable $y$ is uniquely determined by Hensel's lemma in the simple-root regime together with the selector $y\equiv 1\pmod p$. Finally $N$ is uniquely determined by the normalization $0\le N<999$ and $N\equiv x+t+y\pmod{999}$.

technique(s).name: Lifting_the_Exponent_LTE
inputs: $a\equiv 1\pmod p$ and $a\equiv 1\pmod q$ with controlled valuations
technique(s).purpose: prove $A,C\in\mathbb{Z}$ and obtain exact (or sufficient) valuations
outputs: integrality and nondivisibility information for $A$ and $C$

technique(s).name: binomial_expansion_truncation
inputs: expansions of $(1+p^eu)^{q-1}$ and $(1+qm)^{p-1}$ modulo prime powers
technique(s).purpose: compute $A\bmod p^k$ and $C\bmod p^k$ efficiently

technique(s).name: Legendre_formula_v_p_factorial
technique(s).purpose: compute $v_p(\binom{m}{i})$ to justify term vanishing

title: Noncoprime exponent kernel via generator-power target

identity.short_name: Noncoprime exponent kernel via generator-power target

problem_spec.summary: Construct a CRT-synthesized unit modulo $p^kq$, translate local power constraints into a noncoprime exponent system, solve it via gcd reduction and modular inversion, then output the least solution modulo $1000$.
givens: $p,q$ are distinct odd primes with $q>3$.
$k\in\mathbb Z$ with $k\ge 3$.
$g$ is a primitive root modulo $q$, i.e. $\operatorname{ord}_q(g)=q-1$.
$r$ is an integer with $0<r<q-1$ and $p^2\mid r$.
Define $B\equiv g^r\pmod q$.
Define the mixed modulus $M:=p^kq$.
Let $A$ be the unique residue class modulo $M$ such that $A\equiv 1+p\pmod{p^k}$ and $A\equiv g\pmod q$, and take $A\in\{0,1,\dots,M-1\}$ as its least nonnegative representative.

title: CRT-defined $T$ with odd prime-power and $2$-power components; valuation/order forcing; quotient sum mod $1000$ with a noninvertible divisor

identity.short_name: CRT-defined $T$ with odd prime-power and $2$-power components; valuation/order forcing; quotient sum mod $1000$ with a noninvertible divisor

problem_spec.summary: A CRT-defined integer $T$ is constrained modulo $11^5$, $19^2$, and $2^{10}$. The minimal exponent $t$ with $T^t\equiv 1$ simultaneously is forced by (i) a valuation formula near $1$ at $11^5$, (ii) order lifting from $19$ to $19^2$, and (iii) the exact order of $3$ modulo $2^{10}$. A sum of three integer quotients is then computed modulo $1000$, including a quotient by $2^{10}$ handled via residues mod $8$ and $125$.
givens: $T$ is the unique integer with $0\le T<M$ such that $T\equiv 1+4\cdot 11^2\pmod{11^5}$, $T\equiv 2\pmod{19^2}$, and $T\equiv 3\pmod{2^{10}}$.
$t$ is the smallest positive integer such that $11^5\mid (T^t-1)$, $19^2\mid (T^t-1)$, and $2^{10}\mid (T^t-1)$.
$A\equiv \frac{T^t-1}{11^5}+\frac{T^t-1}{19^2}+\frac{T^t-1}{2^{10}}\pmod{1000}$, with integer division.
$N$ is the unique integer in $\{0,1,\dots,999\}$ with $N\equiv A\pmod{1000}$.
ask: Determine $N$.

parameter(s).name: odd_prime_power_1
parameter(s).domain: an odd prime power $p^5$ with a residue $T\equiv 1+u p^2\pmod{p^5}$ and $p\nmid u$
parameter(s).purpose: forces a large exact order via a valuation formula

parameter(s).name: odd_prime_square
parameter(s).domain: an odd prime square $q^2$ with a residue class $T\equiv g\pmod{q^2}$ where $\operatorname{ord}_q(g)=q-1$ and $g^{q-1}\not\equiv 1\pmod{q^2}$
parameter(s).purpose: forces full order $q(q-1)$ by order lifting

parameter(s).name: two_power
parameter(s).purpose: forces a precise $2$-power order via $2$-adic valuation growth

parameter(s).name: output_modulus
parameter(s).purpose: final normalization modulus for the quotient sum

invariant(s).name: CRT_uniqueness_three_moduli
invariant(s).statement: The moduli $p^5$, $q^2$, and $2^k$ are pairwise coprime, so CRT gives a unique class modulo $M$, and $0\le T<M$ fixes the representative.

invariant(s).name: valuation_order_forcing_at_p5
invariant(s).statement: If $T\equiv 1+u p^2\pmod{p^5}$ with $p\nmid u$ and $p$ odd, then $v_p(T^n-1)=2+v_p(n)$, forcing $\operatorname{ord}_{p^5}(T)=p^3$.

invariant(s).name: order_lift_q_to_q2
invariant(s).statement: If $\operatorname{ord}_q(T)=q-1$ and $T^{q-1}\not\equiv 1\pmod{q^2}$, then $\operatorname{ord}_{q^2}(T)=q(q-1)$.

invariant(s).name: two_adic_order_of_3
invariant(s).statement: If $T\equiv 3\pmod{2^k}$ with $k\ge 3$, then $\operatorname{ord}_{2^k}(T)=2^{k-2}$.

invariant(s).name: coupled_minimal_exponent
invariant(s).statement: $t$ is uniquely forced as $\operatorname{lcm}(\operatorname{ord}_{p^5}(T),\operatorname{ord}_{q^2}(T),\operatorname{ord}_{2^k}(T))$.

invariant(s).name: invertible_divisors_mod_1000
invariant(s).statement: $\gcd(p^5,1000)=\gcd(q^2,1000)=1$, so the quotients by $p^5$ and $q^2$ reduce modulo $1000$ via modular inverses.

invariant(s).name: mixed_division_for_two_power
invariant(s).statement: For $K=(T^t-1)/2^k$, compute $K\bmod 8$ from $T^t\bmod 2^{k+3}$ and compute $K\bmod 125$ from $T^t\bmod 125$ (since $2^k$ is invertible mod $125$); CRT uniquely determines $K\bmod 1000$.

essential_assumption(s): The three moduli are pairwise coprime (CRT well-posedness).
$p\nmid u$ so $v_p(T-1)=2$ and valuation forcing is nondegenerate.
$\operatorname{ord}_q(g)=q-1$ and $g^{q-1}\not\equiv 1\pmod{q^2}$ to ensure full order $q(q-1)$.
$k\ge 3$ and $T\equiv 3\pmod{2^k}$ to ensure exact order $2^{k-2}$.
$\gcd(T,1000)=1$ so exponent reduction modulo $100$ is valid.
uniqueness_certificate(s): CRT and $0\le T<M$ give a unique $T$.
Valuation forcing at $p^5$, order lifting at $q^2$, and the $2$-adic order lemma uniquely determine local orders and thus uniquely force $t$ as an $\operatorname{lcm}$.
Odd-denominator quotient residues are unique via modular inverses; the $2^k$-denominator quotient residue is uniquely determined via residues mod $8$ and $125$ and CRT.
Normalization $0\le N<1000$ makes the final answer unique.

title: CRT-defined $T$, local orders modulo $7^4$ and $17^2$, and a mod $1000$ quotient sum

identity.short_name: CRT-defined $T$, local orders modulo $7^4$ and $17^2$, and a mod $1000$ quotient sum

problem_spec.summary: A CRT-defined integer $T$ is simultaneously near $1$ modulo $7^4$ and has maximal order modulo $17^2$. The minimal exponent $t$ with $T^t\equiv 1$ modulo both prime powers is forced as an $\operatorname{lcm}$ of local multiplicative orders. A quotient-sum residue is then computed modulo $1000$ using modular inverses and exponent reduction modulo $\lambda(1000)$.
givens: $T$ is the unique integer with $0\le T<M$ such that $T\equiv 1+2\cdot 7^2\pmod{7^4}$ and $T\equiv 23\pmod{17^2}$.
$t$ is the smallest positive integer such that $7^4\mid T^t-1$ and $17^2\mid T^t-1$.
$N$ is the unique integer in $\{0,1,\dots,999\}$ with $N\equiv A\pmod{1000}$.
ask: Determine $N$.

parameter(s).name: p_part
parameter(s).domain: fixed as $7^4$ in this published instance
parameter(s).purpose: prime-power modulus where $T$ is chosen to be near $1$

parameter(s).name: q_part
parameter(s).domain: fixed as $17^2$ in this published instance
parameter(s).purpose: prime-square modulus where $T$ is chosen to have full order

invariant(s).name: CRT_uniqueness_for_T
invariant(s).statement: $\gcd(7^4,17^2)=1$ implies there is a unique $T$ modulo $M=7^4\cdot 17^2$ satisfying the two congruences, and $0\le T<M$ fixes the representative.

invariant(s).name: order_lifting_at_7_4
invariant(s).statement: If $T\equiv 1+2\cdot 7^2\pmod{7^4}$ then $\operatorname{ord}_{7^4}(T)=7^2=49$.

invariant(s).name: full_order_at_17_2
invariant(s).statement: If $T\equiv 23\pmod{17^2}$ and $23^{136}\equiv -1\pmod{17^2}$ while $23^{16}\not\equiv 1\pmod{17^2}$, then $\operatorname{ord}_{17^2}(T)=\varphi(17^2)=272$.

invariant(s).name: exponent_forcing
invariant(s).statement: $T^t\equiv 1\pmod{7^4}$ and $T^t\equiv 1\pmod{17^2}$ force $t=\operatorname{lcm}(\operatorname{ord}_{7^4}(T),\operatorname{ord}_{17^2}(T))$ as the smallest positive solution.

invariant(s).name: invertibility_mod_1000
invariant(s).statement: $\gcd(7^4,1000)=\gcd(17^2,1000)=1$, so $(7^4)^{-1}$ and $(17^2)^{-1}$ exist modulo $1000$ and the quotients in $A$ can be reduced using inverses.

invariant(s).name: exponent_reduction_mod_1000
invariant(s).statement: $\gcd(T,1000)=1$ implies $T^{100}\equiv 1\pmod{1000}$, hence $T^t\equiv T^{t\bmod 100}\pmod{1000}$.

essential_assumption(s): $\gcd(7^4,17^2)=1$ so $T$ is well-defined by CRT.
$\gcd(7^4,1000)=\gcd(17^2,1000)=1$ so modular inverses exist modulo $1000$.
$\gcd(T,1000)=1$ so exponent reduction modulo $100$ is valid for computing $T^t\pmod{1000}$.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Uniqueness of $T$ follows from CRT and the normalization $0\le T<M$.
Uniqueness of $t$ follows because $t$ is forced to be the smallest positive multiple of $\operatorname{lcm}(\operatorname{ord}_{7^4}(T),\operatorname{ord}_{17^2}(T))$.
Uniqueness of $N$ follows from the normalization $0\le N<1000$.
