title: Capstone: CRT-defined T, local multiplicative orders, and a mod-1000 quotient sum - Forged Instance

identity.short_name: Capstone: CRT-defined T, local multiplicative orders, and a mod-1000 quotient sum - Forged Instance

problem_spec.summary: A CRT-defined integer T is engineered to be near 1 modulo one prime power (enabling binomial order-lifting) and to have full order modulo a second prime power (certified by an order test). The minimal exponent t is forced as an lcm of local multiplicative orders, and a quotient-sum residue is computed modulo 1000 using modular inverses plus Carmichael exponent reduction.
givens: Let p,q be distinct odd primes with p,q not in {2,5}.
Let a>=3 and b>=2 be integers, let u be an integer with gcd(u,p)=1, and let g be an integer that is a primitive root modulo q^b.
Define M=p^a q^b and let T be the least nonnegative integer satisfying T≡1+u p^{a-2} (mod p^a) and T≡g (mod q^b).
ask: Let t be the smallest positive integer such that p^a | (T^t-1) and q^b | (T^t-1). Prove t exists and is unique, and express it via local multiplicative orders.
Define A ≡ (T^t-1)/p^a + (T^t-1)/q^b (mod 1000), where divisions are in the integers. Output the unique representative in {0,1,...,999}.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: prime-power modulus controlling a near-1 unit and an order-lifting regime

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).domain: odd prime q not in {2,5} with q != p
parameter(s).purpose: second prime-power modulus supporting a full-order unit regime

parameter(s).name: a
parameter(s).purpose: exponent of p in p^a; chosen so p^{a-2} is meaningful

parameter(s).name: b
parameter(s).purpose: exponent of q in q^b where primitive roots exist

parameter(s).name: u
parameter(s).purpose: scales the near-1 perturbation 1+u p^{a-2} without changing the p^2-order conclusion

parameter(s).name: g
parameter(s).domain: integer that is a primitive root modulo q^b
parameter(s).purpose: forces ord_{q^b}(T)=phi(q^b), creating a nontrivial lcm forcing for t

invariant(s).name: CRT_fixes_T
invariant(s).statement: Since gcd(p^a,q^b)=1, CRT yields a unique residue class for T modulo M=p^a q^b, and the least nonnegative representative fixes T uniquely.

invariant(s).name: near_one_order_lifting
invariant(s).statement: If T≡1+u p^{a-2} (mod p^a) with a>=3 and gcd(u,p)=1, then ord_{p^a}(T)=p^2 (certified by binomial order-lifting).

invariant(s).name: primitive_root_local_order
invariant(s).statement: If T≡g (mod q^b) and g is a primitive root modulo q^b, then ord_{q^b}(T)=phi(q^b).

invariant(s).name: global_order_is_lcm
invariant(s).statement: For coprime moduli p^a and q^b, ord_{p^a q^b}(T)=lcm(ord_{p^a}(T), ord_{q^b}(T)); thus the minimal t with T^t≡1 modulo both is this lcm.

essential_assumption(s): p^a and q^b are coprime (p != q), ensuring CRT well-definedness of T.
a>=3 and gcd(u,p)=1 so that 1+u p^{a-2} has exact order p^2 modulo p^a.
g is chosen to be a primitive root modulo q^b so its order is exactly phi(q^b).
p,q not in {2,5} ensures p^a and q^b are invertible modulo 1000, so quotient residues are computable via modular inverses.
uniqueness_certificate(s): CRT gives a unique T modulo M and the least nonnegative representative selects a unique integer T.
Multiplicative orders uniquely force the minimal exponent t as lcm of the local orders.
With (T,t) unique, the reduced residue A mod 1000 is uniquely determined.

theorem(s).role: existence and uniqueness of the CRT solution defining T

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Multiplicative order in modular arithmetic controls minimal exponents satisfying coupled congruences.

critical_trick(s): Use CRT to build T with a near-1 p-adic component whose order is certified by binomial order-lifting.
Certify full local order modulo a prime square via a prime-factor order test, then force t as an lcm of local orders.
Compute the mod-1000 quotient sum using modular inverses and reduce the large exponent via Carmichael's lambda(1000).

library(s).name: sympy
library(s).reason: CRT construction, multiplicative order computation, modular inverses, and symbolic canonicalization for the novelty audit

library(s).name: math
library(s).reason: gcd/lcm and lightweight brute scans for minimality preflights

task(s).name: primary_structural_check
task(s).reason:
Correctness checks consistent with the intended math path:
- Compute T by CRT and verify 0<=T<M and both congruences.
- Compute local orders ord_{7^4}(T)=49 and ord_{17^2}(T)=272, then set t=lcm and verify T^t≡1 mod 7^4 and mod 17^2.
- Verify minimality of t by brute search for the first exponent satisfying both congruences.

Preflight attempts (domain-agnostic):
Attempt A: Naive guess A≡000 by incorrectly assuming T^t≡1 (mod 1000); Python shows T^t≡841 (mod 1000) and A≡400, so the guess mismatches.
Attempt B: Bounded search for t in [1,5000] finds no exponent with T^t≡1 mod 7^4 and mod 17^2.
Attempt C: If minimality is dropped and one only knows t is a multiple of the true minimum, then A(k·t) mod 1000 takes multiple values; for k=1..5 it equals 400,800,200,600,0.

task(s).name: secondary_independent_check
task(s).reason:
Independent verification of the quotient residues:
- Compute r1 = T^t mod (1000·7^4) and set q1 = ((r1-1)/7^4) mod 1000 using exact integer division.
- Compute r2 = T^t mod (1000·17^2) and set q2 = ((r2-1)/17^2) mod 1000 similarly.
- Confirm (q1+q2) mod 1000 matches the modular-inverse computation (in the realized instance: q1=840, q2=560, sum=400).

task(s).name: novelty_equivalence_audit
task(s).reason:

title: GCD-sum mod 1000 from power sums of shifted primitive 8th roots

identity.short_name: GCD-sum mod 1000 from power sums of shifted primitive 8th roots

subdomain(s): Ramanujan sums and periodic integer sequences
gcd aggregation by residue-class and divisibility counting

problem_spec.summary: A quartic polynomial is a shifted cyclotomic polynomial Φ_8(y) after translating x=y+a. The shifted roots are primitive 8th roots of unity, so their power sums are Ramanujan sums c_8(k), which take values in {0,-2,4}. Use this periodic structure (including the gcd(k,0)=k case) to evaluate a large gcd-sum and reduce it modulo 1000.
givens: r_1,...,r_4 are the roots of f(x)=0.
a=2 and y_i=r_i-a.
For k>=1, S_k=Σ_i y_i^k.
T=Σ_{k=1}^K gcd(k,|S_k|).
Output T mod 1000.
ask: Compute T mod 1000.

parameter(s).name: a
parameter(s).purpose: translation x=y+a revealing a cyclotomic polynomial

parameter(s).name: m
parameter(s).purpose: cyclotomic order (here m=8 so Φ_8 has degree 4)

parameter(s).name: K
parameter(s).purpose: cutoff in the gcd-sum

parameter(s).name: modulus
parameter(s).purpose: final reduction (here 1000)

parameter(s).name: gcd_convention
parameter(s).purpose: specifies the standard extension of gcd to zero

invariant(s).name: shift_to_cyclotomic
invariant(s).statement: Substituting x=y+a yields g(y)=f(y+a)=Φ_m(y). In this instance, f(y+2)=y^4+1=Φ_8(y).

invariant(s).name: power_sum_equals_ramanujan
invariant(s).statement: If {y_i} are primitive m-th roots of unity, then S_k=Σ y_i^k = c_m(k), the Ramanujan sum, hence S_k is an integer depending only on gcd(m,k).

invariant(s).name: ramanujan_closed_form_for_m8
invariant(s).statement: For m=8 and g=gcd(8,k), c_8(k)=μ(8/g)φ(g), giving: S_k=4 if 8|k; S_k=-2 if k\equiv4 (mod 8); S_k=0 if 4∤k.

invariant(s).name: gcd_sum_reduction_by_divisibility
invariant(s).statement: Because |S_k|\in{0,2,4}, gcd(k,|S_k|) reduces to k on 4∤k, to 2 on k\equiv4 (mod 8), and to 4 on 8|k, enabling counting by divisibility classes.

essential_assumption(s): The shift x=y+2 is exact and f(y+2)=y^4+1 holds identically.
y^4+1 is identified as Φ_8(y), so {y_i} are primitive 8th roots of unity.
Ramanujan sums are used with the standard μ and φ functions.
gcd(k,0)=k is used in the gcd-sum.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The identity f(y+a)=Φ_m(y) uniquely determines the shifted roots {y_i}. Therefore S_k is uniquely c_m(k), which is uniquely determined by gcd(m,k). The gcd-sum over k=1..K is then a deterministic arithmetic evaluation, and reduction mod 1000 yields a unique residue.

technique(s).name: translate_polynomial_to_cyclotomic
technique(s).purpose: compute g(y)=f(y+a) and recognize it as Φ_m(y)

technique(s).name: ramanujan_sum_formula
technique(s).purpose: evaluate S_k=c_m(k)=μ(m/g)φ(g) with g=gcd(m,k)

technique(s).name: gcd_sum_split_by_divisibility
technique(s).purpose: rewrite Σ gcd(k,|S_k|) as a combination of a partial sum of integers plus small constant contributions

technique(s).name: arithmetic_progression_and_floor_counts
technique(s).purpose: count multiples and residue classes (4∤k, k\equiv4 mod8, 8|k) and evaluate the resulting sums

technique(s).name: final_mod_postprocess
technique(s).purpose: reduce T modulo 1000

theorem(s).name: cyclotomic_polynomial_root_characterization
theorem(s).role: Φ_m(y)=0 iff y is a primitive m-th root of unity

theorem(s).name: Ramanujan_sum_closed_form
theorem(s).role: c_m(k)=μ(m/g)φ(g) with g=gcd(m,k)

theorem(s).name: residue_class_counting
theorem(s).role: counts of numbers in arithmetic progressions up to K via floor functions

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: roots-of-unity cancellation and periodicity of Ramanujan sums reduce large sums to residue-class counting

critical_trick(s): Identify the shifted polynomial as Φ_8, so the power sums are Ramanujan sums c_8(k).
Exploit that c_8(k) often vanishes, converting gcd(k,0) terms into a simple sum of integers.
Split k by divisibility mod 8 and evaluate using closed-form summations and floor counts.

title: Base-$b$ ramp digits with order-$4$ cycle and exact $p$-adic/$q$-adic divisibility; sum of valid shifts modulo $1000$

identity.short_name: Base-$b$ ramp digits with order-$4$ cycle and exact $p$-adic/$q$-adic divisibility; sum of valid shifts modulo $1000$

problem_spec.summary: A length-$L$ base-$b$ integer is built from the digit ramp $(t,t+1,\dots,t+L-1)$. The $p$-side uses $b\equiv 1\pmod{p^k}$ so $N_t$ reduces to a digit sum modulo $p^k$, and LTE controls $v_p(\sum_{i=0}^{L-1} b^i)=v_p(L)$. The $q$-side uses an order-$4$ cycle coming from $q\mid(b^2+1)$ so $b^4\equiv 1\pmod q$ and $1+b+b^2+b^3\equiv 0\pmod q$. Exactness $v_p(N_t)=k$ and $v_q(N_t)=1$ is enforced by one-step lifts to $p^{k+1}$ and $q^2$ using first-order binomial expansions. The final task is the sum of all valid shifts $t$, reduced modulo $1000$.
givens:
Fix integers $b\ge 6$, distinct odd primes $p
e q$, an exponent $k\ge 4$, and a length $L\ge 5$ such that $p^k\parallel(b-1)$, $q\mid(b^2+1)$, $q^2
mid(b^4-1)$, $p\parallel L$, and $L
ot\equiv 0\pmod{4}$. Set $n=p^k q$ and assume $b\ge n+L$.
For each $t$ with $0\le t\le n-1$, define $N_t=\sum_{i=0}^{L-1} (t+i)b^i$.
ask: Let $S=\{t\in\{0,1,\dots,n-1\}: v_p(N_t)=k\ \text{and}\ v_q(N_t)=1\}$ and let $T=\sum_{t\in S} t$. Determine the unique $N\in\{0,1,\dots,999\}$ such that $N\equiv T\pmod{1000}$.

parameter(s).name: b
parameter(s).purpose: controls digit validity and congruence cycles modulo $p^k$ and $q^2$

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: supplies the prime-power constraint $v_p(N_t)=k$

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: supplies the order-$4$ cycle constraint $v_q(N_t)=1$

parameter(s).name: k
parameter(s).purpose: forces a nontrivial exactness check between $p^k$ and $p^{k+1}$

parameter(s).name: L
parameter(s).purpose: ensures the $p$-side congruence reduces to a single class modulo $p^{k-1}$ and the $q$-side depends on $t$

invariant(s).name: structured_modulus
invariant(s).statement: The modulus is $n=p^k q$ with $p,q$ distinct odd primes and $k\ge 4$.

invariant(s).name: base_alignment_two_regimes
invariant(s).statement: $p^k\parallel(b-1)$ gives $b\equiv 1\pmod{p^k}$, while $q\mid(b^2+1)$ gives $b^2\equiv -1\pmod q$ and $b^4\equiv 1\pmod q$, so $1+b+b^2+b^3\equiv 0\pmod q$.

invariant(s).name: length_guards
invariant(s).statement: $p\parallel L$ implies $v_p(\sum_{i=0}^{L-1} b^i)=v_p(L)=1$ by LTE, and $L\not\equiv 0\pmod 4$ prevents $\sum_{i=0}^{L-1} b^i\equiv 0\pmod q$.

invariant(s).name: digit_ramp_definition
invariant(s).statement: For $0\le t\le n-1$ and $0\le i\le L-1$, we have $0\le t+i\le n+L-2<b$, so the digits $t+i$ define a valid base-$b$ integer $N_t=\sum_{i=0}^{L-1} (t+i)b^i$.

essential_assumption(s):
The alignment $p^k\parallel(b-1)$ and $q\mid(b^2+1)$ creates two modular regimes: order $1$ modulo $p^k$ and order $4$ modulo $q$.
Exactness is required: $v_p(N_t)=k$ and $v_q(N_t)=1$, so the exclusions $p^{k+1}
mid N_t$ and $q^2
mid N_t$ must be enforced by lifting.
The guard $q^2
mid(b^4-1)$ ensures $b^4=1+q w$ with $q
mid w$, enabling a first-order lift modulo $q^2$.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The congruences $p^k\mid N_t$ and $q\mid N_t$ reduce to explicit linear congruences for $t$, and CRT yields a finite arithmetic progression of candidates in $\{0,\dots,n-1\}$. One-step lifts yield necessary-and-sufficient exclusion congruences for $p^{k+1}\mid N_t$ and $q^2\mid N_t$. Removing these forced excluded indices uniquely determines $S$ and hence uniquely determines $T$ and its residue modulo $1000$.

technique(s).name: positional_value_expansion
technique(s).purpose: rewrite $N_t$ as the linear form $tA+B$

technique(s).name: divisibility_via_digits
technique(s).purpose: reduce $N_t$ modulo $p^k$ to a digit-sum expression

technique(s).name: lte_valuation
technique(s).purpose: compute $v_p(A)=v_p(L)$

technique(s).name: cycle_decomposition_mod
technique(s).purpose: compute $A$ and $B$ modulo $q$ and modulo $q^2$

technique(s).name: linear_congruence_solving
technique(s).purpose: enumerate all candidate $t$ modulo $n$
outputs: an explicit arithmetic progression of candidates in $\{0,\dots,n-1\}$

technique(s).name: prime_power_lifting
technique(s).purpose: lift to decide exactness at $p$ and $q$
outputs: exclusion congruences for $p^{k+1}\mid N_t$ and $q^2\mid N_t$

technique(s).name: residue_class_summation
technique(s).purpose: compute $T=\sum_{t\in S} t$ and reduce modulo $1000$

theorem(s).name: Chinese_remainder_theorem
theorem(s).role: combine coprime congruences into a unique residue class

theorem(s).name: lifting_the_exponent_LTE
theorem(s).role: compute $v_p(b^L-1)=v_p(b-1)+v_p(L)$ for $p\mid(b-1)$

theorem(s).name: cyclotomic_order_four_digits_test
theorem(s).role: from $q\mid(b^2+1)$ deduce $b^2\equiv -1\pmod q$, $b^4\equiv 1\pmod q$, and $1+b+b^2+b^3\equiv 0\pmod q$

theorem(s).name: first_order_binomial_mod_prime_power
theorem(s).role: use $(1+m)^j\equiv 1+jm\pmod{m^2}$ for one-step lifts

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: A base-$b$ integer is a linear combination of powers of $b$; short multiplicative cycles of $b$ modulo prime powers collapse the expansion to linear constraints.

critical_trick(s):
Use $b\equiv 1\pmod{p^k}$ and LTE to reduce $p^k\mid N_t$ to a linear congruence in $t$ modulo $p^{k-1}$.
Use an order-$4$ cycle modulo $q$ and a first-order lift modulo $q^2$ to enforce $v_q(N_t)=1$ exactly.

title: Base-$b$ ramp digits with order-3 cycle test and exact $p$-adic/$q$-adic divisibility

identity.short_name: Base-$b$ ramp digits with order-3 cycle test and exact $p$-adic/$q$-adic divisibility

problem_spec.summary: A base-$b$ number is built from a length-$L$ ramp of digits $(t,t+1,\dots,t+L-1)$. Divisibility by a prime power $p^k$ is exposed by the digit-sum reduction $b\equiv 1\pmod{p^k}$, while divisibility by a distinct prime $q$ is exposed by an order-3 cycle reduction coming from $q\mid (b^2+b+1)$ (so $b^3\equiv 1\pmod q$). Exactness ($p^{k+1}\nmid N_t$ and $q^2\nmid N_t$) is forced by one-step lifts to $p^{k+1}$ and $q^2$, the latter using a 3-cycle lift via $b^3=1+q w$.
givens:
Fix an integer base $b\ge 6$ and distinct odd primes $p\ne q$ with an exponent $k\ge 3$ such that $p^k\parallel (b-1)$ and $q\parallel (b^2+b+1)$; set $n=p^k q$.
Fix a length $L\ge 5$ satisfying $p\parallel L$ and $L\equiv 2\pmod{3}$, and assume $b\ge n+L$.
For each integer $t$ with $0\le t\le n-1$, define digits $d_i(t)=t+i$ for $i=0,\dots,L-1$ and define the base-$b$ integer

title: Base-b digit ramp with mixed prime-power exactness plus carry parity and quadratic-residue lifting filters

identity.short_name: Base-b digit ramp with mixed prime-power exactness plus carry parity and quadratic-residue lifting filters

problem_spec.summary: A base-b integer is defined by a length-L ramp of digits (t, t+1, ..., t+L-1). Exact v_p and v_q constraints are enforced using a digit-collapse regime b\equiv1 (mod p^k), LTE to control v_p of the geometric sum, a one-step lift to p^{k+1}, and a q-order-4 cycle b^2\equiv-1 (mod q) with computation mod q^2. A CRT candidate list is then coupled to (i) binomial parity via Kummer/Lucas carries for K a power of two, and (ii) quadratic residuosity modulo r^2 reduced via Hensel and evaluated via quadratic reciprocity. The ask is the sum of the admissible shifts reduced mod 1000.
givens:
Integers b,L with L\geq5 and b>n+L, odd primes p\neqq, integer k\geq4, a power of two K=2^m, and an odd prime r not in {p,q} satisfy: p^k||(b-1), v_p(L)=2, q|(b^2+1), and q^2∤(b^4-1). Define n=p^k q.
For 0\leqt\leqn-1, define N_t=\sum_{i=0}^{L-1}(t+i)b^i.
ask: For S={t in {0,...,n-1}: v_p(N_t)=k, v_q(N_t)=1, binom(t+K,K) odd, and ∃x (x^2\equivt mod r^2)}, set T=\sum_{t in S} t. Determine the unique N in {0,...,999} with N\equivT (mod 1000).

parameter(s).name: b
parameter(s).domain: integer base with b>n+L and the stated congruence constraints to p,q
parameter(s).purpose: controls digit validity and the modular regimes (digit-collapse and order-4 cycle)

parameter(s).name: L
parameter(s).purpose: forces v_p(\sum b^i)=2 via LTE and determines the p-adic lift obstruction

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: p-adic exact valuation constraint

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: order-4 cycle enabling q-adic linearization

parameter(s).name: k
parameter(s).purpose: depth of the p-adic exactness requirement

parameter(s).name: K
parameter(s).purpose: makes binomial parity equivalent to a single carry/bit constraint

parameter(s).name: r
parameter(s).purpose: quadratic-residue filter modulo r^2 via Hensel + reciprocity

invariant(s).name: digit_ramp_validity
invariant(s).statement: For 0\leqt\leqn-1, t+L-1\leqn+L-2<b, so digits t,...,t+L-1 define N_t uniquely in base b.

invariant(s).name: p_digit_collapse
invariant(s).statement: If p^k||(b-1) then b\equiv1 (mod p^k) and N_t mod p^k collapses to \sum_{i=0}^{L-1}(t+i).

invariant(s).name: lte_geometric_sum_depth
invariant(s).statement: With p|(b-1) and p odd, LTE yields v_p(\sum_{i=0}^{L-1} b^i)=v_p(L)=2.

invariant(s).name: q_order_four_cycle
invariant(s).statement: If q|(b^2+1) then b^2\equiv-1 (mod q), hence b^4\equiv1 (mod q) and 1+b+b^2+b^3\equiv0 (mod q).

invariant(s).name: binomial_parity_carry_invariant
invariant(s).statement: For K=2^m, binom(t+K,K) is odd iff there is no carry in base 2 when adding t and K, equivalently (t & K)=0.

invariant(s).name: quadratic_residue_lift
invariant(s).statement: For odd prime r and r∤t, t is a square mod r^2 iff t is a square mod r (Hensel lifting for x^2-t).

essential_assumption(s): b\equiv1 (mod p^k) and b^2\equiv-1 (mod q) both hold.
K is a power of 2 so parity is governed by carries (Kummer/Lucas).
r is an odd prime distinct from p,q so Hensel lifting and Legendre symbols apply cleanly.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The exact-valuation constraints yield explicit congruences and exclusion classes modulo p^{k-1} and q^2; CRT produces a finite candidate progression in {0,...,n-1}. The carry-parity invariant filters this list by a bit condition, while the quadratic-residue lifting invariant reduces the residue test mod r^2 to a Legendre-symbol computation mod r, evaluated via quadratic reciprocity. The intersection contains exactly one t, forcing a unique T and unique T mod 1000.

title: Carry invariants in base-$b$ arithmetic (addition/multiplication carries)

identity.short_name: Carry invariants in base-$b$ arithmetic (addition/multiplication carries)

problem_spec.summary: In base $b$, quantify how carries occur in standard addition/multiplication. Given integers $x,y$ (and optionally $z$) in base $b$, compute a canonical carry statistic (e.g., total number of carries in addition) using digit-sum drop or related invariants.
givens: $b\in\mathbb{N},\ b\ge 2$
$x,y\in\mathbb{N}$ (written in base $b$ with the usual digit set $\{0,1,\dots,b-1\}$)
Carry statistic is defined by the standard grade-school algorithm in base $b$.
ask: Compute $C$, the total carry count (or total carry-sum, depending on variant) produced when adding $x$ and $y$ in base $b$.

parameter(s).name: b
parameter(s).purpose: sets the carry threshold and digit-sum modulus

parameter(s).name: x
parameter(s).purpose: first addend/multiplicand

parameter(s).name: y
parameter(s).purpose: second addend/multiplicand

parameter(s).name: operation_mode
parameter(s).purpose: selects whether carries arise from addition or multiplication

invariant(s).name: digit_sum_drop_equals_carry_mass
invariant(s).statement: Let $S_b(n)$ be the base-$b$ digit sum. In base-$b$ addition, $S_b(x)+S_b(y)-S_b(x+y)=(b-1)\cdot K$, where $K$ is the total carried amount aggregated across digit positions (in binary, this equals the number of carries).

invariant(s).name: parity_carry_link_binary
invariant(s).statement: In base $2$, carries correspond to positions where two $1$ bits overlap; this links carry counts to Hamming weights and enables parity/size arguments.

essential_assumption(s): The carry statistic is defined with the standard deterministic grade-school algorithm (left-to-right carry propagation).
For the binary-special-case reasoning, the structural fact is that base $2$ digits are in $\{0,1\}$ and carries are exactly the overlaps of $1$ bits.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Given $b,x,y$ and the fixed carry rule, the carry process is deterministic, so the total carry count (or carry mass) is uniquely determined.

technique(s).name: carry_invariants
technique(s).purpose: relate carries to digit-sum changes and modular invariants
outputs: closed formula linking digit sums and carry mass

technique(s).name: addition_carries
technique(s).purpose: track carry propagation position-by-position

technique(s).name: pigeonhole_on_digits
technique(s).purpose: force repetitions/overlaps leading to carry lower bounds or contradictions

theorem(s).name: base_b_representation
theorem(s).role: enables translating carry rules into digit-level algebra

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Carries are bookkeeping artifacts of representing integers in a fixed base; expressing addition in digit form reveals invariant relations among digit sums and carries.

critical_trick(s): Compute the digit-sum drop $S_b(x)+S_b(y)-S_b(x+y)$ to read off total carry mass (especially clean in base $2$).
For bounds/uniqueness problems, combine carry counts with parity and size inequalities.

title: Carry invariants in base-$b$ arithmetic (addition/multiplication carries) - Buffed Seed

identity.short_name: Carry invariants in base-$b$ arithmetic (addition/multiplication carries) - Buffed Seed

problem_spec.summary: In base $b$, carries in the standard addition algorithm can be extracted from the drop in the base-$b$ digit sum. In this family, the base $b$ is forced to have a structured composite modulus $b-1=p^{k}q$ (with $k\ge 2$), and a least exponent $t$ is pinned down by simultaneous prime-power divisibility constraints on $b^{t}-1$; this exponent then controls a nontrivial carry statistic. The final reported answer is normalized modulo $1000$.
givens: $p,q$ are distinct odd primes and $k\in\mathbb{Z}_{\ge 2}$.
Define the base $b:=p^{k}q+1$ and write all integers in base $b$ using digits $\{0,1,\dots,b-1\}$.
For a prime $\ell$, let $v_\ell(n)$ denote the $\ell$-adic valuation (largest $e$ with $\ell^e\mid n$).
Let $t$ be the *least* positive integer such that $p^{2k}q^{2}\mid b^{t}-1$.

When adding $X$ and $Y$ in base $b$ with the standard grade-school algorithm, let $c_i$ be the carry sent from digit position $i$ to $i+1$ (so $c_i\in\mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}$), and define the total carry mass $C:=\sum_{i\ge 0} c_i$.
ask: (i) Prove that the integer $t$ exists and is uniquely determined by the conditions above, and determine $t$ explicitly in terms of $p,q,k$.
(ii) Using the digit-sum/carry invariant, compute $N\equiv (b\cdot C+t)\pmod{1000}$ and report the unique residue in $\{0,1,\dots,999\}$.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: first prime factor in the structured modulus $b-1=p^k q$

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: second prime factor in the structured modulus $b-1=p^k q$

parameter(s).name: k
parameter(s).purpose: forces a genuine prime-power factor $p^k$ in $b-1$ and controls the valuation target

invariant(s).name: anti_triviality_structured_modulus
invariant(s).statement: $b-1=p^{k}q$ with $p,q$ distinct odd primes and $k\ge 2$; in particular $b-1$ is composite and contains a nontrivial prime power factor.

invariant(s).name: anti_triviality_base_large_enough
invariant(s).statement: $b\ge 5$, so in particular $2<b$ and the final digit $2$ appearing in $X+Y$ cannot create an extra carry.

invariant(s).name: digit_sum_mod_b_minus_1
invariant(s).statement: For all integers $n\ge 0$, if $S_b(n)$ is the base-$b$ digit sum then $n\equiv S_b(n)\pmod{b-1}$.

invariant(s).name: digit_sum_drop_equals_carry_mass
invariant(s).statement: In base-$b$ addition of $X$ and $Y$ (with carry masses $c_i$ as defined), $S_b(X)+S_b(Y)-S_b(X+Y)=(b-1)\,C$.

invariant(s).name: prime_power_lifting_formula
invariant(s).statement: Because $b=p^{k}q+1$ and $p,q$ are odd with $p,q\mid (b-1)$ and $p,q\nmid b$, lifting-the-exponent gives $v_p(b^{t}-1)=v_p(b-1)+v_p(t)=k+v_p(t)$ and $v_q(b^{t}-1)=v_q(b-1)+v_q(t)=1+v_q(t)$.

invariant(s).name: anti_triviality_minimal_exponent_forces_nonzero_carry
invariant(s).statement: From $p^{2k}q^2\mid b^{t}-1$ and the lifting formula, one must have $p^{k}\mid t$ and $q\mid t$; hence the minimality of $t$ forces $t\ge p^{k}q>1$, so the carry mass $C$ is nonzero.

essential_assumption(s): Carries are counted with the standard deterministic grade-school base-$b$ addition algorithm (right-to-left carry propagation).
The exponent $t$ is defined with a *minimality* condition; without this guard the prime-power divisibility constraints admit infinitely many solutions.

Parameters may be large; any approach that requires explicitly writing the full base-$b$ expansions of $b^{t}$ or $b^{2t}$ is excluded by design-use prime-power divisibility identities and digit-sum/carry invariants instead.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Prime-power lifting translates $p^{2k}q^2\mid b^{t}-1$ into exact lower bounds on $v_p(t)$ and $v_q(t)$, so the imposed *least* $t$ is unique.
With $b$ and $t$ fixed, $X$ and $Y$ are uniquely determined; the carry sequence $(c_i)$ in their base-$b$ sum is deterministic, hence $C$ is unique.
The reported output is the canonical residue $N\in\{0,1,\dots,999\}$ of $b\cdot C+t$ modulo $1000$, so the final answer is unique under the stated normalization.

title: Carry invariants in base-$b$ arithmetic (addition/multiplication carries) - Buffed Seed

identity.short_name: Carry invariants in base-$b$ arithmetic (addition/multiplication carries) - Buffed Seed

problem_spec.summary: In base $b$, carries in the standard addition algorithm can be extracted from the drop in the base-$b$ digit sum. In this family, the base $b$ is forced to have a structured composite modulus $b-1=p^{k}q$ (with $k\ge 2$), and a least exponent $t$ is pinned down by simultaneous prime-power divisibility constraints on $b^{t}-1$; this exponent then controls a nontrivial carry statistic. The final reported answer is normalized modulo $1000$.
givens: $p,q$ are distinct odd primes and $k\in\mathbb{Z}_{\ge 2}$.
Define the base $b:=p^{k}q+1$ and write all integers in base $b$ using digits $\{0,1,\dots,b-1\}$.
For a prime $\ell$, let $v_\ell(n)$ denote the $\ell$-adic valuation (largest $e$ with $\ell^e\mid n$).
Let $t$ be the *least* positive integer such that $p^{2k}q^{2}\mid b^{t}-1$.

When adding $X$ and $Y$ in base $b$ with the standard grade-school algorithm, let $c_i$ be the carry sent from digit position $i$ to $i+1$ (so $c_i\in\mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}$), and define the total carry mass $C:=\sum_{i\ge 0} c_i$.
ask: (i) Prove that the integer $t$ exists and is uniquely determined by the conditions above, and determine $t$ explicitly in terms of $p,q,k$.
(ii) Using the digit-sum/carry invariant, compute $N\equiv (b\cdot C+t)\pmod{1000}$ and report the unique residue in $\{0,1,\dots,999\}$.

parameter(s).purpose: first prime factor in the structured modulus $b-1=p^k q$

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: second prime factor in the structured modulus $b-1=p^k q$

parameter(s).purpose: forces a genuine prime-power factor $p^k$ in $b-1$ and controls the valuation target

invariant(s).name: anti_triviality_structured_modulus
invariant(s).statement: $b-1=p^{k}q$ with $p,q$ distinct odd primes and $k\ge 2$; in particular $b-1$ is composite and contains a nontrivial prime power factor.

invariant(s).name: anti_triviality_base_large_enough
invariant(s).statement: $b\ge 5$, so in particular $2<b$ and the final digit $2$ appearing in $X+Y$ cannot create an extra carry.

invariant(s).name: digit_sum_mod_b_minus_1
invariant(s).statement: For all integers $n\ge 0$, if $S_b(n)$ is the base-$b$ digit sum then $n\equiv S_b(n)\pmod{b-1}$.

invariant(s).name: digit_sum_drop_equals_carry_mass
invariant(s).statement: In base-$b$ addition of $X$ and $Y$ (with carry masses $c_i$ as defined), $S_b(X)+S_b(Y)-S_b(X+Y)=(b-1)\,C$.

invariant(s).name: prime_power_lifting_formula
invariant(s).statement: Because $b=p^{k}q+1$ and $p,q$ are odd with $p,q\mid (b-1)$ and $p,q\nmid b$, lifting-the-exponent gives $v_p(b^{t}-1)=v_p(b-1)+v_p(t)=k+v_p(t)$ and $v_q(b^{t}-1)=v_q(b-1)+v_q(t)=1+v_q(t)$.

invariant(s).name: anti_triviality_minimal_exponent_forces_nonzero_carry
invariant(s).statement: From $p^{2k}q^2\mid b^{t}-1$ and the lifting formula, one must have $p^{k}\mid t$ and $q\mid t$; hence the minimality of $t$ forces $t\ge p^{k}q>1$, so the carry mass $C$ is nonzero.

essential_assumption(s): Carries are counted with the standard deterministic grade-school base-$b$ addition algorithm (right-to-left carry propagation).
The exponent $t$ is defined with a *minimality* condition; without this guard the prime-power divisibility constraints admit infinitely many solutions.

Parameters may be large; any approach that requires explicitly writing the full base-$b$ expansions of $b^{t}$ or $b^{2t}$ is excluded by design-use prime-power divisibility identities and digit-sum/carry invariants instead.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Prime-power lifting translates $p^{2k}q^2\mid b^{t}-1$ into exact lower bounds on $v_p(t)$ and $v_q(t)$, so the imposed *least* $t$ is unique.
With $b$ and $t$ fixed, $X$ and $Y$ are uniquely determined; the carry sequence $(c_i)$ in their base-$b$ sum is deterministic, hence $C$ is unique.
The reported output is the canonical residue $N\in\{0,1,\dots,999\}$ of $b\cdot C+t$ modulo $1000$, so the final answer is unique under the stated normalization.

title: Digits/base-$b$ representation with divisibility tests (mod $9$, $11$, repunits)

identity.short_name: Digits/base-$b$ representation with divisibility tests (mod $9$, $11$, repunits)

problem_spec.summary: Given digit constraints of an integer in base $b$ (including possible repunit/palindrome structure), determine a uniquely specified value derived from the number using digit-based divisibility tests (e.g., remainder modulo $b-1$ / $b+1$, or reconstruction of the number when combined with other constraints).
givens: $b\in\mathbb{N},\ b\ge 2$ (base)
A digit vector $(d_0,\dots,d_{L-1})$ with $0\le d_i<b$ describing $N=\sum_{i=0}^{L-1} d_i b^i$
Additional constraints may be imposed: fixed length $L$, palindrome condition, repunit condition, or congruence conditions like $N\equiv 0\pmod m$.
ask: Compute the requested digit-derived quantity (e.g., $N\bmod(b-1)$ via digit sum; $N\bmod(b+1)$ via alternating sum; or recover $N$ uniquely from combined digit and congruence constraints).

parameter(s).name: b
parameter(s).purpose: sets the positional value system and the relevant moduli $b\pm1$

parameter(s).name: L
parameter(s).purpose: controls number of digits

parameter(s).name: digits
parameter(s).purpose: encodes the number in base $b$

parameter(s).name: structure_mode
parameter(s).purpose: adds structured digit constraints

parameter(s).name: mod_test_mode
parameter(s).purpose: selects which digit test is used

invariant(s).name: positional_expansion
invariant(s).statement: $N=\sum_{i=0}^{L-1} d_i b^i$ (positional value expansion) determines $N$ uniquely from its base-$b$ digits.

invariant(s).name: digit_sum_mod_b_minus_1
invariant(s).statement: Because $b\equiv 1\pmod{b-1}$, we have $N\equiv \sum_i d_i\pmod{b-1}$ (digit sum test).

essential_assumption(s): Digit-based congruence tests rely on congruences like $b\equiv 1\pmod{b-1}$ and $b\equiv -1\pmod{b+1}$. For the classical decimal "mod 11 alternating sum" rule, the structural fact is $10\equiv -1\pmod{11}$.
If the task is "recover $N$", instances must impose enough independent constraints (digit relations + congruences) to make $N$ unique.
uniqueness_certificate(s): If the output is a remainder, it is normalized to the unique representative in $\{0,1,\dots,m-1\}$. If the output is the reconstructed number $N$, uniqueness follows from the positional expansion together with the instance’s constraint system.

technique(s).name: positional_value_expansion
technique(s).purpose: rewrite the integer in a form suitable for modular reduction

technique(s).name: divisibility_via_digits
technique(s).purpose: reduce $b^i$ modulo the modulus to get a digit-based test
outputs: congruence linking $N$ to digit sum / alternating sum

technique(s).name: pigeonhole_on_digits
technique(s).purpose: prove existence of repetitions or force a structured digit pattern
outputs: a reduced finite set of candidate digit configurations

theorem(s).name: mod_3_mod_9_digits
theorem(s).role: decimal special case of the $b-1$ digit-sum test

theorem(s).name: mod_11_alternating_sum_optional
theorem(s).role: decimal special case of the $b+1$ alternating-sum test

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: A base-$b$ number is a linear combination of powers of $b$; reducing those powers modulo a chosen modulus yields digit tests.

critical_trick(s): Reduce $b^i$ modulo a convenient modulus so that $b^i$ collapses to $\pm 1$ (or another short cycle), turning the congruence into a digit-sum/alternating-sum condition.
When reconstruction is required, combine congruences with digit constraints to isolate a unique digit vector.

title: Digits/base-$b$ representation with divisibility tests (mod $9$, $11$, repunits) - Buffed Seed

identity.short_name: Digits/base-$b$ representation with divisibility tests (mod $9$, $11$, repunits) - Buffed Seed

problem_spec.summary: Define a structured base-$b$ digit sequence whose reduction modulo divisors of $b-1$ and $b+1$ yields coupled linear congruences, then enforce exact prime-power divisibility by a one-step lift. The task is to count the admissible residue classes; the lifting step is essential.
givens: Fix an integer base $b\ge 6$ and distinct odd primes $p

title: Kummer valuation from forced borrow-chain carry blocks

identity.short_name: Kummer valuation from forced borrow-chain carry blocks

problem_spec.summary: A sparse base-$p$ digit pattern forces long borrow-chains in $n-k$, which forces a predictable carry-count in $k+(n-k)=n$. Kummer's theorem converts this carry-count into $v_p\!\left(\binom{n}{k}\right)$, and the final answer is a mod-$1000$ normalization mixing these invariants.
givens: $p=19$ is prime.
In base $p$, when adding $k$ and $n-k$ by the standard grade-school algorithm, let $c_i$ be the carry sent from digit position $i$ to $i+1$, and define $C:=\sum_{i\ge 0} c_i$.
Let $V:=v_p\!\left(\binom{n}{k}\right)$.
Define $N$ to be the unique integer with $0\le N<1000$ satisfying $N\equiv (p^2+7)C+(p+11)V+n\pmod{1000}$.
ask: Compute $N$ (with the stated normalization to the unique representative in $\{0,1,\dots,999\}$).

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: prime base controlling carries and the $p$-adic valuation

parameter(s).name: L
parameter(s).purpose: length of each forced borrow-chain block in base $p$ subtraction

parameter(s).name: m
parameter(s).purpose: number of borrow-chain blocks (and hence the carry-count multiplier)

invariant(s).name: sparse_power_sum_definition
invariant(s).statement: Let $g:=L+1$. Set $n:=1+\sum_{j=1}^{m} p^{jg}$ and $k:=\sum_{j=0}^{m-1} p^{jg+1}$. Then in base $p$, $n$ has digit $1$ at positions $0,g,2g,\dots,mg$ and $k$ has digit $1$ at positions $1,g+1,\dots,(m-1)g+1$.

invariant(s).name: borrow_chain_blocks_in_n_minus_k
invariant(s).statement: The base-$p$ subtraction $n-k$ forces exactly $m$ borrow-chains, each spanning $L$ consecutive zero digits, so $n-k$ has exactly $mL$ digits equal to $p-1$.

invariant(s).name: digit_sum_drop_encodes_carry_mass
invariant(s).statement: For $A+B=n$ computed in base $p$ with carry digits $(c_i)$, $S_p(A)+S_p(B)-S_p(n)=(p-1)\sum_{i\ge 0} c_i=(p-1)C$.

invariant(s).name: kummer_bridge
invariant(s).statement: For prime $p$, $V=v_p\!\left(\binom{n}{k}\right)$ equals the number of carries in base $p$ addition of $k$ and $n-k$, hence equals $C$ in this binary-carry setting.

essential_assumption(s): $p$ is prime so Kummer's theorem applies.
Carries are taken from the standard deterministic grade-school base-$p$ addition algorithm.
$0<k<n$ so $\binom{n}{k}$ is defined as a positive integer.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The base-$p$ expansions of $n$ and $k$ are unique, so $n-k$ is uniquely determined by deterministic borrow-chains; this fixes the carry sequence $(c_i)$ and $C$.
Kummer's theorem forces a unique value of $V$ from that carry sequence.
The congruence class modulo $1000$ has a unique representative in $\{0,\dots,999\}$, so $N$ is unique.

technique(s).name: sparse_base_p_representation
technique(s).purpose: extract explicit base-$p$ digit locations without expanding huge integers

technique(s).name: borrow_chain_decomposition
technique(s).purpose: determine the exact digit pattern of $n-k$ via forced borrow-chains across zero blocks
outputs: digit pattern and digit sum of $n-k$

technique(s).name: digit_sum_computation
technique(s).purpose: compute $S_p(n)$, $S_p(k)$, and $S_p(n-k)$ efficiently
outputs: closed forms for the digit sums

technique(s).name: base_p_carry_rules
technique(s).purpose: show the carries are binary so the carry mass equals the carry-count needed by Kummer
outputs: $c_i\in\{0,1\}$ and $C$ equals the number of carry positions

technique(s).name: carry_mass_via_digit_sum_drop
technique(s).purpose: convert digit-sum drop into the total carry mass $C$

technique(s).name: kummer_carry_valuation_bridge
technique(s).purpose: convert the carry-count to $V=v_p\!\left(\binom{n}{k}\right)$

technique(s).name: modular_reduction_mod_1000
technique(s).purpose: evaluate the final congruence and normalize to $\{0,\dots,999\}$

theorem(s).name: Digit-sum carry identity (base $p$)
theorem(s).role: Relates $S_p(A)+S_p(B)-S_p(A+B)$ to $(p-1)C$, enabling carry computation without simulating all digits.

theorem(s).name: Kummer's theorem
theorem(s).role: Identifies $v_p\!\left(\binom{n}{k}\right)$ with the number of base-$p$ carries in $k+(n-k)$; this is the new kernel bridge.

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: place-value decomposition
bookkeeping with carries/borrows
valuation as carry-count via base representation

critical_trick(s): A forced borrow-chain across each $L$-digit zero block makes $n-k$ contain long runs of digits $p-1$.
The digit-sum drop identity turns those runs into an exact carry mass $C$ (an invariant-driven count).
Kummer's theorem converts the carry-count into $V=v_p\!\left(\binom{n}{k}\right)$, changing the kernel from LTE to carry/valuation forcing.
A final modular reduction yields the normalized $N\in\{0,\dots,999\}$.

library(s).reason: symbolic canonicalization for novelty_equivalence_audit

library(s).name: math

library(s).name: itertools

task(s).name: primary_structural_check
task(s).reason: Compute base-$p$ digits of $n,k,n-k$; simulate grade-school carries in $k+(n-k)$ to get $C$; compute $V$ via Kummer carry-count; compute $N$ modulo $1000$ and normalize. Preflight attempts: Attempt A: naive guess $C=V=3$ (counting nonzero digits of $k$) gives $N=394$, mismatching the verified $N=976$. Attempt B: bounded digit-position scan only up to positions $0\le i\le 9$ counts $C_{\le 9}=8$ carries and yields $N_{\le 9}=384\ne 976$, showing truncation cannot shortcut the answer. Attempt C: using only $n\bmod 1000=200$ and crude bounds $0\le C,V\le 20$ leaves 402 distinct candidates for $N$ in $\{0,\dots,999\}$, so partial information does not determine the output.

task(s).name: secondary_independent_check
task(s).reason: Independently compute $V$ using Legendre's formula $v_p(n!)=\sum_{j\ge 1}\lfloor n/p^j\rfloor$ and $V=v_p(n!)-v_p(k!)-v_p((n-k)!)$; independently compute $C$ via digit-sum identity from explicit digit sums; confirm both equal 12 and reproduce $N=976$.

task(s).name: novelty_equivalence_audit
task(s).reason: Canonicalize parent vs daughter defining expressions with sympy simplify+together+cancel+expand+factor; normalize by renaming free symbols to $x_0,x_1,\dots$ and dividing by gcd/content when polynomial. Try deterministic symmetry renamings/permutations (identity; swap $X\leftrightarrow Y$; swap $b\leftrightarrow t$). Confirm no match, so daughter is not equivalent to parent under allowed disguises.

problem_spec.summary: Define a structured base-$b$ digit sequence whose reduction modulo divisors of $b-1$ and $b+1$ yields coupled linear congruences, then enforce *exact* prime-power divisibility by a one-step lift. The task is to count the admissible residue classes, where the lifting step is essential.
givens:
Fix an integer base $b\ge 6$ and distinct odd primes $p\ne q$ with an exponent $k\ge 2$ such that $p^k\parallel (b-1)$ and $q\parallel (b+1)$; set $n=p^k q$.
Fix an odd length $L\ge 3$ satisfying $p\parallel L$, $q\nmid L$, and $b\ge n+L$.
For each integer $t$ with $0\le t\le n-1$, define digits $d_i(t)=t+i$ for $i=0,\dots,L-1$ and define the base-$b$ integer

title: Weighted carry index from disjoint borrow blocks

identity.short_name: Weighted carry index from disjoint borrow blocks

problem_spec.summary: A sparse base-$p$ power-sum definition forces disjoint borrow blocks in $n-k$. These blocks force a deterministic carry pattern in the base-$p$ addition of $k$ and $n-k$, allowing computation of both the carry mass $C$ and the weighted carry index sum $W=\sum i c_i$. Kummer's theorem identifies this carry pattern with $v_p\left(\binom{n}{k}\right)$, and CRT is used to compute $n\bmod 1000$ for the final modular normalization.
givens: $p=17$ is prime.
Write $k=\sum_{i\ge 0} a_i p^i$ and $n-k=\sum_{i\ge 0} b_i p^i$ with $0\le a_i,b_i<p$, and define carries by $c_{-1}=0$ and $a_i+b_i+c_{i-1}=d_i+p c_i$ with $0\le d_i<p$.
Define $C=\sum_{i\ge 0} c_i$ and $W=\sum_{i\ge 0} i c_i$.
Let $V=v_p\left(\binom{n}{k}\right)$.
Define $N$ by $0\le N<1000$ and $N\equiv (p^3+7)C+(p^2+11)V+(p+5)W+n \pmod{1000}$.
ask: Compute $N$.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: base for digit expansions and the prime for $v_p(\cdot)$

parameter(s).name: m
parameter(s).purpose: number of disjoint borrow blocks

parameter(s).name: e_list
parameter(s).purpose: locations of the nonzero digits of $n$ above position $0$

parameter(s).name: L_list
parameter(s).domain: list of $m$ integers with $1\le L_j<e_j-e_{j-1}$ for $j=1,\dots,m$ and $e_0=0$
parameter(s).purpose: lengths of the borrow blocks, allowed to vary with $j$

invariant(s).name: sparse_power_sum_definition
invariant(s).statement: Let $e_0=0$. Define $n=1+\sum_{j=1}^m p^{e_j}$ and $k=\sum_{j=1}^m p^{e_j-L_j}$. Then the base-$p$ digits of $n$ are $1$ at positions $0,e_1,\dots,e_m$ and $0$ elsewhere, and the base-$p$ digits of $k$ are $1$ at positions $e_j-L_j$ and $0$ elsewhere.

invariant(s).name: disjoint_borrow_blocks_in_n_minus_k
invariant(s).statement: Under $1\le L_j<e_j-e_{j-1}$, the expansion of $n-k$ has digits $p-1$ on each interval $[e_j-L_j,e_j-1]$ and digit $0$ at $e_j$, with these intervals pairwise disjoint.

invariant(s).name: carry_positions_match_borrow_blocks
invariant(s).statement: In the base-$p$ addition of $k$ and $n-k$, the indices with $c_i=1$ are exactly the union of the intervals $[e_j-L_j,e_j-1]$. Hence $C=\sum_{j=1}^m L_j$ and $W=\sum_{j=1}^m \sum_{t=0}^{L_j-1} (e_j-L_j+t)$.

invariant(s).name: digit_sum_drop_encodes_carry_mass
invariant(s).statement: For any base-$p$ addition $A+B=n$ with carries $(c_i)$, $S_p(A)+S_p(B)-S_p(n)=(p-1)\sum_{i\ge 0} c_i=(p-1)C$.

invariant(s).name: kummer_bridge
invariant(s).statement: For prime $p$, $V=v_p\left(\binom{n}{k}\right)$ equals the number of carries in the base-$p$ addition of $k$ and $n-k$, hence $V=C$.

operator(s): base-$p$ digit expansion and digit sum $S_p(\cdot)$
essential_assumption(s): $p$ is prime so Kummer's theorem applies.
$0<k<n$ so $\binom{n}{k}$ is a positive integer.
$\gcd(p,1000)=1$ so modular computations modulo $1000$ are non-degenerate.
$1\le L_j<e_j-e_{j-1}$ so borrow blocks are disjoint and forced.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The base-$p$ digits of $n$ and $k$ determine disjoint borrow blocks in $n-k$, which forces a unique carry pattern and hence unique invariants $C$ and $W$.
Kummer's theorem forces a unique $V$ from that carry pattern.
The congruence modulo $1000$ has a unique representative in $\{0,1,\dots,999\}$, forcing $N$ uniquely.

technique(s).name: sparse_base_p_representation
technique(s).purpose: read digit positions directly without expanding large integers

technique(s).name: borrow_chain_decomposition
technique(s).purpose: expand each $p^{e_j}-p^{e_j-L_j}$ into a block of digits equal to $p-1$

technique(s).name: carry_position_interval_analysis
technique(s).purpose: determine the carry indices and compute both $C$ and $W$

technique(s).name: digit_sum_computation
technique(s).purpose: compute $S_p(n)$, $S_p(k)$, and $S_p(n-k)$

technique(s).name: carry_mass_via_digit_sum_drop
technique(s).purpose: recover $C$ as a consistency check from digit sums

technique(s).name: kummer_carry_valuation_bridge
technique(s).purpose: compute $v_p\left(\binom{n}{k}\right)$ from carries

technique(s).name: CRT_mod_1000
technique(s).purpose: compute $n\bmod 1000$ via residues mod $8$ and mod $125$

technique(s).name: modular_reduction_mod_1000
technique(s).purpose: evaluate the defining congruence for $N$ and normalize to $0\le N<1000$

theorem(s).name: base-$p$ digit-sum carry identity
theorem(s).role: converts digit-sum drop into $(p-1)C$

theorem(s).name: Kummer's theorem
theorem(s).role: identifies $v_p\left(\binom{n}{k}\right)$ with the number of base-$p$ carries in $k+(n-k)$

theorem(s).name: Chinese remainder theorem
theorem(s).role: reconstructs a residue modulo $1000$ from mod $8$ and mod $125$

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: place-value expansion
deterministic carry bookkeeping
modular reconstruction from coprime moduli

critical_trick(s): Disjoint borrow blocks force disjoint carry intervals, enabling computation of both $C$ and the weighted invariant $W$.
Kummer's theorem converts the carry pattern into $v_p\left(\binom{n}{k}\right)$.
CRT reduces the sparse power sum for $n$ modulo $1000$.

title: Exponential Diophantine systems (modular + growth + LTE)

identity.short_name: Exponential Diophantine systems (modular + growth + LTE)

problem_spec.summary: Solve an exponential Diophantine system where integer exponents are constrained simultaneously by modular periodicity (orders/cycles) and by growth/inequality bounds. Output the canonical smallest solution pair in positive integers.
givens: $a,b\in\mathbb{N}$ with $a,b>1$
$c\in\mathbb{Z}$ (fixed offset)
We consider solutions $(x,y)\in\mathbb{N}^2$ to a specified exponential relation, e.g. $a^x-b^y=c$, possibly with extra congruence constraints (system form).
ask: Among all positive integer solutions $(x,y)$ of the given exponential system, output the lexicographically smallest solution (smallest $x$, then smallest $y$).

parameter(s).name: a
parameter(s).purpose: first base

parameter(s).name: b
parameter(s).purpose: second base

parameter(s).name: c
parameter(s).purpose: offset/target difference

parameter(s).name: equation_mode
parameter(s).purpose: selects a representative exponential Diophantine form

parameter(s).name: side_constraints
parameter(s).domain: finite set of congruence or gcd constraints on $(x,y)$
parameter(s).purpose: turns the task into a simultaneous system and enables modular obstructions

invariant(s).name: modulo_cycle_interface
invariant(s).statement: Reducing the equation modulo suitable primes yields periodicity constraints on exponents via multiplicative orders; these constrain $(x,y)$ modulo cycle lengths.

invariant(s).name: growth_gap_interface
invariant(s).statement: Comparing sizes $a^x$ and $b^y$ (and their difference) produces inequality bounds that restrict $(x,y)$ to a finite search region.

essential_assumption(s): A canonical output is defined by taking the lexicographically smallest positive solution; this avoids ambiguity when multiple solutions exist.

Instances should be chosen so that modular constraints plus growth bounds make the solution set finite and computationally checkable within reasonable limits (consistent with olympiad-style arguments).
uniqueness_certificate(s): The set of positive solutions is a subset of $\mathbb{N}^2$ and hence well-ordered lexicographically; if at least one solution exists, there is a unique lexicographically smallest solution, which is the reported output.

technique(s).name: modular_obstructions
inputs: equation reduced modulo primes or prime powers
technique(s).purpose: rule out large classes of exponent pairs by residue impossibilities
outputs: congruence restrictions on $(x,y)$ or impossibility certificates

technique(s).name: order_arguments
technique(s).purpose: convert congruences like $a^x\equiv t\pmod p$ into constraints on $x\bmod \mathrm{ord}_p(a)$

technique(s).name: lifting_exponents_LTE
inputs: valuation constraints at primes dividing $a\pm b$ or $c$
technique(s).purpose: tighten exponent bounds by comparing $v_p$ on both sides of the equation

technique(s).name: growth_bounds_for_exponents
technique(s).purpose: bound exponents using monotonicity/log-growth and the fixed offset $c$

technique(s).name: bounding_then_check
technique(s).purpose: finish by direct checking once the search space is bounded

theorem(s).name: mod_prime_obstructions
theorem(s).role: use reductions modulo primes to eliminate candidates

theorem(s).name: LTE_difference_of_powers
theorem(s).role: when the equation reduces to $a^x-b^y$ structure, LTE yields decisive $p$-adic constraints

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Exponential expressions exhibit both modular periodicity and rapid growth; combining these two controls often pins exponents down to finitely many options.

critical_trick(s): Choose a modulus where the exponent cycle length is small (order arguments) and combine with a size comparison to trap $(x,y)$.
Use LTE at a carefully chosen prime to sharpen the bounds when the equation has a difference-of-powers structure.

task(s).reason: Validate that the produced object(s) satisfy all defining congruences/divisibility/Diophantine constraints for randomly sampled parameters within the declared domain.

task(s).name: secondary_independent_check
task(s).reason: Cross-check the main result via an alternative computation (e.g., brute force for small bounds, or an independent formula such as factorization vs. valuation, or CRT reconstruction vs. direct search).

task(s).name: bounded_search_verification
task(s).reason: After deriving bounds, brute force all candidate $(x,y)$ pairs within the window to confirm the lexicographically smallest solution matches the theoretical conclusion for sample parameters.

$p,q$ are distinct odd primes with $\gcd(ab,pq)=1$
$p\mid(a-b)$, $v_p(a-b)=1$, and $p\equiv 3\pmod 8$
$q\nmid(a-b)$ and $q\nmid ab$; let $t=\operatorname{ord}_q(a\cdot b^{-1}\bmod q)$ (well-defined)

title: Exponential Diophantine systems (modular + growth + LTE) - Buffed Seed

identity.short_name: Exponential Diophantine systems (modular + growth + LTE) - Buffed Seed

problem_spec.summary: Find the least index in a difference-of-powers sequence whose exponent is forced simultaneously by a nontrivial multiplicative-order cycle modulo an odd prime and by an exact p-adic valuation constraint via LTE; report the resulting least index modulo 1000.
givens: $a,b\in\mathbb{N}$ with $a>b>1$ and $\gcd(a,b)=1$
$p,q$ are distinct odd primes with $\gcd(ab,pq)=1$
$p\mid(a-b)$, $v_p(a-b)=1$, and $p\equiv 3\pmod 8$
$q\nmid(a-b)$ and $q\nmid ab$; let $t=\operatorname{ord}_q(a\cdot b^{-1}\bmod q)$ (well-defined)
The order profile is nontrivial: $p\mid t$ but $p^2\nmid t$, and $t$ has at least one prime divisor different from $p$
The modulus-visibility guards hold: $\gcd(t,1000)=1$ and $\frac{t}{p}\not\equiv 1\pmod 8$
$k\in\mathbb{N}$ is even with $k\ge 4$
Define the integer sequence $u_n=a^n-b^n$ for $n\ge 1$
ask:
With $u_n=a^n-b^n$, consider the set of indices
\[
  S=\{n\in\mathbb{N}: \ q\mid u_n \ \text{and}\ v_p(u_n)=v_p(a-b)+k\}.
\]
Prove that $S$ is nonempty, let $N=\min S$, and report the final answer as the unique integer in $\{0,1,\dots,999\}$ congruent to $N \pmod{1000}$.

parameter(s).name: a
parameter(s).purpose: first base defining the exponential sequence

parameter(s).name: b
parameter(s).purpose: second base defining the exponential sequence

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: valuation prime enabling a sharp LTE constraint on $v_p(a^n-b^n)$

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).domain:
odd prime $q\ge 3$ with $q
e p$, $q
mid ab(a-b)$, and $p\mid \operatorname{ord}_q(a b^{-1})$ but $p^2
mid \operatorname{ord}_q(a b^{-1})$
parameter(s).purpose: modulus for the multiplicative-order cycle that forces a nontrivial congruence class for the exponent

parameter(s).name: k
parameter(s).purpose: prescribed valuation lift amount beyond $v_p(a-b)$, ensuring a genuinely nontrivial $p$-adic constraint on the exponent

invariant(s).name: modulo_cycle_interface
invariant(s).statement: Let $t=\operatorname{ord}_q(a b^{-1}\bmod q)$. Then $q\mid(a^n-b^n)$ holds if and only if $t\mid n$, so the congruence condition forces $n$ into a fixed arithmetic progression (multiples of $t$).

invariant(s).name: order_profile_forced_divisor_and_nondivisor
invariant(s).statement: The order $t=\operatorname{ord}_q(a b^{-1}\bmod q)$ satisfies $p\mid t$ but $p^2\nmid t$, and $t$ is not a pure $p$-power (it has a prime factor $\neq p$); this forbids trivial order cases ($t=1,2,p$) and forces a genuine order-structure step.

invariant(s).name: p_adic_nontriviality_interface
invariant(s).statement: With $p$ odd, $p\mid(a-b)$, $v_p(a-b)=1$, and $p\nmid ab$, LTE gives $v_p(a^n-b^n)=v_p(a-b)+v_p(n)=1+v_p(n)$ for all $n\ge 1$; since $k\ge 4$, the constraint $v_p(a^n-b^n)=v_p(a-b)+k$ forces $v_p(n)=k$ and hence cannot be met by the order condition alone.

invariant(s).name: mod1000_visibility_guards
invariant(s).statement: Writing $t=\operatorname{ord}_q(a b^{-1})$, we have $\gcd(t,1000)=1$, $p\equiv 3\pmod 8$, $k$ is even, and $\frac{t}{p}\not\equiv 1\pmod 8$. These guards ensure the reported residue $N\bmod 1000$ changes if either the order constraint ($t\mid n$) or the valuation constraint ($v_p(n)=k$) is removed.

invariant(s).name: growth_gap_interface
invariant(s).statement: Since $a>b>1$, the sequence $u_n=a^n-b^n$ is strictly increasing in $n$, so an extremal index defined by simultaneous congruence and valuation conditions is intrinsically rigid once the admissible congruence classes for $n$ are pinned down.

essential_assumption(s): The order constraint is not handed as $t\mid n$; it must be derived from the definition of multiplicative order in $(\mathbb{Z}/q\mathbb{Z})^\times$, while the valuation constraint must be derived from LTE. The extremal index $N$ is therefore forced only by combining ORDER and VP reasoning, not by a single template step.

The visibility guards ($\gcd(t,1000)=1$, $p\equiv 3\pmod 8$, $k$ even, and $(t/p)\not\equiv 1\pmod 8$) are enforced so that the normalized output modulo $1000$ genuinely depends on both regimes (dropping either constraint changes the reported residue).
uniqueness_certificate(s): By the order invariant, $q\mid(a^n-b^n)$ is equivalent to $t\mid n$, so every admissible $n$ has the form $n=t\cdot m$. By LTE and $v_p(a-b)=1$, the valuation condition becomes $v_p(n)=k$. Using $p\mid t$ but $p^2\nmid t$ gives $v_p(n)=1+v_p(m)$, hence $v_p(m)=k-1$, whose least solution is $m=p^{k-1}$. Therefore $S$ is nonempty and has a unique least element $N=t\cdot p^{k-1}$; the reported output is the unique residue class $N\bmod 1000$ in $\{0,1,\dots,999\}$.

technique(s).name: modular_obstructions
technique(s).purpose: translate the divisibility condition $q\mid u_n$ into a constraint on $n$ via multiplicative periodicity, excluding all $n$ outside a fixed cycle class
outputs: a forced divisibility condition $t\mid n$ where $t=\operatorname{ord}_q(a b^{-1})$

technique(s).name: order_arguments
inputs: the residue class $a b^{-1}\bmod q$ in $(\mathbb{Z}/q\mathbb{Z})^\times$
technique(s).purpose: compute/characterize the cycle length $t=\operatorname{ord}_q(a b^{-1})$ and use the forced divisor/nondivisor profile ($p\mid t$, $p^2\nmid t$) to control $v_p(n)$ once $n$ is restricted to multiples of $t$
outputs: the structural constraint $n=t\cdot m$ with $v_p(t)=1$ and $t$ not a pure $p$-power

technique(s).name: lifting_exponents_LTE
inputs: the difference-of-powers form $a^n-b^n$ with $p\mid(a-b)$ and $p\nmid ab$
technique(s).purpose: convert the exact valuation constraint $v_p(a^n-b^n)=v_p(a-b)+k$ into an exact condition on $v_p(n)$
outputs: the equivalence $v_p(a^n-b^n)=1+v_p(n)$ and hence the requirement $v_p(n)=k$

technique(s).name: order_LTE_interaction_step
inputs: the decomposition $n=t\cdot m$ from order arguments and the valuation identity from LTE
technique(s).purpose: enforce two-regime coupling by substituting the order-forced form of $n$ into the LTE valuation identity to solve for the minimal admissible multiplier $m$
outputs: a closed-form expression for the extremal index $N=t\cdot p^{k-1}$ and a minimality/uniqueness argument

technique(s).name: growth_bounds_for_exponents
technique(s).purpose: support extremality discipline (no hidden secondary minima) and provide a sanity-check bound window for computational verification if desired
outputs: confirmation that the least admissible index is the unique extremal object

technique(s).name: bounding_then_check
inputs: the explicit formula for $N$ and the defining constraints
technique(s).purpose: optionally verify (for sampled parameters) that no smaller $n$ satisfies both constraints by checking $n<t\cdot p^{k-1}$ within the forced residue classes
outputs: independent confirmation of minimality for audit/verification

theorem(s).name: multiplicative_order_characterization
theorem(s).role: $q\mid(a^n-b^n)\iff (a b^{-1})^n\equiv 1\pmod q \iff \operatorname{ord}_q(a b^{-1})\mid n$

theorem(s).name: mod_prime_obstructions
theorem(s).role: use reductions modulo an odd prime to impose cycle constraints on exponents

theorem(s).name: LTE_difference_of_powers
theorem(s).role: for odd $p\mid(a-b)$ with $p\nmid ab$, deduce $v_p(a^n-b^n)=v_p(a-b)+v_p(n)$

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Exponential sequences simultaneously exhibit rigid periodicity modulo primes (via multiplicative order) and rigid valuation growth at primes dividing a base difference (via LTE); forcing both at once pins down the least admissible exponent.

critical_trick(s): Use the order condition modulo $q$ to write every candidate as $n=t\cdot m$, where $t=\operatorname{ord}_q(a b^{-1})$ has $p\mid t$ but $p^2\nmid t$ (forced divisor/nondivisor).
Apply LTE to rewrite the valuation constraint as $v_p(n)=k$, then combine with $v_p(t)=1$ to force $v_p(m)=k-1$, giving the unique minimal multiplier $m=p^{k-1}$.

Use the visibility guards (especially $\gcd(t,1000)=1$ and $p^{k-1}\not\equiv 1\pmod 8$) to ensure the normalized residue modulo $1000$ genuinely changes if either the ORDER or the VP condition is removed.

Check the guards $\gcd(t,1000)=1$, $p\equiv 3\pmod 8$, $k$ even, and $(t/p)
ot\equiv 1\pmod 8$, then confirm $N
