title: GCD of exponentials with CRT-composite modulus and lcm-order forcing

identity.short_name: GCD of exponentials with CRT-composite modulus and lcm-order forcing

problem_spec.summary: A gcd-of-exponentials sequence with affine exponents, where the base is specified by CRT congruences modulo two prime powers. The divisibility condition by the composite modulus is forced by an lcm-of-orders computation (CRT) and then reduced to a single linear congruence in the index via a determinant Bezout reduction.
givens: $p,q$ are distinct odd primes; $k,l\ge 2$; set $M=p^kq^l$.
$a>1$ is the least positive integer satisfying $a\equiv 1+p\pmod{p^k}$ and $a\equiv 1+q\pmod{q^l}$ (hence $\gcd(a,M)=1$).
$m,n,r,s\in\mathbb N$ and for each $t\ge 1$ define $G_t=\gcd(a^{mt+r}-1,\ a^{nt+s}-1)$.
Define $L:=\mathrm{lcm}(p^{k-1},q^{l-1})$ and $D:=ms-nr$; assume $D\neq 0$ and $L\mid D$.
(Uniqueness guard) $\gcd(m,L)=1$.
(Anti-triviality) $L\nmid(m+r)$.
ask:
Prove that $\{t\ge 1:\ M\mid G_t\}$ is nonempty and that for all $t\ge 1$,

  M\mid G_t \iff \mathrm{ord}_{M}(a)\mid \gcd(mt+r,\ nt+s)\iff L\mid (mt+r).
Let $t_0$ be the least positive integer with $M\mid G_{t_0}$.
Since the natural output is the integer $t_0$, report the unique $N$ with $0\le N<1000$ and $N\equiv t_0\pmod{1000}$.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: first prime-power factor in the composite modulus

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: second prime-power factor in the composite modulus

parameter(s).name: k
parameter(s).purpose: exponent of $p$ in $M=p^kq^l$

parameter(s).name: l
parameter(s).purpose: exponent of $q$ in $M=p^kq^l$

parameter(s).name: a
parameter(s).purpose: CRT-specified base with forced prime-power orders in each modulus component

parameter(s).name: m
parameter(s).purpose: slope of the first affine exponent; invertible modulo $L$ to force a unique solution class

parameter(s).name: n
parameter(s).purpose: slope of the second affine exponent; participates in $D=ms-nr$

parameter(s).name: r
parameter(s).purpose: offset of the first affine exponent; prevents $t=0$ shortcut and controls the extremal index

parameter(s).name: s
parameter(s).purpose: offset of the second affine exponent; couples with $(m,n,r)$ through $D=ms-nr$

invariant(s).name: exponent_gcd_identity
invariant(s).statement: For all positive integers $u,v$, $\gcd(a^u-1,\ a^v-1)=a^{\gcd(u,v)}-1$.

invariant(s).name: forced_order_on_each_prime_power_component
invariant(s).statement:
For odd prime $p$ and $k\ge 2$, $\mathrm{ord}_{p^k}(1+p)=p^{k-1}$, and similarly for $(q,l)$.
Under the congruences defining $a$, this yields
$\mathrm{ord}_{p^k}(a)=p^{k-1}$ and $\mathrm{ord}_{q^l}(a)=q^{l-1}$.

invariant(s).name: CRT_order_lcm_composition
invariant(s).statement:
With $\gcd(p^k,q^l)=1$ and $M=p^kq^l$, CRT gives an isomorphism
$(\mathbb Z/M\mathbb Z)^\times\cong (\mathbb Z/p^k\mathbb Z)^\times\times(\mathbb Z/q^l\mathbb Z)^\times$,
hence
$\mathrm{ord}_{M}(a)=\mathrm{lcm}(\mathrm{ord}_{p^k}(a),\mathrm{ord}_{q^l}(a))=L$.

invariant(s).name: determinant_Bezout_reduction_of_linear_exponent_gcd
invariant(s).statement:
Let $D=ms-nr$. For every $t\ge 1$, any common divisor of $(mt+r)$ and $(nt+s)$ divides $D$.
If moreover $\gcd(m,D)=1$, then $\gcd(mt+r,\ nt+s)=\gcd(mt+r,\ D)$.

invariant(s).name: existence_guard_for_composite_modulus_divisibility
invariant(s).statement: The condition $L\mid D$ (with $D=ms-nr\ne 0$) is necessary and sufficient, together with $\gcd(m,L)=1$, for the existence of some $t\ge 1$ with $M\mid G_t$.

invariant(s).name: anti_triviality_guard
invariant(s).statement: The constraint $L\nmid(m+r)$ ensures $t_0>1$.

essential_assumption(s): The modulus is composite ($M=p^kq^l$) and the base $a$ is specified by CRT congruences; this makes the order computation depend essentially on CRT and lcm-of-orders rather than a single prime-power order.
The affine exponents are coupled through $D=ms-nr$ with $L\mid D$; without this coupling the simultaneous divisibility condition may be empty or split into unrelated congruences.
The guard $\gcd(m,L)=1$ is required to force a unique residue class for $t$ modulo $L$, enabling a unique least positive $t_0$.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Under $\gcd(m,L)=1$, the congruence $mt\equiv -r\pmod{L}$ has a unique solution class modulo $L$. Together with $L\mid (ms-nr)$, this class also forces $nt+s\equiv 0\pmod{L}$, hence there is a unique least positive $t_0$ and thus a unique normalized output $t_0\bmod 1000$.

technique(s).name: Euclidean_algorithm_on_exponents
technique(s).purpose: derive $\gcd(a^u-1,a^v-1)=a^{\gcd(u,v)}-1$ and reduce the exponential gcd to a gcd of exponents

technique(s).name: order_arguments_mod_prime_power
technique(s).purpose: compute $\mathrm{ord}_{p^k}(a)$ and $\mathrm{ord}_{q^l}(a)$ using the forced congruence classes

title: Triple exponential gcd with CRT base, order forcing, LTE valuations, Hensel lifting, and Kummer carries

identity.short_name: Triple exponential gcd with CRT base, order forcing, LTE valuations, Hensel lifting, and Kummer carries

problem_spec.summary: A base a is fixed by CRT modulo three prime powers. A triple gcd of exponential expressions collapses to a^{gcd(E1(t),E2(t),E3(t))}-1. Determinant-style Bezout identities force this exponent gcd to equal gcd(E1(t), ord_M(a)), so the condition M | G_t becomes one linear congruence for t. After computing valuations of a^{ord_M(a)}-1 via LTE, two coupled postprocesses depending on t0 are required: counting square roots of 2^{t0} modulo r^2 using quadratic reciprocity and Hensel lifting, and computing v_r(binomial(2 t0, t0)) via Kummer carry counting.
givens: $a$ is the least positive integer with $a\equiv 3\pmod{2^7}$, $a\equiv 13\pmod{3^4}$, $a\equiv 34\pmod{11^2}$.
$t_0$ is the least positive integer such that $M\mid G_{t_0}$.
For prime $p$, $v_p(n)$ is the exponent of $p$ in $n$.
$R$ is the number of integers $x$ with $0\le x<13^2$ satisfying $x^2\equiv 2^{t_0}\pmod{13^2}$.
ask: Determine the unique integer $N$ with $0\le N<1000$ and $N\equiv t_0+S+R+T\pmod{1000}$.

parameter(s).name: e
parameter(s).purpose: exponent of the 2-power in the CRT modulus

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: first odd prime in the CRT modulus

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: second odd prime in the CRT modulus

parameter(s).name: k
parameter(s).purpose: exponent of p in the CRT modulus

parameter(s).name: l
parameter(s).purpose: exponent of q in the CRT modulus

parameter(s).name: r
parameter(s).purpose: auxiliary prime for quadratic lifting and Kummer carry valuation

parameter(s).name: E1
parameter(s).purpose: first affine exponent controlling the forced index t0

parameter(s).name: E2
parameter(s).purpose: second affine exponent coupled to E1 via a determinant identity

parameter(s).name: E3
parameter(s).purpose: third affine exponent coupled to E1 via a determinant identity

invariant(s).name: order_divisibility_bridge
invariant(s).statement: If $\gcd(a,M)=1$ then $M\mid(a^n-1)$ if and only if $\operatorname{ord}_M(a)\mid n$.

invariant(s).name: CRT_order_lcm
invariant(s).statement: If $M=m_1m_2m_3$ with pairwise coprime $m_i$, then $\operatorname{ord}_M(a)=\operatorname{lcm}(\operatorname{ord}_{m_1}(a),\operatorname{ord}_{m_2}(a),\operatorname{ord}_{m_3}(a))$.

invariant(s).name: determinant_collapse_of_affine_exponent_gcd
invariant(s).statement: If $D_{12}=m_1r_2-m_2r_1$ and $D_{13}=m_1r_3-m_3r_1$ are multiples of $L$ and $\gcd(m_1,L)=1$, then for all $t$, $\gcd(E1(t),E2(t),E3(t))=\gcd(E1(t),L)$.

invariant(s).name: quadratic_character_parity_coupling
invariant(s).statement: For an odd prime $r$ with $\left(\frac{2}{r}\right)=-1$, one has $\left(\frac{2^{t_0}}{r}\right)=(-1)^{t_0}$, so solvability of $x^2\equiv 2^{t_0}\pmod r$ depends only on the parity of $t_0$.

invariant(s).name: hensel_square_root_lift
invariant(s).statement: If $r$ is an odd prime and $c$ is not divisible by $r$, then each solution of $x^2\equiv c\pmod r$ lifts uniquely to a solution modulo $r^2$; hence the number of solutions modulo $r^2$ equals the number modulo $r$.

invariant(s).name: kummer_carry_binomial_valuation
invariant(s).statement: $v_r\!\binom{2t_0}{t_0}$ equals the number of carries when adding $t_0+t_0$ in base $r$.

essential_assumption(s): The CRT congruences determine a unique residue class for a modulo M and ensure $\gcd(a,M)=1$.

The affine exponents satisfy determinant identities forcing $\gcd(E1(t),E2(t),E3(t))=\gcd(E1(t),L)$ with $L=\operatorname{ord}_M(a)$, and $\gcd(m_1,L)=1$ so that $E1(t)\equiv 0\pmod L$ has a unique solution class.
The auxiliary prime r is odd (so Hensel simple-root lifting applies) and satisfies $\left(\frac{2}{r}\right)=-1$ so quadratic solvability couples to parity of t0.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Determinant collapse yields $g_t=\gcd(E1(t),L)$ for $L=\operatorname{ord}_M(a)$. Then $M\mid G_t$ iff $L\mid g_t$, which (since $g_t\mid L$) is equivalent to $E1(t)\equiv 0\pmod L$. With $\gcd(m_1,L)=1$, this has exactly one residue class modulo L and hence a unique least positive $t_0$. The values $S$ are forced by LTE valuation formulas applied to $a^{L}-1$. The value of $R$ is forced by the Legendre symbol computation of $\left(\frac{2^{t_0}}{r}\right)$ together with Hensel lifting. The value of $T$ is forced by Kummer carry counting in base r. Therefore the normalized N is unique.

title: Triple exponential gcd with CRT base, order forcing, and valuation postprocess

identity.short_name: Triple exponential gcd with CRT base, order forcing, and valuation postprocess

problem_spec.summary: A base $a$ is defined by CRT modulo $2^e p^k q^l$. A three-term gcd of exponential expressions collapses to $a^{\gcd(E1(t),E2(t),E3(t))}-1$. A determinant-style Bezout reduction forces $\gcd(E1(t),E2(t),E3(t))=\gcd(E1(t),L)$ for $L=\mathrm{ord}_M(a)$, reducing $M\mid G_t$ to one linear congruence in $t$. A valuation postprocess computes $v_2,v_p,v_q$ of the extremal term using valuation lifting lemmas.
givens: $a$ is the least positive integer with $a\equiv 5\pmod{2^6}$, $a\equiv 4\pmod{3^3}$, $a\equiv 6\pmod{5^3}$.
$t_0$ is the least positive integer with $M\mid G_{t_0}$.
For prime $p$, $v_p(n)$ is the exponent of $p$ in $n$.
ask: Determine the unique integer $N$ with $0\le N<1000$ and $N\equiv t_0+S\pmod{1000}$.

parameter(s).name: e
parameter(s).purpose: exponent of the $2$-power in $M=2^e p^k q^l$

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: first odd prime in the modulus

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: second odd prime in the modulus

parameter(s).name: k
parameter(s).purpose: exponent of $p$ in $M$

parameter(s).name: l
parameter(s).purpose: exponent of $q$ in $M$

parameter(s).name: E1
parameter(s).purpose: first affine exponent

parameter(s).name: E2
parameter(s).purpose: second affine exponent

parameter(s).name: E3
parameter(s).purpose: third affine exponent

invariant(s).name: order_divisibility_bridge
invariant(s).statement: If $\gcd(a,M)=1$ then $M\mid(a^n-1)$ if and only if $\mathrm{ord}_M(a)\mid n$.

invariant(s).name: CRT_order_lcm
invariant(s).statement: If $M=m_1m_2m_3$ with pairwise coprime $m_i$, then $\mathrm{ord}_M(a)=\mathrm{lcm}(\mathrm{ord}_{m_1}(a),\mathrm{ord}_{m_2}(a),\mathrm{ord}_{m_3}(a))$.

invariant(s).name: determinant_collapse_of_affine_exponent_gcd
invariant(s).statement: If $D_{12}=m_1r_2-m_2r_1$ and $D_{13}=m_1r_3-m_3r_1$ are multiples of $L$ and $\gcd(m_1,L)=1$, then for all $t$, $\gcd(E1(t),E2(t),E3(t))=\gcd(E1(t),L)$.

essential_assumption(s): The CRT congruences fix $a$ modulo $M$ and ensure $\gcd(a,M)=1$.
Determinant-style identities force $\gcd(E1,E2,E3)$ to divide $\mathrm{ord}_M(a)$, collapsing $M\mid G_t$ to a single congruence in $t$.
uniqueness_certificate(s): If $\gcd(m_1,L)=1$, the congruence $E1(t)\equiv 0\pmod L$ has exactly one residue class modulo $L$, hence a unique least positive $t_0$. The valuation postprocess depends only on $a$ modulo $M$ and the fixed exponent $L$, so $S$ and the normalized $N$ are unique.

title: Coupled-RHS linear congruence via LTE + Kummer carry-count

identity.short_name: Coupled-RHS linear congruence via LTE + Kummer carry-count

problem_spec.summary: Let p,q be distinct odd primes and k>=4. Put n=p^k q. Choose a with gcd(a,pq)=1 and v_p(a-1)=v_q(a-1)=1, with v_p(q-1)=k-1 and v_q(p-1)=0. Define A=(a^{q-1}-1)/p^k and C=(a^{p-1}-1)/q. Set b=A+p^{k-3}C, which forces the p^k-congruence to depend on both A and a p-adic truncation of C. Solve ax \equiv b (mod n) and output x mod 1000.
givens: $p,q$ are distinct odd primes and $k\in\mathbb{N}$ with $k\ge 4$ and $p\le 2k-1$.
$a\in\mathbb{Z}$ with $1<a<p^kq$, $\gcd(a,pq)=1$, and $v_p(a-1)=v_q(a-1)=1$.
$n=p^k q$.
ask: Prove that $A$ and $C$ are integers and that in fact $p\nmid A$ and $q\nmid C$.
Let $x$ be the least nonnegative solution to $ax\equiv b\pmod n$. Determine $x\bmod p^k$ and $x\bmod q$, then report the normalized output $N\in\{0,1,\dots,999\}$ defined by $N\equiv x\pmod{1000}$.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: controls the prime-power part $p^k$ and the base for carry-count binomial valuations

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: second prime factor; its $p$-adic constraint forces exact $p$-valuation in $a^{q-1}-1$

parameter(s).name: k
parameter(s).purpose: forces a prime-power modulus and ensures the truncation range reaches $i\ge p$, making Kummer carry-count indispensable

parameter(s).name: a
parameter(s).purpose: invertible coefficient with controlled near-1 structure; couples valuation data into the linear congruence

invariant(s).name: structured_modulus
invariant(s).statement: $n=p^kq$ with $p,q$ distinct odd primes and $k\ge 4$.

invariant(s).name: exact_p_valuation_for_A
invariant(s).statement: From $v_p(a-1)=1$ and $v_p(q-1)=k-1$, LTE gives $v_p(a^{q-1}-1)=k$, hence $A\in\mathbb{Z}$ and $p\nmid A$.

invariant(s).name: exact_q_valuation_for_C
invariant(s).statement: From $v_q(a-1)=1$ and $v_q(p-1)=0$, LTE gives $v_q(a^{p-1}-1)=1$, hence $C\in\mathbb{Z}$ and $q\nmid C$.

invariant(s).name: coupled_rhs
invariant(s).statement: The right-hand side is $b=A+p^{k-3}C$, so modulo $p^k$ one needs $A\bmod p^k$ together with $C\bmod p^3$.

invariant(s).name: carry_sensitive_truncation_regime
invariant(s).statement: The guard $p\le 2k-1$ guarantees that among potentially contributing terms in $(1+pu)^{q-1}$ after division by $p^k$, at least one index $i\ge p$ appears; deciding which such terms survive modulo $p^k$ requires Kummer's carry criterion.

invariant(s).name: invertibility_condition
invariant(s).statement: Since $a\equiv 1\pmod p$ and $a\equiv 1\pmod q$, we have $\gcd(a,n)=1$.

essential_assumption(s): The modulus is restricted to $n=p^kq$ with $k\ge 4$ and $p,q$ odd primes, and we impose $p\le 2k-1$ to force the carry-sensitive binomial regime (so Kummer is genuinely load-bearing).
The RHS is coupled as $b=A+p^{k-3}C$ (not $A+p^kC$), so solving mod $p^k$ requires a nontrivial $p$-adic truncation of $C$ in addition to $A$.
The reported answer is the canonical residue $N\equiv x\pmod{1000}$ with $0\le N<1000$.
uniqueness_certificate(s): By the valuation invariants, $A,C,b$ are well-defined integers. Since $\gcd(a,n)=1$, the congruence $ax\equiv b\pmod n$ has exactly one solution class modulo $n$; choosing $x\in\{0,\dots,n-1\}$ fixes a unique integer, and the normalization $N\equiv x\pmod{1000}$ produces a unique output.

technique(s).name: Lifting_the_Exponent_LTE
technique(s).purpose: establish exact $p$- and $q$-adic valuations needed for integrality and nondivisibility of $A$ and $C$

technique(s).name: binomial_expansion_truncation
technique(s).purpose: compute quotients like $(a^m-1)/p^k$ modulo $p^k$ by truncating the binomial expansion at the last potentially contributing index
outputs: explicit residues $A\bmod p^k$ and truncations of $C$

technique(s).name: Kummer_carry_count
inputs: $p$ and binomial coefficients $\binom{m}{i}$ with $i$ possibly $\ge p$
technique(s).purpose: determine $v_p\!\left(\binom{m}{i}\right)$ via carry counts in base $p$, which decides which binomial terms survive after dividing by $p^k$
outputs: survival/vanishing decisions for terms in $A\bmod p^k$

technique(s).name: modular_reduction
technique(s).purpose: split the problem into congruences modulo $p^k$ and modulo $q$ and simplify each using the valuation-derived structure of $b$
outputs: reduced congruences determining $x\bmod p^k$ and $x\bmod q$

technique(s).name: Euclidean_algorithm
technique(s).purpose: compute gcd data and remainder chains

technique(s).name: Bezout_identity
technique(s).purpose: extract explicit modular inverses needed to solve the reduced congruences

technique(s).name: Chinese_remainder_theorem
technique(s).purpose: recombine into the unique solution modulo $n=p^kq$

technique(s).name: solvability_condition_gcd
technique(s).purpose: justify existence and uniqueness when $\gcd(a,n)=1$

technique(s).name: normalization_mod_1000
technique(s).purpose: map the solution to the required output range

theorem(s).name: gcd_condition_for_solvability
theorem(s).role: $ax\equiv b\pmod n$ is solvable iff $\gcd(a,n)\mid b$, and if $\gcd(a,n)=1$ the solution class is unique

theorem(s).name: Lifting_the_Exponent_LTE
theorem(s).role: computes exact prime-adic valuations of $a^m-1$ from valuations of $a-1$ and $m$ in the $a\equiv 1$ regime

theorem(s).name: Binomial_theorem
theorem(s).role: expands $(1+pu)^m$ to enable controlled truncation modulo powers of $p$

theorem(s).name: Kummer_theorem
theorem(s).role: gives $v_p\!\left(\binom{m}{i}\right)$ as the number of carries in base $p$ when adding $i$ and $m-i$

theorem(s).name: Chinese_remainder_theorem
theorem(s).role: recombines congruences modulo coprime moduli into a unique congruence modulo their product

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Integers form a Euclidean domain; the Euclidean algorithm produces Bezout coefficients and modular inverses.
p-adic valuation tracks exact divisibility and controls which binomial terms survive modulo prime powers.
Base-p digit carries encode divisibility information for binomial coefficients (Kummer).

critical_trick(s): Use LTE to guarantee $A,C\in\mathbb{Z}$ with controlled nondivisibility, so $b=A+p^{k-3}C$ is an integer and the linear congruence is well-defined.
Because the RHS is coupled, solving modulo $p^k$ requires both $A\bmod p^k$ and a $p$-adic truncation of $C$; compute these via binomial truncation.
Impose $p\le 2k-1$ so that an index $i\ge p$ lies in the potentially contributing binomial range for $A\bmod p^k$; decide term survival using Kummer's carry criterion (kernel forcing step).
Solve reduced congruences using modular inverses and recombine via CRT; finally output $x\bmod 1000$.

title: Linear congruence via Euclid/Bezout (unique modular inverse)

identity.short_name: Linear congruence via Euclid/Bezout (unique modular inverse)

problem_spec.summary: Given integers $a,b,n$ with $n\ge 2$ and $\gcd(a,n)=1$, determine the unique residue class $x\pmod n$ solving $ax\equiv b\pmod n$, reported as the least nonnegative representative.
givens: $n\in\mathbb{N},\ n\ge 2$
$a,b\in\mathbb{Z}$
ask: Find the least $x\in\{0,1,\dots,n-1\}$ such that $ax\equiv b\pmod n$.

parameter(s).name: n
parameter(s).purpose: varies the modulus and the ambient residue class ring

parameter(s).name: a
parameter(s).purpose: controls invertible coefficient; forces uniqueness of the residue

parameter(s).name: b
parameter(s).purpose: shifts the target residue class

invariant(s).name: invertibility_condition
invariant(s).statement: $\gcd(a,n)=1$ implies existence of $a^{-1}\pmod n$ and uniqueness of solutions to $ax\equiv b\pmod n$.

invariant(s).name: bezout_certificate
invariant(s).statement: There exist $u,v\in\mathbb{Z}$ with $au+nv=1$; then $x\equiv bu\pmod n$ solves $ax\equiv b\pmod n$.

essential_assumption(s): The instance enforces $\gcd(a,n)=1$ (invertibility), so the solution class is unique modulo $n$.
Output is normalized as the least nonnegative representative in $[0,n-1]$; this is a structural convention, not instance-specific.
uniqueness_certificate(s): If $\gcd(a,n)=1$ then multiplication by $a$ is a bijection on $\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}$, so exactly one residue class solves $ax\equiv b\pmod n$; choosing the least nonnegative representative makes the reported integer unique.

technique(s).name: Euclidean_algorithm
technique(s).purpose: compute $\gcd(a,n)$ and extended coefficients

technique(s).name: Bezout_identity
technique(s).purpose: turn $au+nv=1$ into a modular inverse statement

technique(s).name: solvability_condition_gcd
technique(s).purpose: justify that solvability is equivalent to $\gcd(a,n)\mid b$; under the family assumption this always holds
outputs: existence of a solution class mod $n$

technique(s).name: solving_ax_equals_b_mod_n
technique(s).purpose: construct the unique solution residue class

technique(s).name: parametrization_linear_solutions
technique(s).purpose: interpret congruence solutions as integer solutions to a linear Diophantine equation
outputs: equivalence between congruence class and integer solution family

theorem(s).name: gcd_condition_for_solvability
theorem(s).role: connects $ax\equiv b\pmod n$ solvability to a gcd divisibility condition

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Integers form a Euclidean domain; the Euclidean algorithm produces linear combinations realizing the gcd.

critical_trick(s): Replace the congruence $ax\equiv b\pmod n$ by the Diophantine equation $ax+ny=b$ and solve it using extended Euclid.
Normalize the residue class by taking the least nonnegative representative.

title: Linear congruence via Euclid/Bezout (unique modular inverse) - Buffed Seed

identity.short_name: Linear congruence via Euclid/Bezout (unique modular inverse) - Buffed Seed

problem_spec.summary:
Let $p,q$ be distinct odd primes and let $k\ge 2$. Put $n=p^k q$, and for any prime $r$ write $v_r(m)$ for the exponent of $r$ in the prime factorization of a nonzero integer $m$.
Choose an integer $a$ with $\gcd(a,pq)=1$ such that $v_p(a-1)=v_q(a-1)=1$, while $v_p(q-1)=k-1$ and $v_q(p-1)=0$.
Define

A=\frac{a^{q-1}-1}{p^k},\qquad C=\frac{a^{p-1}-1}{q},\qquad b=A+p^kC,

and consider the linear congruence $ax\equiv b\pmod n$ with $x$ taken as the least nonnegative residue.
givens: $p,q$ are distinct odd primes and $k\in\mathbb{N}$ with $k\ge 2$.
$a\in\mathbb{Z}$ with $1<a<p^kq$, $\gcd(a,pq)=1$, and $v_p(a-1)=v_q(a-1)=1$.
$n=p^k q$.
ask: Prove that $A$ and $C$ are integers and that in fact $p\nmid A$ and $q\nmid C$. Deduce that the congruence $ax\equiv b\pmod n$ has a unique solution $x\in\{0,1,\dots,n-1\}$.
Report the value of this $x$ modulo $1000$ (i.e., output $x\bmod 1000\in\{0,1,\dots,999\}$).

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: controls the prime-power part $p^k$ of the structured modulus

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: provides the second prime factor and forces exact $p$- and $q$-adic valuation behavior

parameter(s).name: k
parameter(s).purpose: forces a genuine prime-power modulus $p^k$ (nonlinear lifting regime)

parameter(s).name: a
parameter(s).purpose: invertible coefficient whose near-$1$ structure makes valuation constraints nontrivial and couples to the linear congruence

invariant(s).name: structured_modulus
invariant(s).statement: $n=p^kq$ with $p,q$ distinct odd primes and $k\ge 2$, so $n$ is composite and has a nontrivial prime-power factor.

invariant(s).name: anti_trivial_a_not_one_mod_p2q2
invariant(s).statement: $v_p(a-1)=v_q(a-1)=1$ implies $a\equiv 1\pmod{pq}$ but $a\not\equiv 1\pmod{p^2}$ and $a\not\equiv 1\pmod{q^2}$ (so the valuation step is genuinely nontrivial).

invariant(s).name: exact_p_valuation_for_A
invariant(s).statement: From $v_p(a-1)=1$ and $v_p(q-1)=k-1$, LTE gives $v_p(a^{q-1}-1)=k$, hence $A=(a^{q-1}-1)/p^k\in\mathbb{Z}$ and $p\nmid A$.

invariant(s).name: exact_q_valuation_for_C
invariant(s).statement: From $v_q(a-1)=1$ and $v_q(p-1)=0$, LTE gives $v_q(a^{p-1}-1)=1$, hence $C=(a^{p-1}-1)/q\in\mathbb{Z}$ and $q\nmid C$.

invariant(s).name: invertibility_condition
invariant(s).statement: $\gcd(a,n)=1$ so multiplication by $a$ is a bijection on $\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}$ and $ax\equiv b\pmod n$ has a unique solution class.

invariant(s).name: anti_trivial_rhs_not_multiple_of_p
invariant(s).statement: Since $p\nmid A$ and $p\mid p^kC$, we have $b=A+p^kC\not\equiv 0\pmod p$, so the right-hand side is not $0\pmod p$ and the congruence is not vacuous.

essential_assumption(s): The modulus is structurally restricted to $n=p^kq$ with $k\ge 2$ and $p,q$ odd primes, excluding the one-step prime-modulus inverse template.

The right-hand side $b$ is defined via exact divisibility of $a^{q-1}-1$ by $p^k$ and of $a^{p-1}-1$ by $q$; a valuation/LTE step is essential to guarantee $b\in\mathbb{Z}$ and to control $p\nmid A$, $q\nmid C$ before the linear congruence step is legitimate.
The reported answer is the canonical residue $x\bmod 1000\in\{0,1,\dots,999\}$, enforcing a single integer output even when $1000\nmid n$.
uniqueness_certificate(s): By the valuation invariants, $A,C,b$ are well-defined integers with $p\nmid A$ and $q\nmid C$, so the instance is non-degenerate. Since $\gcd(a,n)=1$, the congruence $ax\equiv b\pmod n$ has exactly one solution class in $\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}$, and choosing $x\in\{0,1,\dots,n-1\}$ makes $x$ unique. Finally, the mandated normalization $x\mapsto x\bmod 1000$ yields a unique output in $\{0,1,\dots,999\}$.

technique(s).name: Euclidean_algorithm
technique(s).purpose: compute $\gcd(a,n)$ and extended coefficients

technique(s).name: Bezout_identity
technique(s).purpose: turn $au+nv=1$ into a modular inverse statement

technique(s).name: solvability_condition_gcd
technique(s).purpose: justify that solvability is equivalent to $\gcd(a,n)\mid b$; under the family assumption this always holds
outputs: existence of a solution class mod $n$

technique(s).name: solving_ax_equals_b_mod_n
technique(s).purpose: construct the unique solution residue class

technique(s).name: parametrization_linear_solutions
technique(s).purpose: interpret congruence solutions as integer solutions to a linear Diophantine equation
outputs: equivalence between congruence class and integer solution family

technique(s).name: Lifting_the_Exponent_LTE
inputs: odd primes $p,q$ and integers with $v_p(a-1)=v_q(a-1)=1$, plus valuation data for $q-1$ and $p-1$
technique(s).purpose: compute exact prime-adic valuations of $a^{q-1}-1$ at $p$ and of $a^{p-1}-1$ at $q$ so that the quantities $A$ and $C$ are integral with controlled nondivisibility (VP regime feeding the LIN step)
outputs: integrality and exactness statements for $A$ and $C$ (e.g., $p\nmid A$, $q\nmid C$)

technique(s).name: valuation_to_congruence_interface
inputs: the integers $A,C,b$ certified by valuation arguments
technique(s).purpose: tie the VP-derived integrality/exactness of $b$ to the LIN regime by ensuring $ax\equiv b\pmod n$ is well-defined and non-degenerate (this interaction is the kernel coupling VP+LIN)
outputs: a valid linear congruence instance with invertible coefficient and structured modulus

technique(s).name: normalization_mod_1000
technique(s).purpose: enforce the required output range $\{0,1,\dots,999\}$ by reducing the unique solution modulo $1000$

theorem(s).name: gcd_condition_for_solvability
theorem(s).role: connects $ax\equiv b\pmod n$ solvability to a gcd divisibility condition

theorem(s).name: Lifting_the_Exponent_LTE
theorem(s).role: gives exact prime-adic valuations for expressions of the form $a^m-1$ under the stated divisibility hypotheses

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Integers form a Euclidean domain; the Euclidean algorithm produces linear combinations realizing the gcd.
The $p$-adic valuation measures the exponent of a prime dividing an integer and controls exact divisibility.

critical_trick(s): Use LTE to certify that $A=(a^{q-1}-1)/p^k$ and $C=(a^{p-1}-1)/q$ are integers with controlled nondivisibility; this valuation preprocessing is what makes the subsequent linear congruence step well-defined.
Replace the congruence $ax\equiv b\pmod n$ by the Diophantine equation $ax+ny=b$ and solve it using extended Euclid.
Normalize the residue class by taking the least nonnegative representative.
After finding the unique $x\in\{0,1,\dots,n-1\}$, report the mandated normalized output $x\bmod 1000$.

library(s).name: sympy

task(s).name: primary_structural_check
task(s).reason:
Correctness checks + NT5 preflight:

- "Run NT6 collision: wrong-branch CRT splice and check its normalization differs."

task(s).name: secondary_independent_check
task(s).reason: Brute-force all x in [0,n-1] to confirm there is exactly one solution and that it matches the modular-inverse computation; then reduce mod1000.

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Integers form a Euclidean domain; the Euclidean algorithm produces linear combinations realizing the gcd.
The $p$-adic valuation measures the exponent of a prime dividing an integer and controls exact divisibility.

critical_trick(s): Use LTE to certify that $A=(a^{q-1}-1)/p^k$ and $C=(a^{p-1}-1)/q$ are integers with controlled nondivisibility; this valuation preprocessing is what makes the subsequent linear congruence step well-defined.
Replace the congruence $ax\equiv b\pmod n$ by the Diophantine equation $ax+ny=b$ and solve it using extended Euclid.
Normalize the residue class by taking the least nonnegative representative.
After finding the unique $x\in\{0,1,\dots,n-1\}$, report the mandated normalized output $x\bmod 1000$.

task(s).name: secondary_independent_check

title: Linear congruence with $p$-adic binomial truncation and an order output

identity.short_name: Linear congruence with $p$-adic binomial truncation and an order output

problem_spec.summary: Solve $ax\equiv b\pmod{n}$ with $n=p^kq$ and a coupled right-hand side $b=\frac{a^{q-1}-1}{p^k}+p^{k-4}\frac{a^{p-1}-1}{q}$ in a regime where $v_p(a-1)=v_q(a-1)=1$ and $q-1=2p^{k-1}$. Compute $x$ by splitting modulo $p^k$ and $q$ and using a $p$-adic binomial truncation with Kummer carry counts, then compute the multiplicative order of $x$ modulo $q$ using quadratic-residue information, and finally output $N\equiv x+\mathrm{ord}_q(x)\pmod{999}$ in $[0,998]$.
givens: $p$ and $q$ are distinct odd primes, $k$ is an integer with $k\ge 5$, and $n=p^kq$.
$a$ is an integer with $1<a<p^kq$, $\gcd(a,pq)=1$, and $v_p(a-1)=v_q(a-1)=1$.
$x$ is the least nonnegative solution to $ax\equiv b\pmod n$.
$t$ is the multiplicative order of $x$ modulo $q$.
$N$ is the unique integer with $0\le N<999$ and $N\equiv x+t\pmod{999}$.
ask: Determine $N$.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: prime base for the modulus $p^k$ and for Kummer carry counts

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: second prime modulus and order group size $q-1$ with only prime factors $2$ and $p$

parameter(s).name: k
parameter(s).purpose: controls the modulus $p^k$ and the shifted coupling exponent $p^{k-4}$

parameter(s).name: a
parameter(s).purpose: invertible coefficient with near-$1$ structure enabling LTE and $p$-adic expansion

invariant(s).name: structured_modulus
invariant(s).statement: $n=p^kq$ with $p,q$ distinct odd primes and $k\ge 5$.

invariant(s).name: exact_p_valuation_for_A
invariant(s).statement: LTE gives $v_p(a^{q-1}-1)=v_p(a-1)+v_p(q-1)=1+(k-1)=k$, so $A\in\mathbb{Z}$ and $p\nmid A$.

invariant(s).name: exact_q_valuation_for_C
invariant(s).statement: LTE gives $v_q(a^{p-1}-1)=v_q(a-1)+v_q(p-1)=1+0=1$, so $C\in\mathbb{Z}$ and $q\nmid C$.

invariant(s).name: shifted_coupling
invariant(s).statement: The right-hand side is $b=A+p^{k-4}C$, forcing a mixed $p$-adic computation of $b\bmod p^k$.

invariant(s).name: order_barrier
invariant(s).statement: Since $q-1=2p^{k-1}$, to prove $\mathrm{ord}_q(u)=q-1$ it suffices to show $u^{(q-1)/2}\not\equiv 1$ and $u^{(q-1)/p}\not\equiv 1\pmod q$.

invariant(s).name: invertibility
invariant(s).statement: From $a\equiv 1\pmod p$ and $a\equiv 1\pmod q$, we have $\gcd(a,n)=1$.

essential_assumption(s): $k\ge 5$ so $p^{k-4}$ is integral and the coupling term changes $b\bmod p^k$ nontrivially.
$q-1=2p^{k-1}$ so the order computation reduces to testing the exponents $(q-1)/2$ and $(q-1)/p$.
The reported output is the canonical residue $N$ with $0\le N<999$ and $N\equiv x+t\pmod{999}$.
uniqueness_certificate(s): By the integrality invariants, $A,C,b$ are integers. Since $\gcd(a,n)=1$, the congruence $ax\equiv b\pmod n$ has a unique solution class modulo $n$, and $0\le x<n$ fixes a unique integer $x$. Because $x\not\equiv 0\pmod q$, the order $t=\mathrm{ord}_q(x)$ is uniquely defined. The normalization $0\le N<999$ and $N\equiv x+t\pmod{999}$ yields a unique output.

technique(s).name: Lifting_the_Exponent_LTE
technique(s).purpose: prove $A,C\in\mathbb{Z}$ and obtain exact nondivisibility $p\nmid A$, $q\nmid C$

technique(s).name: binomial_expansion_truncation
technique(s).purpose: compute $A\bmod p^k$ after dividing by $p^k$

technique(s).name: Kummer_carry_count
technique(s).purpose: decide which binomial terms survive modulo $p^k$ after division by $p^k$
outputs: vanishing of higher-index terms in $A\bmod p^k$

technique(s).name: Euclidean_algorithm_and_Bezout
inputs: pairs of integers for modular inverses
technique(s).purpose: compute explicit inverses needed in modular linear equations and CRT

technique(s).name: Chinese_remainder_theorem
technique(s).purpose: recombine to a unique $x\bmod p^kq$

technique(s).name: Euler_criterion_and_quadratic_reciprocity
technique(s).purpose: compute $u^{(q-1)/2}\bmod q$ to rule out order dividing $(q-1)/2$

technique(s).name: order_divisor_tests
technique(s).purpose: determine $\mathrm{ord}_q(u)$ by ruling out the maximal proper divisors

theorem(s).name: gcd_condition_for_linear_congruence
theorem(s).role: if $\gcd(a,n)=1$ then $ax\equiv b\pmod n$ has a unique solution class

theorem(s).name: Lifting_the_Exponent_LTE
theorem(s).role: computes $v_r(a^m-1)$ from $v_r(a-1)$ and $v_r(m)$ when $a\equiv 1\pmod r$

theorem(s).name: Binomial_theorem
theorem(s).role: expands $(1+pu)^{q-1}$ to compute $A\bmod p^k$

theorem(s).name: Kummer_theorem
theorem(s).role: gives $v_p(\binom{m}{i})$ via base-$p$ carry counts

theorem(s).name: Chinese_remainder_theorem
theorem(s).role: recombines congruences modulo coprime moduli

theorem(s).name: Fermat_little_theorem
theorem(s).role: ensures $\mathrm{ord}_q(u)\mid (q-1)$ for $u\not\equiv 0\pmod q$

theorem(s).name: Euler_criterion
theorem(s).role: relates $u^{(q-1)/2}\bmod q$ to the Legendre symbol $(u/q)$

theorem(s).name: quadratic_reciprocity
theorem(s).role: evaluates Legendre symbols such as $(2/q)$

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: the Euclidean algorithm produces Bezout coefficients and modular inverses
$p$-adic valuation controls divisibility and truncation of expansions modulo $p^k$
in a finite multiplicative group modulo a prime, an element's order divides the group order

critical_trick(s): compute $A\bmod p^k$ by $p$-adic binomial truncation with Kummer carry counts
use Euler's criterion and a Legendre symbol to force $u^{(q-1)/2}\equiv -1\pmod q$
combine congruences with CRT and then add the order for the final normalization

title: Coupled sum and difference with exact $p$-adic depth

identity.short_name: Coupled sum and difference with exact $p$-adic depth

problem_spec.summary: Find the least $n$ such that $q\mid(a^n-b^n)$, $r\mid(a^n+b^n)$, and $v_p(a^n-b^n)=k$, then output $n$ modulo $1000$.
givens: $u_n=a^n-b^n$ for $n\ge 1$
$v_\ell(m)$ is the largest $e\ge 0$ with $\ell^e\mid m$
ask: Determine the least $n\ge 1$ such that $q\mid u_n$, $r\mid(a^n+b^n)$, and $v_p(u_n)=k$, then output the unique integer in $\{0,1,\dots,999\}$ congruent to $n$ modulo $1000$.

parameter(s).name: $a$
parameter(s).purpose: base of $a^n\pm b^n$

parameter(s).name: $b$
parameter(s).purpose: base of $a^n\pm b^n$, assumed invertible modulo primes used

parameter(s).name: $p$
parameter(s).purpose: valuation prime for $v_p(a^n-b^n)$

parameter(s).name: $q$
parameter(s).domain: odd prime distinct from $p$ with $q\nmid ab$
parameter(s).purpose: enforces an order-divisibility constraint from $q\mid(a^n-b^n)$

parameter(s).name: $r$
parameter(s).domain: odd prime distinct from $p,q$ with $r\nmid ab$
parameter(s).purpose: enforces a half-order congruence class from $r\mid(a^n+b^n)$

parameter(s).name: $k$
parameter(s).purpose: target exact valuation $v_p(a^n-b^n)=k$

invariant(s).name: order_cycle_mod_q
invariant(s).statement: Let $g_q\equiv ab^{-1}\pmod q$ and $t=\operatorname{ord}_q(g_q)$. Then $q\mid(a^n-b^n)$ if and only if $t\mid n$.

invariant(s).name: half_order_class_mod_r
invariant(s).statement: Let $g_r\equiv ab^{-1}\pmod r$ and $s=\operatorname{ord}_r(g_r)$. If $s$ is even and $m=s/2$, then $r\mid(a^n+b^n)$ if and only if $n\equiv m\pmod s$.

invariant(s).name: valuation_as_congruence_depth
invariant(s).statement: If $p\nmid b$, then $v_p(a^n-b^n)=k$ if and only if $(ab^{-1})^n\equiv 1\pmod{p^k}$ but $(ab^{-1})^n\not\equiv 1\pmod{p^{k+1}}$.

invariant(s).name: rigid_order_lift
invariant(s).statement: If $g\equiv ab^{-1}\pmod{p^2}$ has $d=\operatorname{ord}_p(g\bmod p)$ and $g^d\not\equiv 1\pmod{p^2}$, then $\operatorname{ord}_{p^e}(g)=d p^{e-1}$ for all $e\ge 1$.

essential_assumption(s): $p,q,r$ are odd primes with $pqr\nmid b$ so that $b^{-1}$ exists modulo each modulus used.
For the valuation constraint via order lifting, the nontriviality $g^d\not\equiv 1\pmod{p^2}$ is required.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The conditions force $n$ to be a multiple of $L=\operatorname{lcm}(t,\operatorname{ord}_{p^k}(g))$ and simultaneously to lie in a unique residue class modulo $\operatorname{ord}_r(g_r)$ corresponding to $-1$; the least positive solution is unique, and then the exclusion modulo $p^{k+1}$ certifies exact valuation.

technique(s).name: modular_obstructions
technique(s).purpose: rewrite divisibility as power congruences for $ab^{-1}$

technique(s).name: order_arguments
technique(s).purpose: compute multiplicative orders and the half-order class for $-1$

technique(s).name: quadratic_residue_computation
technique(s).purpose: use Legendre symbols to show $g_r^{(r-1)/2}\equiv -1\pmod r$
outputs: certify even order and identify the half-order exponent

technique(s).name: exact_padic_congruence
technique(s).purpose: translate exact valuation into congruence depth modulo $p^k$ and $p^{k+1}$

technique(s).name: prime_power_order_lifting
technique(s).purpose: lift $\operatorname{ord}_p(g)$ to $\operatorname{ord}_{p^e}(g)$

technique(s).name: chinese_remainder_theorem
inputs: a divisibility constraint and a congruence class constraint
technique(s).purpose: solve for the unique smallest compatible exponent

theorem(s).name: Euler_criterion
theorem(s).role: For odd prime $r$, $g^{(r-1)/2}\equiv (g/r)\pmod r$.

theorem(s).name: quadratic_reciprocity
theorem(s).role: Compute $(3/r)$ and hence $(6/r)$ for $r=41$.

theorem(s).name: order_lifting_prime_powers
theorem(s).role: If $\operatorname{ord}_p(g)=d$ and $g^d\not\equiv 1\pmod{p^2}$, then $\operatorname{ord}_{p^e}(g)=d p^{e-1}$.

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: In a unit group modulo a prime power, congruence depth is governed by the multiplicative order and its lifting.

critical_trick(s): Couple $q\mid(a^n-b^n)$ and $r\mid(a^n+b^n)$ via orders and half-orders, then enforce $v_p(a^n-b^n)=k$ via order lifting to $p^k$ and an exclusion at $p^{k+1}$.

title: Prime-square half-order with LTE valuation and carry-coupled binomial valuation

identity.short_name: Prime-square half-order with LTE valuation and carry-coupled binomial valuation

problem_spec.summary: Find the least exponent n satisfying: (i) a difference divisibility at a prime, (ii) a sum divisibility at a prime-square, (iii) an exact p-adic valuation via LTE, and (iv) a fixed binomial p-adic valuation via digit carries; then output n modulo a stated modulus.
givens: $u_n=a^n-b^n$ for $n\ge 1$
$v_p(m)$ is the largest $e\ge 0$ such that $p^e\mid m$
ask: Determine the least $n\ge 1$ such that $23\mid(a^n-b^n)$, $13^2\mid(a^n+b^n)$, $v_5(a^n-b^n)=4$, and $v_5\!\left(\binom{2n}{n}\right)=1$. Let $N$ be this least $n$. Output the unique integer in $\{0,1,\dots,998\}$ congruent to $N$ modulo $999$.

parameter(s).name: $a$
parameter(s).purpose: base for $a^n\pm b^n$

parameter(s).name: $b$
parameter(s).purpose: base for $a^n\pm b^n$; must be invertible modulo all moduli used

parameter(s).name: $q$
parameter(s).purpose: difference divisibility constraint via $\operatorname{ord}_q(ab^{-1})$

parameter(s).name: $r$
parameter(s).domain: odd prime distinct from $q$ with $r\nmid ab$
parameter(s).purpose: sum divisibility constraint at prime-square modulus $r^2$ via lifted order and a forced half-order

parameter(s).name: $p$
parameter(s).domain: odd prime distinct from $q,r$ with $p\nmid ab$ and $p\mid(a-b)$
parameter(s).purpose: valuation prime for LTE-controlled exact depth

parameter(s).name: $k$
parameter(s).purpose: target exact valuation $v_p(a^n-b^n)=k$

parameter(s).name: $c$
parameter(s).purpose: target binomial valuation $v_p\!\left(\binom{2n}{n}\right)=c$ via digit carries

parameter(s).name: $M$
parameter(s).purpose: final reduction modulus (here $M=999$)

invariant(s).name: difference_order_mod_q
invariant(s).statement: Let $g_q\equiv ab^{-1}\pmod q$ and $t=\operatorname{ord}_q(g_q)$. Then $q\mid(a^n-b^n)$ iff $t\mid n$.

invariant(s).name: prime_square_sum_to_half_order
invariant(s).statement: Let $g\equiv ab^{-1}\pmod{r^2}$ and $T=\operatorname{ord}_{r^2}(g)$. If $T$ is even then $g^{T/2}$ is the unique nontrivial solution to $x^2\equiv 1\pmod{r^2}$, hence $g^{T/2}\equiv -1\pmod{r^2}$ and $r^2\mid(a^n+b^n)$ iff $n\equiv T/2\pmod T$.

invariant(s).name: order_lift_prime_to_square
invariant(s).statement: If $d=\operatorname{ord}_r(ab^{-1}\bmod r)$ and $(ab^{-1})^d\not\equiv 1\pmod{r^2}$, then $\operatorname{ord}_{r^2}(ab^{-1})=dr$.

invariant(s).name: LTE_exact_valuation_difference
invariant(s).statement: If $p\mid(a-b)$ and $p\nmid ab$, then $v_p(a^n-b^n)=v_p(a-b)+v_p(n)$. Hence $v_p(a^n-b^n)=k$ forces $v_p(n)=k-v_p(a-b)$.

invariant(s).name: binomial_valuation_digit_carries
invariant(s).statement: $v_p\!\left(\binom{2n}{n}\right)$ equals the number of base-$p$ carries when adding $n+n$.

essential_assumption(s): $\gcd(a,b)=1$ and $q r p\nmid b$ so $b^{-1}$ exists modulo $q$ and $r^2$.
For the prime-square order lift, require $(ab^{-1})^d\not\equiv 1\pmod{r^2}$ so that the order multiplies by $r$.
For LTE, require $p\mid(a-b)$ and $p\nmid ab$.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The order constraints force $n$ into a single residue class modulo $\operatorname{lcm}(\operatorname{ord}_q(g_q),\operatorname{ord}_{r^2}(g))$. LTE fixes $v_p(n)$ (and excludes higher $p$-divisibility when required). The binomial valuation is a carry-count invariant, which selects the unique smallest admissible element of the resulting arithmetic progression; hence the least $n$ and its reduction modulo $M$ are unique.

technique(s).name: modular_obstructions
inputs: $a^n\pm b^n$ modulo primes and prime squares
technique(s).purpose: rewrite divisibility as congruences for $g\equiv ab^{-1}$

technique(s).name: order_arguments
technique(s).purpose: convert $g^n\equiv 1$ into divisibility constraints on $n$

technique(s).name: prime_power_order_lifting
technique(s).purpose: lift multiplicative order from $r$ to $r^2$

technique(s).name: square_root_uniqueness_mod_prime_power
technique(s).purpose: identify the unique nontrivial square root of $1$ as $-1$

technique(s).name: LTE_valuation
technique(s).purpose: translate exact valuation into an exact constraint on $v_p(n)$

technique(s).name: digit_sum_and_carry_invariant
technique(s).purpose: compute $v_p\!\left(\binom{2n}{n}\right)$ via digit sums / carries

technique(s).name: chinese_remainder_theorem
inputs: multiple congruence and divisibility constraints on $n$
technique(s).purpose: solve coupled congruences and parametrize solutions as an arithmetic progression

theorem(s).name: Fermat_little_theorem
theorem(s).role: Used to certify $4^{11}\equiv 1\pmod{23}$ and bound $\operatorname{ord}_{23}(4)$.

theorem(s).name: order_lifting_prime_to_square
theorem(s).role: If $\operatorname{ord}_r(g)=d$ and $g^d\not\equiv 1\pmod{r^2}$ then $\operatorname{ord}_{r^2}(g)=dr$.

theorem(s).name: square_roots_of_one_mod_prime_power
theorem(s).role: For odd prime $r$, $x^2\equiv 1\pmod{r^k}$ implies $x\equiv \pm 1\pmod{r^k}$.

theorem(s).name: LTE
theorem(s).role: If $p\mid(a-b)$ and $p\nmid ab$, then $v_p(a^n-b^n)=v_p(a-b)+v_p(n)$.

theorem(s).name: Legendre_formula
theorem(s).role: Gives $v_p(m!)$ and implies a digit-sum expression for $v_p\!\left(\binom{2n}{n}\right)$.

theorem(s).name: digit_sum_carry_identity
theorem(s).role: Relates digit sums to number of carries in base-$p$ addition.

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Unit groups modulo prime powers and digit-carry invariants jointly control admissible exponents.

critical_trick(s): Use prime-square order lifting to turn $r^2\mid(a^n+b^n)$ into a forced half-order congruence for $n$, combine with $q\mid(a^n-b^n)$ by CRT, enforce an exact $p$-adic depth via LTE, then use digit carries to force $v_p\!\left(\binom{2n}{n}\right)=c$ and select the unique smallest progression element.

title: Binomial coefficients mod $p$ and $v_p\\binom{n}{k}$ (Lucas/Legendre/Kummer) -- Buffed Seed

identity.short_name: Binomial coefficients mod $p$ and $v_p\\binom{n}{k}$ (Lucas/Legendre/Kummer) -- Buffed Seed

problem_spec.summary: Let $p$ be an odd prime and let $n$ be a positive integer whose base-$p$ expansion ends with a block of $t\ge1$ digits equal to $p-1$, followed by a digit $a\in\{0,1,\dots,p-2\}$, followed by a block of $s\ge1$ zeros, and then a nonzero digit $b\in\{1,2,\dots,p-1\}$. Consider the sequence $A_k=\binom{n}{k}$ for $0\le k\le n$. Define $k^*$ to be the smallest index $1\le k\le n-1$ for which $p\mid A_k$, and put $e=v_p(A_{k^*})$.
givens: $p\ge5$ is an odd prime
$t\ge1$ and $s\ge1$ are integers
$u\in\mathbb{Z}_{\ge0}$

Let $n=u\,p^{t+s+2}+b\,p^{t+s+1}+a\,p^{t}+(p^{t}-1)$ (equivalently, the base-$p$ digits of $n$ are $(p-1,\dots,p-1,a,\underbrace{0,\dots,0}_{s\text{ zeros}},b,\text{digits of }u)$ from least to most significant).
For $0\le k\le n$, define $A_k=\binom{n}{k}$. Let $k^*=\min\{1\le k\le n-1: p\mid A_k\}$ and $e=v_p(A_{k^*})$.
ask: (i) Prove that $k^*$ exists and is uniquely determined by the above digit pattern of $n$, and show that in fact $k^*=(a+1)p^{t}$.
(ii) Compute $N\equiv k^*+p^{e}\pmod{1000}$, and report the unique integer $N\in\{0,1,\dots,999\}$.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: base for Lucas/Kummer; determines the digit system and the $p$-adic valuation

parameter(s).name: t
parameter(s).purpose: length of the trailing $(p-1)$-digit block in the base-$p$ expansion of $n$; forces the location of the first Lucas-vanishing digit

parameter(s).name: s
parameter(s).purpose: length of the zero block after digit $a$; forces a nontrivial carry chain of length $s+1$ in Kummer carry counting

parameter(s).name: a
parameter(s).purpose: the first base-$p$ digit of $n$ (from the least significant side) that is not equal to $p-1$

parameter(s).name: b
parameter(s).purpose: a nonzero base-$p$ digit above the zero block; ensures $k^*<n$ and terminates the carry chain

parameter(s).name: u
parameter(s).purpose: higher base-$p$ digits of $n$ (above the controlled suffix); allows structural variation without changing the suffix interface

invariant(s).name: structured_base_p_suffix
invariant(s).statement: The integer $n=u\,p^{t+s+2}+b\,p^{t+s+1}+a\,p^{t}+(p^{t}-1)$ has base-$p$ suffix $(\underbrace{p-1,\dots,p-1}_{t\text{ digits}},a,\underbrace{0,\dots,0}_{s\text{ zeros}},b)$ (least to most significant within the suffix), with $a\le p-2$ and $b\ge1$.

invariant(s).name: anti_triviality_guards_1
invariant(s).statement: Non-degeneracy: $p\ge5$, $t\ge1$, and $s\ge1$, so the row index $n\ge p^{t+s+1}$ and the carry count in the valuation step is at least $2$.

invariant(s).name: anti_triviality_guards_2
invariant(s).statement: Non-degeneracy: $a\in\{0,1,\dots,p-2\}$ (so the first base-$p$ digit of $n$ that is not $p-1$ occurs at position $t$) and $b\in\{1,2,\dots,p-1\}$ (so $k^*<n$ and the carry chain terminates).

invariant(s).name: Lucas_nonvanishing_criterion
invariant(s).statement: For $0\le k\le n$, Lucas' theorem implies $\binom{n}{k}\not\equiv 0\pmod p$ iff every base-$p$ digit of $k$ is at most the corresponding digit of $n$.

invariant(s).name: Kummer_carry_valuation
invariant(s).statement: Kummer's carry interpretation: for $0\le k\le n$, $v_p\!\left(\binom{n}{k}\right)$ equals the number of carries when adding $k$ and $n-k$ in base $p$.

invariant(s).name: forced_carry_chain_for_kstar
invariant(s).statement: For $k^*=(a+1)p^{t}$, subtracting $k^*$ from $n$ triggers a borrow at digit-position $t$ that propagates through exactly the $s$ consecutive zero digits above it and stops at the digit $b\ge1$; hence the base-$p$ addition $k^*+(n-k^*)$ produces exactly $s+1$ carries.

essential_assumption(s): Digits are taken in base $p$; the instance must keep $p$ prime to use Lucas/Kummer in their standard forms.

The index $k^*$ is defined implicitly (as a minimality object); solving an instance requires first using Lucas' digitwise nonvanishing criterion to determine $k^*$, and only then using a $p$-adic valuation method (Kummer carries / Legendre) to determine $e$.
The reported output is the canonical representative of an integer modulo $1000$ in $\{0,1,\dots,999\}$; intermediate quantities may be larger but must be reduced deterministically at the end.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Lucas' theorem makes the predicate $p\mid\binom{n}{k}$ equivalent to a base-$p$ digit inequality, so the least such $k^*$ is forced by the controlled suffix of $n$ (hence existence and uniqueness of $k^*$ under the guards).
Kummer's carry theorem then forces $e=v_p\!\left(\binom{n}{k^*}\right)$ as an exact carry count determined by the same digit pattern, so $N\equiv k^*+p^e\pmod{1000}$ is uniquely determined.
Normalization: reduce modulo $1000$ and select the canonical representative in $\{0,1,\dots,999\}$.

technique(s).purpose: Compute $e=v_p\!\left(\binom{n}{k^*}\right)$ by counting carries.
outputs: exact value of $e$ as a carry count

technique(s).name: Lucas_Kummer_interface_step
technique(s).purpose: Use Lucas to pin down $k^*$ via the first digit where $k$ must exceed the corresponding digit of $n$, then use Kummer carry counting on that specific $k^*$ to obtain $e$ for the modulus-$1000$ computation.
outputs: two-phase pipeline $(n\text{ digits})\Rightarrow k^*\Rightarrow e$ that determines $N$

task(s).name: primary_structural_check
task(s).reason:
Correctness checks:
- Verify the base-$17$ digit vector of $n$ matches the declared suffix.
- Compute $k^*$ using Lucas' digit-inequality criterion and confirm that $k^*-1$ is nonvanishing mod $17$ while $k^*$ is vanishing.
- Compute $e$ by Kummer carry counting, and compute $N\equiv k^*+17^e\pmod{1000}$.

NT5 preflight attempts:
- Small-mod-only probes with moduli in {2,3,4,5,7,8,9,11} produce very small first-zero indices unrelated to $k^*$.
- A bounded search up to $2\times 10^5$ for $k$ with $17\mid\binom{n}{k}$ finds none (the true $k^*$ is about $1.7\times10^7$).
- Template guesses such as $N\equiv n\pmod{1000}$, $N\equiv k^*\pmod{1000}$, or $N\equiv 17^e\pmod{1000}$ fail.

NT6 collision:
- A plausible wrong-branch guess $k=a\,17^t$ (using $a=11$) gives a different normalized residue modulo $1000$.

task(s).name: secondary_independent_check
task(s).reason:
Independent route:
- Determine $k^*$ by scanning the base-$17$ digits of $n$ for the first digit $<16$ and applying the explicit formula $k^*=(n_{i_0}+1)17^{i_0}$.
- Compute $e$ via Legendre's digit-sum formula for $v_{17}\binom{n}{k^*}$, and recompute $N$.

title: Binomial coefficients mod $p$ and $v_p\binom{n}{k}$ (Lucas/Legendre/Kummer)

identity.short_name: Binomial coefficients mod $p$ and $v_p\binom{n}{k}$ (Lucas/Legendre/Kummer)

problem_spec.summary: Given a prime $p$ and integers $0\le k\le n$, compute simultaneously (i) the exponent $e=v_p\!\left(\binom{n}{k}\right)$ and (ii) the residue $r\equiv\binom{n}{k}\pmod p$, using base-$p$ expansions (Lucas theorem / carry counting) and factorial valuation formulas.
givens: $p$ is a prime
$n\in\mathbb{N}$
ask: Output the pair $(e,r)$ where $e=v_p\!\left(\binom{n}{k}\right)$ and $r\equiv\binom{n}{k}\pmod p$ with $0\le r<p$.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: sets the base for digit expansions and the valuation

parameter(s).name: n
parameter(s).purpose: top index of the binomial coefficient

parameter(s).name: k
parameter(s).purpose: bottom index; controls carry structure in base $p$

parameter(s).name: variant
parameter(s).purpose: extends to related combinatorial divisibility tasks while keeping the same base-$p$ interface

invariant(s).name: base_p_digit_interface
invariant(s).statement: Write $n=\sum n_i p^i$ and $k=\sum k_i p^i$ with digits $0\le n_i,k_i<p$. The digit data $(n_i,k_i)$ drive both Lucas (mod $p$ residue) and Kummer/Legendre (valuation via carries).

invariant(s).name: carry_count_equals_vp
invariant(s).statement: (Optional/Kummer) $v_p\!\left(\binom{n}{k}\right)$ equals the number of carries when adding $k$ and $n-k$ in base $p$.

essential_assumption(s): Digits are taken in base $p$; the instance must keep $p$ prime to use Lucas/Kummer in their standard forms.
When outputting a residue $r$, normalization is $0\le r<p$ (structural).
uniqueness_certificate(s): Both $e=v_p\!\left(\binom{n}{k}\right)$ and the reduced residue $r\in\{0,\dots,p-1\}$ are uniquely determined by $n,k,p$; thus the pair $(e,r)$ is globally unique.

technique(s).purpose: compute $v_p\binom{n}{k}$ by subtracting factorial valuations

technique(s).name: Lucas_theorem
technique(s).purpose: compute $\binom{n}{k}\bmod p$ digitwise

technique(s).name: Kummer_theorem_optional
technique(s).purpose: (optional) compute $e$ via carry counting rather than floor sums
outputs: interpretation of $e$ as number of carries

theorem(s).name: combinatorial_count_as_integer
theorem(s).role: justify integrality of expressions by counting interpretations (often used to prove divisibility)

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Base-$p$ expansions expose how factorials accumulate prime powers and how binomial coefficients decompose digitwise modulo $p$.

critical_trick(s): Use the shared interface "base-$p$ digits of $n$ and $k$": Lucas gives the mod $p$ residue and Kummer/Legendre gives the $p$-adic valuation.
For sum-of-binomial divisibility variants, interpret the sum as counting objects modulo $p$ or track valuation term-by-term.

title: Binomial coefficients mod $p$ and $v_p\binom{n}{k}$ (Lucas/Legendre/Kummer) - Buffed Seed

identity.short_name: Binomial coefficients mod $p$ and $v_p\binom{n}{k}$ (Lucas/Legendre/Kummer) - Buffed Seed

problem_spec.summary: Let $p$ be an odd prime and let $n$ be a positive integer whose base-$p$ expansion ends with a block of $t\ge1$ digits equal to $p-1$, followed by a digit $a\in\{0,1,\dots,p-2\}$, followed by a block of $s\ge1$ zeros, and then a nonzero digit $b\in\{1,2,\dots,p-1\}$. Consider the sequence $A_k=\binom{n}{k}$ for $0\le k\le n$. Define $k^*$ to be the smallest index $1\le k\le n-1$ for which $p\mid A_k$, and put $e=v_p(A_{k^*})$.
givens: $p\ge5$ is an odd prime
$t\ge1$ and $s\ge1$ are integers
$u\in\mathbb{Z}_{\ge0}$

Let $n=u\,p^{t+s+2}+b\,p^{t+s+1}+a\,p^{t}+(p^{t}-1)$ (equivalently, the base-$p$ digits of $n$ are $(p-1,\dots,p-1,a,\underbrace{0,\dots,0}_{s\text{ zeros}},b,\text{digits of }u)$ from least to most significant).
For $0\le k\le n$, define $A_k=\binom{n}{k}$. Let $k^*=\min\{1\le k\le n-1: p\mid A_k\}$ and $e=v_p(A_{k^*})$.
ask: (i) Prove that $k^*$ exists and is uniquely determined by the above digit pattern of $n$, and show that in fact $k^*=(a+1)p^{t}$.
(ii) Compute $N\equiv k^*+p^{e}\pmod{1000}$, and report the unique integer $N\in\{0,1,\dots,999\}$.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: base for Lucas/Kummer; determines the digit system and the $p$-adic valuation

parameter(s).name: t
parameter(s).purpose: length of the trailing $(p-1)$-digit block in the base-$p$ expansion of $n$; forces the location of the first Lucas-vanishing digit

parameter(s).name: s
parameter(s).purpose: length of the zero block after digit $a$; forces a nontrivial carry chain of length $s+1$ in Kummer carry counting

parameter(s).name: a
parameter(s).purpose: the first base-$p$ digit of $n$ (from the least significant side) that is not equal to $p-1$

parameter(s).name: b
parameter(s).purpose: a nonzero base-$p$ digit above the zero block; ensures $k^*<n$ and terminates the carry chain

parameter(s).name: u
parameter(s).purpose: higher base-$p$ digits of $n$ (above the controlled suffix); allows structural variation without changing the suffix interface

invariant(s).name: structured_base_p_suffix
invariant(s).statement: The integer $n=u\,p^{t+s+2}+b\,p^{t+s+1}+a\,p^{t}+(p^{t}-1)$ has base-$p$ suffix $(\underbrace{p-1,\dots,p-1}_{t\text{ digits}},a,\underbrace{0,\dots,0}_{s\text{ zeros}},b)$ (least to most significant within the suffix), with $a\le p-2$ and $b\ge1$.

invariant(s).name: anti_triviality_guards_1
invariant(s).statement: Non-degeneracy: $p\ge5$, $t\ge1$, and $s\ge1$, so the row index $n\ge p^{t+s+1}$ and the carry count in the valuation step is at least $2$.

invariant(s).name: anti_triviality_guards_2
invariant(s).statement: Non-degeneracy: $a\in\{0,1,\dots,p-2\}$ (so the first base-$p$ digit of $n$ that is not $p-1$ occurs at position $t$) and $b\in\{1,2,\dots,p-1\}$ (so $k^*<n$ and the carry chain terminates).

invariant(s).name: Lucas_nonvanishing_criterion
invariant(s).statement: For $0\le k\le n$, Lucas' theorem implies $\binom{n}{k}\not\equiv 0\pmod p$ iff every base-$p$ digit of $k$ is at most the corresponding digit of $n$.

invariant(s).name: Kummer_carry_valuation
invariant(s).statement: Kummer's carry interpretation: for $0\le k\le n$, $v_p\!\left(\binom{n}{k}\right)$ equals the number of carries when adding $k$ and $n-k$ in base $p$.

invariant(s).name: forced_carry_chain_for_kstar
invariant(s).statement: For $k^*=(a+1)p^{t}$, subtracting $k^*$ from $n$ triggers a borrow at digit-position $t$ that propagates through exactly the $s$ consecutive zero digits above it and stops at the digit $b\ge1$; hence the base-$p$ addition $k^*+(n-k^*)$ produces exactly $s+1$ carries.

essential_assumption(s): Digits are taken in base $p$; the instance must keep $p$ prime to use Lucas/Kummer in their standard forms.

The index $k^*$ is defined implicitly (as a minimality object); solving an instance requires first using Lucas' digitwise nonvanishing criterion to determine $k^*$, and only then using a $p$-adic valuation method (Kummer carries / Legendre) to determine $e$-a direct one-shot evaluation of a fixed $\binom{n}{k}$ is intentionally blocked by the size/structure guards.
The reported output is the canonical representative of an integer modulo $1000$ in $\{0,1,\dots,999\}$; intermediate quantities may be larger but must be reduced deterministically at the end.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Lucas' theorem makes the predicate $p\mid\binom{n}{k}$ equivalent to a base-$p$ digit inequality, so the least such $k^*$ is forced by the controlled suffix of $n$ (hence existence and uniqueness of $k^*$ under the guards).
Kummer's carry theorem then forces $e=v_p\!\left(\binom{n}{k^*}\right)$ as an exact carry count determined by the same digit pattern, so $N\equiv k^*+p^e\pmod{1000}$ is uniquely determined.
Normalization: reduce modulo $1000$ and select the canonical representative in $\{0,1,\dots,999\}$.

technique(s).name: Lucas_theorem
inputs: base-$p$ digits of $n$ and a variable index $k$
technique(s).purpose: Characterize when $\binom{n}{k}\equiv0\pmod p$ and locate the first index $k^*$ where this vanishing occurs under the structured digit suffix (BINOM regime).
outputs: closed-form identification of the minimal $k^*$ from digit constraints

technique(s).name: v_p_via_Kummer_carries
technique(s).purpose: Compute $e=v_p\!\left(\binom{n}{k^*}\right)$ by counting carries; the length of the forced borrow/carry chain is read off from the same digit block that defined $k^*$ (VP regime).
outputs: exact value of $e$ as a carry count (here, forced to equal $s+1$)

technique(s).name: Lucas_Kummer_interface_step
technique(s).purpose: Interaction kernel: use Lucas to pin down $k^*$ via the first digit where $k$ must exceed the corresponding digit of $n$, then use Kummer carry counting on that specific $k^*$ to obtain $e$ for the modulus-$1000$ computation.
outputs: two-phase pipeline $(n\text{ digits})\Rightarrow k^*\Rightarrow e$ that determines $N$

technique(s).name: mod_1000_reduction
technique(s).purpose: Compute $k^*+p^e$ modulo $1000$ using deterministic modular arithmetic.

theorem(s).name: combinatorial_count_as_integer
theorem(s).role: justify integrality of expressions by counting interpretations (often used to prove divisibility)

theorem(s).name: Lucas_theorem
theorem(s).role: digitwise evaluation of binomial coefficients modulo a prime

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Base-$p$ expansions simultaneously control (i) vanishing/nonvanishing of $\binom{n}{k}\bmod p$ (Lucas) and (ii) accumulation of $p$-powers in factorial ratios via carries (Kummer), enabling a coupled minimality+valuation determination.

critical_trick(s): Use the shared interface "base-$p$ digits of $n$ and $k$": Lucas gives the mod $p$ residue and Kummer/Legendre gives the $p$-adic valuation.
For sum-of-binomial divisibility variants, interpret the sum as counting objects modulo $p$ or track valuation term-by-term.

Exploit the engineered base-$p$ suffix of $n$: Lucas forces the first vanishing coefficient to occur at $k^*=(a+1)p^{t}$, and the block of $s$ zeros forces exactly $s+1$ carries in Kummer's theorem, giving $e=s+1$.
After determining $(k^*,e)$, compute $k^*+p^e$ and normalize by reduction modulo $1000$ into $\{0,1,\dots,999\}$.

title: Digit-box moments for indices with $\binom{n}{k}\not\equiv 0\pmod p$ (Lucas + factorized second moment)

identity.short_name: Digit-box moments for indices with $\binom{n}{k}\not\equiv 0\pmod p$ (Lucas + factorized second moment)

problem_spec.summary: For a prime $p$ and an integer $n$ specified by a fixed base-$p$ digit vector (with at least one controlled zero digit), use Lucas' theorem to identify the set of indices $k$ for which $\binom{n}{k}\not\equiv0\pmod p$ as a Cartesian digit-box. Compute both the size of this set and the sum of squares of its elements, and output their sum modulo $1000$.
givens: $p\ge5$ is an odd prime
$d_0,d_1,d_2,d_3,d_4,d_5\in\{0,1,\dots,p-1\}$ with a guard that at least one $d_i\ne p-1$
Define $n=d_0+d_1p+d_2p^2+d_3p^3+d_4p^4+d_5p^5$ (i.e., the base-$p$ digits of $n$ are $(d_0,\dots,d_5)$ from least to most significant)
For $0\le k\le n$, define $A_k=\binom{n}{k}$
Define $S=\{k\in\{0,1,\dots,n\}: p\nmid A_k\}$, $C=|S|$, and $Q=\sum_{k\in S}k^2$
ask: (i) Using Lucas' theorem, show that $S$ is exactly the set of $k$ whose base-$p$ digits $(k_0,\dots,k_5)$ satisfy $0\le k_i\le d_i$, and deduce $C=\prod_{i=0}^5(d_i+1)$.
(ii) Compute $Q=\sum_{k\in S}k^2$ by digit-box moment factorization and report the normalized integer $N\in\{0,\dots,999\}$ with $N\equiv C+Q\pmod{1000}$.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: modulus/base for Lucas' theorem and for the base-$p$ digit system

parameter(s).name: d_0
parameter(s).purpose: least significant base-$p$ digit of $n$

parameter(s).name: d_1
parameter(s).purpose: second base-$p$ digit of $n$

parameter(s).name: d_2
parameter(s).purpose: third base-$p$ digit of $n$

parameter(s).name: d_3
parameter(s).purpose: fourth base-$p$ digit of $n$

parameter(s).name: d_4
parameter(s).purpose: fifth base-$p$ digit of $n$

parameter(s).name: d_5
parameter(s).purpose: sixth base-$p$ digit of $n$; ensures a fixed 6-digit digit-box interface

invariant(s).name: fixed_six_digit_base_p_n
invariant(s).statement: The integer $n=d_0+d_1p+d_2p^2+d_3p^3+d_4p^4+d_5p^5$ has base-$p$ digits $(d_0,\dots,d_5)$ (least to most significant).

invariant(s).name: Lucas_nonvanishing_digit_criterion
invariant(s).statement: For $0\le k\le n$, Lucas' theorem implies $\binom{n}{k}\not\equiv 0\pmod p$ iff every base-$p$ digit of $k$ is at most the corresponding digit of $n$.

invariant(s).name: digit_box_model_for_S
invariant(s).statement: The set $S=\{k: p\nmid \binom{n}{k}\}$ is in bijection with the Cartesian product $\prod_{i=0}^{5}\{0,\dots,d_i\}$ (a digit-box).

invariant(s).name: digit_box_second_moment_factorization
invariant(s).statement: The second moment $Q=\sum_{k\in S}k^2$ can be computed by expanding $k^2$ in base $p$ digits and factoring the resulting sums over the digit-box into one-digit moments.

essential_assumption(s): $p$ is prime so Lucas' theorem applies.
Binomial coefficients are integers, so divisibility by $p$ is well-defined.
Normalization: reduce $C+Q$ modulo $1000$ and take the canonical representative in $\{0,\dots,999\}$.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Lucas' theorem forces $S$ exactly as a digitwise-inequality set, hence $S$ (and thus $C$) is unique.
The digit-box structure and digit-box factorization force the moment computation for $Q=\sum_{k\in S}k^2$, hence $Q$ is unique.
Normalization modulo $1000$ selects a unique $N\in\{0,\dots,999\}$.

technique(s).purpose: Compute $Q=\sum_{k\in S}k^2$ by factoring one-digit moments and cross moments.

technique(s).purpose: Reduce $C+Q$ modulo $1000$ and report the canonical representative.

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Base-$p$ expansions convert a modular binomial constraint into digitwise independent constraints, enabling separable enumeration and moment computation.

critical_trick(s): Replace $\{k: p\nmid\binom{n}{k}\}$ by a digit-box via Lucas, then compute $Q=\sum k^2$ by digit-box moment-factorization of the square expansion.

Correctness checks:

Preflight attempts:

task(s).reason:
Canonicalize the parent-instance and daughter-instance defining $n$ expressions as polynomials in a formal variable $p$ using sympy (expand + simplify + together + factor + Poly).

Confirm the canonical forms do not match under these transforms.

invariant(s).name: fixed_six_digit_base_p_n
invariant(s).statement: $n=d_0+d_1p+d_2p^2+d_3p^3+d_4p^4+d_5p^5$ has base-$p$ digits $(d_0,\dots,d_5)$ from least to most significant.

invariant(s).name: Lucas_digitwise_nonvanishing
invariant(s).statement: For prime $p$, Lucas' theorem implies $p\nmid \binom{n}{k}$ if and only if each base-$p$ digit of $k$ is at most the corresponding base-$p$ digit of $n$.

invariant(s).name: digit_box_structure
invariant(s).statement: The set $S=\{k: p\nmid \binom{n}{k}\}$ is in bijection with the Cartesian product $\prod_{i=0}^5\{0,\dots,d_i\}$.

invariant(s).name: digit_box_second_moment
invariant(s).statement: The sum $Q=\sum_{k\in S}k^2$ can be computed by expanding $k^2$ in base-$p$ digits and factoring digitwise moments over the digit-box.

essential_assumption(s): $p$ is prime so Lucas' theorem applies.
Normalization: choose the unique $N$ with $0\le N<1000$ and $N\equiv C+Q\pmod{1000}$.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Lucas' theorem forces a unique digitwise description of $S$, hence $S$ and $C$ are unique.
Digit-box factorization forces a unique value of $Q=\sum_{k\in S}k^2$.
Reduction modulo $1000$ and the condition $0\le N<1000$ produce a unique output.

outputs: criterion $p\nmid\binom{n}{k}$ iff $k_i\le n_i$ for all digits

technique(s).purpose: Compute $Q=\sum_{k\in S}k^2$ from one-digit moments and cross moments.

technique(s).name: mod_1000_reduction
technique(s).purpose: Compute $N\equiv C+Q\pmod{1000}$ with $0\le N<1000$.

theorem(s).name: finite_sum_identities

critical_trick(s): Use Lucas' theorem to turn $p\nmid \binom{n}{k}$ into digit inequalities, then compute $\sum_{k\in S}k^2$ by digit moment factorization.

title: Lucas digit-box: square-moment mod $2^3$ and exponential sum mod $5^3$, coupled by CRT

identity.short_name: Lucas digit-box: square-moment mod $2^3$ and exponential sum mod $5^3$, coupled by CRT

problem_spec.summary: Use Lucas' theorem to identify indices k for which p does not divide binom(n,k) as a digit-box in base p. Compute a polynomial digit-box sum Q = sum_{k in S} k^2 modulo 2^3, and an exponential digit-box sum R = sum_{k in S} a^k modulo 5^3 using prime-power binomial expansions; then couple the residues with CRT to obtain a unique N modulo 1000.
givens: $p$ is a prime
$p\equiv 1\pmod{8}$
$a\equiv 1\pmod{5}$
For $0\le k\le n$, $A_k=\binom{n}{k}$
$S=\{k\in\{0,1,\dots,n\}: p\nmid A_k\}$
$Q=\sum_{k\in S}k^2$ and $R=\sum_{k\in S}a^k$
$N$ satisfies $N\equiv Q\pmod{8}$ and $N\equiv R\pmod{125}$
ask: Determine the unique integer $N$ with $0\le N<1000$ satisfying $N\equiv Q\pmod{8}$ and $N\equiv R\pmod{125}$.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: Lucas modulus and base; also makes $p^{i}\equiv 1\pmod{8}$.

parameter(s).name: a
parameter(s).purpose: base for the exponential sum modulo $5^3$ enabling binomial truncation.

parameter(s).name: d_0
parameter(s).purpose: least significant base-$p$ digit of $n$

parameter(s).name: d_1
parameter(s).purpose: second base-$p$ digit of $n$

parameter(s).name: d_2
parameter(s).purpose: third base-$p$ digit of $n$

parameter(s).name: d_3
parameter(s).purpose: fourth base-$p$ digit of $n$

parameter(s).name: d_4
parameter(s).purpose: fifth base-$p$ digit of $n$

parameter(s).name: d_5
parameter(s).purpose: sixth base-$p$ digit of $n$

invariant(s).name: fixed_six_digit_base_p_n
invariant(s).statement: $n=d_0+d_1p+d_2p^2+d_3p^3+d_4p^4+d_5p^5$ has base-$p$ digits $(d_0,\dots,d_5)$.

invariant(s).name: Lucas_digit_box_for_S
invariant(s).statement: Lucas' theorem implies $S=\{k: p\nmid\binom{n}{k}\}$ is the digit-box $0\le k_i\le d_i$ in base $p$.

invariant(s).name: square_moment_digit_factorization
invariant(s).statement: Expanding $k^2$ in base-$p$ digits and factoring digit moments yields a closed formula for $Q$.

invariant(s).name: mod8_collapse_when_p_is_1_mod_8
invariant(s).statement: If $p\equiv 1\pmod{8}$ then all powers $p^{i}\equiv 1\pmod{8}$, simplifying $Q\bmod 8$.

invariant(s).name: exponential_sum_digit_factorization
invariant(s).statement: $R=\sum_{k\in S}a^k$ factors as a product of digit-wise geometric sums $\sum_{d=0}^{d_i}(a^{p^i})^d$.

invariant(s).name: prime_power_binomial_truncation_mod_125
invariant(s).statement: If $a\equiv 1\pmod{5}$ then writing $a=1+5t$ and truncating the binomial expansion computes powers modulo $125$.

invariant(s).name: CRT_coupling_for_unique_N
invariant(s).statement: Since $\gcd(8,125)=1$, the pair $(Q\bmod 8,\;R\bmod 125)$ determines a unique class $N\bmod 1000$.

essential_assumption(s): $p$ is prime (Lucas applies).
$p\equiv 1\pmod{8}$.
$a\equiv 1\pmod{5}$.
Normalization: output the unique $N$ with $0\le N<1000$.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Lucas' theorem uniquely determines $S$ from the digits of $n$.
Digit-box moment and exponential factorization uniquely determine $Q\bmod 8$ and $R\bmod 125$.
CRT uniquely determines $N\bmod 1000$ since $\gcd(8,125)=1$, and $0\le N<1000$ fixes $N$.

title: Gaussian norm representation drives a quadratic congruence modulo $q^2$ with a prime-square obstruction

identity.short_name: Gaussian norm representation drives a quadratic congruence modulo $q^2$ with a prime-square obstruction

problem_spec.summary: A prime $p\equiv 1\pmod 4$ is encoded via its normalized Gaussian norm representation $p=x^2+y^2$, producing a canonical residue $t\equiv x y^{-1}\pmod p$ with $t^2\equiv -1\pmod p$. This $t$ defines a quadratic polynomial $f(n)$ whose roots are counted modulo $q^2$. Under the guard $q\mid (t^2-4(t+\delta))$, completion of the square reduces the problem to square roots of the discriminant $D$ modulo $q^2$, and a valuation obstruction shows $D$ is not a square when $v_q(D)=1$. The output is a fixed linear form in $x,y,M,\delta$ reduced modulo $1000$.
givens: Let $p$ and $q$ be distinct odd primes with $p\equiv 1\pmod 4$.
Let $(x,y)$ be the unique integers with $x>y>0$ and $x^2+y^2=p$.
Let $\delta$ be a fixed integer parameter.
Define $t$ as the least positive residue of $x y^{-1}\pmod p$.
Define $f(n)\equiv n^2+t n+(t+\delta)\pmod{q^2}$.
Let $M=|\{0\le n\le q^2-1: f(n)\equiv 0\pmod{q^2}\}|$.
Assume the singular discriminant guard $q\mid (t^2-4(t+\delta))$.
ask: Compute the unique $A$ with $0\le A<1000$ such that $A\equiv 2x+7y+9M+\delta\pmod{1000}$.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: ensures existence and normalized uniqueness of $p=x^2+y^2$ in $\mathbb{Z}[i]$

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: prime-square modulus $q^2$ where square-root lifting can fail in the singular regime

parameter(s).name: delta
parameter(s).purpose: shifts the discriminant $D=t^2-4(t+\delta)$ and controls the $q$-adic valuation regime

parameter(s).name: normalization_rule
parameter(s).purpose: removes unit and conjugation ambiguity so $(x,y)$ and $t$ are unique

invariant(s).name: unique_gaussian_norm_representation
invariant(s).statement: For prime $p\equiv 1\pmod 4$ there exist integers $x,y$ with $p=x^2+y^2$, and the rule $x>y>0$ selects a unique pair.

invariant(s).name: t_is_sqrt_minus_one_mod_p
invariant(s).statement: With $t\equiv x y^{-1}\pmod p$, one has $t^2\equiv -1\pmod p$.

invariant(s).name: completion_of_square_mod_q2
invariant(s).statement: For $D=t^2-4(t+\delta)$ and odd $q$, $f(n)\equiv 0\pmod{q^2}$ is equivalent to $(2n+t)^2\equiv D\pmod{q^2}$.

invariant(s).name: prime_square_sqrt_obstruction
invariant(s).statement: If $q\mid D$ but $q^2\nmid D$, then $z^2\equiv D\pmod{q^2}$ has no solutions.

essential_assumption(s): The condition $p\equiv 1\pmod 4$ ensures $p=x^2+y^2$ and supports defining $t\equiv x y^{-1}\pmod p$.
The rule $x>y>0$ is essential to make $(x,y)$ and hence $t$ unique.
$q$ is odd so $2$ and $4$ are invertible modulo $q^2$, enabling completion of the square.
The guard $q\mid (t^2-4(t+\delta))$ forces the singular discriminant regime modulo $q$.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Normalization $x>y>0$ gives a unique $(x,y)$ and hence a unique $t$ and $D$.
If $q\mid D$ and $v_q(D)=1$ then $z^2\equiv D\pmod{q^2}$ has no solutions, so $M=0$ is forced.
The congruence modulo $1000$ together with $0\le A<1000$ makes $A$ unique.

technique(s).name: unique_factorization_Zi_optional
technique(s).purpose: justify existence and normalized uniqueness of $p=x^2+y^2$

technique(s).name: gaussian_euclidean_algorithm
inputs: $p$ and a square root $u$ of $-1\pmod p$
technique(s).purpose: compute a Gaussian gcd to obtain an explicit representation $p=x^2+y^2$

technique(s).name: norm_to_modular_interface
technique(s).purpose: produce $t\equiv x y^{-1}\pmod p$ and deduce $t^2\equiv -1\pmod p$

technique(s).name: completion_square_prime_power
technique(s).purpose: rewrite $f(n)\equiv 0\pmod{q^2}$ as $(2n+t)^2\equiv D\pmod{q^2}$
outputs: reduction to a square-root congruence for $D$ modulo $q^2$

technique(s).name: valuation_obstruction_prime_square
technique(s).purpose: decide solvability of $z^2\equiv D\pmod{q^2}$ in the singular case $q\mid D$
outputs: either no solutions when $v_q(D)=1$, or $q$ solutions when $v_q(D)\ge 2$

technique(s).name: modular_reduction_output
technique(s).purpose: apply the required normalization modulo $1000$

theorem(s).name: sum_of_two_squares_theorem
theorem(s).role: ensures existence and uniqueness up to signs and swap of $p=x^2+y^2$ for prime $p\equiv 1\pmod 4$

theorem(s).name: Euler_criterion_for_-1
theorem(s).role: implies existence of $u$ with $u^2\equiv -1\pmod p$ when $p\equiv 1\pmod 4$

theorem(s).name: Hensel_lemma_singular_obstruction
theorem(s).role: explains why a root modulo $q$ at $z\equiv 0$ does not lift to a root modulo $q^2$ unless $q^2\mid D$

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Local information at a prime power controls global root structure for polynomial congruences.

critical_trick(s): Compute $p=x^2+y^2$ via a Gaussian gcd using an explicit $u$ with $u^2\equiv -1\pmod p$.
Complete the square to reduce $f(n)\equiv 0\pmod{q^2}$ to $z^2\equiv D\pmod{q^2}$.
Use the valuation obstruction: $v_q(D)=1$ implies $D$ is not a square modulo $q^2$, forcing $M=0$.
